This depth-first behavior is what makes DFS uniquely suited to detect **cycles, strongly connected components, and topological orderings** in a digital netlist.

---

## 1.3 Core Vocabulary for KTU Examinations

| Term | Symbol | Meaning in VLSI CAD Context |
|------|--------|-----------------------------|
| Vertex | $v \in V$ | A logic gate, standard cell, or netlist node |
| Edge | $(u,v) \in E$ | A wire/net connecting two circuit nodes |
| Discovery Time | $d[v]$ | Timestamp when node $v$ is first visited |
| Finish Time | $f[v]$ | Timestamp when DFS finishes processing $v$ (after backtracking) |
| Color (White/Gray/Black) | — | White = unvisited, Gray = on stack, Black = finished |
| Spanning Tree/Forest | $T$ | Subgraph containing all DFS tree edges |
| Back Edge | — | Edge to an ancestor (in DFS tree) — indicates a **cycle** |

---

## 1.4 Why DFS Matters in CAD-Based VLSI Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Industrial Relevance:**
> - **Combinational Loop Detection:** A back edge in DFS = a combinational loop, which is illegal in synchronous digital circuits.
> - **Topological Sorting:** The reverse of DFS finish times yields a valid gate-level netlist ordering for simulation.
> - **Reachability Analysis:** Determines whether a clock-gating cell can affect a register — critical for **clock domain crossing (CDC)** checks.
> - **Connected Components:** Identifies isolated power domains or floating nets in physical design.
> - **Static Timing Analysis (STA) Prerequisite:** DFS over the timing graph precedes longest-path algorithms.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The DFS Operational Model

DFS can be understood as operating on three logical phases per node:

1. **Pre-Visit Phase (Discovery):** Mark the node as visited (gray). Record discovery time $d[v]$.
2. **Recursive Descent:** For each unvisited (white) neighbor $w$ of $v$, recurse into $w$. The edge $(v, w)$ is recorded as a **tree edge**.
3. **Post-Visit Phase (Finish):** When all neighbors are processed, mark $v$ as finished (black). Record finish time $f[v]$. Backtrack to parent.

### Color Encoding Logic
- **White** — Vertex has not yet been discovered.
- **Gray** — Vertex has been discovered but DFS has not yet finished exploring it (still on the recursion stack).
- **Black** — DFS has finished with this vertex (all descendants explored).

This color discipline is the **foundation of cycle detection** in netlists.

---

## 2.2 Edge Classification Theorem

During DFS, every edge $(u, v)$ in the graph is classified into exactly one of four categories. This classification is a **high-yield KTU exam topic**.

| Edge Type | Condition | VLSI CAD Meaning |
|-----------|-----------|------------------|
| **Tree Edge** | $v$ is white when $(u,v)$ is examined | $v$ is discovered for the first time through $u$ |
| **Back Edge** | $v$ is gray when $(u,v)$ is examined | Points from descendant to ancestor → **combinational loop** |
| **Forward Edge** | $v$ is black and $d[u] < d[v]$ | Non-tree edge to a descendant (only in **directed** graphs) |
| **Cross Edge** | $v$ is black and $d[u] > d[v]$ | Connects two unrelated subtrees (only in **directed** graphs) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Undirected Graph Theorem:** In an undirected graph, DFS produces **only tree edges and back edges**. Forward and cross edges cannot occur. This is a frequently-asked 3-mark question.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Expression | Units / Domain | Exam Relevance |
|---|--------------------|------------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | Time Complexity | $T(n) = O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Vertices + Edges | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | Space Complexity (recursion stack) | $S(n) = O(\vert V \vert)$ | Worst case: linear chain | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | Time Stamp Interval | $1 \le d[v] < f[v] \le 2\vert V \vert$ | Integer timestamps | ⭐⭐ |
| 4 | Parenthesis Theorem | $[d[u], f[u]]$ and $[d[v], f[v]]$ are either nested or disjoint | Valid for DFS order | ⭐⭐ |
| 5 | Edge Type via Timestamps (directed) | Back edge: $d[v] < d[u] < f[u] < f[v]$ | $v$ is gray ancestor of $u$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | Cycle Existence Criterion | $\exists$ back edge $\iff$ graph has cycle | Boolean | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | Total DFS Iterations (timestamp counter) | $\text{counter} = 2\vert V \vert$ at termination | Count invariant | ⭐ |
| 8 | Topological Sort Output | Reverse of decreasing $f[v]$ | Linear ordering of DAG | ⭐⭐⭐ |

> **Note on notation:** $\vert V \vert$ denotes the cardinality (count) of the vertex set. Use `\vert V \vert` in LaTeX (never plain `|V|` inside tables) to avoid markdown rendering issues.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| CAD Stage | DFS Application | Consequence of Failure |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------------|
| **Logic Synthesis** | Cycle detection in gate-level netlists | Combinational loops cause oscillation |
| **Place & Route** | Reachability in clock-tree topology | Missing paths = unclocked flops |
| **Formal Verification** | State-space exploration in equivalence checking | Liveness property violations |
| **DFT (Design for Test)** | Scan-chain connectivity validation | Reduced fault coverage |
| **Power Analysis** | Identifying switching-activity propagation chains | Inaccurate power estimation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Trace: DFS on a Sample Netlist Graph

Consider a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ representing a small CMOS combinational block with **6 vertices** and **8 edges**:

$$V = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$$

$$E = \{(1,2),\ (1,3),\ (2,4),\ (2,5),\ (3,5),\ (4,6),\ (5,6),\ (6,3)\}$$

We will perform DFS starting at vertex **1**. A global counter `time` begins at 0 and is incremented at every discovery and finish event.

---

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Execution Trace

| Step | Action | Current Vertex | Counter | Stack (Call) | Edge Classified |
|------|--------|----------------|---------|--------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Visit vertex 1 (discover) | 1 | time = 1 → $d[1]=1$ | [1] | — |
| 2 | Examine neighbor 2 of 1 → white | 1 → 2 | time = 2 → $d[2]=2$ | [1, 2] | Tree edge (1,2) |
| 3 | Examine neighbor 4 of 2 → white | 2 → 4 | time = 3 → $d[4]=3$ | [1, 2, 4] | Tree edge (2,4) |
| 4 | Examine neighbor 6 of 4 → white | 4 → 6 | time = 4 → $d[6]=4$ | [1, 2, 4, 6] | Tree edge (4,6) |
| 5 | Examine neighbor 3 of 6 → white | 6 → 3 | time = 5 → $d[3]=5$ | [1, 2, 4, 6, 3] | Tree edge (6,3) |
| 6 | Examine neighbor 5 of 3 → white | 3 → 5 | time = 6 → $d[5]=6$ | [1, 2, 4, 6, 3, 5] | Tree edge (3,5) |
| 7 | Examine neighbor 6 of 5 → **black** (already finished? no — still gray) | 5 | time = 6 | [1, 2, 4, 6, 3, 5] | **Back edge (5,6)** ⛔ CYCLE! |
| 8 | All neighbors of 5 done → finish 5 | — | time = 7 → $f[5]=7$ | [1, 2, 4, 6, 3] | — |
| 9 | All neighbors of 3 done → finish 3 | — | time = 8 → $f[3]=8$ | [1, 2, 4, 6] | — |
| 10 | All neighbors of 6 done → finish 6 | — | time = 9 → $f[6]=9$ | [1, 2, 4] | — |
| 11 | All neighbors of 4 done → finish 4 | — | time = 10 → $f[4]=10$ | [1, 2] | — |
| 12 | Examine neighbor 5 of 2 → **black** (finished) | 2 | time = 10 | [1, 2] | **Cross edge (2,5)** |
| 13 | All neighbors of 2 done → finish 2 | — | time = 11 → $f[2]=11$ | [1] | — |
| 14 | Examine neighbor 3 of 1 → **black** (finished) | 1 | time = 11 | [1] | **Cross edge (1,3)** |
| 15 | All neighbors of 1 done → finish 1 | — | time = 12 → $f[1]=12$ | [ ] | — |

### 3.1.2 Final Timestamp Table

| Vertex $v$ | $d[v]$ | $f[v]$ | Interval $[d,f]$ | Status |
|------------|--------|--------|------------------|--------|
| 1 | 1 | 12 | $[1, 12]$ | Root |
| 2 | 2 | 11 | $[2, 11]$ | Nested in 1 |
| 3 | 5 | 8 | $[5, 8]$ | Nested in 6 |
| 4 | 3 | 10 | $[3, 10]$ | Nested in 2 |
| 5 | 6 | 7 | $[6, 7]$ | Nested in 3 |
| 6 | 4 | 9 | $[4, 9]$ | Nested in 4 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parenthesis Theorem Verification:** Notice that the intervals are either **perfectly nested** (e.g., $[2,11] \supset [3,10]$) or **completely disjoint** (e.g., $[1,12]$ contains all others as root). No two intervals ever partially overlap. This is a hallmark of correct DFS execution.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Time Complexity $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$

We derive this bound rigorously by accounting for **every** operation performed by DFS.

### Step 1 — Initialization Cost

The adjacency-list representation of the graph is built once. The initialization of color, parent, and timestamp arrays requires:

$$T_{\text{init}} = \Theta(\vert V \vert)$$

This is a one-time setup cost. (If adjacency-matrix is used, initialization is $\Theta(\vert V \vert^2)$, but the **standard KTU 2024 assumption is adjacency-list**.)

### Step 2 — Cost of Vertex Visits

Each vertex $v \in V$ is discovered exactly once and finished exactly once. These are $O(1)$ operations. Total:

$$T_{\text{vertices}} = \sum_{v \in V} O(1) = O(\vert V \vert)$$

### Step 3 — Cost of Edge Examinations

The total work done while scanning adjacency lists is proportional to the **sum of degrees** of all vertices. For a directed graph, the sum of out-degrees equals $\vert E \vert$:

$$T_{\text{edges}} = \sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = \vert E \vert$$

For an undirected graph, each edge $(u,v)$ appears in both $u$'s and $v$'s adjacency list, so the sum of degrees is $2\vert E \vert$:

$$T_{\text{edges, undirected}} = 2\vert E \vert = O(\vert E \vert)$$

### Step 4 — Combine via Sum of Asymptotics

$$T(n) = T_{\text{init}} + T_{\text{vertices}} + T_{\text{edges}} = \Theta(\vert V \vert) + \Theta(\vert V \vert) + \Theta(\vert E \vert)$$

Applying the rule of asymptotic addition (the dominant term governs):

$$\boxed{T_{\text{DFS}} = \Theta(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for VLSI:** Real netlists have $\vert E \vert \approx k \cdot \vert V \vert$ for small average fan-out $k$ (typically 2–4 for gate-level netlists). So DFS on a million-gate circuit runs in $O(5 \cdot 10^6)$ operations — extremely fast, which is why it is embedded in production EDA tools.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Space Complexity $O(\vert V \vert)$

The space used by DFS is dominated by:

- **Visited/Color/Parent arrays:** $\Theta(\vert V \vert)$
- **Recursion stack (or explicit stack):** In the worst case (a linear chain of $n$ vertices), depth equals $\vert V \vert$, so the stack holds $\vert V \vert$ frames.

$$S_{\text{DFS}} = \Theta(\vert V \vert) + \Theta(\vert V \vert) = \Theta(\vert V \vert)$$

For a 10-million-gate design, this means up to 10 million stack frames in recursive DFS — which is why **industrial CAD tools use iterative DFS with an explicit heap-allocated stack** to avoid OS-level stack overflow.

---

## 3.4 The Cycle Detection Theorem (Formal Proof Sketch)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem:** A directed graph $G$ contains a cycle **if and only if** DFS on $G$ discovers at least one back edge.

**Proof (⇒) — Cycle ⇒ Back Edge:**
Suppose $G$ has a cycle $C = v_1 \to v_2 \to \dots \to v_k \to v_1$. Let $v_i$ be the **first vertex** of $C$ discovered by DFS. When DFS explores $v_i$, all other vertices of $C$ are still white (undiscovered). As DFS proceeds along the cycle, it eventually reaches $v_{i-1}$, whose neighbor $v_i$ is already **gray** (on the stack). The edge $(v_{i-1}, v_i)$ is therefore a back edge.

**Proof (⇐) — Back Edge ⇒ Cycle:**
A back edge $(u, v)$ connects a descendant $u$ to an ancestor $v$. The tree path from $v$ down to $u$ plus the back edge from $u$ to $v$ forms a closed walk — a cycle.

$$\blacksquare$$

This theorem is the **mathematical foundation of combinational loop detection** in VLSI static timing analysis.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DFS Algorithm Flowchart (Iterative Variant with Explicit Stack)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START DFS]) --> initA[Initialize: color all WHITE, time = 0, stack = EMPTY]
    initA --> loopA{For each vertex v in V}
    loopA -- color WHITE --> visitA[Push v onto stack]
    visitA --> markA[Mark v GRAY, d v = time++]
    markA --> innerA{Stack not empty?}
    innerA -- Yes --> peekA[Peek top of stack: u]
    peekA --> scanA[Scan adjacency list of u]
    scanA --> checkA{Unvisited WHITE neighbor w exists?}
    checkA -- Yes --> recurseA[Mark w GRAY, d w = time++, Push w, edge u-w is TREE edge]
    recurseA --> innerA
    checkA -- No --> finishA[Pop u from stack, Mark u BLACK, f u = time++]
    finishA --> innerA
    innerA -- No --> loopA
    loopA -- All vertices processed --> endA([END DFS])
```

> **Reading Guide:** The flowchart shows the **iterative DFS** variant, which is preferred in industrial CAD tools. The recursion path corresponds to "Push," and the backtrack corresponds to "Pop." The classification of edges occurs at the moment a neighbor's color is examined.

---

## 4.2 DFS Spanning Forest on the Sample Netlist

The following Mermaid graph visualizes the traversal order and edge classification from Section 3.1.

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1((1)) --- node2((2))
    node1 --- node3((3))
    node2 --- node4((4))
    node2 --- node5((5))
    node3 --- node5
    node4 --- node6((6))
    node5 --- node6
    node6 --- node3

    classDef treeEdge stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000,fill:#cce5ff
    classDef backEdge stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff,fill:#ffcccc
    classDef crossEdge stroke:#ff8800,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray:5,color:#000000,fill:#ffe5cc

    class node1,node2,node3,node4,node5,node6 treeEdge
```

> **Diagram Note:** In the rendered version, **solid blue lines** denote tree edges (the DFS spanning tree), the **red highlighted edge (5 → 6)** is a back edge indicating a combinational cycle, and **dashed orange lines** are cross edges (e.g., 2 → 5 and 1 → 3) that connect unrelated subtrees.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: DFS in a VLSI CAD Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Netlist Parser] --> B[Graph Builder Adjacency Lists]
    B --> C[DFS Engine Color, d, f Arrays]
    C --> D{Output Subsystem}
    D -- Back Edges --> E[Combinational Loop Reporter]
    D -- Finish Timestamps --> F[Topological Sorter]
    D -- Reachability Map --> G[STA Pre-Processor]
    D -- Spanning Forest --> H[Connected Components Analyzer]

    classDef engine fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#fff0e6,stroke:#993300,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    class A,B engine
    class C engine
    class D,E,F,G,H output
```

> **Industrial Mapping:** The DFS Engine is a single shared module whose output data structures feed **four different downstream CAD tools** — loop detection, topological sort, timing analysis, and partition analysis. This reusability is precisely why DFS is hard-coded into commercial EDA frameworks.

---

## 4.4 DFS Recursion Call Tree (Stack Evolution)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rec1["DFS 1 d=1"] --> rec2["DFS 2 d=2"]
    rec2 --> rec4["DFS 4 d=3"]
    rec4 --> rec6["DFS 6 d=4"]
    rec6 --> rec3["DFS 3 d=5"]
    rec3 --> rec5["DFS 5 d=6 BACK EDGE 5 to 6"]
    rec5 -.Finish f=7.-> rec3
    rec3 -.Finish f=8.-> rec6
    rec6 -.Finish f=9.-> rec4
    rec4 -.Finish f=10.-> rec2
    rec2 -.Finish f=11.-> rec1
    rec1 -.Finish f=12.-> endNode([All vertices processed])
```

> **Reading Guide:** The dashed arrows represent function returns (backtracking). The back edge is detected at the deepest level (vertex 5) when it tries to reach an already-gray vertex 6. This visualizes exactly how the recursion stack unwinds.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define Depth First Search. State its time and space complexity. Why is DFS preferred over BFS for cycle detection in VLSI netlists?*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Depth First Search (DFS) is a graph traversal algorithm that explores a graph by visiting an unvisited adjacent vertex, recursively descending into it, and backtracking only when no unvisited neighbor remains. Each vertex is visited exactly once.

**Complexity (1 mark):**
- Time Complexity: $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$
- Space Complexity: $O(\vert V \vert)$

**Preference for Cycle Detection (0.5 marks):** DFS naturally maintains ancestor-descendant relationships through the recursion stack (gray nodes). The presence of a gray ancestor during neighbor examination directly indicates a back edge, which is mathematically equivalent to a cycle. BFS lacks this ancestor-tracking mechanism and would require a separate algorithm to identify cycles.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is a back edge in DFS? How can back edges be used to detect combinational loops in digital circuits?*

**Model Answer:**

**Back Edge Definition (1.5 marks):** A back edge is an edge $(u, v)$ in a directed graph where $v$ is a gray (currently on the DFS stack) ancestor of $u$ in the DFS tree. Mathematically, $d[v] < d[u] < f[u] < f[v]$ — the intervals are nested, and $v$ is the ancestor.

**Combinational Loop Detection (1.5 marks):** In a digital netlist, a combinational loop exists when a signal propagates through a chain of combinational gates and returns to its starting point without passing through a flip-flop (sequential element). During DFS over the gate-level directed graph:
- A back edge from node $u$ to ancestor $v$ means a path exists from $v$ to $u$ in the DFS tree, plus the direct edge from $u$ back to $v$.
- This forms a directed cycle $v \to \dots \to u \to v$.
- Since the edge has no intervening flip-flop, this is precisely a **combinational loop** — an illegal construct in synchronous digital design that the EDA tool must flag for designer correction.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### 📘 **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** For the directed graph given below, perform a Depth First Search starting from vertex $A$. Clearly show the discovery and finish times of every vertex and classify each edge as Tree, Back, Forward, or Cross edge.

$$V = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$$

$$E = \{(A,B),\ (A,C),\ (B,D),\ (B,E),\ (C,E),\ (D,F),\ (E,F),\ (F,C)\}$$

**(b) [7 Marks)** Prove that in an undirected graph, DFS produces only Tree edges and Back edges. Hence explain why Forward and Cross edges cannot appear.

---

### 📗 **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat flowchart and pseudocode, explain the iterative implementation of DFS using an explicit stack. Discuss why iterative DFS is preferred over recursive DFS in industrial VLSI CAD tools.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe any **three** real-world applications of DFS in CAD-based VLSI design flow. For each application, specify the graph model (vertices, edges) used and the algorithmic role of DFS.

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solution — Question A

### Solution to A(a) — DFS Trace (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Visit A (discovery)**
- $d[A] = 1$, color(A) = GRAY, stack = [A]

**Step 2: From A, visit B (white neighbor)**
- Edge (A,B) is a **Tree edge**
- $d[B] = 2$, color(B) = GRAY, stack = [A, B]

**Step 3: From B, visit D (white neighbor)**
- Edge (B,D) is a **Tree edge**
- $d[D] = 3$, color(D) = GRAY, stack = [A, B, D]

**Step 4: From D, visit F (white neighbor)**
- Edge (D,F) is a **Tree edge**
- $d[F] = 4$, color(F) = GRAY, stack = [A, B, D, F]

**Step 5: From F, examine neighbor C — WHITE**
- Edge (F,C) is a **Tree edge**
- $d[C] = 5$, color(C) = GRAY, stack = [A, B, D, F, C]

**Step 6: From C, examine neighbor E — WHITE**
- Edge (C,E) is a **Tree edge**
- $d[E] = 6$, color(E) = GRAY, stack = [A, B, D, F, C, E]

**Step 7: From E, examine neighbor F — BLACK (already finished? check color)**
- F is **GRAY** (on stack), so edge (E,F) is a **Back edge** → CYCLE detected (E → F → C → E)

**Step 8: Finish E** — $f[E] = 7$, pop from stack

**Step 9: All neighbors of C done — Finish C** — $f[C] = 8$, pop

**Step 10: All neighbors of F done — Finish F** — $f[F] = 9$, pop

**Step 11: All neighbors of D done — Finish D** — $f[D] = 10$, pop

**Step 12: From B, examine neighbor E — BLACK (finished)**
- Edge (B,E) is a **Cross edge** (B is ancestor, E is in different subtree of F)

**Step 13: All neighbors of B done — Finish B** — $f[B] = 11$, pop

**Step 14: From A, examine neighbor C — BLACK (finished)**
- Edge (A,C) is a **Cross edge**

**Step 15: All neighbors of A done — Finish A** — $f[A] = 12$, pop

### Final Timestamp Table

| Vertex | $d[v]$ | $f[v]$ |
|--------|--------|--------|
| A | 1 | 12 |
| B | 2 | 11 |
| C | 5 | 8 |
| D | 3 | 10 |
| E | 6 | 7 |
| F | 4 | 9 |

### Edge Classification Summary

| Edge | Type |
|------|------|
| (A,B) | Tree |
| (A,C) | Cross |
| (B,D) | Tree |
| (B,E) | Cross |
| (C,E) | Tree |
| (D,F) | Tree |
| (E,F) | **Back (Cycle!)** |
| (F,C) | Tree |

---

### Solution to A(b) — Proof (7 Marks)

**Theorem:** In an undirected graph, DFS produces only Tree edges and Back edges.

**Proof:**

Let $G = (V, E)$ be an undirected graph, and let DFS execute from some root. Consider any non-tree edge $e = (u, v)$ examined by DFS.

**Case 1: $u$ and $v$ are in different DFS trees (different connected components).**
Without loss of generality, suppose DFS first visits the tree containing $u$ before the tree containing $v$. When DFS later enters $v$'s tree, the edge $(u, v)$ is examined. At this moment, $u$ is **black** (fully explored). However, in an undirected graph, the same edge will also be examined when DFS is at $v$ and looks at $u$ — at that moment, $u$ is white. This contradicts $e$ being a non-tree edge from the perspective of the **first** component to be explored.

More rigorously: For an undirected graph, each edge is encountered exactly twice — once from each endpoint. The **first** encounter discovers the unvisited endpoint, making the edge a **Tree edge**. Therefore, every edge must be a tree edge of some DFS tree. This eliminates the possibility of forward, cross, or true back edges **in the classical sense**.

**Case 2: $u$ and $v$ are in the same DFS tree.**
Suppose $u$ is discovered before $v$, so the DFS tree has $u$ as ancestor (or in the subtree) of $v$. The edge $(u, v)$ is examined from the later-discovered vertex. If $v$ is the later one and $(u, v)$ connects $v$ to its ancestor $u$, then $u$ is **gray** at the time of examination → **Back edge**.

**Conclusion:** Every edge in an undirected graph is either a **Tree edge** (first encounter discovers the other endpoint) or a **Back edge** (subsequent encounter with an already-discovered ancestor). Hence **forward and cross edges are impossible**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.4 Complete Model Solution — Question B

### Solution to B(a) — Iterative DFS (7 Marks)

**Pseudocode (Stack-based DFS):**

```
PROCEDURE IterativeDFS(G = (V, E), source s)
    FOR each vertex v in V DO
        color[v]  := WHITE
        parent[v] := NIL
    END FOR
    time := 0
    CREATE empty stack S
    PUSH(S, s)
    color[s] := GRAY
    time := time + 1
    d[s] := time

    WHILE S is not empty DO
        u := TOP(S)
        IF exists white neighbor w of u THEN
            color[w] := GRAY
            parent[w] := u
            time := time + 1
            d[w] := time
            PUSH(S, w)
            CLASSIFY edge (u, w) as TREE edge
        ELSE
            POP(S, u)
            color[u] := BLACK
            time := time + 1
            f[u] := time
        END IF
    END WHILE
END PROCEDURE
```

**Flowchart** — see Section 4.1 of these notes (identical iterative flow).

**Why Iterative DFS in Industrial CAD (3 marks for discussion):**

1. **Stack Overflow Avoidance:** Real VLSI netlists can have **millions of vertices in a single connected component** (e.g., a large datapath). Recursive DFS would push millions of stack frames onto the OS call stack, causing a **stack overflow crash**. Iterative DFS uses a heap-allocated stack whose size is configurable.

2. **Deterministic Memory Usage:** Embedded EDA tools (Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus) run with bounded memory budgets. Iterative DFS allows precise pre-allocation of the working stack.

3. **Interruptibility & Checkpointing:** Long-running CAD jobs need to be **pauseable and resumable**. The explicit stack state can be saved to disk between iterations, enabling incremental processing — impossible with the hidden call stack of recursion.

---

### Solution to B(b) — Three DFS Applications in VLSI CAD (7 Marks)

**Application 1: Combinational Loop Detection (2.5 marks)**
- **Graph Model:** Vertices = combinational gates; Directed edges = gate-to-gate signal nets.
- **Algorithmic Role:** DFS marks each node with discover/finish times. The appearance of a **back edge** (gray-to-gray) is mathematically equivalent to the existence of a cycle. EDA tools report the loop and the participating gates so the designer can break the cycle by inserting a flip-flop or buffer chain.

**Application 2: Topological Sorting for Gate-Level Simulation (2.5 marks)**
- **Graph Model:** Vertices = logic gates; Edges = signal dependencies.
- **Algorithmic Role:** After DFS, vertices are sorted in **decreasing order of finish time $f[v]$**. This produces a valid topological order — each gate is evaluated only after all its predecessors. The simulator processes gates in this order, eliminating the need for iterative convergence.

**Application 3: Reachability Analysis for Clock Domain Crossing (CDC) (2 marks)**
- **Graph Model:** Vertices = flip-flops; Edges = combinational paths between flops.
- **Algorithmic Role:** DFS from each clock-domain source determines which flops are reachable. Any flop reachable from **two different clock domains** is a **CDC violation** — it requires synchronizer insertion to prevent metastability. Without DFS, this multi-million-gate reachability computation would be infeasible.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Black and Gray:** Many students mark a node "black" the moment it is discovered, which destroys cycle detection logic. The rule is: **GRAY = on stack (cycle possible), BLACK = fully processed (no cycles through this node).**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Update Counter for Finishes:** The timestamp counter must be incremented at BOTH discovery and finish. A common error is to increment only at discovery, giving finish times = discovery times. This breaks the Parenthesis Theorem and edge classification.
>
> 3. **Claiming Cross/Forward Edges Exist in Undirected Graphs:** The proof in A(b) explicitly rules this out. Examiners will deduct 2 marks for stating "DFS can produce cross edges" without specifying the graph is directed.
>
> 4. **Writing $O(n)$ instead of $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$:** The correct time complexity has **two terms**. Writing only $O(\vert V \vert)$ is incomplete because edge processing contributes $\vert E \vert$.
>
> 5. **Confusing DFS Spanning Tree with BFS Spanning Tree:** The DFS tree has **maximum depth** in a connected graph (can be a Hamiltonian path), whereas BFS trees have **minimum height**. Examiners test this distinction explicitly.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

- ✅ **DFS Definition:** Recursive descent graph traversal that explores deeply before backtracking.
- ✅ **Time Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ — derived from initialization + vertex visits + edge scans.
- ✅ **Space Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert)$ — driven by recursion stack or explicit stack in worst case (linear graph).
- ✅ **Three Colors:** White (unvisited), Gray (on stack / cycle candidate), Black (finished / safe).
- ✅ **Four Edge Types (Directed Graphs):** Tree, Back, Forward, Cross.
- ✅ **Two Edge Types (Undirected Graphs):** Tree and Back only — proof in Question A(b).
- ✅ **Parenthesis Theorem:** DFS intervals are either nested or disjoint; never partially overlapping.
- ✅ **Cycle Theorem:** Back edge exists $\iff$ directed cycle exists (used in netlist loop detection).
- ✅ **Topological Sort:** Vertices listed in decreasing order of finish time $f[v]$.
- ✅ **Industrial Preference:** Iterative DFS is used in production EDA tools to avoid stack overflow on million-gate netlists.
- ✅ **CAD Applications:** Combinational loop detection, topological sort, reachability/CDC analysis, connected component isolation, and scan-chain validation.
- ✅ **Whiteboard Tip:** Always draw the recursion stack alongside the graph to visually verify color transitions and edge classification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Topological Sort
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT525 - CAD BASED VLSI DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Topological Sort

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Topological Sort (Topological Ordering)** is a linear ordering of the vertices of a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** such that for every directed edge $(u, v)$ connecting vertex $u$ to vertex $v$, the vertex $u$ appears **before** $v$ in the ordering.

In the context of **VLSI Computer-Aided Design (CAD)**, topological sort is the foundational combinatorial algorithm used to impose a valid execution order on dependent tasks such as synthesis passes, placement iterations, routing tasks, timing analysis, and design rule checking (DRC). Since the EDA design flow contains inherent **precedence constraints** (e.g., floorplanning must precede placement, placement must precede routing), topological sort is the mathematical engine that resolves these dependencies into a single executable schedule.

A DAG $G = (V, E)$ admits a topological ordering **if and only if** it contains **no directed cycles**. If a cycle exists, the algorithm must report a failure, which in VLSI corresponds to detecting **combinational loops** in the netlist — a fatal design error.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — University Course Registration**
>
> Imagine you are registering for university courses. Every course has a list of **prerequisites**:
> - To register for *VLSI Design*, you must have completed *Digital Electronics*.
> - To register for *Physical Design*, you must have completed *VLSI Design*.
> - To register for *Design Verification*, you must have completed *VLSI Design*.
>
> Topological sort is the algorithm your registration system runs internally. It scans all prerequisite chains and produces a **valid semester-wise course schedule** such that you never attempt a course before its prerequisites. If the system detects a loop (e.g., Course A requires Course B, and Course B requires Course A), it **rejects the plan** because no such schedule exists.
>
> In VLSI CAD, *courses* represent **synthesis/physical-design tasks**, and *prerequisites* represent **data dependencies** in the design flow graph.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Time Complexity (Kahn / DFS) | $T(n, m)$ | $\mathbf{O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)}$ |
| Space Complexity | $S(n)$ | $\mathbf{O(\vert V \vert)}$ |
| In-degree maximum | $\delta^{-}(v)$ | $\mathbf{0 \leq \delta^{-}(v) < \vert V \vert}$ |
| Cycle detection threshold | $k$ | $\mathbf{k < \vert V \vert}$ (order incomplete) |

---

### 1.4 Formal Properties of Topological Sort

1. **Existence**: A DAG $G$ has at least one topological ordering. (Guaranteed because DAGs have no cycles, ensuring no contradictions in precedence.)
2. **Uniqueness**: A topological sort is **unique** if and only if the DAG has a **Hamiltonian path** (a directed path visiting every vertex exactly once).
3. **Count**: The number of distinct topological orderings of a DAG is given by the number of **linear extensions** of its partial order. This can range from **1** (e.g., a single chain) to $\mathbf{\vert V \vert !}$ (e.g., an edgeless graph).
4. **Cycle Implication**: If the topological sort produces a sequence of length $< \vert V \vert$, the graph contains a **directed cycle** and is not a DAG.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PEEVT525 Module 1):**
> Topological sort appears as a **graph-algorithmic primitive** underpinning combinational circuit analysis, scheduling of CAD tool flow, and feedback-loop detection in netlist traversal.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation — Partial Orders and Posets

Topological sort is fundamentally a **linear extension** of a **partially ordered set (poset)**.

A **partially ordered set** is a pair $(S, \preceq)$ where $S$ is a set and $\preceq$ is a binary relation satisfying:
- **Reflexivity**: $\forall a \in S, \; a \preceq a$
- **Antisymmetry**: $a \preceq b \text{ and } b \preceq a \Rightarrow a = b$
- **Transitivity**: $a \preceq b \text{ and } b \preceq c \Rightarrow a \preceq c$

A **linear extension** converts this partial order into a total order $\leq$ such that $a \preceq b \Rightarrow a \leq b$.

---

### 2.2 Two Canonical Algorithms

#### A. Kahn's Algorithm (BFS / Source-Removal Method)
This is the **inductively defined** version, widely used in industrial EDA tools because of its natural ability to detect cycles during execution.

**Logical Steps:**
1. Compute the **in-degree** $\delta^{-}(v)$ of every vertex $v \in V$.
2. Initialize a **queue** (or min-heap for lexicographic order) with all vertices whose in-degree is **0** (the **sources** of the DAG).
3. While the queue is **non-empty**:
   - Dequeue vertex $u$ (smallest-id if heap is used).
   - Append $u$ to the output order.
   - For every outgoing edge $(u, v) \in E$:
     - Decrement $\delta^{-}(v)$ by 1.
     - If $\delta^{-}(v) = 0$, enqueue $v$.
4. **Cycle Check**: If the output order contains fewer than $\vert V \vert$ vertices, the graph contains a **directed cycle**.

#### B. Depth-First Search (DFS) Based Algorithm
1. Mark all vertices as **unvisited**.
2. For every unvisited vertex $u$, invoke `DFS-Visit(u)`:
   - Mark $u$ as **visiting** (gray).
   - For every outgoing edge $(u, v)$:
     - If $v$ is unvisited, recurse on $v$.
     - If $v$ is **visiting** (gray), a **back-edge** exists $\Rightarrow$ cycle detected.
   - Mark $u$ as **visited** (black) and **prepend** $u$ to a global output list.
3. The final output list (read in order) is the topological sort.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Utility |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | In-degree of vertex $v$ | $\delta^{-}(v) = \sum_{u \in V} \mathbb{1}[(u,v) \in E]$ | Identifies **source** vertices ($=0$) ready to be scheduled. |
| 2 | Out-degree of vertex $v$ | $\delta^{+}(v) = \sum_{w \in V} \mathbb{1}[(v,w) \in E]$ | Identifies **sink** vertices ($=0$) — final tasks. |
| 3 | Kahn's algorithm runtime | $T = \mathbf{O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)}$ | Linear in graph size; scales to **millions of cells** in modern netlists. |
| 4 | DFS-based runtime | $T = \mathbf{O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)}$ | Same asymptotic bound; deeper recursion stack needed. |
| 5 | Cycle detection criterion | $\vert \text{Output} \vert < \vert V \vert$ | Detects **combinational loops** in netlist — fatal synthesis error. |
| 6 | Sum of in-degrees identity | $\sum_{v \in V} \delta^{-}(v) = \vert E \vert$ | Validates graph construction; used in BFS initialization. |
| 7 | Critical path length (longest) | $L_{crit} = \max_{v \in V} \big[ \, L(v) \, \big]$ | Determines **minimum clock period** in pipelined scheduling. |
| 8 | Topological level of $v$ | $L(v) = 1 + \max_{(u,v) \in E} L(u)$ | Computed *during* topological sort; enables **ASAP scheduling**. |
| 9 | Mobility of task $v$ | $\mu(v) = L_{ALAP}(v) - L_{ASAP}(v)$ | High-level synthesis slack calculation. |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in VLSI CAD

- **High-Level Synthesis (HLS)**: Operations (additions, multiplications, memory loads) are modeled as nodes; data dependencies are edges. Topological sort produces an **ASAP (As-Soon-As-Possible) schedule**.
- **Static Timing Analysis (STA)**: The combinational logic graph is topologically sorted before forward propagation of arrival times, ensuring all predecessor delays are known.
- **Netlist Concretization**: Gates whose outputs feed other gates must be processed before those consumers.
- **Clock-Tree Synthesis Preprocessing**: Buffer insertion ordering uses the topological depth of sinks.
- **Incremental Synthesis**: Re-running topological sort on a modified sub-DAG efficiently reschedules changed regions.

> [!NOTE]
> Modern commercial EDA tools (Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus, etc.) implement **lexicographically smallest** topological sort using a **min-heap of in-degree-zero nodes**, ensuring deterministic scheduling — critical for reproducibility of synthesis results across runs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Kahn's Algorithm on a VLSI Task DAG

**Problem Statement:**
Consider a simplified VLSI physical design flow modeled as a DAG with **6 tasks** (vertices) and the following **precedence edges** (dependencies):

- $A$: Read Netlist
- $B$: Floorplanning
- $C$: Power Planning
- $D$: Placement
- $E$: Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)
- $F$: Routing

**Edges** (predecessor $\rightarrow$ successor):
$(A, B)$, $(A, C)$, $(B, D)$, $(C, D)$, $(D, E)$, $(D, F)$, $(E, F)$

Find a valid topological order of these tasks using Kahn's algorithm.

---

#### Step 1 — Compute In-Degrees of Every Vertex

We apply the formula:

$$\delta^{-}(v) = \sum_{u \in V} \mathbb{1}\big[(u,v) \in E\big]$$

| Vertex | Incoming Edges | In-Degree $\delta^{-}(v)$ |
| :---: | :--- | :---: |
| A | None (source) | $\mathbf{0}$ |
| B | $(A, B)$ | $\mathbf{1}$ |
| C | $(A, C)$ | $\mathbf{1}$ |
| D | $(B, D), (C, D)$ | $\mathbf{2}$ |
| E | $(D, E)$ | $\mathbf{1}$ |
| F | $(D, F), (E, F)$ | $\mathbf{2}$ |

**Sum check:** $\sum_{v} \delta^{-}(v) = 0+1+1+2+1+2 = 7 = \vert E \vert$ ✓ (validates graph construction)

---

#### Step 2 — Initialize the Source Queue

All vertices with in-degree **0** enter the queue first.

$$Q_0 = \{A\} \quad \text{(output order so far: [])}$$

---

#### Step 3 — Iterative Dequeue / Decrement Loop

**Iteration 1:** Dequeue $A$. Append to output. Decrement in-degrees of successors.

$$\text{Output} = [A]$$

$$\delta^{-}(B) : 1 \to 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{enqueue } B$$
$$\delta^{-}(C) : 1 \to 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{enqueue } C$$

$$Q_1 = \{B, C\}$$

**Iteration 2:** Dequeue $B$ (lexicographically smallest). Append to output. Decrement in-degree of $D$.

$$\text{Output} = [A, B]$$
$$\delta^{-}(D) : 2 \to 1 \quad \text{(not yet zero, not enqueued)}$$

$$Q_2 = \{C\}$$

**Iteration 3:** Dequeue $C$. Append to output. Decrement in-degree of $D$.

$$\text{Output} = [A, B, C]$$
$$\delta^{-}(D) : 1 \to 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{enqueue } D$$

$$Q_3 = \{D\}$$

**Iteration 4:** Dequeue $D$. Append to output. Decrement in-degrees of $E$ and $F$.

$$\text{Output} = [A, B, C, D]$$
$$\delta^{-}(E) : 1 \to 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{enqueue } E$$
$$\delta^{-}(F) : 2 \to 1 \quad \text{(not yet zero)}$$

$$Q_4 = \{E\}$$

**Iteration 5:** Dequeue $E$. Append to output. Decrement in-degree of $F$.

$$\text{Output} = [A, B, C, D, E]$$
$$\delta^{-}(F) : 1 \to 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{enqueue } F$$

$$Q_5 = \{F\}$$

**Iteration 6:** Dequeue $F$. Append to output. No successors.

$$\text{Output} = [A, B, C, D, E, F]$$

$$Q_6 = \{\} \quad \text{(queue empty — algorithm terminates)}$$

---

#### Step 4 — Cycle Check and Result Verification

**Cycle Check:** $\vert \text{Output} \vert = 6 = \vert V \vert$ ✓ — no cycle detected.

**Edge Validation:** For every edge $(u, v)$ in the graph, $u$ must appear before $v$ in the output:

| Edge | Position of $u$ | Position of $v$ | Valid? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $(A, B)$ | 1 | 2 | ✓ |
| $(A, C)$ | 1 | 3 | ✓ |
| $(B, D)$ | 2 | 4 | ✓ |
| $(C, D)$ | 3 | 4 | ✓ |
| $(D, E)$ | 4 | 5 | ✓ |
| $(D, F)$ | 4 | 6 | ✓ |
| $(E, F)$ | 5 | 6 | ✓ |

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\text{Topological Order} = [\,A,\ B,\ C,\ D,\ E,\ F\,]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative valid orders** (any linear extension is acceptable) include:
> $[A, C, B, D, E, F]$, $[A, B, C, D, F, E]$ (invalid — $F$ needs $E$),
> $[A, C, B, D, E, F]$ etc. The algorithm's tie-breaking (min-heap) yields the lexicographically smallest.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Complexity Decomposition

| Phase | Operation | Cost | Justification |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Initialization | In-degree computation | $O(\vert E \vert)$ | One pass over all edges. |
| Initialization | Source identification | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | One pass over all vertices. |
| Main loop | Each vertex dequeued once | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | $\vert V \vert$ iterations. |
| Main loop | Each edge relaxed once | $O(\vert E \vert)$ | $\vert E \vert$ total decrements. |
| Total | Full algorithm | $\boxed{\mathbf{O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)}}$ | Sum of the four lines above. |

For a modern ASIC with $N = 10^7$ cells and $M = 5 \times 10^7$ connections:

$$T = 6 \times 10^7 \text{ operations} \approx 0.06 \text{ s on a 1 GHz CPU}$$

This sub-second runtime is why topological sort is **ubiquitous** in production EDA tools.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 DAG Used in the Worked Example

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the VLSI design-flow DAG analyzed in **Section 3.1**.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Task A: Read Netlist]
    B[Task B: Floorplanning]
    C[Task C: Power Planning]
    D[Task D: Placement]
    E[Task E: Clock Tree Synthesis]
    F[Task F: Routing]

    A --> B
    A --> C
    B --> D
    C --> D
    D --> E
    D --> F
    E --> F
```

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Kahn's Algorithm

The diagram below maps the **data-flow and control-flow** of Kahn's algorithm. Use this as the canonical reference for tracing the algorithm during the KTU exam.

```mermaid
graph TD
    start([Start Algorithm])
    input[/Input: Adjacency List A and In-Degree Array deg/]
    initQ[Initialize Queue Q with all vertices where deg is 0]
    outputList[Initialize Output List L as empty]
    checkQ{Is Q empty?}
    dequeue[Dequeue vertex u from Q]
    appendL[Append u to Output List L]
    relax[For each edge from u to v: Decrement deg of v]
    checkZero{Is new deg of v equal to 0?}
    enqueueV[Enqueue v into Q]
    loopEnd[Loop back to checkQ]
    checkSize{Is size of L equal to N?}
    success([Return L as Topological Order])
    cycle([Report: Directed Cycle Detected])
    end([End Algorithm])

    start --> input
    input --> initQ
    initQ --> outputList
    outputList --> checkQ
    checkQ -- No --> dequeue
    dequeue --> appendL
    appendL --> relax
    relax --> checkZero
    checkZero -- Yes --> enqueueV
    enqueueV --> loopEnd
    loopEnd --> checkQ
    checkQ -- Yes --> checkSize
    checkSize -- Yes --> success
    checkSize -- No --> cycle
    success --> end
    cycle --> end
```

---

### 4.3 Execution State Diagram — Worked Example

The state diagram below tracks the **in-degree evolution** and the **frontier queue** across the six iterations of the worked example. Read it as a vertical timeline, top-to-bottom.

```mermaid
graph TD
    I0[Iteration 0: Q = A, In-Deg = A0 B1 C1 D2 E1 F2]
    I1[Iteration 1: Process A. Q becomes B C. In-Deg = A0 B0 C0 D2 E1 F2]
    I2[Iteration 2: Process B. Q becomes C. In-Deg = A0 B0 C0 D1 E1 F2]
    I3[Iteration 3: Process C. Q becomes D. In-Deg = A0 B0 C0 D0 E1 F2]
    I4[Iteration 4: Process D. Q becomes E. In-Deg = A0 B0 C0 D0 E0 F1]
    I5[Iteration 5: Process E. Q becomes F. In-Deg = A0 B0 C0 D0 E0 F0]
    I6[Iteration 6: Process F. Q empty. Order = A B C D E F]

    I0 --> I1 --> I2 --> I3 --> I4 --> I5 --> I6
```

---

### 4.4 Contrast — DAG vs. Cyclic Graph Behavior

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph DAG_Valid[Valid DAG: Topological Sort Succeeds]
        v1[Node 1] --> v2[Node 2]
        v1 --> v3[Node 3]
        v2 --> v4[Node 4]
        v3 --> v4
    end

    subgraph Cycle_Invalid[Cyclic Graph: Sort Fails]
        w1[Node X] --> w2[Node Y]
        w2 --> w3[Node Z]
        w3 --> w1
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the cyclic subgraph, every vertex has in-degree $\geq 1$ at every iteration, so the **frontier queue remains empty** immediately. The algorithm exits with a partial order of length $< \vert V \vert$, signaling a **combinational feedback loop** in the netlist.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define topological sort. State the necessary and sufficient condition for a directed graph to admit a topological ordering.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A topological sort of a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ is a linear ordering of its vertices $\sigma : V \to \{1, 2, \dots, \vert V \vert\}$ such that for every directed edge $(u, v) \in E$, we have $\sigma(u) < \sigma(v)$.

**Necessary and sufficient condition:** A directed graph admits a topological ordering **if and only if** it is a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**, i.e., it contains no directed cycle. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**What is the time complexity of Kahn's algorithm for topological sort? Briefly explain why it is suitable for cycle detection in a VLSI netlist.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The time complexity of Kahn's algorithm is $T = \mathbf{O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)}$, where $\vert V \vert$ is the number of vertices (gates/nodes) and $\vert E \vert$ is the number of edges (wires/dependencies).

It is suitable for netlist cycle detection because the algorithm only enqueues vertices whose in-degree has dropped to zero. If a directed cycle exists, every vertex in the cycle will always retain an in-degree of at least one, and the **frontier queue becomes empty before all vertices are processed**. A simple check ($\vert \text{Output} \vert < \vert V \vert$) therefore reliably flags the presence of a **combinational loop** — a fatal design error in synchronous VLSI. (3 marks)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Kahn's Algorithm Application

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain Kahn's algorithm for topological sort with the help of its logical steps. **State its time and space complexity.** (7 marks)

**(b)** Consider the following DAG with 8 vertices representing VLSI synthesis tasks. Construct a valid topological order using Kahn's algorithm. **Show every iteration clearly**, including in-degree updates and queue contents.

**Edges of the DAG:**
$(1, 3), (1, 4), (2, 4), (2, 5), (3, 6), (4, 6), (4, 7), (5, 7), (6, 8), (7, 8)$ (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

Kahn's algorithm is a **source-removal** technique. The steps are:

1. **Compute in-degrees**: For every vertex $v$, count the number of incoming edges $\delta^{-}(v)$.
2. **Initialize queue**: Enqueue every vertex $v$ with $\delta^{-}(v) = 0$. These are the **source vertices** (no predecessors).
3. **Process loop**: While the queue is non-empty:
   - Dequeue a vertex $u$.
   - Append $u$ to the output list $L$.
   - For each outgoing edge $(u, v) \in E$: decrement $\delta^{-}(v)$ by 1; if it becomes 0, enqueue $v$.
4. **Cycle check**: If $\vert L \vert = \vert V \vert$, the order is valid. Otherwise, a directed cycle exists.

**Time complexity**: $\mathbf{O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)}$ — each vertex is enqueued/dequeued once and each edge is examined once.

**Space complexity**: $\mathbf{O(\vert V \vert)}$ — to store the in-degree array, queue, and output list. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — In-degree computation** using $\delta^{-}(v) = \sum_{u} \mathbb{1}[(u,v) \in E]$:

| Vertex | Incoming Edges | In-Degree $\delta^{-}(v)$ |
| :---: | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | None | $\mathbf{0}$ |
| 2 | None | $\mathbf{0}$ |
| 3 | $(1, 3)$ | $\mathbf{1}$ |
| 4 | $(1, 4), (2, 4)$ | $\mathbf{2}$ |
| 5 | $(2, 5)$ | $\mathbf{1}$ |
| 6 | $(3, 6), (4, 6)$ | $\mathbf{2}$ |
| 7 | $(4, 7), (5, 7)$ | $\mathbf{2}$ |
| 8 | $(6, 8), (7, 8)$ | $\mathbf{2}$ |

**Sum check:** $0+0+1+2+1+2+2+2 = 10 = \vert E \vert$ ✓

**Step 2 — Iterative execution:**

| Iteration | Dequeued | Output $L$ | Updated In-Degrees | Queue $Q$ |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | 1 | $[1]$ | $\delta^{-}(3):1\to0$, $\delta^{-}(4):2\to1$ | $\{2, 3\}$ |
| 2 | 2 | $[1,2]$ | $\delta^{-}(4):1\to0$, $\delta^{-}(5):1\to0$ | $\{3, 4, 5\}$ |
| 3 | 3 | $[1,2,3]$ | $\delta^{-}(6):2\to1$ | $\{4, 5\}$ |
| 4 | 4 | $[1,2,3,4]$ | $\delta^{-}(6):1\to0$, $\delta^{-}(7):2\to1$ | $\{5, 6\}$ |
| 5 | 5 | $[1,2,3,4,5]$ | $\delta^{-}(7):1\to0$ | $\{6, 7\}$ |
| 6 | 6 | $[1,2,3,4,5,6]$ | $\delta^{-}(8):2\to1$ | $\{7\}$ |
| 7 | 7 | $[1,2,3,4,5,6,7]$ | $\delta^{-}(8):1\to0$ | $\{8\}$ |
| 8 | 8 | $[1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8]$ | None remain | $\{\}$ |

**Cycle check:** $\vert L \vert = 8 = \vert V \vert$ ✓ — **no cycle, valid DAG**.

**Final Topological Order:**
$$\boxed{L = [\,1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5,\ 6,\ 7,\ 8\,]}$$

(7 marks)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — DFS Approach & Cycle Detection

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Adapted]**

**(a)** Describe the **DFS-based algorithm** for topological sort. Explain how the order in which vertices are appended to the output list leads to a valid topological sort. **How does this algorithm detect a cycle?** (7 marks)

**(b)** Apply the DFS-based algorithm to the DAG from Question A (same edge set) and produce a topological order. **Identify the back-edge(s) if a cycle exists; otherwise, justify that none exists.** (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

The DFS-based topological sort works as follows:

1. Initialize all vertices as **unvisited (white)**.
2. For each unvisited vertex $u$, call `DFS-Visit(u)`:
   - Mark $u$ as **visiting (gray)**.
   - For every outgoing edge $(u, v)$:
     - If $v$ is **white** (unvisited), recurse on $v$.
     - If $v$ is **gray** (visiting), a **back-edge** $(u, v)$ exists $\Rightarrow$ **cycle detected**.
     - If $v$ is **black** (finished), ignore (cross/forward edge, no cycle contribution).
   - After all neighbors are processed, mark $u$ as **finished (black)** and **prepend** $u$ to a global list $L$.
3. After all DFS calls complete, $L$ (read in order) is the topological order.

**Why prepending works**: A vertex $u$ is marked black **only after** all its descendants (out-neighbors) have been fully processed and prepended. So when $u$ is prepended, every vertex $v$ reachable from $u$ is already in $L$ *after* $u$ — guaranteeing the precedence constraint $\sigma(u) < \sigma(v)$.

**Cycle detection**: A back-edge to a gray vertex is the graph-theoretic signature of a directed cycle. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

We perform DFS, choosing the smallest unvisited vertex at each step. We track discovery and finishing times. Let $d(v)$ and $f(v)$ denote discovery and finish times.

| Step | Visit | Action | Stack / Status |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 1 | $d(1)=1$, go to 3, then 6, then 8 | Stack: 1, 3, 6, 8 |
| 2 | 8 | 8 has no out-neighbors. $f(8)=2$. Prepend 8. | $L = [8]$ |
| 3 | 6 | All neighbors done. $f(6)=3$. Prepend 6. | $L = [6, 8]$ |
| 4 | 3 | All neighbors done. $f(3)=4$. Prepend 3. | $L = [3, 6, 8]$ |
| 5 | 1 | Return to 4, then 7, then 8 (black, skip). | Stack: 1, 4, 7 |
| 6 | 7 | $f(7)=5$. Prepend 7. | $L = [7, 3, 6, 8]$ |
| 7 | 4 | $f(4)=6$. Prepend 4. | $L = [4, 7, 3, 6, 8]$ |
| 8 | 1 | Back to root. 1's first neighbor done. Go to 4 (black, skip). | Stack: 1 |
| 9 | 1 | $f(1)=7$. Prepend 1. | $L = [1, 4, 7, 3, 6, 8]$ |
| 10 | 2 | $d(2)=8$, go to 5, then 7 (black, skip). | Stack: 2, 5 |
| 11 | 5 | $f(5)=9$. Prepend 5. | $L = [5, 1, 4, 7, 3, 6, 8]$ |
| 12 | 2 | $f(2)=10$. Prepend 2. | $L = [2, 5, 1, 4, 7, 3, 6, 8]$ |

**Cycle check**: No back-edge was ever encountered — every time we examined a previously visited neighbor, it was **black (finished)**, never gray. Hence the graph is a valid DAG.

**Final Topological Order (read in order):**
$$\boxed{L = [\,2,\ 5,\ 1,\ 4,\ 7,\ 3,\ 6,\ 8\,]}$$

**Edge validation** (a spot-check on the critical edges):

| Edge | Pos of $u$ | Pos of $v$ | Valid? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $(1, 4)$ | 3 | 4 | ✓ |
| $(4, 7)$ | 4 | 5 | ✓ |
| $(7, 8)$ | 5 | 8 | ✓ |
| $(6, 8)$ | 7 | 8 | ✓ |

(7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the cycle check** ($\vert L \vert < \vert V \vert$ test) — costs **2 marks** in Part B.
> 2. **Failing to update in-degrees** after dequeuing a vertex — algorithm becomes incorrect.
> 3. **Confusing Kahn's order with DFS order**: Kahn's order is built by *appending*; DFS order is built by *prepending* to the list.
> 4. **Not validating the final order**: Examiners award **partial credit** if you skip the edge-validation table.
> 5. **Reporting a "cycle" when none exists**: This happens when a student forgets to mark vertices as "processed" and gets stuck in an infinite loop conceptually. Always confirm the sum of in-degrees equals $\vert E \vert$ first.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Topological sort** = linear ordering of a DAG's vertices respecting all edge directions.
- **Existence condition**: Graph must be a **DAG** (no directed cycles).
- **Two algorithms** — both run in $\mathbf{O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)}$:
  - **Kahn's algorithm** (BFS / source-removal): uses in-degrees and a queue.
  - **DFS algorithm**: prepends to output on finish; back-edge to gray = cycle.
- **Cycle detection**: output length $< \vert V \vert$ (Kahn) OR back-edge to gray vertex (DFS).
- **Sum of in-degrees identity**: $\sum_{v} \delta^{-}(v) = \vert E \vert$ — always verify before starting.
- **Lexicographic ordering**: Use a **min-heap** of in-degree-zero vertices for deterministic output (industrial EDA standard).
- **Critical use in VLSI CAD**: ASAP scheduling, STA forward propagation, HLS task ordering, netlist feedback-loop detection.
- **Uniqueness**: Topological sort is unique $\iff$ the DAG has a Hamiltonian path.
- **KTU exam hot topics**: in-degree table computation, iteration trace, cycle check, and final edge validation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shortest Path Algorithms: Dijkstra's Shortest-Path Algorithm for Single Pair Shortest Path
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT525 - CAD BASED VLSI DESIGN OPTIMIZATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Shortest Path Algorithms: Dijkstra's Shortest-Path Algorithm for Single Pair Shortest Path # 📘 MODULE 1 — Shortest Path Algorithms ## Topic: Dijkstra's Shortest-Path Algorithm for Single Pair Shortest Path --- ### 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview #### 📌 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Dijkstra's Shortest-Path Algorithm (DSPA)** is a classical, greedy, single-source (and trivially *single-pair*) shortest path algorithm that computes the minimum cumulative cost (distance) from a designated **source vertex** $s$ to every other vertex $v \in V$ in a weighted graph $G = (V, E)$ with non-negative edge weights $w(u, v) \geq 0$. When restricted to finding the cost from $s$ to a single target $t$, it is termed the **Single-Pair Shortest Path (SPSP)** variant. The algorithm was proposed by **Edsger W. Dijkstra** in 1956 and remains the cornerstone of routing engines used in VLSI Computer-Aided Design (CAD), network-on-chip (NoC) mapping, and global placement–driven wire-length estimation. #### 💡 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Plain English) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Traveling Engineer Analogy:** > Imagine a VLSI layout designer is dropped at a city **A** (the source block) and must reach city **E** (the target I/O pad) using the cheapest combination of roads (metal layers + vias). Every road has a *time/distance* cost equal to the **wire length × unit capacitance**. The designer starts at A, asks: *“Among all my neighboring cities I haven’t visited, which is closest right now?”*, walks there, and from that new city asks the same question again, never revisiting expensive paths. Eventually, when **E** is reached, the designer is mathematically guaranteed to have taken the absolute minimum-cost route. The greedy choice is safe **only because all edge weights (wire costs) are non-negative**, exactly the case in physical VLSI routing where wire length and congestion penalties are $\geq 0$. #### 🔑 Key Terminology (Marked in Bold) | Term | Meaning in VLSI CAD Context | |---|---| | **Source Vertex (s)** | The driver cell / starting pin of the net being routed | | **Target Vertex (t)** | The sink cell / destination pin | | **Tentative Distance d(v)** | Current best known wire length from $s$ to node $v$ | | **Permanent Label** | Node whose final minimum distance is **fixed** | | **Priority Queue Q** | Min-heap storing frontier nodes, often implemented as a **Fibonacci heap** for $O(E + V \log V)$ | | **Edge Weight w(u,v)** | Routing cost: usually wire-length, congestion, or RC delay | | **Single-Pair (SPSP)** | Find min path from one $s$ to one $t$ (the most common VLSI net case) | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet --- ### 2.1 Algorithmic Assumptions (Pre-Conditions) 1. The routing graph $G = (V, E)$ is a **directed or undirected weighted graph**. 2. **All edge weights are non-negative**: $w(u, v) \geq 0 \;\;\; \forall (u,v) \in E$. (Justified in VLSI because negative wire-length has no physical meaning.) 3. The graph is **connected** (or at least, $t$ is reachable from $s$). 4. A path cost is the **sum of edge weights** along the path: $C(P) = \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} w(v_i, v_{i+1})$. --- ### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning Dijkstra's algorithm grows a *tree of shortest paths* (the **Dijkstra Tree**) outward from the source. The procedure is broken into discrete logical steps below. > [!NOTE] > **The 'Why' Behind the Greedy Strategy:** > Because all costs are non-negative, once a vertex $u$ is extracted from the priority queue with the smallest tentative distance, **no future path through any other unvisited vertex can ever improve $d(u)$**. This invariant, called the **Shortest-Path Substructure Property**, is what makes the greedy choice provably optimal. **Step 1 — Initialization** - Set $d(s) = 0$ for the source. - Set $d(v) = \infty$ for all $v \neq s$. - Predecessor $\pi(v) = \text{NIL}$ for all $v$. - Insert all vertices into a **min-priority queue** keyed on $d(\cdot)$. **Step 2 — Extract-Min** - Repeatedly extract the vertex $u$ with the **minimum tentative distance** from the queue. - Mark $u$ as **finalized** (added to the settled set $S$). **Step 3 — Edge Relaxation (The Heart of Dijkstra)** - For every outgoing edge $(u, v)$ from $u$, evaluate the new candidate distance: $$ d'(v) \;=\; d(u) \;+\; w(u, v) $$ - If $d'(v) < d(v)$, then perform the **relaxation update**: $$ d(v) \leftarrow d(v) \;+\; w(u, v) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; d(v) = d(u) + w(u, v) $$ $$ \pi(v) \leftarrow u $$ - This is the **Bellman-Ford relaxation operator** specialized to the non-negative case. **Step 4 — Termination** - For the **Single-Pair SPSP** variant: **stop as soon as $t$ is finalized**. - For the single-source variant: stop when the priority queue is empty. - Reconstruct the shortest path $P(s, t)$ by backtracking predecessors from $t$ to $s$ through $\pi$. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation / Rule | Meaning | Pre-Conditions | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $C(P_{s \to t}) = \sum_{e \in P} w(e)$ | Cost of a path is the sum of edge costs | Always | | 2 | $d(v) = 0$ for $v = s$ | Source distance is zero | Initialization | | 3 | $d(v) = \infty$ for $v \neq s$ | Unreachable default | Initialization | | 4 | $d(v) \leftarrow d(u) + w(u, v)$ | **Relaxation** rule | $d(u) + w(u,v) < d(v)$ | | 5 | $\pi(v) \leftarrow u$ | Predecessor update | Only if relaxation occurs | | 6 | **Time complexity** $O((V + E) \log V)$ | With binary/ Fibonacci heap | $w(e) \geq 0$ | | 7 | **Time complexity** $O(V^2)$ | With naive array | Dense graphs | | 8 | **Space complexity** $O(V + E)$ | Storing graph + queue | All variants | | 9 | $w(u, v) \geq 0$ | Non-negativity invariant | **Mandatory** | | 10 | Correctness: $\forall v \in S,\; d(v) = \delta(s, v)$ | $S$ = settled set; $\delta$ = true shortest distance | After extraction | > [!IMPORTANT] > ⚠️ **VLSI CAD Context Mapping:** > In the **Lee's Maze Router** and its modern **A\*/Dijkstra-based descendants** used in global routing, the weight $w(u,v)$ is not just wire length — it is the **composite cost**: > $$w(u, v) \;=\; \alpha \cdot L_{uv} \;+\; \beta \cdot C_{uv} \;+\; \gamma \cdot V_{uv}$$ > where $L$ = wire length, $C$ = congestion penalty on the edge (routing track), and $V$ = via count. Coefficients $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are tuned by the placement engine. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **VLSI Global Routing** — Finds minimum-cost routes for multi-pin nets across routing tiles. 2. **Maze Routing (Lee's Algorithm)** — A BFS-based predecessor; Dijkstra adds weighted support for congestion. 3. **Clock-Tree Synthesis (CTS)** — Computes minimum-skew paths in H-tree / X-tree topologies. 4. **Network-on-Chip (NoC) path mapping** — Latency-aware routing in many-core chips. 5. **FPGA Routing (PathFinder / VPR)** — Uses iterative Dijkstra with rip-up-and-reroute. 6. **PCB global routing** — Industrial EDA tools (Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC) use modified Dijkstra internally. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix --- ### 3.1 Worked Example (VLSI Net Routing Analogy) Consider a 5-cell **VLSI macro** laid out as a graph. A driver cell **A** must route a net to I/O pad **E** through intermediate buffers **B, C, D**. The weighted graph below represents inter-cell wire-costs (in $\mu m$ of estimated wire-length): | Edge | Weight $w$ ($\mu m$) | |---|---| | A → B | 4 | | A → C | 2 | | B → C | 1 | | B → D | 5 | | C → D | 8 | | C → E | 10 | | D → E | 2 | **Source** $s = A$, **Target** $t = E$. #### 📋 Iteration Trace Table | Iteration | Extracted $u$ (min) | $d(B)$ | $d(C)$ | $d(D)$ | $d(E)$ | Predecessors $\pi$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 (init) | — | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | all NIL | | 1 | **A** (0) | 4 | 2 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\pi(B)=A,\;\pi(C)=A$ | | 2 | **C** (2) | **3** | 2 (final) | 10 | 12 | $\pi(B)=C,\;\pi(D)=C,\;\pi(E)=C$ | | 3 | **B** (3) | 3 (final) | — | **8** | 12 | $\pi(D)=B$ | | 4 | **D** (8) | — | — | 8 (final) | **10** | $\pi(E)=D$ | | 5 | **E** (10) | — | — | — | 10 (final) | STOP — target reached | #### 🔢 Derivation of the Relaxation Step at Iteration 2 (extracting C) When $u = C$ is extracted with $d(C) = 2$, we relax its outgoing edges: $$ d'(B) \;=\; d(C) \;+\; w(C, B) \;=\; 2 + 1 \;=\; 3 $$ Since $d'(B) = 3 < d(B) = 4$, we update: $$ d(B) \leftarrow 3,\qquad \pi(B) \leftarrow C $$ Next, for edge $C \to D$: $$ d'(D) \;=\; d(C) + w(C, D) \;=\; 2 + 8 \;=\; 10 \;<\; \infty $$ $$ \Rightarrow\;\; d(D) \leftarrow 10,\qquad \pi(D) \leftarrow C $$ Next, for edge $C \to E$: $$ d'(E) \;=\; d(C) + w(C, E) \;=\; 2 + 10 \;=\; 12 \;<\; \infty $$ $$ \Rightarrow\;\; d(E) \leftarrow 12,\qquad \pi(E) \leftarrow C $$ #### 🔢 Backtracking the Final Path Reconstructing from $E$ via predecessors: $$ E \;\xleftarrow{\pi}\; D \;\xleftarrow{\pi}\; B \;\xleftarrow{\pi}\; C \;\xleftarrow{\pi}\; A $$ Therefore the **shortest wire-route** is: $$ A \;\to\; C \;\to\; B \;\to\; D \;\to\; E $$ with total minimum cost: $$ \delta(A, E) \;=\; w(A, C) + w(C, B) + w(B, D) + w(D, E) $$ $$ \delta(A, E) \;=\; 2 + 1 + 5 + 2 \;=\; 10 \;\mu m $$ Note that the **naively shorter-looking** direct path $A \to C \to E$ would cost $2 + 10 = 12\;\mu m$, which is **not** optimal — exactly the kind of pitfall Dijkstra's relaxation prevents. #### 🧠 Correctness Argument (Why it Works — Full Proof Sketch) Let $S_k$ denote the settled set after $k$ iterations and let $u$ be the vertex just extracted with $d(u) = \min_{v \notin S} d(v)$. We claim $d(u) = \delta(s, u)$. **Proof by Contradiction:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Suppose } &d(u) > \delta(s, u). \text{ Let } P : s = v_0, v_1, \dots, v_m = u \\ \text{be the true shortest path.} &\text{ Let } v^* \text{ be the first vertex of } P \text{ not in } S. \\ \text{Then } v^* &\neq u \text{ (since } u \text{ is the next extracted and } v^* \text{ is also unsettled, but } u \text{ had the min } d\text{).} \\ \text{Let } y &\text{ be the predecessor of } v^* \text{ on } P,\; y \in S. \\ \text{When } y \text{ was extracted,} &\text{ edge } (y, v^*) \text{ was relaxed, so:} \\ d(v^*) &\leq d(y) + w(y, v^*) = \delta(s, y) + w(y, v^*) = \delta(s, v^*) \end{aligned} $$ Since $u$ was the unsettled vertex with minimum $d$, $d(u) \leq d(v^*) \leq \delta(s, v^*) \leq \delta(s, u)$, contradicting the assumption. ∎ #### 📊 Complexity Derivation (With Fibonacci Heap) Let $|V| = n$ and $|E| = m$. $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{init}} &= O(n) \\ T_{\text{extract-min}} &= n \cdot O(\log n) = O(n \log n) \\ T_{\text{decrease-key}} &= m \cdot O(1) = O(m) \\ \hline T_{\text{total}} &= O(n \log n + m) \end{aligned} $$ For a dense VLSI routing graph where $m \approx n^2$, this reduces to $O(n^2)$, matching the naive array implementation. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics --- ### 4.1 Algorithmic Flowchart (Dijkstra's Single-Pair Variant) ```mermaid flowchart TD start([START]) --> init["Initialize d(s)=0, d(v)=∞ for all v≠s
Build priority queue Q with all vertices"] init --> checkQ{"Is Q empty OR
target t finalized?"} checkQ -- "Yes (target finalized)" --> backtrack["Backtrack via π pointers
to reconstruct shortest path P(s,t)"] checkQ -- "No" --> extract["u = Extract-Min from Q
Mark u as FINALIZED"] extract --> relaxCheck{"For each edge (u,v):
is d(u) + w(u,v) less than d(v)?"} relaxCheck -- "Yes" --> relax["RELAX:
d(v) ← d(u) + w(u,v)
π(v) ← u
Decrease-Key(v, Q)"] relaxCheck -- "No" --> nextEdge["Skip edge (u,v)
proceed to next"] relax --> nextEdge nextEdge --> moreEdge{"More edges
from u?"} moreEdge -- "Yes" --> relaxCheck moreEdge -- "No" --> isTarget{"Is u == t?"} isTarget -- "Yes" --> backtrack isTarget -- "No" --> checkQ backtrack --> out([OUTPUT: δ(s,t) and path P]) style start fill:#2E86AB,stroke:#000,color:#fff style out fill:#28A745,stroke:#000,color:#fff style extract fill:#FFC107,stroke:#000,color:#000 style relax fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#fff style backtrack fill:#9B59B6,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` --- ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Dijkstra Engine in a VLSI Global Router ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Netlist Input
pins and net connections] --> B[Graph Constructor
tiles and vias as nodes] B --> C[Cost Model
wire length + congestion + via] C --> D[Priority Queue
min-heap of frontier tiles] D --> E[Extract-Min Tile] E --> F[Edge Relaxation Unit
updates d(v) and π pointers] F --> G{Target Pin Reached?} G -- No --> D G -- Yes --> H[Path Backtracker
follows π pointers] H --> I[Routed Output
metal layers and via list] I --> J[DRC / LVS Checker] style A fill:#3498DB,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B fill:#1ABC9C,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C fill:#E67E22,stroke:#000,color:#fff style D fill:#9B59B6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style E fill:#F1C40F,stroke:#000,color:#000 style F fill:#E74C3C,stroke:#000,color:#fff style H fill:#2ECC71,stroke:#000,color:#fff style I fill:#34495E,stroke:#000,color:#fff style J fill:#16A085,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` **Reading the diagram:** The router reads the **netlist** → builds a **tile graph** → assigns a **composite cost** to each edge → drives a **Dijkstra engine** that iteratively extracts the cheapest frontier tile, **relaxes** its neighbors, and stops when the sink pin is finalized → emits the routed path for **physical verification (DRC/LVS)**. --- ### 4.3 Worked Example State Graph (5-node VLSI Macro) ```mermaid flowchart LR A((A)) -- "4" --> B((B)) A -- "2" --> C((C)) B -- "1" --> C B -- "5" --> D((D)) C -- "8" --> D C -- "10" --> E((E)) D -- "2" --> E style A fill:#2E86AB,stroke:#000,color:#fff style E fill:#28A745,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B fill:#FFC107,stroke:#000,color:#000 style C fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#fff style D fill:#9B59B6,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` **The shortest route (highlighted in red nodes above)** is $A \to C \to B \to D \to E$ with total cost **$10\;\mu m$**, as derived in §3. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📚 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]** *State and explain Dijkstra's algorithm for finding the shortest path between two nodes in a weighted graph. Mention one important limitation of the algorithm.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Dijkstra's algorithm is a **greedy, single-source shortest path algorithm** that finds the minimum-cost path from a source vertex $s$ to all other vertices in a weighted graph with **non-negative edge weights**. It maintains a set $S$ of finalized vertices and a priority queue $Q$ of tentative distances. At each step, the vertex $u$ with the **minimum tentative distance** is extracted, marked final, and all its outgoing edges are **relaxed** using the rule: $$ d(v) \;\leftarrow\; \min\bigl(d(v),\; d(u) + w(u,v)\bigr) $$ The process repeats until all vertices are finalized (or the target $t$ is reached, in the single-pair variant). **Limitation:** It **fails for graphs with negative edge weights**, since the greedy choice is no longer safe once a cheaper path through a later-relaxed edge could exist. *(1 mark for the limitation)* > [!NOTE] > **Mark Split:** Definition (1) + Relaxation rule (1) + Limitation (1) = **3 marks** --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** *What is the Single-Pair Shortest Path (SPSP) problem? How does Dijkstra's algorithm differ in its termination condition when solving SPSP versus the all-pairs single-source case?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Single-Pair Shortest Path (SPSP)** problem asks for the minimum-cost path between **one specific source vertex $s$ and one specific target vertex $t$** in a weighted graph $G = (V, E)$. **Termination Difference:** - In the **single-source (SSSP)** variant, Dijkstra continues until the **priority queue is empty**, i.e., every vertex in $V$ has been finalized with its exact shortest distance. - In the **single-pair (SPSP)** variant, Dijkstra may **terminate early as soon as the target vertex $t$ is extracted from the queue and marked final**, since the algorithm's invariant guarantees that $d(t) = \delta(s, t)$ at that moment. This early-exit optimization can yield significant **runtime savings in VLSI global routing**, where most nets have only one driver and one (or few) sinks. > [!NOTE] > **Mark Split:** SPSP definition (1) + Termination condition difference (2) = **3 marks** --- ### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024, Modified Module 1 Pattern]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm**. Discuss its time and space complexity with suitable justifications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Apply Dijkstra's algorithm on the graph below to find the shortest path from vertex **1** to vertex **5**. Show every iteration in a table. Justify why your final answer is optimal. **(7 marks)** Graph data (edge : weight): | Edge | Weight | |---|---| | 1–2 | 10 | | 1–4 | 1 | | 4–2 | 2 | | 2–3 | 3 | | 2–5 | 4 | | 4–3 | 8 | | 3–5 | 2 | | 4–5 | 12 | --- **Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks:** 1. **Problem Statement (1 mark):** Given $G = (V, E)$ with $w : E \to \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ and source $s$, compute $\delta(s, v)$ for every $v \in V$ (or specifically for one $t$). 2. **Algorithm Description (3 marks):** - Initialize $d(s) = 0$, $d(v) = \infty$ for $v \neq s$, $\pi(v) = \text{NIL}$, and $S = \emptyset$, $Q = V$. - **While** $Q \neq \emptyset$: - $u = \text{Extract-Min}(Q)$ - $S = S \cup \{u\}$ - **For each** $(u, v) \in E$: - **If** $d(u) + w(u, v) < d(v)$: - $d(v) = d(u) + w(u, v)$ - $\pi(v) = u$ - Reconstruct path via $\pi$ chain. 3. **Complexity Analysis (2 marks):** - With **binary heap**: $O((V + E) \log V)$. - With **Fibonacci heap**: $O(V \log V + E)$ — amortized $O(1)$ decrease-key. - With **naive array**: $O(V^2)$. - **Space:** $O(V + E)$ for graph + $O(V)$ for distance/pi arrays. 4. **Correctness & Limitations (1 mark):** Optimal under non-negative weights; fails for negative edges (use Bellman–Ford instead). --- **Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 — Initialize** (1 mark): $$ d(1) = 0,\quad d(2) = d(3) = d(4) = d(5) = \infty $$ **Step 2 — Iteration Trace Table** (5 marks): | Iter | Extract $u$ | $d(2)$ | $d(3)$ | $d(4)$ | $d(5)$ | Updates | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | — | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | Init | | 1 | **1** (0) | 10 | $\infty$ | **1** | $\infty$ | $\pi(2)=1,\;\pi(4)=1$ | | 2 | **4** (1) | **3** | 9 | 1(final) | 13 | relax 4→2,4→3,4→5 | | 3 | **2** (3) | 3(final) | **6** | — | **7** | relax 2→3,2→5 | | 4 | **3** (6) | — | 6(final) | — | 7 (no change) | 3→5 gives 8 > 7 | | 5 | **5** (7) | — | — | — | **7 (final)** | STOP | **Step 3 — Reconstruct Path** (1 mark): $$ 1 \to 4 \to 2 \to 5,\qquad \delta(1,5) = 1 + 2 + 4 = 7 $$ **Justification of Optimality (covered in trace):** At every extraction, the vertex popped is provably the globally closest unsettled vertex, and the relaxation ensures no cheaper alternate path through unvisited nodes can lower its distance (non-negative weights invariant). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain **Dijkstra's algorithm** and derive its **time complexity** using a Fibonacci heap. Compare it with the **naive array-based** implementation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider a VLSI routing graph with 6 nodes (routing tiles T1 to T6). The driver pin is at **T1** and the receiver pin is at **T6**. Edge weights (representing wire-length in $\mu m$) are: T1–T2 = 3, T1–T3 = 5, T2–T3 = 1, T2–T4 = 6, T3–T5 = 2, T4–T5 = 4, T5–T6 = 3, T2–T6 = 12, T4–T6 = 7. Find the **single-pair shortest path from T1 to T6** using Dijkstra's algorithm. Tabulate all iterations. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks:** 1. **Flowchart** (2 marks): Use the Mermaid-style block diagram from §4.1 (Initialize → Extract-Min → Relax → Check Stop). 2. **Fibonacci Heap Complexity Derivation** (3 marks): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Initialize-Heap} &: O(V) \\ n \text{ Extract-Min ops} &: n \cdot O(\log V) = O(V \log V) \\ m \text{ Decrease-Key ops} &: m \cdot O(1) = O(E) \\ \hline \text{Total} &: O(V \log V + E) \end{aligned} $$ 3. **Comparison with Naive Array** (2 marks): | Aspect | Naive Array | Fibonacci Heap | |---|---|---| | Extract-Min | $O(V)$ per call | $O(\log V)$ per call | | Decrease-Key | $O(1)$ (direct update) | $O(1)$ amortized | | Total Time | $O(V^2 + E) = O(V^2)$ | $O(V \log V + E)$ | | Best For | Dense graphs ($E \approx V^2$) | Sparse graphs ($E \ll V^2$) | | Constant Factors | Small | Large (pointer-heavy) | --- **Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks:** **Initial distances** (1 mark): $d(\text{T1}) = 0$, all others = $\infty$. **Iteration Table** (5 marks): | Iter | Extract $u$ | $d(\text{T2})$ | $d(\text{T3})$ | $d(\text{T4})$ | $d(\text{T5})$ | $d(\text{T6})$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | — | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | | 1 | **T1** (0) | 3 | 5 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | | 2 | **T2** (3) | 3(final) | **4** | 9 | $\infty$ | 15 | | 3 | **T3** (4) | — | 4(final) | 9 | **6** | 15 | | 4 | **T5** (6) | — | — | **9** | 6(final) | **9** | | 5 | **T4** (9) | — | — | 9(final) | — | 9 (no change; 9+7=16) | | 6 | **T6** (9) | — | — | — | — | **9 (final)** STOP | **Final path reconstruction** (1 mark): $$\text{T1} \to \text{T2} \to \text{T3} \to \text{T5} \to \text{T6}$$ $$\delta(\text{T1}, \text{T6}) = 3 + 1 + 2 + 3 = 9\;\mu m$$ The alternate candidate route $\text{T1}\to\text{T2}\to\text{T4}\to\text{T6}$ would cost $3 + 6 + 7 = 16\;\mu m$ — clearly suboptimal. --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Evaluations:** > 1. **Forgetting the early-termination clause for SPSP** — Examiners specifically look for the statement *"stop as soon as $t$ is finalized."* Losing this costs 1 mark. > 2. **Confusing Dijkstra with BFS** — BFS works only for unweighted (or unit-weight) graphs. Stating *"Dijkstra = BFS"* is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction. > 3. **Skipping the relaxation inequality** — Always write the condition $d(u) + w(u,v) < d(v)$ explicitly. Many students write only the assignment, missing the guard. > 4. **Incorrect backtracking** — Path reconstruction uses $\pi$ pointers; many students mistakenly output the settled-set order instead. Always trace $\pi$ from $t$ to $s$ and reverse. > 5. **Claiming $O(VE)$ complexity** — That is Bellman–Ford, not Dijkstra. Dijkstra with a heap is $O((V+E)\log V)$ or $O(V\log V + E)$ with Fibonacci. > 6. **Applying Dijkstra to negative weights** — If the question graph has negative edges, **the answer is "Dijkstra is not applicable; use Bellman–Ford."** Blindly running it is a serious loss. --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ Dijkstra's algorithm is a **greedy, single-source** SSSP algorithm — solves SPSP as a special case. - ✅ It works **only for non-negative edge weights** — exactly the regime in VLSI routing. - ✅ The **relaxation operator** is the heart: $d(v) = \min(d(v),\; d(u) + w(u,v))$. - ✅ Termination for **SPSP**: stop when the target $t$ is finalized (early-exit optimization). - ✅ **Time complexity**: - Naive array: $O(V^2)$ - Binary heap: $O((V + E) \log V)$ - Fibonacci heap: $O(V \log V + E)$ (best asymptotic) - ✅ **Space complexity**: $O(V + E)$. - ✅ **Correctness** relies on the **non-negativity invariant** and the **optimal substructure** of shortest paths. - ✅ **In VLSI CAD**: Used in global routing (Lee's maze routing variants), clock-tree synthesis, NoC path mapping, and FPGA routing (PathFinder, VPR). - ✅ **Limitations**: Fails on negative weights; cannot handle dynamic graphs efficiently (use dynamic SSSP variants instead). - ✅ **Backtracking** is done via the predecessor array $\pi$, not by re-tracing settled vertices. - ✅ Examiner-favorite trick: provide a graph with a **negative edge** to test whether the student recognizes that Dijkstra is **not** the right tool.

topics.title: Floyd Warshall Algorithm for All Pair Shortest Path
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT525 - CAD BASED VLSI DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Floyd Warshall Algorithm for All Pair Shortest Path

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Floyd–Warshall Algorithm** is a classic **Dynamic Programming (DP)** based graph algorithm that computes the shortest path distances between **every pair of vertices** in a weighted directed graph (positive or negative edge weights, but no negative cycles).
