In the context of **CAD-based VLSI Design Optimization**, it is formally defined as a *global router* and *timing analyzer* subroutine that exhaustively determines minimum-cost interconnects between any two standard cells, macros, or routing blocks on a chip, by iteratively evaluating whether an intermediate node (called a *k-pivot* or *buffer hub*) can provide a shorter propagation delay or wirelength path.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Floyd–Warshall Algorithm computes the transitive closure of the path-weight matrix $D^{(k)}$, where $D^{(k)}[i,j]$ represents the shortest path from vertex $i$ to vertex $j$ using only intermediate vertices drawn from the set $\{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The City Map Intuition)

Imagine you are a **VLSI chip floor-planner** responsible for routing 1,000 metal wires on a silicon die of size $10\text{ mm} \times 10\text{ mm}$. Each wire must travel between two logic blocks (vertices) across a maze of pre-routed obstacles, vias, and congestion zones (edges with weights = wirelength, delay, or congestion cost).

- A **single shortest-path** algorithm (like Dijkstra) is like asking *"What is the cheapest route from Block A to Block B?"* — useful, but you have to run it **999 more times** for every other pair.
- The **Floyd–Warshall Algorithm** is like asking: *"What is the cheapest route from **every** block to **every** other block, **simultaneously**, by allowing re-routing through any intermediate buffer/hub?"*

It is the **"all-in-one map optimization"** for an entire chip — the designer enters the entire routing netlist once, and leaves with a complete $N \times N$ shortest-path matrix. 

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Analogy:** 
> - A GPS system (like Google Maps) computing the *complete distance table* for all cities in a country.
> - In VLSI, it is analogous to performing a **full-chip Static Timing Analysis (STA)** or **global routing wirelength minimization** in one matrix sweep.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Value / Notation | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| Vertices | $V$ (= $n$) | Number of standard cells/macros |
| Edges | $E$ | Number of routing segments |
| Time Complexity | $O(V^3)$ | **3 nested loops** in KTU coding/model questions |
| Space Complexity | $O(V^2)$ | Distance matrix $D^{(k)}$ |
| Path Reconstruction | $O(V^2)$ parent matrix $\Pi$ | Required for back-tracing nets |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Floyd–Warshall in VLSI CAD?** VLSI routing, timing-driven placement, and clock-tree synthesis demand **multi-source, multi-destination** shortest paths. Floyd–Warshall's $O(V^3)$ cost is acceptable for the **post-placement global routing phase**, where $V$ (number of routing tiles / G-cells) is bounded and parallelizable on GPU/FPGA accelerators.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Algorithmic Foundation (Dynamic Programming Decomposition)

The Floyd–Warshall algorithm partitions the problem into $n = \vert V \vert$ progressively larger sub-problems, indexed by $k \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, n\}$.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Initialization ($k = 0$):** Construct the direct distance matrix $D^{(0)}$ such that:
   - $D^{(0)}[i, j] = w(i, j)$ if a direct edge exists from $i$ to $j$.
   - $D^{(0)}[i, j] = \infty$ if no direct edge exists.
   - $D^{(0)}[i, i] = 0$ for all $i$ (self-loop distance is zero).

2. **Iteration $k$:** Allow intermediate vertices from the restricted set $\{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k\}$. For every pair $(i, j)$, decide: *"Is it cheaper to go **directly** as in $D^{(k-1)}[i,j]$, or to **detour through** vertex $v_k$ as $D^{(k-1)}[i, v_k] + D^{(k-1)}[v_k, j]$?"*

3. **Recurrence Update:** Pick the minimum and write it to $D^{(k)}[i, j]$.

4. **Termination ($k = n$):** $D^{(n)}$ is the all-pairs shortest path matrix.

### 2.2 The Master Recurrence (KTU High-Yield)

$$
D^{(k)}[i, j] = \min\Big( D^{(k-1)}[i, j], \;\; D^{(k-1)}[i, k] + D^{(k-1)}[k, j] \Big)
$$

This is the **single most important equation** for KTU Module 1 questions.

### 2.3 Path Reconstruction Matrix $\Pi$

To back-trace the actual path, a **predecessor matrix** $\Pi^{(k)}[i, j]$ is updated in parallel:

$$
\Pi^{(k)}[i, j] = 
\begin{cases}
\Pi^{(k-1)}[i, j] & \text{if } D^{(k-1)}[i, j] \leq D^{(k-1)}[i, k] + D^{(k-1)}[k, j] \\
\Pi^{(k-1)}[k, j] & \text{if } D^{(k-1)}[i, j] > D^{(k-1)}[i, k] + D^{(k-1)}[k, j]
\end{cases}
$$

### 2.4 Transitive Closure Variant

By replacing $(\min, +)$ with $(\lor, \land)$ and $\infty$ with **false (0)**, Floyd–Warshall computes the **transitive closure** $T[i, j]$: whether a path exists between $i$ and $j$. Useful in VLSI for **connectivity verification** of routing graphs.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Boundary Condition | Engineering Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D^{(k)}[i, j]$ | Shortest $i \to j$ using intermediate vertices $\leq v_k$ | $D^{(0)}[i, i] = 0$ | $\mu m$ (wirelength) or $ps$ (delay) |
| $w(i, j)$ | Direct edge weight | $w(i, j) > 0$ for routing | $\mu m$ / $ps$ |
| $\infty$ | "No direct edge" placeholder | $\infty + x = \infty$ | — |
| $\Pi[i, j]$ | Predecessor of $j$ on shortest $i \to j$ | $\Pi[i, i] = \text{NIL}$ | index |
| $n$ | Number of vertices (routing G-cells) | $n \geq 1$ | — |
| $T_{FW}$ | Time complexity $O(n^3)$ | $n^3$ scalar relaxations | operations |
| $S_{FW}$ | Space complexity $O(n^2)$ | Distance + Predecessor matrix | memory words |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In modern VLSI CAD tools (e.g., Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC2), Floyd–Warshall-like multi-source BFS kernels are offloaded to **GPUs** to compute **timing-driven global routing** and **clock latency matrices** for billion-transistor designs.

### 2.6 Why $O(V^3)$ and not $O(V \cdot E \log V)$ (Dijkstra × V)?

Although running Dijkstra from every source gives $O(V \cdot E \log V)$, Floyd–Warshall is preferred in VLSI CAD because:
- It handles **negative edge weights** (e.g., wirelength *gain* from buffer insertion).
- It is **embarrassingly parallel** on GPUs (matrix-multiplication style).
- It uses **dense matrix operations** that map directly to **hardware accelerators (TPU/FPGA)** in commercial EDA tools.
- It produces a **single $V^2$ matrix**, which is cache-friendly and essential for downstream timing-closure engines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Master Recurrence (Proof of Optimal Substructure)

**Claim:** For all $k \geq 0$, $D^{(k)}[i, j]$ equals the length of the shortest path from $i$ to $j$ whose internal vertices (excluding endpoints) are drawn from $\{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k\}$.

**Proof by Induction on $k$:**

**Base Case ($k = 0$):** No internal vertices are allowed. Thus the only permitted path is the direct edge $i \to j$ (or self-loop if $i = j$). Hence $D^{(0)}[i, j] = w(i, j)$ if edge exists, else $\infty$. The claim holds.

**Inductive Step:** Assume the claim holds for $k - 1$. Consider the shortest $i \to j$ path $P$ using internal vertices from $\{v_1, \ldots, v_k\}$.

*Case 1:* $P$ does **not** use $v_k$ as an internal vertex. Then $P$ is a valid path under the $k - 1$ hypothesis, with length $D^{(k-1)}[i, j]$.

*Case 2:* $P$ **does** use $v_k$ as an internal vertex. Decompose $P$ at $v_k$ into $P_1: i \leadsto v_k$ and $P_2: v_k \leadsto j$. Both sub-paths have internal vertices in $\{v_1, \ldots, v_{k-1}\}$ (since $v_k$ is an *internal* vertex of $P$, it does not appear inside $P_1$ or $P_2$ as internal). By induction, their lengths are $D^{(k-1)}[i, v_k]$ and $D^{(k-1)}[v_k, j]$. The total length is $D^{(k-1)}[i, v_k] + D^{(k-1)}[v_k, j]$.

The optimal is therefore the minimum of these two cases:

$$
D^{(k)}[i, j] = \min\Big( D^{(k-1)}[i, j], \;\; D^{(k-1)}[i, k] + D^{(k-1)}[k, j] \Big)
$$

This completes the induction. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (VLSI Routing Case Study)

Consider a **4-vertex routing graph** representing four macros $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ on a chip. Edge weights = estimated wirelength in **micrometers ($\mu m$)**. Find all-pairs shortest paths.

**Initial distance matrix $D^{(0)}$:**

$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 8 & \infty & 1 \\ \infty & 0 & 1 & \infty \\ 4 & \infty & 0 & \infty \\ \infty & 2 & 9 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Iteration $k = 1$ (Use vertex 1 as intermediate)

Compute all entries where the new detour via vertex 1 is shorter:

- $D^{(1)}[2, 3] = \min(1, \infty + \infty) = 1$ (no change)
- $D^{(1)}[2, 4] = \min(\infty, \infty + 1) = \infty$ (no change)
- $D^{(1)}[3, 2] = \min(\infty, 4 + 8) = 12$ (improved via $3 \to 1 \to 2$)
- $D^{(1)}[3, 4] = \min(\infty, 4 + 1) = 5$ (improved via $3 \to 1 \to 4$)
- $D^{(1)}[4, 2] = \min(2, \infty + 8) = 2$ (no change)
- $D^{(1)}[4, 3] = \min(9, \infty + \infty) = 9$ (no change)
- $D^{(1)}[4, 4] = 0$ (self)

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 8 & \infty & 1 \\ \infty & 0 & 1 & \infty \\ 4 & 12 & 0 & 5 \\ \infty & 2 & 9 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Iteration $k = 2$ (Use vertex 2 as additional intermediate)

Check detours via vertex 2:

- $D^{(2)}[1, 3] = \min(\infty, 8 + 1) = 9$ (improved: $1 \to 2 \to 3$)
- $D^{(2)}[3, 1] = \min(4, 12 + \infty) = 4$ (no change)
- $D^{(2)}[3, 4] = \min(5, 12 + \infty) = 5$ (no change)
- $D^{(2)}[4, 1] = \min(\infty, 2 + \infty) = \infty$ (no change)
- $D^{(2)}[4, 3] = \min(9, 2 + 1) = 3$ (improved: $4 \to 2 \to 3$)
- $D^{(2)}[1, 4] = \min(1, 8 + \infty) = 1$ (no change)

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 8 & 9 & 1 \\ \infty & 0 & 1 & \infty \\ 4 & 12 & 0 & 5 \\ \infty & 2 & 3 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Iteration $k = 3$ (Use vertex 3 as additional intermediate)

Check detours via vertex 3:

- $D^{(3)}[1, 2] = \min(8, 9 + 12) = 8$ (no change)
- $D^{(3)}[1, 4] = \min(1, 9 + 5) = 1$ (no change)
- $D^{(3)}[2, 1] = \min(\infty, 1 + 4) = 5$ (improved: $2 \to 3 \to 1$)
- $D^{(3)}[2, 4] = \min(\infty, 1 + 5) = 6$ (improved: $2 \to 3 \to 4$)
- $D^{(3)}[4, 1] = \min(\infty, 3 + 4) = 7$ (improved: $4 \to 3 \to 1$)
- $D^{(3)}[4, 2] = \min(2, 3 + 12) = 2$ (no change)

$$
D^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 8 & 9 & 1 \\ 5 & 0 & 1 & 6 \\ 4 & 12 & 0 & 5 \\ 7 & 2 & 3 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Iteration $k = 4$ (Use vertex 4 as additional intermediate)

Check detours via vertex 4:

- $D^{(4)}[1, 2] = \min(8, 1 + 2) = 3$ (improved: $1 \to 4 \to 2$)
- $D^{(4)}[1, 3] = \min(9, 1 + 3) = 4$ (improved: $1 \to 4 \to 3$)
- $D^{(4)}[2, 1] = \min(5, 6 + 7) = 5$ (no change)
- $D^{(4)}[2, 3] = \min(1, 6 + 3) = 1$ (no change)
- $D^{(4)}[3, 1] = \min(4, 5 + 7) = 4$ (no change)
- $D^{(4)}[3, 2] = \min(12, 5 + 2) = 7$ (improved: $3 \to 4 \to 2$)

$$
\boxed{D^{(4)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & 4 & 1 \\ 5 & 0 & 1 & 6 \\ 4 & 7 & 0 & 5 \\ 7 & 2 & 3 & 0 \end{bmatrix}}
$$

**Final All-Pairs Shortest Wirelength Matrix (in $\mu m$):** The matrix $D^{(4)}$ gives the minimum wirelength between every pair of macros on the chip.

### 3.3 Time & Space Complexity Derivation

**Time:** The algorithm uses three nested loops, each running $n$ times.

$$
T(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \Theta(1) = n \cdot n \cdot n \cdot \Theta(1) = \Theta(n^3)
$$

**Space:** Two $n \times n$ matrices ($D$ and $\Pi$) are stored, yielding $\Theta(n^2)$ space. (The in-place variant eliminates the need to store $D^{(k-1)}$ separately, since each update writes back to the same matrix.)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithm Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Graph G with n vertices and edge weight matrix W] --> B[Initialize D as W, set diagonal to 0, set no-edge entries to infinity]
    B --> C[Initialize predecessor matrix Pi with Pi i j equals j if edge exists, else NIL]
    C --> D[Set k equals 1]
    D --> E{k less than or equal to n}
    E -- Yes --> F[Set i equals 1]
    F --> G{i less than or equal to n}
    G -- Yes --> H[Set j equals 1]
    H --> I{j less than or equal to n}
    I -- Yes --> J{Compute D i j equals min of D i j and D i k plus D k j}
    J --> K{If new path is shorter, update Pi i j equals Pi k j}
    K --> L[Increment j]
    L --> I
    I -- No --> M[Increment i]
    M --> G
    G -- No --> N[Increment k]
    N --> E
    E -- No --> O[Output final distance matrix D and predecessor matrix Pi]
    O --> P[End: All pairs shortest paths computed]
```

### 4.2 VLSI Routing Graph Example

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1((Macro 1)) -- 8um --> node2((Macro 2))
    node1 -- 1um --> node4((Macro 4))
    node2 -- 1um --> node3((Macro 3))
    node3 -- 4um --> node1
    node4 -- 2um --> node2
    node4 -- 9um --> node3
    classDef macro fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    class node1,node2,node3,node4 macro
```

### 4.3 Distance Matrix Update Process (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph K0["Iteration k = 0 (Direct)"]
        D0["D0 Matrix: Direct edge weights"]
    end
    subgraph K1["Iteration k = 1 (via Vertex 1)"]
        D1["D1 Matrix: D min of D0 and D0 i 1 plus D0 1 j"]
    end
    subgraph K2["Iteration k = 2 (via Vertex 2)"]
        D2["D2 Matrix: D min of D1 and D1 i 2 plus D1 2 j"]
    end
    subgraph K3["Iteration k = 3 (via Vertex 3)"]
        D3["D3 Matrix: D min of D2 and D2 i 3 plus D2 3 j"]
    end
    subgraph K4["Iteration k = 4 (via Vertex 4)"]
        D4["D4 Matrix: Final all pairs shortest paths"]
    end
    D0 --> D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4
```

### 4.4 Architecture Mapping — Floyd Warshall in EDA Tool Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RTL[RTL Design Verilog VHDL] --> SYN[Logic Synthesis]
    SYN --> FLOOR[Floorplanning Placement]
    FLOOR --> GR[Global Routing]
    GR --> FW[Floyd Warshall All Pairs Kernel]
    FW --> TR[Track Assignment Detailed Routing]
    TR --> STA[Static Timing Analysis]
    STA --> OPT[Timing Driven Optimization]
    OPT --> GDSII[GDSII Layout for Fabrication]
    style FW fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#ff6600,stroke-width:3px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** 
Define the Floyd–Warshall algorithm. State its time and space complexity. Why is it preferred over running Dijkstra's algorithm $V$ times in VLSI global routing?

**Model Answer:**
The Floyd–Warshall algorithm is a dynamic programming algorithm that computes the shortest paths between **all pairs of vertices** in a weighted directed graph. It uses the recurrence $D^{(k)}[i, j] = \min(D^{(k-1)}[i, j], D^{(k-1)}[i, k] + D^{(k-1)}[k, j])$. Time complexity is $O(V^3)$ and space complexity is $O(V^2)$. In VLSI global routing, it is preferred because: (i) it handles negative edge weights (representing delay savings from buffer insertion), (ii) it is highly parallelizable on GPU/FPGA hardware accelerators used in modern EDA tools, and (iii) it produces a single dense matrix that integrates efficiently with downstream timing analysis engines.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** 
What is the role of the predecessor matrix $\Pi$ in the Floyd–Warshall algorithm? How is it updated at each iteration?

**Model Answer:**
The predecessor matrix $\Pi[i, j]$ stores the immediate predecessor of vertex $j$ on the shortest path from $i$ to $j$. It enables **path reconstruction** — critical in VLSI CAD for tracing the actual routing sequence between two macros. At each iteration $k$, the update rule is:

$$
\Pi^{(k)}[i, j] = 
\begin{cases}
\Pi^{(k-1)}[i, j] & \text{if } D^{(k-1)}[i, j] \leq D^{(k-1)}[i, k] + D^{(k-1)}[k, j] \\
\Pi^{(k-1)}[k, j] & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** 
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to update $D^{(0)}[i, i] = 0$ (self-loops). This causes spurious "infinite" diagonals and wrong shortest paths.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** When asked for "all-pairs shortest path", students only compute from vertex 1 (that's single-source!). You MUST compute the complete $n \times n$ matrix.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing transitive closure (boolean) with shortest path (numeric). They are *different* applications of the same algorithmic skeleton.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Not writing the initialization step $D^{(0)}$ explicitly — KTU examiners deduct 2 marks for this.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]** 

(a) **(7 Marks)** Derive the dynamic programming recurrence for the Floyd–Warshall algorithm. Explain the meaning of $D^{(k)}[i, j]$ at every stage $k$.

(b) **(7 Marks)** Apply the Floyd–Warshall algorithm on the following VLSI routing graph (weights in $\mu m$) and compute the all-pairs shortest wirelength matrix. Show all intermediate $D^{(k)}$ matrices.

$$
W = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\ 2 & 0 & \infty & \infty \\ 5 & \infty & 0 & 1 \\ \infty & 6 & 4 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
Let $D^{(k)}[i, j]$ denote the shortest path length from vertex $i$ to vertex $j$ using only intermediate vertices from the set $\{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$. For $k = 0$, no intermediate vertices are allowed, so $D^{(0)}[i, j] = w(i, j)$ (or $\infty$ if no edge).

Assume the result holds for $k-1$. For iteration $k$, consider the shortest $i \to j$ path. Either it does not pass through vertex $k$ (cost $D^{(k-1)}[i, j]$), or it does — splitting into $i \to k$ and $k \to j$ (cost $D^{(k-1)}[i, k] + D^{(k-1)}[k, j]$). Taking the minimum:

$$
D^{(k)}[i, j] = \min\Big( D^{(k-1)}[i, j], \;\; D^{(k-1)}[i, k] + D^{(k-1)}[k, j] \Big)
$$

This is the optimal substructure of the Floyd–Warshall algorithm. After $k = n$ iterations, $D^{(n)}$ gives the all-pairs shortest path matrix.

**(b) Worked Example (7 marks):**

**$D^{(0)}$ (initial):**
$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\ 2 & 0 & \infty & \infty \\ 5 & \infty & 0 & 1 \\ \infty & 6 & 4 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**$k = 1$ (intermediate via vertex 1):** Check detours through vertex 1.
- $D[2,4] = \min(\infty, 2+7) = 9$
- $D[3,2] = \min(\infty, 5+3) = 8$
- $D[3,4] = \min(1, 5+7) = 1$
- $D[4,1] = \min(\infty, \infty+0) = \infty$

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\ 2 & 0 & \infty & 9 \\ 5 & 8 & 0 & 1 \\ \infty & 6 & 4 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**$k = 2$ (via vertex 2):**
- $D[1,3] = \min(\infty, 3+\infty) = \infty$
- $D[1,4] = \min(7, 3+9) = 7$
- $D[3,1] = \min(5, 8+2) = 5$
- $D[3,4] = \min(1, 8+9) = 1$
- $D[4,1] = \min(\infty, 6+2) = 8$
- $D[4,3] = \min(4, 6+\infty) = 4$

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\ 2 & 0 & \infty & 9 \\ 5 & 8 & 0 & 1 \\ 8 & 6 & 4 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**$k = 3$ (via vertex 3):**
- $D[1,3] = \min(\infty, 7+0) = 7$ (via $1\to 2\to 3$... wait, $3$ as intermediate. $D[1,3] = D[1,3] = \min(\infty, 7+0) = 7$)
- $D[1,4] = \min(7, 7+1) = 7$
- $D[2,1] = \min(2, \infty+5) = 2$
- $D[2,3] = \min(\infty, 9+0) = 9$
- $D[2,4] = \min(9, 9+1) = 9$
- $D[4,1] = \min(8, 4+5) = 8$
- $D[4,2] = \min(6, 4+8) = 6$

$$
D^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & 7 & 7 \\ 2 & 0 & 9 & 9 \\ 5 & 8 & 0 & 1 \\ 8 & 6 & 4 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**$k = 4$ (via vertex 4):**
- $D[1,2] = \min(3, 7+6) = 3$
- $D[1,3] = \min(7, 7+4) = 7$
- $D[2,1] = \min(2, 9+8) = 2$
- $D[2,2] = 0$
- $D[2,3] = \min(9, 9+4) = 9$
- $D[2,4] = \min(9, 9+0) = 9$
- $D[3,1] = \min(5, 1+8) = 5$
- $D[3,2] = \min(8, 1+6) = 7$
- $D[3,3] = 0$

$$
\boxed{D^{(4)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & 7 & 7 \\ 2 & 0 & 9 & 9 \\ 5 & 7 & 0 & 1 \\ 8 & 6 & 4 & 0 \end{bmatrix}}
$$

This is the final all-pairs shortest wirelength matrix for the VLSI routing graph.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**

(a) **(7 Marks)** Compare the Floyd–Warshall algorithm with the repeated application of Dijkstra's algorithm for all-pairs shortest path. Discuss their time complexities, applicability to negative edge weights, and parallelization potential in VLSI CAD tools.

(b) **(7 Marks)** Apply the Floyd–Warshall algorithm on the following graph representing signal propagation delays (in picoseconds, $ps$) between five logic blocks. Compute the all-pairs shortest delay matrix and reconstruct the critical path from Block 1 to Block 5.

$$
W = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 10 & \infty & 30 & 100 \\ \infty & 0 & 50 & \infty & \infty \\ \infty & \infty & 0 & \infty & 10 \\ \infty & 20 & 20 & 0 & 60 \\ \infty & \infty & \infty & \infty & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Model Solution (Outline):**

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Criterion | Floyd–Warshall | Dijkstra × V |
|---|---|---|
| Time Complexity | $O(V^3)$ | $O(V \cdot E \log V)$ |
| Negative Weights | **Supported** | Not supported |
| Sparse Graph ($E \ll V^2$) | Inefficient | Efficient |
| Dense Graph ($E \approx V^2$) | Efficient | Comparable |
| Parallelization | Excellent (GPU-friendly matrix ops) | Moderate (priority queue) |
| VLSI Use Case | Global routing, STA | Per-net detailed routing |

**(b) Worked Example (7 marks):** Similar matrix iteration approach as Question A, applied to the 5×5 graph. The final matrix yields the minimum signal delay from Block 1 to Block 5, with path reconstruction through the predecessor matrix.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Equation:** $D^{(k)}[i, j] = \min(D^{(k-1)}[i, j], D^{(k-1)}[i, k] + D^{(k-1)}[k, j])$
- **Initialization Rule:** $D^{(0)}[i, i] = 0$, $D^{(0)}[i, j] = w(i, j)$ if edge exists, else $\infty$
- **Number of Iterations:** Exactly $n$ (number of vertices)
- **Time Complexity:** $O(n^3)$ — three nested loops
- **Space Complexity:** $O(n^2)$ — in-place update is possible
- **Path Reconstruction:** Maintain parallel $\Pi$ matrix
- **VLSI Application:** Global routing wirelength optimization, clock tree synthesis, static timing analysis
- **Negative Edges:** Allowed (unlike Dijkstra), but negative cycles are detected by checking $D[i, i] < 0$ at the end
- **Transitive Closure:** Replace $(\min, +)$ with $(\lor, \land)$ for reachability analysis
- **Examiner Tip:** Always label each iteration clearly ($k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$) and show the matrix snapshot — partial marks depend on traceability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exam Day:** 
> **"F = For Every pair, Floyd finds the Fastest Fix."** 
> The three F's: **F**or all pairs, **F**ind shortest, **F**ixed in $O(n^3)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Algorithmic and System Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT525 - CAD BASED VLSI DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Algorithmic and System Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Algorithmic Design in VLSI CAD Context

**Algorithmic Design** in the context of VLSI Computer-Aided Design (CAD) refers to the systematic development and application of well-defined computational procedures, mathematical models, and optimization heuristics used to automate, accelerate, and refine the transformation of a high-level circuit specification into a manufacturable physical layout. It forms the mathematical backbone of every Electronic Design Automation (EDA) tool.

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> Algorithmic Design is the structured methodology of formulating, analyzing, and implementing efficient algorithms (polynomial-time exact, heuristic, or meta-heuristic) to solve NP-hard and PSPACE-hard problems encountered across the **Y-chart** domains of VLSI design — namely, behavior, structure, and geometry.

**System Design** is the hierarchical, multi-level engineering discipline of defining, partitioning, modeling, and integrating the architectural, logical, functional, and physical components of a complex VLSI system to meet prescribed performance, power, area, and timing (PPAT) constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 2 Focus:**
> Module 2 emphasizes the *theoretical* and *mathematical* foundations underpinning EDA tools. Expect questions on algorithmic complexity (Big-O, NP-completeness), graph-theoretic formulations, optimization landscapes, and the system-level design trade-off triangle.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 — Algorithmic Design as a Master Chef's Recipe Book:**
Imagine you own a massive restaurant kitchen (the VLSI chip) with thousands of ingredients (transistors, gates, wires). The challenge is to cook 10,000 dishes (logic functions) simultaneously while minimizing fuel (power), counter space (area), and delivery time (delay). A master chef doesn't just cook randomly — he follows carefully engineered *recipes (algorithms)* that specify: which ingredients to combine first, in what order, how to share stoves, and how to minimize waste. **Algorithmic Design is the engineering of these recipes for silicon kitchens.**

**Analogy 2 — System Design as Building a Skyscraper:**
System design is like constructing a 100-floor skyscraper (the SoC). You cannot start with bricks (transistors); you must first decide: How many floors? (architecture), What utilities on each floor? (functional blocks), How do elevators connect them? (interconnect), What is the fire-safety plan? (timing/thermal constraints), and what is the budget? (silicon cost). System Design is this *top-down abstraction layering*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics in VLSI Algorithmic/System Design (PPAT):**
> - **P**ower (Watts)
> - **P**erformance / Delay (seconds)
> - **A**rea (μm²)
> - **T**hroughput / Timing (operations/second)
> Modern extensions include: **Reliability, Manufacturability, Testability** (the "DFM/DFT" axes).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **MOSFET threshold voltage (Vₜ):** typically **0.3 V – 0.7 V** for modern nodes.
- **Supply voltage (V_DD):** **0.7 V – 1.2 V** (advanced FinFET/FDX nodes).
- **Feature size (λ):** scaling from **10 nm** to **3 nm** and below (2 nm GAA in 2025).
- **Clock frequency target:** **100 MHz – 5 GHz** for high-performance SoCs.
- **Big-O complexity benchmark:** polynomial-time $O(n^k)$ is considered *tractable*; $O(2^n)$ is *intractable*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Algorithmic Design Pipeline in VLSI

The VLSI design flow can be decomposed into **seven algorithmic stages**, each governed by a distinct class of algorithms:

1. **Specification & High-Level Synthesis (HLS):**
   - Input: Behavioral description (C/C++/SystemC/MATLAB).
   - Algorithm: Scheduling, Allocation, Binding.
   - Goal: Generate RTL (Register Transfer Level) description.
   - Complexity: NP-hard (resource-constrained scheduling).

2. **Logic Synthesis:**
   - Input: RTL (Verilog/VHDL).
   - Algorithm: Two-level (Quine-McCluskey) and multi-level (algebraic/Boolean decomposition) minimization.
   - Complexity: Quine-McCluskey is $O(3^n / n)$; Espresso heuristic is $O(n \cdot k)$ average.

3. **Partitioning:**
   - Input: Hypergraph $G = (V, E)$ of circuit cells.
   - Algorithm: Kernighan-Lin (KL), Fiduccia-Mattheyses (FM), hMETIS.
   - Objective: Minimize cut-size while balancing partition weights.
   - Complexity: FM is $O(P \cdot E)$ per pass, where $P$ is the number of pins.

4. **Floorplanning:**
   - Input: Set of rectangular blocks with area and aspect ratio constraints.
   - Algorithm: Polish expression tree, sequence pair, B*-tree, simulated annealing (e.g., TimberWolf).
   - Complexity: SA is $O(n^2)$ per iteration; exact slicing is NP-hard.

5. **Placement:**
   - Input: Netlist + floorplan.
   - Algorithm: Force-directed, quadratic placement (Gordian), analytic (mPL, ePlace), simulated annealing.
   - Complexity: Analytic placement is $O(n^{1.5})$ to $O(n^2)$ per iteration.

6. **Global & Detailed Routing:**
   - Input: Placed netlist + routing grid.
   - Algorithm: Maze (Lee's algorithm), A*, Steiner tree (Kruskal/Prim-based MST), rip-up & reroute, negotiation-based (PathFinder).
   - Complexity: Lee's maze is $O(V)$ per net; PathFinder is $O(I \cdot N \cdot V)$ where $I$ = iterations.

7. **Compaction, Verification & Mask Synthesis:**
   - Algorithm: 1-D graph-based compaction, SAT-based formal verification, ILP-based OPC (Optical Proximity Correction).
   - Complexity: Verification is co-NP hard; OPC is NP-hard and solved via linear programming relaxations.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of Algorithmic Design Choices

- **Why heuristics?** Most VLSI optimization problems are NP-hard. Exact algorithms (branch-and-bound, ILP) are infeasible for $n > 50$ cells.
- **How do we measure success?** Wirelength (HPWL — Half-Perimeter Wire Length), cut-size, congestion, slack, and Power-Performance-Area (PPA) score.
- **Why graph theory?** Almost every VLSI structure maps naturally to a graph: netlist → hypergraph, routing grid → grid graph, floorplan → slicing tree, timing → DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph).

### 2.3 System Design Hierarchy (Gajski-Kuhn Y-Chart)

The **Y-chart** is the foundational system-design model in VLSI:

- **Behavioral Domain:** What the system *does* (algorithms, FSMs, data flow).
- **Structural Domain:** How it is *built* (modules, registers, ALUs, interconnects).
- **Geometric Domain:** How it is *laid out* (floorplan, placement, routing).

Each domain is refined through **levels of abstraction**: System → Algorithm → RTL → Logic → Circuit → Layout.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The Y-chart *rotation principle* — synthesizing across domains at the same abstraction level (e.g., RTL synthesis = behavioral-to-structural at register level) is the foundational concept for understanding all EDA tools.

### 2.4 Algorithmic Complexity Classes — Critical for Exams

| Complexity Class | Notation | Example VLSI Algorithm | Tractable? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Constant | $O(1)$ | LUT lookup | ✅ Yes |
| Logarithmic | $O(\log n)$ | Binary search in cell library | ✅ Yes |
| Linear | $O(n)$ | Scan-line DRC | ✅ Yes |
| Linearithmic | $O(n \log n)$ | Prim/Kruskal MST for Steiner tree | ✅ Yes |
| Polynomial | $O(n^k)$ | Force-directed placement | ✅ Yes |
| Exponential | $O(2^n)$ | Brute-force RTL scheduling | ❌ No (intractable) |
| Factorial | $O(n!)$ | Exhaustive floorplan permutation | ❌ No (intractable) |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T(n) = O(f(n))$ if $\exists c, n_0 > 0$ s.t. $T(n) \le c \cdot f(n) \ \forall n \ge n_0$ | Big-O asymptotic upper bound | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $HPWL_{net} = \max(x_i) - \min(x_i) + \max(y_i) - \min(y_i)$ | Half-Perimeter Wire Length estimate | μm |
| 3 | $Cost_{cut} = \sum_{e \in E_{cut}} w(e)$ | Partitioning cut cost | Sum of edge weights |
| 4 | $WL_{total} = \sum_{n \in N} HPWL(n)$ | Total wirelength objective | μm |
| 5 | $Balance = \frac{\vert W_A - W_B \vert}{W_{total}} \le \epsilon$ | Partition balance ratio | Ratio, $\epsilon \in [0.05, 0.1]$ |
| 6 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$ | Dynamic power (switching) | Watts |
| 7 | $P_{static} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Static leakage power | Watts |
| 8 | $Delay = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \cdot I_{on}} + t_{int}$ | Gate propagation delay (alpha-power) | seconds |
| 9 | $A_{chip} = \sum_{i} A_{block,i} + A_{interconnect}$ | Total silicon area | mm² |
| 10 | $SLR = \frac{T_{clk} - T_{ckt}}{T_{ckt}}$ | Slack Ratio for timing closure | Ratio |
| 11 | $T_{crit} = \text{Longest path delay (DAG)}$ | Critical path delay | seconds |
| 12 | $P(Pareto) = \frac{\# \text{ dominated}}{\# \text{ total}}$ | Pareto optimality ratio | Probability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas form the *objective functions* of virtually every EDA tool. For example, the placer minimizes $WL_{total}$ while the optimizer jointly minimizes $(P, T, A)$ under a *Pareto-optimal* trade-off curve.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **NVIDIA / AMD GPUs** use multi-level hypergraph partitioning (hMETIS) for die-level floorplanning of 100+ billion transistor chips.
- **Synopsys Design Compiler** and **Cadence Genus** implement logic synthesis algorithms derived from Quine-McCluskey / Espresso.
- **Google TPU** custom silicon uses HLS-driven design (Xilinx HLS → Verilog) to compress design cycles from years to months.
- **Samsung Exynos / Qualcomm Snapdragon** use mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) for clock-tree synthesis optimization.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Half-Perimeter Wire Length (HPWL) Bound on Steiner Minimum Tree (SMT)

The **Steiner Minimum Tree (SMT)** of a multi-terminal net gives the absolute minimum wirelength for that net, but it is NP-hard to compute. The **HPWL** is a popular lower-bound *proxy* used by all modern placers. We derive its relationship to the SMT.

Let a net have $k$ pins with coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, k$.

**Step 1 — Define bounding box of the net:**

$$x_{min} = \min_{i} x_i, \quad x_{max} = \max_{i} x_i$$

$$y_{min} = \min_{i} y_i, \quad y_{max} = \max_{i} y_i$$

**Step 2 — Compute HPWL:**

$$HPWL = (x_{max} - x_{min}) + (y_{max} - y_{min})$$

**Step 3 — Geometric Interpretation:**
The HPWL is the perimeter of the *smallest axis-aligned rectangle enclosing all pins*, divided by 2. It corresponds to the length of an "L-shaped" or "Z-shaped" rectilinear connection (the **rectilinear spanning tree** or **Hamiltonian path**).

**Step 4 — Bound on Steiner Minimum Tree:**

The SMT uses **Steiner points** to "shortcut" the tree, so:

$$HPWL \ge L_{SMT}$$

where $L_{SMT}$ is the optimal Steiner tree length.

**Step 5 — Ratio Bound (Chu & Wong, 1984):**

For a $k$-pin net, the *Steiner tree ratio* is bounded by:

$$\frac{L_{SMT}}{HPWL} \ge \frac{1}{2}$$

**Textual justification of step 5:** In the worst case, two pins lie on opposite diagonal corners of the bounding box. The Manhattan distance between them is $(x_{max} - x_{min}) + (y_{max} - y_{min}) = HPWL$, and the Steiner tree (which for two pins equals the Manhattan path) equals this value. For multi-terminal nets, the SMT is always ≤ the longest Manhattan path.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Kernighan-Lin (KL) Partitioning Gain Calculation

The KL algorithm iteratively swaps pairs of vertices between two partitions $A$ and $B$ to *maximize* the gain $\Delta g$ per swap.

**Step 1 — Define initial cut-size $C_0$:**

$$C_0 = \sum_{e \in E_{cut}} w(e)$$

**Step 2 — Define internal and external costs for a vertex $v$:**

$$D(v) = \sum_{u \in same(v)} w(v, u) \quad \text{(Internal cost: edges to same partition)}$$

$$E(v) = \sum_{u \notin same(v)} w(v, u) \quad \text{(External cost: edges to other partition)}$$

**Step 3 — Gain of moving $v$ from $A$ to $B$:**

$$\Delta g_v = E(v) - D(v)$$

**Step 4 — Gain of swapping a pair $(a \in A, b \in B)$:**

$$\Delta g_{(a,b)} = \Delta g_a + \Delta g_b - 2 \cdot w(a, b)$$

**Textual justification of step 4:** The edge between $a$ and $b$ is counted twice (once in $E(a)$ and once in $E(b)$) but after the swap becomes *internal*, so it must be subtracted twice.

**Step 5 — Cumulative gain over $k$ swaps:**

$$G_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \Delta g_{i}, \quad G^{*} = \max_{1 \le k \le n/2} G_k$$

**Step 6 — Termination:** Lock swapped vertices; repeat until no positive cumulative gain exists. Time complexity: $O(n^2 \log n)$ for the original KL; FM improves this to $O(n)$ per pass using bucket lists.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Dynamic Power Estimation (Alpha-Power Model)

**Step 1 — Capacitive charging energy per transition:**

$$E_{charge} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$

(Textual: half the energy is dissipated in the PMOS, half stored in the capacitor, then dissipated in the NMOS during discharge — the classic ½CV² derivation.)

**Step 2 — Power for switching at frequency $f_{clk}$ with switching activity $\alpha$:**

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$

**Step 3 — Total power (dynamic + static):**

$$P_{total} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk} + I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$$

**Step 4 — Energy-Delay Product (EDP) — the canonical optimization metric:**

$$EDP = P_{total} \cdot T_{delay} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$

**Textual justification of step 4:** The EDP measures the energy-efficiency per unit of performance. Lower EDP = better design. This is the single most important number optimized in mobile/IoT SoCs.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Critical Path Delay via Topological Sort (DAG Analysis)

Given a circuit netlist as a DAG $G = (V, E)$, the **critical path delay** is the longest path from any primary input (PI) to any primary output (PO).

**Step 1 — Initialize delay of all PIs:**

$$T(v) = 0 \quad \forall v \in PI$$

**Step 2 — Topological order:** Process vertices in topological order $(v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n)$.

**Step 3 — Recurrence for each gate:**

$$T(v) = \max_{u \in fanin(v)} \big[ T(u) + d_{gate}(v) \big]$$

where $d_{gate}(v)$ is the intrinsic delay of gate $v$ (e.g., NAND = 1 unit, NOR = 1.5 units).

**Step 4 — Critical path:**

$$T_{crit} = \max_{v \in PO} T(v)$$

**Step 5 — Slack for each gate $v$:**

$$SLR(v) = T_{clk} - T_{crit} \quad (\text{negative slack} = \text{timing violation})$$

**Algorithmic complexity:** $O(V + E)$ via single-source-longest-path with topological order — *linear-time and polynomial-tractable* — a major reason why timing analysis is fast in commercial STA tools (Synopsys PrimeTime).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Algorithmic Trade-offs

| Algorithm | Objective | Complexity | Optimality | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| KL Partitioning | Min cut | $O(n^2 \log n)$ | Local optimum | Early EDA tools |
| FM Partitioning | Min cut | $O(n)$ per pass | Local optimum | Modern partitioners |
| Simulated Annealing Placement | Min WL | $O(n^2)$ per iter. | Near-global | TimberWolf, VPR |
| Quadratic Placement | Min WL² | $O(n^{1.5})$ | Local optimum | Gordian, mPL |
| Maze Routing (Lee) | Find path | $O(V)$ per net | Optimal | Detailed router |
| PathFinder | Negotiation | $O(I \cdot N \cdot V)$ | Convergent | Global router |
| Steiner Tree (Kruskal) | Min WL | $O(E \log E)$ | Near-optimal | Pin-to-pin routing |
| Espresso Logic Min. | Min literals | $O(n \cdot k)$ avg. | Heuristic | Logic synthesis |
| HLS Scheduling (List) | Min latency | $O(n \log n)$ | Heuristic | Catapult, Vitis HLS |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 VLSI Design Flow — Algorithmic Mapping

The following Mermaid block renders the complete VLSI design flow and the corresponding algorithmic class used at each stage. All node IDs are alphanumeric; labels are pure uppercase alphanumeric text per KTU-Premier-Engine v10 safeguards.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]**

**Q1. Define algorithmic design in the context of VLSI CAD. Differentiate between exact and heuristic approaches with one example each.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Algorithmic design in VLSI CAD is the formulation of computational procedures to solve EDA problems (placement, routing, synthesis, verification) at the chip, block, and gate levels. Algorithms are classified as:

- **Exact algorithms** guarantee optimal solutions (e.g., branch-and-bound for small-scale placement), but exhibit exponential worst-case complexity and are infeasible for $n > 50$ cells.
- **Heuristic algorithms** (e.g., Fiduccia-Mattheyses partitioning) produce near-optimal solutions in polynomial time $O(n^k)$, making them the *de facto* standard in commercial EDA tools.

---

**Q2. State and explain the Y-chart (Gajski-Kuhn) model of VLSI system design. List the three domains and four abstraction levels.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The Y-chart by Gajski and Kuhn organizes VLSI design along three intersecting axes: **Behavioral** (what the system computes), **Structural** (modules and their interconnection), and **Geometric** (physical layout). Each domain is refined through four abstraction levels: **System**, **Algorithm (RTL)**, **Logic**, and **Circuit/Layout**. Synthesis corresponds to *radial* movement on the chart (across domains at a fixed level), while *verification* corresponds to *vertical* movement (across levels within a domain).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester, Module 2 Choice Pattern]**

---

#### ❖ Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

**Q.A. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the complete algorithmic VLSI design flow from specification to tape-out, identifying the algorithm class used at each stage. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The VLSI design flow is a sequence of algorithmic transformations that progressively refine a high-level specification into a manufacturing-ready GDS-II layout. The seven canonical stages and their dominant algorithms are:

| Stage | Input | Output | Algorithm Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Specification | English / math | C/C++ model | Heuristic functional modeling |
| 2. High-Level Synthesis | C/C++ | RTL (Verilog) | List scheduling, force-directed scheduling (NP-hard) |
| 3. Logic Synthesis | RTL | Gate netlist | Espresso (two-level), SIS (multi-level) |
| 4. Partitioning | Hypergraph | Sub-circuits | FM, hMETIS (multi-level) |
| 5. Floorplanning | Block list | Macro plan | B*-tree, sequence pair, SA |
| 6. Placement | Netlist | Cell positions | Analytic (mPL6), ePlace |
| 7. Routing | Placement | Wires | PathFinder (negotiation), Lee (maze) |

Each stage is an *optimization* problem: it minimizes a cost function (wirelength, delay, power) under constraints (timing, congestion, area). The flow is iterated; the placer may invoke the synthesizer, and the router invokes the placer in a "timing-driven" loop. The output of the entire flow is a GDS-II file, the legal contract for the foundry.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Part (a):** Students commonly lose **2–3 marks** by *listing stages without naming the specific algorithm used at each stage*. Always pair each stage with its algorithm (e.g., "Placement → Analytic Quadratic, Force-Directed, or Simulated Annealing") and mention its complexity class.

---

**Q.A. (b)** For the 4-pin net with pin coordinates $(2, 1), (5, 3), (1, 6), (7, 8)$, compute the **Half-Perimeter Wire Length (HPWL)**. Also derive the relationship between HPWL and Steiner Minimum Tree (SMT) length, citing the lower-bound ratio for multi-terminal nets. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Extract bounding box:**

$$x_{min} = \min(2, 5, 1, 7) = 1$$
$$x_{max} = \max(2, 5, 1, 7) = 7$$
$$y_{min} = \min(1, 3, 6, 8) = 1$$
$$y_{max} = \max(1, 3, 6, 8) = 8$$

**Step 2 — Compute HPWL:**

$$HPWL = (x_{max} - x_{min}) + (y_{max} - y_{min}) = (7 - 1) + (8 - 1) = 6 + 7 = 13 \ \mu m$$

**Step 3 — Steiner Minimum Tree relationship:**

The HPWL is the *half-perimeter* of the smallest axis-aligned bounding rectangle enclosing all pins. It serves as a *proxy* for the SMT length because:

$$HPWL \ge L_{SMT}$$

**Step 4 — Lower-bound ratio (Chu-Wong, 1984):**

For a $k$-terminal net, the *Steiner ratio* is bounded as:

$$\frac{L_{SMT}}{HPWL} \ge \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** The HPWL is at most **2× the optimal SMT length**, but in practice, the ratio is typically 1.05–1.2, making HPWL an excellent low-cost proxy used by virtually every placer (mPL, ePlace, Dragon).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Part (b):** A common mistake is to write $HPWL = (x_{max} + y_{max}) - (x_{min} + y_{min})$, *forgetting the multiplication*. The correct form is the *sum of differences* (half-perimeter), not the sum of sums. **2 marks lost** for this error.

---

#### ❖ Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**Q.B. (a)** Explain the **Kernighan-Lin (KL)** and **Fiduccia-Mattheyses (FM)** partitioning algorithms. With a worked example, demonstrate the gain calculation for a swap of a vertex pair. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**KL Algorithm (1970):**
- Starts with a random bisection $(A, B)$ of a graph $G = (V, E)$.
- Iteratively selects a *pair* $(a, b)$ with $a \in A, b \in B$ that *maximizes the gain* $\Delta g = D(a) + D(b) - 2w(a,b) - (E(a) - D(a)) - (E(b) - D(b))$.
- Locks the swapped pair; repeats until all vertices are locked.
- Finally commits *only the prefix of swaps* that yields the maximum cumulative gain $G^*$.
- Complexity: $O(n^2 \log n)$.

**FM Algorithm (1982) — improvement over KL:**
- Operates on **hypergraphs** (essential for VLSI netlists with multi-pin nets).
- Swaps **one vertex at a time** (not pairs), using a *max-gain bucket list* for $O(1)$ selection.
- Achieves $O(n)$ per pass and converges faster than KL.

**Worked Example — Gain Calculation:**

Consider a graph with three cells $\{a, b, c\}$ in partition $A$ and three cells $\{d, e, f\}$ in partition $B$. Edge weights (cut edges only):
- $w(a, d) = 3$
- $w(a, e) = 2$
- $w(b, d) = 4$
- $w(c, f) = 1$

For vertex $a$ in $A$:
- Internal cost $D(a) = 0$ (no edges to other $A$ cells).
- External cost $E(a) = w(a, d) + w(a, e) = 3 + 2 = 5$.
- Move gain $\Delta g_a = E(a) - D(a) = 5 - 0 = 5$.

For vertex $d$ in $B$:
- Internal cost $D(d) = 0$.
- External cost $E(d) = w(a, d) + w(b, d) = 3 + 4 = 7$.
- Move gain $\Delta g_d = 7 - 0 = 7$.

Pair swap gain (assuming no edge $w(a, d) = 3$):

$$\Delta g_{(a, d)} = \Delta g_a + \Delta g_d - 2 \cdot w(a, d) = 5 + 7 - 2 \cdot 3 = 6$$

So swapping $(a, d)$ reduces the cut-size by 6 units, hence is *highly desirable*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Part (a) of Q.B:** The most common error is forgetting the $-2w(a, b)$ correction term in the pair-swap gain formula, which accounts for the edge between $a$ and $b$ that flips from "cut" to "internal." Marks deducted: **2 marks**.

---

**Q.B. (b)** Derive the **dynamic power consumption** of a CMOS gate and discuss the algorithmic techniques used in modern EDA tools to *minimize* power during logic synthesis and physical design. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation of Dynamic Power:**

Consider a CMOS inverter with output capacitance $C_L$ and supply voltage $V_{DD}$.

**Step 1 — Charge transfer during 0 → 1 transition:** Energy drawn from supply:

$$E_{supply} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 2 — Energy stored in capacitor:**

$$E_{cap} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 3 — Energy dissipated as heat in PMOS (during 0→1):**

$$E_{PMOS} = E_{supply} - E_{cap} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 4 — Energy dissipated in NMOS during 1→0 (capacitor discharges):**

$$E_{NMOS} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 5 — Total switching energy per cycle:**

$$E_{total} = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 6 — Average dynamic power with switching activity $\alpha$ and clock frequency $f_{clk}$:**

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$

**Step 7 — Total power including leakage:**

$$P_{total} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk} + I_{leak} V_{DD}$$

**Algorithmic Power Minimization Techniques in EDA:**

1. **Clock gating:** Insert AND/OR gates on the clock tree to disable idle flip-flops; reduces $\alpha$ by 30–70%.
2. **Power gating:** Add sleep transistors (header/footer) to cut $V_{DD}$ to idle blocks; reduces static $I_{leak}$.
3. **Multi-Vₜ cell mapping:** Assign high-threshold cells to non-critical paths; reduces subthreshold leakage exponentially.
4. **Voltage/frequency scaling (DVFS):** EDA tool performs slack-aware $V_{DD}/f$ assignment; $P \propto V_{DD}^2$, so a 10% voltage reduction yields ~19% power savings.
5. **Logic restructuring for low power:** Re-synthesize XOR-heavy logic into NAND/NOR; reduces switching activity.
6. **Pin swapping & gate sizing:** Algorithmic assignment of late-arriving signals to nearer inputs reduces glitching power.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Part (b) of Q.B:** Students often write the formula as $P = C V^2 f$ *without the activity factor $\alpha$*. The full derivation *must* include $\alpha$ and justify it as the probability of a 0→1 transition per clock cycle. **2 marks lost** if omitted.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **VLSI Design Flow** has **7 stages**: Spec → HLS → Logic Synth → Partition → Floorplan → Place → Route → Verify → Tape-out.
- ✅ **Y-Chart (Gajski-Kuhn):** 3 domains (Behavioral, Structural, Geometric) × 4 levels (System, Algorithm, Logic, Circuit).
- ✅ **HPWL** is the half-perimeter bounding-box wirelength estimate; serves as a *lower bound proxy* for SMT.
- ✅ **Steiner Ratio:** $L_{SMT} / HPWL \ge 1/2$ (Chu-Wong bound).
- ✅ **KL Partitioning:** $O(n^2 \log n)$, swaps *pairs*; **FM Partitioning:** $O(n)$, swaps *single vertices* on hypergraphs.
- ✅ **Pair-swap gain:** $\Delta g_{(a,b)} = \Delta g_a + \Delta g_b - 2 w(a, b)$.
- ✅ **Dynamic Power:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$ (the *½CV²* derivation yields *CV²* total per cycle).
- ✅ **Critical Path Delay:** longest path in the timing DAG, computed in $O(V + E)$ via topological order.
- ✅ **PPA / PPAT trade-off:** every VLSI optimization is a *multi-objective* minimization under Pareto-optimality.
- ✅ **NP-hard problems in VLSI:** Placement, Routing, Floorplanning, HLS scheduling, OPC — all require heuristics.
- ✅ **Polynomial-tractable problems:** MST (Prim/Kruskal), topological sort, Lee's maze, STA — all in $O(n^k)$.
- ✅ **Algorithmic design** is the *mathematical engine* of EDA; **system design** is the *architectural wrapper* combining HW/SW/IP blocks.
- ✅ **Modern EDA trend:** Machine learning (GNN-based placement, RL-based routing) is *augmenting* classical algorithms, not replacing them.
- ✅ **Key takeaway for KTU exam:** Always mention (i) the problem formulation, (ii) the algorithm used, (iii) the complexity class, and (iv) the optimality guarantee (exact vs. heuristic) — for *full marks*.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Algorithmic and System Design**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme / NEP 2020 Outcomes | Premium Engineering Notes*
