> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *List any three advantages and two disadvantages of FPGA-based design.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Advantages:** (i) Zero NRE cost and no masks required. (ii) Re-programmability allows in-field updates. (iii) Very short time-to-market; ideal for prototyping.
- **Disadvantages:** (i) 10×–100× higher silicon area per logic function compared to ASIC. (ii) Higher dynamic and static power consumption due to programmable switches and routing fabric.

---

### Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Format)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain the **Full Custom Design Style** with a neat block diagram. Mention its advantages, limitations, and two real-world applications. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** With a clear diagram, describe the **Standard Cell Design methodology**. Explain the role of cell height fixedness, routing channels, and feedthrough cells. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — Full Custom Design:**

1. **Definition:** In full custom design, every transistor, every wire, and every geometric shape is hand-crafted by the designer. The layout is not constrained by any pre-defined grid, cell library, or routing pattern.
2. **Block Diagram Concept:**

```
[Behavioral Spec] → [Schematic] → [Manual Transistor Sizing] → [Hand DRC Clean Layout] → [LVS Verified GDSII] → [Fab]
```

3. **Advantages:**
   - Minimum possible silicon area (no unused transistors).
   - Best possible speed (transistor W/L tuned for each gate).
   - Lowest power (no idle/leakage current from uncommitted logic).
   - Full control over matching, parasitic, and analog effects.
4. **Limitations:**
   - Extremely high NRE cost (man-years of design effort).
   - Very long design cycle; requires expert layout engineers.
   - Difficult to port across process nodes.
5. **Applications:** CPU datapath, cache SRAM bitcells, RF front-end, PLL/VCO.

**Model Solution for (b) — Standard Cell Design:**

1. **Process Flow:** RTL → Logic Synthesis → Technology Mapping → Placement → Routing → GDSII.
2. **Cell Library:** Library contains pre-characterized cells — INV, NAND, NOR, AOI, OAI, MUX, DFF, LATCH, adder, etc. Each cell has identical **height** but variable **width**.
3. **Cell Row Architecture:** Cells are placed in **rows**. Power rails (VDD and GND) run horizontally on the top and bottom of every row on **metal-1**. Signal pins are placed on the inner top/bottom edges of the cell to interface with the routing channel.
4. **Routing Channels:** Horizontal channels between rows hold metal-2 wires. Vertical channels are formed implicitly by the cell pin positions; metal-3 (or higher) wires traverse vertically across the chip.
5. **Feedthrough Cells:** Special empty cells that allow a signal to pass from the bottom of a row to the top, enabling signals to cross between rows without breaking the row-based placement grid.

---

> **Q3B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Internal Choice]** **(a)** Describe the **Gate Array Design Style**. Differentiate between *Channeled Gate Array* and *Sea-of-Gates* with diagrams. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the **FPGA architecture** in detail. Discuss SRAM-based vs. Anti-fuse-based programming technologies. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — Gate Array:**

1. **Concept:** A **master slice** is pre-fabricated with a fixed array of uncommitted transistor pairs (one PMOS + one NMOS per site). The designer only customizes the **metal interconnect layers** to implement the desired logic.
2. **Channeled Gate Array:** Reserves explicit routing channels between rows of transistor sites. The channel width is fixed at fabrication. Easy to route, but wastes area dedicated to channels.
3. **Sea-of-Gates (Channel-less):** No dedicated channels. Transistor sites cover the entire die. Routing is done by "stealing" unused transistors as routing area using over-the-cell routing. Higher density, but routing congestion and power-rail distribution are challenging.
4. **Trade-off:** Gate array reduces NRE by ~70% and TTM by 50% compared to full custom, but has ~20–30% area overhead.

**Model Solution for (b) — FPGA Architecture:**

1. **Core Building Blocks:**
   - **Configurable Logic Block (CLB):** Contains an N-input **Look-Up Table (LUT)** implemented as a small SRAM, a flip-flop, and a multiplexer. An N-input LUT can implement any Boolean function of N variables.
   - **Programmable Interconnect:** A network of **switch matrices** and **connection boxes** that route signals between CLBs. Programming bits control the pass transistors or buffers.
   - **I/O Blocks (IOB):** Buffer and configure pad drivers for external signals.
2. **Programming Technologies:**
   - **SRAM-based:** Uses 6-transistor SRAM cells to control pass gates. Re-programmable in-circuit. Volatile (must reload on power-up). Dominant in modern Xilinx and Intel FPGAs.
   - **Anti-fuse-based:** A normally open circuit that, when programmed by a high voltage, creates a permanent low-resistance link. One-time programmable (OTP). Non-volatile, smaller, more secure. Used in aerospace and Actel/Microsemi devices.
   - **Flash-based:** Uses floating-gate transistors (similar to Flash memory) to store the configuration. Non-volatile and re-programmable, but slower write and limited endurance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Semi-Custom" with "Custom":** Many students incorrectly classify Standard Cell as "Custom." It is **Semi-Custom**. Only Full Custom and analog layout are true Custom.
> 2. **Skipping the Master Slice concept:** In Gate Array questions, *never* omit the phrase **"pre-fabricated master slice with uncommitted transistors"** — this is the defining feature.
> 3. **Forgetting VDD/GND rails:** When drawing Standard Cell row diagrams, examiners specifically look for **VDD and GND rails on metal-1** along the row edges. Missing this costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Mis-stating FPGA programming:** Do not say "FPGA is permanently programmed" — most FPGAs are **volatile SRAM-based** and require bitstream loading at every power-up.
> 5. **Quantitative questions:** Always include **units** (µm², fF, ns, mW) in numerical answers. The absence of units is a common 0.5-mark deduction per KTU valuation patterns.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Design Style Spectrum:** Full Custom (best APD, worst TTM) → Standard Cell → Gate Array → FPGA (worst APD, best TTM).
- **Selection is driven by:** Production **volume**, **performance** requirement, **NRE budget**, and **time-to-market**.
- **Standard Cell = Fixed Height Cells in Rows + Power Rails + Routing Channels + Feedthroughs.**
- **Gate Array = Pre-fabricated Master Slice + Custom Metal Layers Only.**
- **FPGA = Pre-fabricated + Pre-wired + User-Programmable; No masks ever made by user.**
- **NRE cost decreases:** Full Custom > Standard Cell > Gate Array > FPGA.
- **Unit cost decreases:** FPGA > Gate Array > Standard Cell > Full Custom (for high volume).
- **Key Formulae to memorize:**
  - $T_{delay} = 0.69 \cdot RC$ (logic delay)
  - $T_{wire} \propto L^{2}$ (long-wire delay)
  - $P_{dynamic} = \alpha C V_{DD}^{2} f$
  - $N_{BE} = \frac{C_{ASIC}^{NRE}}{C_{FPGA}^{unit} - C_{ASIC}^{unit}}$
  - $\text{Yield} = e^{-D \cdot A_{chip}}$
- **Rent's Rule** $T = K \cdot G^{p}$ helps predict the **pin count** and **routing demand** of a circuit block — frequently asked in KTU Part A.
- **FPGA LUT** is essentially a **truth-table memory** (SRAM) addressed by inputs.
- **Anti-fuse = OTP, Non-volatile;** **SRAM = Volatile, Re-programmable.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Partitioning: Levels of Partitioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT525 - CAD BASED VLSI DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Partitioning: Levels of Partitioning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Partitioning** is the foundational, hierarchical decomposition process in the VLSI physical design automation flow where a complex electronic system, chip, or circuit netlist is recursively subdivided into smaller, manageable, and logically coherent sub-systems or blocks, while simultaneously optimizing objectives such as **minimization of interconnections (cut-size)**, **area balancing**, **timing performance**, and **routability**.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus**, partitioning is treated as the *zeroth and most critical step* of the VLSI design hierarchy. The **Levels of Partitioning** specifically refers to the **hierarchical stratification** at which this decomposition is applied — beginning from a complete electronic product and recursively descending down to the gate/transistor level.

## 1.2 The Three Canonical Levels of Partitioning

The VLSI design hierarchy follows a strict top-down recursive decomposition model. The three primary levels are:

> [!NOTE]
> **Level 1 — System-Level Partitioning**
> Decomposes an entire electronic product (e.g., a smartphone motherboard) into a set of individual Integrated Circuits (ICs / Chips). Example: CPU chip + Memory chip + Power Management IC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Level 2 — Chip-Level Partitioning**
> Decomposes a single IC into major functional blocks. Example: ALU, Control Unit, Register File, Cache, I/O pads — all inside one chip.

> **Level 3 — Block-Level (Sub-Block) Partitioning**
> Decomposes a functional block into smaller leaf-level cells, standard cells, or custom logic gates suitable for placement and routing.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Library Organization Problem:**
> Imagine you have inherited **1,000,000 unsorted books** (your circuit netlist) and you must build a functional national library system. You cannot organize all books at once. So you proceed hierarchically:
> 1. **System Level:** Decide how many physical library buildings to construct in the country (one for Sciences, one for Arts, one for Medicine, etc.). Each building = one IC chip.
> 2. **Chip Level:** Inside each building, decide the floor plan — Reference Section, Stacks, Reading Halls, Reception. Each section = one functional block.
> 3. **Block Level:** Inside each Reference Section, decide the exact shelf arrangement — shelf A for History, shelf B for Geography, etc. Each shelf = one cell.
>
> The constraint at every level: **minimize the number of books (wires) that must travel between buildings, floors, or shelves** — because inter-building transport is extremely expensive. This is exactly what partitioning does for VLSI circuits.

## 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics (Highlighted)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Range | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cut-size | $C$ | Number of nets crossing the cut | Lower is better for routability |
| Cut-cost | $\Phi$ | Weighted sum of cut nets | Handles multi-fanout nets |
| Balance factor | $\alpha$ | Typically **0.0 to 0.5** | Controls block size skew |
| Number of modules | $n$ | $2$ to $10^{6}$ | Drives algorithmic complexity |
| Number of nets | $m$ | Often $O(n)$ to $O(n^{1.5})$ | Determines netlist density |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Formulation of the Partitioning Problem

The general **k-way partitioning problem** can be rigorously stated as follows:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Given:** A hypergraph $G = (V, E)$ where $V = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n\}$ represents circuit modules (cells/blocks), and $E = \{e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_m\}$ represents nets (multi-terminal connections).
>
> **Find:** A partition of $V$ into $k$ disjoint subsets $V_1, V_2, \ldots, V_k$ such that $V_i \cap V_j = \emptyset$ for $i \neq j$, and $\bigcup_{i=1}^{k} V_i = V$.
>
> **Objectives (in order of KTU priority):**
> 1. Minimize the total cut-cost across all partition boundaries.
> 2. Satisfy the area/balance constraint on each subset.
> 3. Honor timing-critical path constraints.

## 2.2 The Cost Function

The **cut-cost** for a 2-way partition $\{A, B\}$ is formally defined as:

$$\Phi(A, B) = \sum_{e \in E} w(e) \cdot \mathbb{1}_{e \text{ is cut}}$$

where:
- $w(e)$ is the weight of net $e$ (default = 1).
- $\mathbb{1}_{e \text{ is cut}}$ is the indicator function returning **1** if net $e$ has terminals in both $A$ and $B$, and **0** otherwise.

A net $e$ is "cut" if:

$$\mathbb{1}_{e \text{ is cut}} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } e \cap A \neq \emptyset \text{ and } e \cap B \neq \emptyset \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

## 2.3 The Balance Constraint (Capacity Constraint)

To prevent pathological partitions where one block contains nearly all modules and the other is empty, a balance constraint is enforced:

$$\vert V_i \vert \leq (1 + \alpha) \cdot \frac{\vert V \vert}{k}$$

For a 2-way partition with balance factor $\alpha$:

$$\frac{(1 - \alpha)}{2} \cdot \vert V \vert \leq \vert A \vert \leq \frac{(1 + \alpha)}{2} \cdot \vert V \vert$$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The balance constraint directly translates to **silicon area uniformity**. If one block is too large, the chip's aspect ratio becomes irregular, leading to severe routing congestion, longer critical paths, and ultimately yield loss in fabrication.

## 2.4 Level-by-Level Decomposition Objectives

| Level | Granularity | Typical Module Count | Primary Objective | Secondary Objective |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **System-Level** | Whole Product → Multiple ICs | $10$ – $10^{3}$ chips | Minimize inter-chip I/O pins | Minimize packaging cost |
| **Chip-Level** | Single IC → Functional Blocks | $10^{2}$ – $10^{5}$ blocks | Minimize inter-block wires | Balance block areas |
| **Block-Level** | Functional Block → Cells/Gates | $10^{3}$ – $10^{6}$ cells | Minimize cut-size for routability | Optimize timing closure |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Boundary Condition | Physical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2-way Cut-cost | $\Phi = \sum_{e} w(e) \cdot \mathbb{1}_{e \text{ cut}}$ | $0 \leq \Phi \leq \vert E \vert$ | dimensionless / weighted |
| Balance Constraint | $\vert A \vert \leq (1+\alpha)\frac{\vert V \vert}{2}$ | $0 \leq \alpha \leq 1$ | modules |
| Hyperedge Cut Indicator | $\mathbb{1}_{e \text{ cut}} \in \{0, 1\}$ | Boolean | — |
| Gain of a Module (KL/FM) | $g(v) = D(v) - \text{internal}(v)$ | $g \in \mathbb{Z}$ | edge count |
| External Cost | $D(v) = $ edges to opposite partition | $D \geq 0$ | edges |
| Internal Cost | $\text{int}(v) = $ edges within own partition | $\text{int} \geq 0$ | edges |
| KL Pass Gain | $G_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} g_i$ | Maximized per pass | edges |
| FM Bucket Sort Index | $\text{bucket}[g(v)] \rightarrow v$ | $-P_{\max} \leq g \leq P_{\max}$ | gain value |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Balance Constraint from First Principles

Let the total number of modules in the circuit be denoted $\vert V \vert = N$. For a balanced 2-way partition $\{A, B\}$, we require that neither subset is excessively larger than the other. Define:

$$N_A = \vert A \vert, \quad N_B = \vert B \vert, \quad N_A + N_B = N$$

By the **principle of fair division**, an *ideal* balance occurs when $N_A = N_B = N/2$. However, in practical VLSI, exact equality is often infeasible due to the discrete nature of modules. We therefore introduce a **tolerance parameter** $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ that permits a controlled deviation.

**Step 1:** Express the upper allowable deviation from the ideal:

$$N_A \leq \frac{N}{2} + \delta$$

where $\delta$ is the maximum permissible deviation.

**Step 2:** Express $\delta$ in terms of $\alpha$ and $N$:

$$\delta = \alpha \cdot \frac{N}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute back to obtain the standard balance inequality:

$$N_A \leq \frac{N}{2} + \alpha \cdot \frac{N}{2} = (1 + \alpha) \cdot \frac{N}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Similarly for the lower bound:

$$N_A \geq \frac{N}{2} - \alpha \cdot \frac{N}{2} = (1 - \alpha) \cdot \frac{N}{2}$$

**Final Compact Form:**

$$\boxed{(1 - \alpha) \cdot \frac{N}{2} \leq N_A \leq (1 + \alpha) \cdot \frac{N}{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Case:** When $\alpha = 0$, we recover the **strict equality** $N_A = N_B = N/2$, which corresponds to a perfectly balanced bipartition — the theoretical ideal that the Kernighan–Lin algorithm asymptotically approaches.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Module Gain Function (Kernighan–Lin Foundation)

In iterative improvement partitioning, when a module $v$ is moved from partition $A$ to partition $B$, the **gain** $g(v)$ is defined as the *net change in cut-cost*:

**Step 1:** Define the external cost — the number of nets currently connecting $v$ to the *opposite* partition $B$:

$$D(v) = \sum_{e \ni v} \mathbb{1}_{e \cap B \neq \emptyset} \cdot w(e)$$

**Step 2:** Define the internal cost — the number of nets connecting $v$ *within* its current partition $A$:

$$\text{int}(v) = \sum_{e \ni v} \mathbb{1}_{e \cap A \setminus \{v\} \neq \emptyset} \cdot w(e)$$

**Step 3:** When $v$ is moved from $A$ to $B$:

- Nets currently spanning the cut will become **internal** to $B$ → cut-cost **decreases** by $D(v)$.
- Nets currently internal to $A$ will become **cut** nets → cut-cost **increases** by $\text{int}(v)$.

**Step 4:** The net gain is therefore:

$$\boxed{g(v) = D(v) - \text{int}(v)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** A positive gain means moving $v$ reduces inter-block wiring (good for routing), while a negative gain means it would worsen the partition. Iterative algorithms greedily select the module with the **highest positive gain** at each step.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Algorithmic Complexity Comparison

| Algorithm | Time Complexity | Pass Complexity | Optimality | Suitable Partitioning Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Kernighan–Lin (KL)** | $O(n^3)$ overall | $O(n^2)$ per pass | Local optimum | Chip-Level |
| **Fiduccia–Mattheyses (FM)** | $O(P)$ per pass | $O(n)$ per pass | Strong local optimum | Chip-Level / Block-Level |
| **Ratio Cut** | $O(n \log n)$ approx. | Spectral | Eigenvector-based | System-Level |
| **Simulated Annealing** | $O(n^2)$ iter. | Probabilistic | Near-global | Block-Level (small circuits) |
| **Genetic Partitioning** | $O(g \cdot p \cdot n)$ | Generational | Heuristic | System-Level (coarse) |

where $n = \vert V \vert$, $P = $ number of nets (pins), $g = $ generations, $p = $ population size.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Cut-cost Computation

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** Consider a hypergraph with 4 nets and 6 modules.
> - $e_1 = \{v_1, v_2, v_3\}$, $w(e_1) = 2$
> - $e_2 = \{v_4, v_5\}$, $w(e_2) = 1$
> - $e_3 = \{v_3, v_4, v_6\}$, $w(e_3) = 3$
> - $e_4 = \{v_1, v_5\}$, $w(e_4) = 1$
>
> Let the partition be $A = \{v_1, v_2, v_3\}$ and $B = \{v_4, v_5, v_6\}$.

**Step 1:** Check each net:

- $e_1 = \{v_1, v_2, v_3\} \subset A$ → NOT cut → contributes **0**.
- $e_2 = \{v_4, v_5\} \subset B$ → NOT cut → contributes **0**.
- $e_3 = \{v_3, v_4, v_6\}$ has members in both $A$ and $B$ → CUT → contributes $w(e_3) = 3$.
- $e_4 = \{v_1, v_5\}$ has members in both $A$ and $B$ → CUT → contributes $w(e_4) = 1$.

**Step 2:** Sum the contributions:

$$\Phi(A, B) = 0 + 0 + 3 + 1 = \boxed{4}$$

**Step 3:** Verify the balance constraint with $\alpha = 0.5$, $N = 6$:

$$\text{Lower bound} = (1 - 0.5) \cdot 3 = 1.5, \quad \text{Upper bound} = (1 + 0.5) \cdot 3 = 4.5$$

Since $N_A = 3$, the constraint $1.5 \leq 3 \leq 4.5$ is satisfied. ✅

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Block Diagram — Levels of Partitioning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SYS["System Level - Whole Electronic Product e.g., Motherboard"]
    CHIP["Chip Level - Single IC Die e.g., Microprocessor"]
    BLK["Block Level - Functional Sub-units e.g., ALU, Cache, Ctrl"]
    CELL["Cell Level - Standard Cells and Gates e.g., NAND2, DFF"]
    TRANS["Transistor Level - Physical Devices on Silicon"]

    SYS -->|"Step 1: System Partitioning - yields multiple ICs"| CHIP
    CHIP -->|"Step 2: Chip Partitioning - yields functional blocks"| BLK
    BLK -->|"Step 3: Block Partitioning - yields logic cells"| CELL
    CELL -->|"Step 4: Cell Partitioning - yields transistor primitives"| TRANS

    M1["Cost Metric: Inter-chip I/O pin count - minimize package pins"]
    M2["Cost Metric: Inter-block wire count - minimize routing congestion"]
    M3["Cost Metric: Net cut-size - minimize delay and congestion"]
    M4["Cost Metric: Transistor count balance - equalize area"]

    SYS --- M1
    CHIP --- M2
    BLK --- M3
    CELL --- M4
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of a 2-Way Partitioner

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Input Netlist - Hypergraph G of V, E"]
    A2["Initial Partition Generator - Random or Seed-based"]
    A3["Cut-cost Computation Engine - phi A B calculation"]
    A4["Gain Calculator - g v equals D v minus int v"]
    A5["FM Bucket Index - Sort modules by gain"]
    A6["Move Selector - Pick max-gain unlocked module"]
    A7["Balance Validator - Check alpha constraint"]
    A8["Pass Comparator - Track best phi in pass"]
    A9["Convergence Checker - Improvement less than epsilon"]
    A10["Output Partition - Final V1, V2 sets"]

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7
    A7 -->|"Valid move"| A8
    A7 -->|"Violates balance"| A5
    A8 --> A9
    A9 -->|"Not converged"| A6
    A9 -->|"Converged"| A10
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Cut Visualization in a 2-Way Partition

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PART_A["Partition A - Left Block"]
        n1["v1"]
        n2["v2"]
        n3["v3"]
    end

    subgraph PART_B["Partition B - Right Block"]
        n4["v4"]
        n5["v5"]
        n6["v6"]
    end

    n1 ---|"e4: weight 1 - CUT"| n5
    n3 ---|"e3: weight 3 - CUT"| n4
    n3 ---|"e3: weight 3 - CUT"| n6
    n1 --- n2
    n1 --- n3
    n2 --- n3
    n4 --- n5
    n4 --- n6

    CUTLINE{{"CUT BOUNDARY - Total cut-cost phi equals 4"}}

    PART_A -.-> CUTLINE
    PART_B -.-> CUTLINE
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
> **Q:** Define partitioning in VLSI design. List the three levels of partitioning.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Partitioning is the process of dividing a circuit netlist into smaller sub-circuits to optimize area, delay, and interconnect complexity.
- **Level 1 — System-level partitioning:** Divides an electronic system into multiple ICs/chips.
- **Level 2 — Chip-level partitioning:** Divides a single chip into functional blocks (e.g., ALU, memory, control).
- **Level 3 — Block-level partitioning:** Divides functional blocks into smaller logic cells or standard cells. **(3 marks)**

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
> **Q:** What is meant by cut-size in partitioning? Why is minimizing it important?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Cut-size is the number of nets crossing the partition boundary between two or more subsets. It is mathematically expressed as $\Phi(A, B) = \sum_{e \in E} w(e) \cdot \mathbb{1}_{e \text{ is cut}}$.
Minimizing cut-size is crucial because (i) fewer inter-block wires reduce routing congestion, (ii) cut wires often lie on critical timing paths, causing delay, and (iii) I/O pins between blocks are area-expensive. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

> **Q (a)** Explain the three levels of partitioning in VLSI design with suitable examples. Discuss the cost metrics and balance constraints at each level. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** For the hypergraph $G(V, E)$ where $V = \{v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4, v_5, v_6\}$ and the nets are:
> $e_1 = \{v_1, v_4\}$ ($w=2$), $e_2 = \{v_2, v_3, v_5\}$ ($w=3$), $e_3 = \{v_1, v_5, v_6\}$ ($w=1$), $e_4 = \{v_3, v_4\}$ ($w=2$), $e_5 = \{v_2, v_6\}$ ($w=1$),
> compute the cut-cost for the partition $A = \{v_1, v_2, v_6\}$ and $B = \{v_3, v_4, v_5\}$. Verify the balance constraint with $\alpha = 0.4$. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The three levels of hierarchical partitioning in VLSI physical design are:

**1. System-Level Partitioning:** The entire electronic product is divided into separate ICs/chips. *Example:* A smartphone motherboard partitioned into an Application Processor IC, a Power Management IC, an RF Transceiver IC, and a Memory IC. Cost metric: minimize inter-chip pin count and packaging cost.

**2. Chip-Level Partitioning:** A single IC die is divided into major functional blocks. *Example:* A microprocessor partitioned into ALU, Register File, Cache, Decoder, I/O Controllers. Cost metric: minimize inter-block wiring and balance silicon area.

**3. Block-Level Partitioning:** Each functional block is decomposed into smaller standard cells. *Example:* The ALU block partitioned into adder cells, multiplexer cells, comparator cells. Cost metric: minimize cut-size for timing closure and routability.

At every level, the **balance constraint** $\vert V_i \vert \leq (1+\alpha) \cdot \vert V \vert / k$ must be enforced to prevent degenerate partitions. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Identify membership of each net in $A$ vs $B$:
- $A = \{v_1, v_2, v_6\}$, $B = \{v_3, v_4, v_5\}$.

**Step 2:** Test each net:
- $e_1 = \{v_1, v_4\}$: $v_1 \in A$, $v_4 \in B$ → **CUT** (weight 2)
- $e_2 = \{v_2, v_3, v_5\}$: $v_2 \in A$, $v_3, v_5 \in B$ → **CUT** (weight 3)
- $e_3 = \{v_1, v_5, v_6\}$: $v_1, v_6 \in A$, $v_5 \in B$ → **CUT** (weight 1)
- $e_4 = \{v_3, v_4\}$: both in $B$ → **NOT CUT** (weight 0)
- $e_5 = \{v_2, v_6\}$: both in $A$ → **NOT CUT** (weight 0)

**Step 3:** Sum cut-costs:

$$\Phi(A, B) = 2 + 3 + 1 + 0 + 0 = \boxed{6}$$

**Step 4:** Verify balance constraint with $N = 6$, $\alpha = 0.4$:

$$\text{Lower} = (1 - 0.4) \cdot 3 = 1.8, \quad \text{Upper} = (1 + 0.4) \cdot 3 = 4.2$$

Since $\vert A \vert = 3$ and $1.8 \leq 3 \leq 4.2$, the constraint is **satisfied**. ✅ **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **Q (a)** Discuss the formulation of the k-way partitioning problem with proper mathematical notation. Explain the role of the cut-cost function and the balance constraint in physical design automation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** Consider the partition $A = \{1, 2, 3\}$ and $B = \{4, 5, 6\}$ for a netlist with nets:
> $e_1 = \{1, 4\}$ ($w=1$), $e_2 = \{2, 5, 6\}$ ($w=2$), $e_3 = \{3, 5\}$ ($w=1$), $e_4 = \{1, 2, 3\}$ ($w=3$), $e_5 = \{4, 6\}$ ($w=1$).
> Compute: (i) Total cut-cost, (ii) Net cut-size (unweighted), (iii) Balance status with $\alpha = 0.3$. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The k-way partitioning problem is formulated as a combinatorial optimization over a hypergraph $G(V, E)$. Given modules $V$ and nets $E$, find disjoint subsets $V_1, V_2, \ldots, V_k$ that minimize the cut-cost function $\Phi = \sum_{i < j} \Phi(V_i, V_j)$ subject to the balance constraint $\vert V_i \vert \leq (1+\alpha) \vert V \vert / k$ for all $i$. The cut-cost measures the weighted sum of inter-partition nets; lower cut-cost translates to fewer routing resources, reduced congestion, and shorter critical paths. The balance constraint ensures uniform die utilization, preventing irregular chip aspect ratios that degrade yield and routability. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Total cut-cost:**
- $e_1$: cut → contributes 1
- $e_2$: cut → contributes 2
- $e_3$: cut → contributes 1
- $e_4$: not cut → contributes 0
- $e_5$: not cut → contributes 0

$$\Phi = 1 + 2 + 1 + 0 + 0 = \boxed{4}$$

**(ii) Net cut-size (unweighted):** Number of cut nets = 3 (namely $e_1, e_2, e_3$).

**(iii) Balance check:** $N=6$, $\alpha=0.3$. Lower = $0.7 \times 3 = 2.1$, Upper = $1.3 \times 3 = 3.9$. Since $\vert A \vert = 3$, balance is **satisfied**. ✅ **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing cut-size with cut-cost:** Cut-size is the unweighted *count* of cut nets; cut-cost is the *weighted sum*. KTU examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting the balance constraint:** Students often compute only the cut-cost and ignore the $\alpha$-constraint. Always verify balance as a separate sub-step.
> 3. **Misidentifying internal nets:** A net with *all* terminals in $A$ or *all* in $B$ is NOT cut — even if its weight is high. Read net membership carefully.
> 4. **Off-by-one in module indexing:** KTU typically uses 1-indexed modules ($v_1, v_2, \ldots$); do not confuse with 0-indexed pseudocode.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **Partitioning** is a hierarchical decomposition — system → chip → block → cell.
> - **Cost function** = $\Phi(A, B) = \sum_{e} w(e) \cdot \mathbb{1}_{e \text{ cut}}$ — always includes the indicator function.
> - **Balance constraint** = $(1 - \alpha) N / 2 \leq \vert A \vert \leq (1 + \alpha) N / 2$ for 2-way partition.
> - **Gain** of a module = $g(v) = D(v) - \text{int}(v)$ — drives FM/KL iterative improvement.
> - **System-level** cost metric: I/O pins between chips (drives packaging).
> - **Chip-level** cost metric: Inter-block wire count (drives congestion).
> - **Block-level** cost metric: Cut-size on critical nets (drives timing).
> - **KL algorithm** complexity: $O(n^3)$ — suitable for chip-level.
> - **FM algorithm** complexity: $O(P)$ per pass — linear in pins, suitable for block-level.
> - **Hypergraph vs Graph:** VLSI nets are multi-terminal → must use hypergraph representation, not standard graphs.
> - **Trade-off:** Lower cut-cost often conflicts with perfect balance — both must be jointly optimized.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parameters for Partitioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT525 - CAD BASED VLSI DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Parameters for Partitioning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Partitioning Parameters** are the quantitative metrics, constraints, and objective functions used to evaluate, compare, and optimize the quality of a circuit partition in the VLSI physical design flow. They mathematically describe *how good* a given partition is, balancing the competing goals of **minimizing inter-partition communication** (cut size) and **respecting area/density constraints** (balance).

In graph-theoretic terms, given a hypergraph $H = (V, E)$ representing a circuit, where $V$ is the set of cells (vertices) and $E$ is the set of nets (hyperedges), a **k-way partition** divides $V$ into $k$ disjoint subsets $V_1, V_2, \ldots, V_k$ such that every $v \in V$ belongs to exactly one subset.

The fundamental parameters governing this division are:

| **Parameter** | **Role** |
|---|---|
| Cut Size | Optimization objective |
| Balance Factor | Feasibility constraint |
| Absorption | Secondary gain metric |
| Ratio Cut | Normalized cut metric |
| Terminal Count | Edge weight metric |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Floor Reorganization"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a company of **200 employees** that must be split into **2 departments** on two different floors of a building. You are the HR manager.

- **Vertices (V)** = Employees
- **Nets (E)** = Slack channels / communication lines (employees collaborating on projects)
- **Cut Size** = Number of Slack channels connecting Floor A to Floor B (you want this **small** because cross-floor communication is annoying)
- **Balance Constraint** = Each floor must hold approximately **100 employees** (one floor cannot be overcrowded)
- **Absorption** = Slack channels that go to a *third* person (an "external" vertex) — these are less painful than direct employee-to-employee cuts
- **Ratio Cut** = A *fair* metric that penalizes splitting a group unevenly (e.g., 199 + 1 split is far worse than 100 + 100, even if cut sizes are similar)

**The Parameters for Partitioning are the HR rules that govern this reorganization — they decide whether your floor plan is "good" or "bad".**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Cut Size (C)** — dimensionless integer count
- **Balance Tolerance (ε)** — typically **10%** of average partition size in industrial tools
- **Ratio Cut (RC)** — normalized, unit-less metric
- **Absorption Coefficient (a)** — float in [0, 1]
- **Terminal Propagation** — weighted net-cost multiplier (often 1× or 2×)
- **Number of Partitions (k)** — integer, with $k=2$ (bi-partitioning) being the most studied

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of Partitioning Parameters

### 2.1.1 Cut Size (Most Primary Parameter)

The **cut size** $c(P)$ of a partition $P = \{V_1, V_2\}$ is defined as the number of hyperedges (nets) that have at least one terminal in *both* $V_1$ and $V_2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters:** Cut nets become **inter-block wires** in the final layout. More cuts = more routing congestion, longer wirelength, higher delay, and more I/O pins (escape routing problem).

### 2.1.2 Balance Constraint

A partition must respect the **balance constraint** to avoid overloading one block. Given $|V| = n$ cells and $k$ partitions, the ideal block size is:

$$n_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{n}{k}$$

Each block $V_i$ must satisfy $|V_i| \leq (1 + \varepsilon) \cdot n_{\text{ideal}}$, where $\varepsilon$ is the **balance tolerance**.

### 2.1.3 Ratio Cut (Yeh, Cheng & Lin Metric)

To avoid the trivial solution where one block has just 1 cell (cut size = 1 but useless), the **Ratio Cut** metric normalizes by block sizes:

$$RC(P) = \frac{c(P)}{|V_1| \cdot |V_2|}$$

Minimizing RC simultaneously reduces cuts and balances block sizes — a Pareto improvement.

### 2.1.4 Absorption

A net is said to be **absorbed** in a block if *all but one* of its terminals lie inside that block. The absorbed terminal contributes **0** to the cut cost when the cell is moved (because it would no longer be cut).

**Absorption gain** for moving cell $v$ from $V_1$ to $V_2$:

$$\Delta a(v) = |\{n \in E : \text{all other pins of } n \text{ are in } V_1\}| - |\{n \in E : \text{all other pins of } n \text{ are in } V_2\}|$$

### 2.1.5 Terminal Propagation

In **top-down partitioning with fixed outer terminals** (used in multi-level frameworks), nets connected to "pads" outside the current subgraph carry a pre-defined **terminal weight**, ensuring global context is preserved during local decisions.

### 2.1.6 Number of Partitions ($k$)

- **$k = 2$** → **Bi-partitioning** (most common, simpler)
- **$k > 2$** → **$k$-way / Multi-way partitioning**
- A $k$-way partition can be recursively obtained by $\log_2 k$ bi-partitions

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear every year in KTU ESE Module 2 questions.**

| **#** | **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $c(P) = \sum_{e \in E} \mathbb{1}[\text{span}(e) > 1]$ | Cut size: nets spanning multiple blocks | count |
| 2 | $n_{\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{n}{k}$ | Ideal block size | count |
| 3 | $\vert V_i \vert \leq (1+\varepsilon)\, n_{\text{ideal}}$ | Balance constraint | count |
| 4 | $RC(P) = \dfrac{c(P)}{\vert V_1 \vert \cdot \vert V_2 \vert}$ | Ratio cut (penalizes imbalanced splits) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\Delta g(v) = \text{gain}(v) = FS(v) - TE(v)$ | FM gain for moving vertex $v$ | integer |
| 6 | $a(v) = TE(v) - FS(v)$ | Absorption coefficient for $v$ | integer |
| 7 | $w_{\text{norm}}(e) = \dfrac{w(e)}{\text{span}(e) - 1}$ | Net-weight normalization (Wei & Cheng) | weight/count |
| 8 | $T(v) = \sum_{e \ni v} \mathbb{1}[e \text{ is cut}]$ | Terminal count of cell $v$ | count |
| 9 | $C_{\text{total}}(P) = \sum_{i<j} c(V_i, V_j)$ | Total $k$-way cut cost | count |
| 10 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\max \vert V_i \vert - n_{\text{ideal}}}{n_{\text{ideal}}}$ | Imbalance ratio (tolerance) | dimensionless |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cadence Innovus / Synopsys ICC2** — uses balance tolerance of **5–15%** depending on cell density targets
- **ML-based placers (Google's Circuit Training, 2021)** — use ratio-cut style losses in GNN cost functions
- **3D-IC Stacking** — partitioning parameters decide which dies go on top vs bottom (critical thermal constraint)
- **FPGA Partitioning** — cut size determines number of BRAM crossings and clock-domain crossings

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Cut Size to Ratio Cut (Yeh-Cheng-Lin 1992)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Show mathematically why minimizing $c(P)$ alone gives a *biased* trivial solution, and prove why Ratio Cut solves this.

### Step 1 — Define the trivial partition
Suppose we have $n$ cells. The trivial partition $P_{\text{triv}} = \{ \{v_1\}, V \setminus \{v_1\} \}$ has:

- $|V_1| = 1$, $|V_2| = n - 1$

### Step 2 — Compute its cut size
If $v_1$ is connected to $d(v_1)$ nets (its degree), then:

$$c(P_{\text{triv}}) = d(v_1)$$

### Step 3 — Show that minimizing $c(P)$ alone is meaningless
For $n = 100$, the trivial partition could yield $c(P_{\text{triv}}) = 2$ (a low-degree corner cell), but the partition is **useless** because block 1 has only 1 cell.

### Step 4 — Define Ratio Cut to penalize this
$$RC(P_{\text{triv}}) = \frac{2}{1 \times 99} = \frac{2}{99} \approx 0.0202$$

### Step 5 — Compare with a balanced partition
A 50/50 split with $c(P_{\text{bal}}) = 20$ cuts gives:

$$RC(P_{\text{bal}}) = \frac{20}{50 \times 50} = \frac{20}{2500} = 0.0080$$

### Step 6 — Conclusion
Even though $c(P_{\text{triv}}) < c(P_{\text{bal}})$, the ratio cut shows:

$$RC(P_{\text{bal}}) < RC(P_{\text{triv}})$$

Hence the balanced partition is **objectively better** when measured by $RC$. ∎

## 3.2 Derivation: FM Gain Function with Absorption

The **Fiduccia-Mattheyses (1982)** gain for moving a cell $v$ from $V_1$ to $V_2$ is derived as follows:

### Step 1 — Classify the nets of $v$
Let $E(v)$ be the set of nets incident to $v$. For each $e \in E(v)$:
- $FS(e)$ = number of free (unlocked) cells of $e$ in $V_2$ (the destination)
- $TE(e)$ = number of free cells of $e$ in $V_1$ (the source)
- $F(e)$ = number of locked cells

### Step 2 — Apply the FM classification rule
- If $FS(e) = 0$ and $TE(e) > 0$ → moving $v$ **creates a new cut** → contributes $-1$ to gain
- If $FS(e) > 0$ and $TE(e) = 0$ → moving $v$ **removes a cut** → contributes $+1$ to gain
- Otherwise → net remains cut or remains uncut → contributes $0$

### Step 3 — Formal gain definition
$$\begin{aligned}
g(v) &= \sum_{e \in E(v)} \delta(e) \\
\delta(e) &= \begin{cases} -1 & \text{if } FS(e) = 0 \text{ and } TE(e) > 0 \\ +1 & \text{if } FS(e) > 0 \text{ and } TE(e) = 0 \\ \;\;0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Absorption coefficient
$$a(v) = \sum_{e \in E(v)} \mathbb{1}[FS(e) > 0 \text{ and } TE(e) = 0] = -g(v) \text{ (for cut nets)}$$

This is the number of nets that would be **absorbed** into $V_1$ if $v$ moves to $V_2$.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-style worked problem:** Verify the FM gain calculation for a small circuit.

**Circuit:** Consider 4 cells $A, B, C, D$ and 3 nets:
- Net 1: $\{A, B\}$
- Net 2: $\{B, C\}$
- Net 3: $\{C, D\}$

**Current Partition:** $V_1 = \{A, B\}$, $V_2 = \{C, D\}$

**Current cut size:** Net 2 ($\{B, C\}$) is cut. $c(P) = 1$.

**Question:** Compute $g(C)$ — the gain for moving $C$ to $V_1$.

### Solution
For cell $C$, its incident nets are Net 2 = $\{B, C\}$ and Net 3 = $\{C, D\}$.

**For Net 2** $\{B, C\}$ after hypothetically moving $C$:
- $FS$ (other free cells in destination $V_1$): $\{B\}$ → $FS = 1$
- $TE$ (other free cells in source $V_2$): $\emptyset$ → $TE = 0$
- $\Rightarrow$ Net 2 will be **uncut** after move → $\delta = +1$

**For Net 3** $\{C, D\}$ after moving $C$:
- $FS$ (other free cells in $V_1$): $\emptyset$ → $FS = 0$
- $TE$ (other free cells in $V_2$): $\{D\}$ → $TE = 1$
- $\Rightarrow$ Net 3 will be **newly cut** after move → $\delta = -1$

**Total gain:**
$$g(C) = (+1) + (-1) = 0$$

So moving $C$ neither improves nor worsens the cut count — but it *does* shift cells between blocks, which may help later iterations. ∎

## 3.4 Net-Weight Normalization Derivation

For hyperedges (multi-terminal nets) with $\text{span}(e)$ = number of blocks containing terminals, define the **normalized weight**:

$$w_{\text{norm}}(e) = \frac{w(e)}{\text{span}(e) - 1}$$

**Rationale:** A 5-pin net spanning 3 blocks is "less cut" than a 2-pin net spanning 2 blocks. Normalization ensures fair cost contribution per unit of cut.

For a 2-pin net spanning 2 blocks: $w_{\text{norm}} = w/1 = w$ (unchanged).
For a 5-pin net spanning 3 blocks: $w_{\text{norm}} = w/2$ (halved cost contribution).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Partitioning Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Circuit Hypergraph H = V E] --> B[Partitioning Engine]
    B --> C{Parameter Set}
    C --> D[Cut Size cP]
    C --> E[Balance Constraint epsilon]
    C --> F[Ratio Cut RC]
    C --> G[Absorption a v]
    C --> H[Terminal Count T v]
    C --> I[Net Weight Norm w_norm]
    D --> J[Quality Metric]
    E --> K[Feasibility Check]
    F --> J
    G --> L[FM Gain g v]
    H --> L
    I --> J
    J --> M[Optimal Partition P*]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Downstream Placement and Routing]
```

## 4.2 Parameter Interaction Flow (KL/FM Algorithm)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Initial Random Partition] --> P2[Compute cP initial cut]
    P2 --> P3[Lock all cells unlocked]
    P3 --> P4[For each cell compute gv]
    P4 --> P5[Pick max gain cell]
    P5 --> P6{Balance OK?}
    P6 -- Yes --> P7[Move cell lock it]
    P6 -- No --> P8[Skip cell pick next max]
    P7 --> P9[Update gain bucket structure]
    P9 --> P10{All cells locked?}
    P10 -- No --> P4
    P10 -- Yes --> P11[Find prefix with min cumulative g]
    P11 --> P12[Apply moves revert rest]
    P12 --> P13{Improved?}
    P13 -- Yes --> P14[Accept new P*]
    P13 -- No --> P15[STOP local optimum]
    P14 --> P3
    P8 --> P4
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Partitioning Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph OBJECTIVES
        O1[Minimize cP]
        O2[Minimize RC]
        O3[Maximize Absorption]
    end
    subgraph CONSTRAINTS
        C1[Balance: Vi ≤ 1+ε * n_ideal]
        C2[Max cells per block]
        C3[Floorplanning compatibility]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS
        Q1[Cut count]
        Q2[Wirelength estimate]
        Q3[Pin density]
        Q4[Timing slack]
    end
    OBJECTIVES --> E[Partition Evaluator]
    CONSTRAINTS --> E
    E --> OUTPUTS
```

## 4.4 Parameter Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1{Design Goal?} -->|Min Wirelength| S2[Use Cut Size cP]
    S1 -->|Balance critical| S3[Use Ratio Cut RC]
    S1 -->|Iterative improvement| S4[Use FM Gain gv]
    S1 -->|Multi-level framework| S5[Use Terminal Propagation]
    S2 --> R1[Best for Bi-partitioning]
    S3 --> R2[Best for cluster kernels]
    S4 --> R3[Best for FM algorithm]
    S5 --> R4[Best for hMETIS ML approach]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> Define the following partitioning parameters:
> (a) Cut size
> (b) Balance constraint
> (c) Absorption

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Cut Size:** For a partition $P = \{V_1, V_2\}$ of a hypergraph $H = (V, E)$, the cut size $c(P)$ is the number of hyperedges (nets) that have at least one terminal in $V_1$ and at least one terminal in $V_2$. Formally, $c(P) = \sum_{e \in E} \mathbb{1}[\text{span}(e) > 1]$. It represents the number of inter-block connections and is the primary objective to minimize.

**(b) Balance Constraint:** A feasibility constraint ensuring no block is overloaded. For a $k$-way partition of $n$ cells with tolerance $\varepsilon$:
$$|V_i| \leq (1 + \varepsilon) \cdot \frac{n}{k}$$
It prevents trivial solutions where one block is nearly empty.

**(c) Absorption:** The number of nets that would become **uncut** (absorbed into one block) if a particular cell $v$ is moved across the partition. Mathematically, $a(v) = |\{e \in E(v) : FS(e) > 0 \text{ and } TE(e) = 0\}|$. Higher absorption = better gain for the move.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**
> Differentiate between **Cut Size** and **Ratio Cut** as partitioning parameters. Why is Ratio Cut considered more robust?

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Cut Size $c(P)$** | **Ratio Cut $RC(P)$** |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $c(P) = \sum \mathbb{1}[\text{span}(e) > 1]$ | $RC(P) = c(P) / (\vert V_1 \vert \cdot \vert V_2 \vert)$ |
| Normalization | Not normalized | Normalized by block sizes |
| Trivial solution | Allows 1-cell block | Penalizes it heavily |
| Sensitivity | Biased toward low-degree cells | Balanced metric |

**Robustness Reason:** Ratio Cut is normalized by the product of block sizes, which mathematically penalizes imbalanced partitions. Even if $c(P)$ is small, an uneven split gives a *larger* $RC$. This eliminates trivial corner-cell solutions that pure cut-size minimization would erroneously favor.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **(a)** Explain the various **parameters used for partitioning** in VLSI physical design with mathematical formulations. Discuss their role in evaluating partition quality. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** For the circuit given below, perform one pass of the **Fiduccia-Mattheyses algorithm** and compute the gain for moving each cell. Identify the best move. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Circuit Nets:**
> - Net 1: $\{A, B, C\}$
> - Net 2: $\{B, D\}$
> - Net 3: $\{C, E\}$
> - Net 4: $\{D, E\}$
>
> **Initial Partition:** $V_1 = \{A, B, C\}$, $V_2 = \{D, E\}$

### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

The key partitioning parameters are:

**1. Cut Size (Primary Objective):**
$$c(P) = |\{e \in E : \text{span}(e) > 1\}|$$
Counts nets with terminals in both blocks.

**2. Balance Constraint (Feasibility):**
$$|V_i| \leq (1 + \varepsilon) \cdot \frac{n}{k}$$
Standard $\varepsilon = 0.1$ (10%).

**3. Ratio Cut (Normalized Objective):**
$$RC(P) = \frac{c(P)}{|V_1| \cdot |V_2|}$$
Eliminates trivial partitions.

**4. Absorption:**
$$a(v) = |\{e \ni v : \text{all other pins of } e \text{ in } V_{\text{dest}}\}|$$
Number of nets absorbed by the move.

**5. Terminal Count:**
$$T(v) = \sum_{e \ni v} \mathbb{1}[e \text{ is cut}]$$
Total cut-degree of cell $v$.

**6. Net-Weight Normalization:**
$$w_{\text{norm}}(e) = \frac{w(e)}{\text{span}(e) - 1}$$
For multi-terminal fair cost.

**Role in Evaluating Quality:** These parameters collectively balance the trade-off between **minimizing inter-block wiring** (cuts) and **respecting physical feasibility** (balance). The objective function typically combines cut size with constraint penalties, while gain-based metrics drive iterative algorithms like FM.

### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute Initial Cut Size:**
- Net 1 $\{A,B,C\}$: All in $V_1$ → uncut ✓
- Net 2 $\{B,D\}$: $B \in V_1, D \in V_2$ → **CUT** ✗
- Net 3 $\{C,E\}$: $C \in V_1, E \in V_2$ → **CUT** ✗
- Net 4 $\{D,E\}$: All in $V_2$ → uncut ✓
- **$c(P_0) = 2$**

**Step 2 — Compute Gains:**

| Cell | Incident Nets | After-Move Analysis | Gain $g(v)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | Net 1 | Net 1 has $\{B,C\}$ in $V_1$, becomes 1-pin in $V_2$ | $0$ |
| B | Net 1, Net 2 | Net 2: $D \in V_2$ (FS=1), $B$ alone in $V_1$ → new cut ($\delta=-1$); Net 1: $A,C$ in $V_1$ → uncut ($\delta=+1$) | $0$ |
| C | Net 1, Net 3 | Net 3: $E \in V_2$ (FS=1) → new cut ($\delta=-1$); Net 1: $A,B$ in $V_1$ → uncut ($\delta=+1$) | $0$ |
| D | Net 2, Net 4 | Net 2: $B \in V_1$ (TE=1, FS=0) → uncut ($\delta=+1$); Net 4: $E$ alone in $V_2$ → uncut ($\delta=+1$) | $+2$ |
| E | Net 3, Net 4 | Net 3: $C \in V_1$ (TE=1, FS=0) → uncut ($\delta=+1$); Net 4: $D$ alone in $V_2$ → uncut ($\delta=+1$) | $+2$ |

**Step 3 — Identify Best Move:**
Both $D$ and $E$ have the **highest gain of $+2$**. By FM tie-breaking, either is valid. Choosing $D$:
- Move $D: V_2 \to V_1$
- New partition: $V_1 = \{A, B, C, D\}$, $V_2 = \{E\}$
- New cut: Net 3 $\{C, E\}$ and Net 4 $\{D, E\}$ → $c(P_1) = 2$ (no improvement in count, but ratio cut improves)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Forgetting to update $FS/TE$ counts AFTER hypothetically moving the cell — **3 marks lost commonly**
> 2. Not explicitly classifying each net as "becomes uncut" vs "becomes cut" — **2 marks lost**
> 3. Ignoring absorption in gain computation — **1 mark lost**
> 4. Not stating the final cut size after move — **1 mark lost**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Alternative]**

> **(a)** Derive the **Ratio Cut parameter** mathematically. Show with an example why pure cut-size minimization can give a trivial (bad) solution and how Ratio Cut avoids it. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A circuit has 100 cells to be partitioned into 2 blocks with tolerance $\varepsilon = 0.1$. Define the **balance constraint**, compute the **maximum allowed cells per block**, and explain what happens if the constraint is violated. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Derivation:** Let $P = \{V_1, V_2\}$ be a partition of $n$ cells with cut size $c(P)$. Pure cut-size objective is $\min c(P)$.

Consider the trivial partition $P_{\text{triv}} = \{\{v_1\}, V \setminus \{v_1\}\}$ where $v_1$ is a low-degree cell. Then $c(P_{\text{triv}}) = d(v_1)$ which can be very small (e.g., 2).

Yeh, Cheng & Lin (1992) proposed normalizing by block sizes to penalize imbalance:

$$RC(P) = \frac{c(P)}{|V_1| \cdot |V_2|}$$

**Example:** Let $n = 100$, with two candidate partitions:
- $P_{\text{triv}}$: $|V_1| = 1$, $|V_2| = 99$, $c = 2$
  $$RC(P_{\text{triv}}) = \frac{2}{1 \cdot 99} = 0.0202$$
- $P_{\text{bal}}$: $|V_1| = 50$, $|V_2| = 50$, $c = 20$
  $$RC(P_{\text{bal}}) = \frac{20}{50 \cdot 50} = 0.0080$$

Although $c(P_{\text{triv}}) = 2 < 20 = c(P_{\text{bal}})$, we have $RC(P_{\text{bal}}) < RC(P_{\text{triv}})$. The balanced partition is preferred. ∎

### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Balance Constraint Definition:**
$$|V_i| \leq (1 + \varepsilon) \cdot \frac{n}{k} \quad \forall i \in \{1, \ldots, k\}$$

**Given:** $n = 100$, $k = 2$, $\varepsilon = 0.1$.

**Step 1 — Ideal block size:**
$$n_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{n}{k} = \frac{100}{2} = 50 \text{ cells}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum allowed cells per block:**
$$|V_i|_{\max} = (1 + 0.1) \times 50 = 1.1 \times 50 = 55 \text{ cells}$$

**Step 3 — Minimum cells per block (implied by symmetry):**
$$|V_i|_{\min} = 45 \text{ cells}$$

**Step 4 — Consequence of violation:**
If $|V_1| > 55$, the block becomes **over-utilized** in downstream placement:
- **Routing congestion** spikes due to insufficient cell density
- **Timing closure** fails because critical paths cannot be shortened
- **DRC violations** arise from pin accessibility issues
- The **floorplanner rejects** the partition → design iteration required

The FM algorithm enforces this constraint by **skipping high-gain moves** that would push a block beyond its bound (the "balance check" in the bucket structure).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE Module 2 — Parameters for Partitioning**

- [x] **Cut size** = count of nets with terminals in ≥ 2 blocks — *the* primary objective
- [x] **Balance constraint** uses tolerance $\varepsilon$ (typically 10%) with ideal size $n/k$
- [x] **Ratio Cut** = $c(P) / (|V_1| \cdot |V_2|)$ — prevents trivial splits
- [x] **Absorption** = nets absorbed when a cell moves; positive contribution to FM gain
- [x] **Terminal count** $T(v)$ = cut-degree of vertex, used to weight cells
- [x] **Net-weight normalization** = $w(e) / (\text{span}(e) - 1)$ for multi-terminal nets
- [x] **FM gain formula:** $g(v) = \sum \delta(e)$ with $\delta \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$
- [x] **$k$-way total cut:** $C_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i<j} c(V_i, V_j)$
- [x] **Bi-partitioning** ($k=2$) is the base case; multi-way by recursive bi-partitioning
- [x] **Industrial tolerance:** $\varepsilon \in [0.05, 0.15]$ depending on design density targets
- [x] **KTU favourite question type:** FM gain computation for a 4–5 cell toy circuit
- [x] **Common valuation trap:** forgetting to compute $FS/TE$ *after* hypothetical move

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Partitioning Algorithms
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT525 - CAD BASED VLSI DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Classification of Partitioning Algorithms

# 📘 Classification of Partitioning Algorithms — KTU 2024 Scheme Notes

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Partitioning** is the process of dividing a set of circuit components (cells, modules, or blocks) into a prescribed number of disjoint groups such that a defined objective function (typically the number of inter-group connections) is minimized, while satisfying design constraints such as balance, capacity, and timing.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) framework for **PEEVT525**, partitioning is formally defined as: Given a hypergraph $H = (V, E)$ where $V$ represents the set of cells/modules and $E$ represents the set of nets (multi-terminal connections), partition $V$ into $k$ disjoint subsets $V_1, V_2, \ldots, V_k$ such that:

$$
\bigcup_{i=1}^{k} V_i = V \quad \text{and} \quad V_i \cap V_j = \emptyset \;\; \forall\; i \neq j
$$

The **classification of partitioning algorithms** refers to the systematic taxonomy of computational methods used to solve this NP-hard optimization problem, categorized by their operational strategy, objective formulation, and solution-construction paradigm.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are organizing a **large Indian wedding reception** with **600 guests** arriving on buses from **6 different cities**. Your job is to seat them across **10 tables** such that:
- Each table has roughly **60 guests** (balance constraint).
- Guests from the same city or family **sit together as much as possible** (minimize "cut" — friends separated across tables).

You could:
1. **Randomly assign** guests to tables (Constructive Heuristic).
2. **Try to improve** by swapping two unhappy guests between tables (Iterative Improvement — *Kernighan–Lin style*).
3. **Move one guest at a time** to reduce family separation (Single-vertex move — *Fiduccia–Mattheyses style*).
4. **Try random chaotic swaps**, sometimes even accepting worse moves to escape local minima (*Simulated Annealing*).
5. **Combine the best tables from multiple attempts** and evolve them (*Genetic Algorithms*).

This is **exactly** how VLSI partitioning algorithms are classified! 🪔

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics Used in Partitioning:**
> - **Cut Size ($C$)** — Number of nets crossing partition boundaries.
> - **Balance Factor ($r$)** — Ratio of cell counts in the two partitions; ideally $r = 1.0$.
> - **Time Complexity ($O(\cdot)$)** — Critical for circuits with **millions of cells** ($|V| \geq 10^6$).
> - **Hypergraph Cardinality** — $|V|$ cells, $|E|$ nets, average net degree $\bar{d}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Top-Level Classification Tree

Partitioning algorithms are classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

| Axis | Classification | Description |
|------|---------------|-------------|
| **# Partitions** | Bi-partitioning ($k=2$) vs. Multi-way ($k>2$) | Classical KL/FM are bi-partitioning; multi-way extends via recursive or direct methods. |
| **Solution Strategy** | Constructive vs. Iterative Improvement | Constructive builds from scratch; iterative refines an existing solution. |
| **Objective Function** | Min-Cut, Ratio-Cut, Normalized-Cut, Scaled-Cost | Determines what "good" partition means. |
| **Move Mechanism** | Group Migration, Single-Vertex, Probabilistic, Population-Based | Governs how new solutions are explored. |

### 2.2 Detailed Classification Breakdown

#### **(A) Constructive (Initial Partitioning) Algorithms**
- **Random Partitioning** — Assigns cells randomly; serves as a baseline.
- **Cluster Growth / Seed Growth** — Starts with a seed cell, grows cluster by attaching highly-connected neighbors.
- **Connectivity-Based (BFS/DFS)** — Uses graph traversal to identify naturally connected regions.
- **Set Cover / Greedy Heuristics** — Iteratively selects cell groups covering maximum nets.

#### **(B) Iterative Improvement Algorithms** (The Most Exam-Relevant Category)

**B1. Group Migration Methods (Pairwise Swaps)**
- **Kernighan–Lin (KL) Algorithm (1970)** — The classical cornerstone; swaps **pairs** of cells to escape local minima.
- **Extensions of KL** — for hypergraphs and multi-way partitioning.

**B2. Single-Vertex Migration Methods**
- **Fiduccia–Mattheyses (FM) Algorithm (1982)** — Moves **one cell at a time**; introduces **bucket-list data structure** for $O(P)$ per pass.

**B3. Probabilistic / Stochastic Methods**
- **Simulated Annealing (SA)** — Kirkpatrick et al.; accepts worse moves with probability $e^{-\Delta/T}$ to escape local optima.
- **Threshold Accepting** — Deterministic variant of SA.

**B4. Population-Based / Evolutionary Methods**
- **Genetic Algorithms (GA)** — Maintains a population of partitions; crossover + mutation.
- **Evolution Strategies** — Real-coded variants.

**B5. Memory-Based / Meta-heuristics**
- **Tabu Search** — Uses short-term memory (tabu list) to avoid cycling.
- **Ant Colony Optimization** — Probabilistic graph-walk with pheromone trails.

**B6. Algebraic / Spectral Methods**
- **Spectral Partitioning** — Uses eigenvectors of the **Laplacian matrix** of the netlist graph (e.g., Hagen–Kahng).

**B7. Network Flow / Min-Cut Based**
- **Min-Cut Max-Flow based partitioning** — Using Gomory–Hu trees for multi-way.

#### **(C) Multi-Level Partitioning (Modern Paradigm)**
- **Multi-Level FM (ML-FM)** — Coarsen → Partition → Uncoarsen with FM refinement.
- **hMETIS / PaToH** — Industry-standard multi-level hypergraph partitioners.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **mandatory formulas** you must memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Meaning |
|---|-------------------|---------------------|---------|
| 1 | **Cut Size (Edges)** | $C(E) = \sum_{e \in E_{cut}} 1$ | Number of nets with terminals in both partitions |
| 2 | **Balance Constraint** | $\left\lvert \dfrac{\vert V_1 \vert - \vert V_2 \vert}{\vert V \vert} \right\rvert \leq \varepsilon$ | Tolerance $\varepsilon$ typically $0.0$ to $0.1$ |
| 3 | **Ratio Cut** | $R_{cut} = \dfrac{C(E)}{\vert V_1 \vert \cdot \vert V_2 \vert}$ | Penalizes unbalanced partitions |
| 4 | **Normalized Cut** | $N_{cut} = C(E) \left(\dfrac{1}{\sum_{v \in V_1} d_v} + \dfrac{1}{\sum_{v \in V_2} d_v}\right)$ | Used in image segmentation; accounts for node degrees |
| 5 | **KL Gain Function** | $g(v) = E_v - I_v$ | External cost minus internal cost for vertex $v$ |
| 6 | **FM Gain Update** | $g_{\text{new}} = g_{\text{old}} \pm 1$ | Incremental update after each move |
| 7 | **SA Acceptance Probability** | $P(\text{accept}) = e^{-\Delta E / T}$ | $T$ = temperature, $\Delta E > 0$ for worsening moves |
| 8 | **Cooling Schedule** | $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k, \quad 0 < \alpha < 1$ | Geometric cooling, typically $\alpha \approx 0.9$–$0.95$ |
| 9 | **KL Time Complexity** | $O(n^2 \log n)$ | For one pass on $n$ vertices |
| 10 | **FM Time Complexity** | $O(P_{\max})$ per pass | $P_{\max}$ = total net terminals |
| 11 | **Laplacian Matrix** | $L = D - A$ | $D$ = degree matrix, $A$ = adjacency matrix |
| 12 | **Spectral Cut** | $C_{\text{sp}} = \dfrac{x^T L x}{x^T x}$ | Minimized by Fiedler vector |
| 13 | **Multi-way Cut (k=4)** | $C = \sum_{i<j} c_{ij}$ | Sum of pairwise cuts |
| 14 | **Scaled Cost (Sanchis)** | $\sum_i \dfrac{c_i}{\vert V_i \vert^\alpha}$ | Generalized ratio-cut form |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** In KTU answer sheets, **always state the time complexity** when describing any partitioning algorithm — it's a guaranteed 1–2 marks.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **FPGA / ASIC Placement** — Pre-placement partition determines timing closure feasibility.
- **Multi-Chip Module (MCM) Design** — Partition across chips minimizes inter-chip I/O.
- **System-on-Chip (SoC)** — Hardware–software partition across processors/accelerators.
- **PCB Design** — Component placement for minimum trace crossings.
- **Parallel Simulation** — Partition circuit across multiple compute nodes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Kernighan–Lin (KL) Algorithm — Full Derivation

The KL algorithm operates on a **graph** $G = (V, E)$ with vertices having weights, and aims to minimize cut size. Let's derive it step-by-step.

**Step 1: Initial Partition**
Given $V$, create an initial bipartition $(V_1, V_2)$ — typically random or via BFS-based constructive method. Let $n_1 = |V_1|$, $n_2 = |V_2|$.

**Step 2: Define Cost and Gain Functions**

For any vertex $v \in V_1$:
- $E_v$ = number of edges from $v$ to vertices in $V_2$ (External cost)
- $I_v$ = number of edges from $v$ to vertices in $V_1$ (Internal cost)

The **gain** of moving $v$ from $V_1$ to $V_2$ is:

$$
g(v) = E_v - I_v
$$

**Step 3: Identify Maximum-Gain Pair Swap**

At each iteration, KL selects an **unlocked pair** $(v^*, u^*)$ with $v^* \in V_1$, $u^* \in V_2$ such that the **pairwise swap gain** is maximized:

$$
g_{\text{pair}}(v, u) = g(v) + g(u) - 2 \cdot c(v, u)
$$

where $c(v, u)$ is the connection cost between $v$ and $u$ (i.e., 1 if edge exists, else 0). The term $-2 c(v,u)$ accounts for the **double-counting** of the edge after swap.

**Step 4: Lock the Pair**

After selecting $(v^*, u^*)$, they are **locked** (cannot be selected again this pass) and **swapped**. Update external/internal costs of all neighbors.

**Step 5: Cumulative Gain Tracking**

After $k$ pair-swaps in the current pass, compute the cumulative gain:

$$
G(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} g_{\text{pair}}(v_i^*, u_i^*)
$$

**Step 6: Find Optimal Partial Sequence**

Find $k^*$ such that:

$$
k^* = \arg\max_{1 \leq k \leq \min(n_1, n_2)} G(k)
$$

Apply **only** the first $k^*$ swaps (this is the key insight — even if later swaps have negative gains, the prefix may be optimal).

**Step 7: Iterate**

Repeat Steps 2–6 until no positive cumulative gain exists or max iterations reached.

#### Worked Numerical Example (5-vertex graph)

Consider $G = (V, E)$ with $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ and edges:
$E = \{(1,4), (1,5), (2,4), (2,5), (3,4), (3,5)\}$

**Initial Partition:** $V_1 = \{1, 2, 3\}$, $V_2 = \{4, 5\}$

**Initial Cut Size:** Each vertex in $V_1$ connects to both vertices in $V_2$, so cut = **6**.

| Vertex $v$ | $E_v$ | $I_v$ | $g(v) = E_v - I_v$ |
|------------|-------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | 2 | 0 | **+2** |
| 2 | 2 | 0 | **+2** |
| 3 | 2 | 0 | **+2** |
| 4 | 3 | 1 | **+2** |
| 5 | 3 | 1 | **+2** |

**Pairwise swap** of $(1, 4)$:
- $g(1) = +2$, $g(4) = +2$, $c(1,4) = 1$
- $g_{\text{pair}} = 2 + 2 - 2(1) = \mathbf{+2}$

**After swap:** $V_1 = \{2, 3, 4\}$, $V_2 = \{1, 5\}$ — new cut size = **4** (edges: 1-4, 2-1, 3-5, and remaining 2-5, 3-4 → actually 4).

Continuing this process optimally reduces the cut.

### 3.2 Fiduccia–Mattheyses (FM) Algorithm — Analytical Walkthrough

FM improves KL in three critical ways:
1. Operates on **hypergraphs** (multi-terminal nets).
2. Moves **single vertex** per step instead of pairs.
3. Uses **bucket lists** for $O(1)$ max-gain retrieval.

**Step 1: Initialize Bucket Lists**
Maintain two doubly-linked lists (max-gain to min-gain) for $V_1$ and $V_2$, each indexed by gain value. Total gain range: $[-P_{\max}, +P_{\max}]$.

**Step 2: Compute Initial Gains**
For each cell $c$:
$$
g(c) = \text{FS}(c) - \text{TE}(c)
$$
where $\text{FS}(c)$ = number of nets becoming free if $c$ moves, $\text{TE}(c)$ = number of nets becoming critical.

**Step 3: Select Max-Gain Cell**
Pick $c^*$ from the head of the appropriate bucket; move $c^*$ to the opposite partition.

**Step 4: Lock and Update**
Lock $c^*$. For each net containing $c^*$, update gains of all **unlocked** cells on that net using the incremental formula:

$$
g_{\text{new}} = g_{\text{old}} + \Delta
$$

Specifically:
- If net becomes **critical** (was free before move): $\Delta = -1$ for unlocked cells remaining in source partition.
- If net becomes **free** (was critical): $\Delta = +1$ for unlocked cells in source partition.

**Step 5: Track Cumulative Gain & Roll Back**
After moving all cells, find prefix with max cumulative gain $G^*$. Apply first $k^*$ moves permanently; roll back the rest.

**Step 6: Repeat** until $G^* \leq 0$.

**Time Complexity:** $O(P_{\max})$ per pass where $P_{\max} = \sum_{e \in E} |e|$ = sum of net sizes.

### 3.3 Simulated Annealing — Derivation of Acceptance Probability

SA is inspired by statistical mechanics. The Boltzmann distribution gives the probability of a system being in state $s$ at temperature $T$:

$$
P(s) = \dfrac{e^{-E(s) / k_B T}}{Z(T)}, \quad Z(T) = \sum_{s} e^{-E(s)/k_B T}
$$

For partitioning, we **abstract away** the partition function and use the **Metropolis acceptance criterion**:

$$
P(\text{accept worsening move}) = e^{-\Delta C / T}
$$

where $\Delta C = C_{\text{new}} - C_{\text{old}} > 0$.

**Cooling Schedule (Geometric):**
$$
T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k, \quad T_0 \approx 5{-}10 \times \bar{C}
$$

As $T \to 0$, $P(\text{accept}) \to 0$, algorithm reduces to greedy descent.

### 3.4 Spectral Partitioning — Mathematical Foundation

For graph $G = (V, E)$ with adjacency matrix $A$ and degree matrix $D$, define the **Laplacian**:

$$
L = D - A
$$

For any vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^{|V|}$, the Rayleigh quotient gives:

$$
x^T L x = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{(i,j) \in E} (x_i - x_j)^2
$$

The **Fiedler vector** is the eigenvector of $L$ corresponding to the **second-smallest eigenvalue** $\lambda_2$. Partition by sign:

$$
V_1 = \{v : x_v \geq 0\}, \quad V_2 = \{v : x_v < 0\}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Top-Level Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Partitioning Algorithms] --> B[Constructive]
    A --> C[Iterative Improvement]
    A --> D[Multi-Level]
    B --> B1[Random Partitioning]
    B --> B2[Cluster / Seed Growth]
    B --> B3[BFS DFS Based]
    B --> B4[Greedy Set Cover]
    C --> C1[Group Migration]
    C --> C2[Single Vertex Migration]
    C --> C3[Stochastic]
    C --> C4[Population Based]
    C --> C5[Spectral]
    C --> C6[Network Flow]
    C1 --> C1a[Kernighan Lin KL]
    C1 --> C1b[Extended KL for Hypergraphs]
    C2 --> C2a[Fiduccia Mattheyses FM]
    C2 --> C2b[Improved FM Variants]
    C3 --> C3a[Simulated Annealing SA]
    C3 --> C3b[Threshold Accepting]
    C4 --> C4a[Genetic Algorithms]
    C4 --> C4b[Evolution Strategies]
    C5 --> C5a[Laplacian Eigenvector]
    C5 --> C5b[Hagen Kahng]
    C6 --> C6a[Gomory Hu Tree]
    C6 --> C6b[Min Cut Max Flow]
    D --> D1[Coarsening Phase]
    D --> D2[Initial Partitioning]
    D --> D3[Uncoarsening with FM]
    D1 --> D1a[Edge Coarsening]
    D1 --> D1b[Hyperedge Coarsening]
```

### 4.2 FM Algorithm — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start FM Algorithm] --> B[Input: Hypergraph H = V E]
    B --> C[Constructive Initial Partition V1 V2]
    C --> D[Compute Gain for All Cells]
    D --> E[Build Bucket Lists Max to Min Gain]
    E --> F[Select Highest Gain Cell from Bucket Head]
    F --> G[Lock Cell and Move to Opposite Partition]
    G --> H[Update Gains of Neighboring Unlocked Cells]
    H --> I[Record Cumulative Gain at this step]
    I --> J{All Cells Locked?}
    J -->|No| F
    J -->|Yes| K[Find k star maximizing cumulative gain prefix]
    K --> L{G star greater than 0?}
    L -->|No| M[Stop Algorithm]
    L -->|Yes| N[Apply First k star Moves Permanently]
    N --> O[Unlock All Cells Reset Bucket Lists]
    O --> D
```

### 4.3 KL vs FM — Comparative Architecture Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph KL[Kernighan Lin 1970]
        K1[Start with Graph G V E] --> K2[Initial Bipartition]
        K2 --> K3[Compute Pairwise Gain for all pairs]
        K3 --> K4[Select Best Pair Swap and Lock]
        K4 --> K5{All pairs locked?}
        K5 -->|No| K3
        K5 -->|Yes| K6[Find Max Cumulative Prefix]
        K6 --> K7[Apply First k Star Swaps]
    end
    subgraph FM[Fiduccia Mattheyses 1982]
        F1[Start with Hypergraph H V E] --> F2[Initial Bipartition]
        F2 --> F3[Compute Single Vertex Gains]
        F3 --> F4[Build Bucket Lists O 1 Access]
        F4 --> F5[Pick Max Gain Cell Move and Lock]
        F5 --> F6[Incremental Gain Update Neighbors]
        F6 --> F7{All cells locked?}
        F7 -->|No| F5
        F7 -->|Yes| F8[Find Max Cumulative Prefix]
        F8 --> F9[Apply First k Star Moves]
    end
```

### 4.4 Multi-Level Partitioning — Three-Phase Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Original Hypergraph Level 0] --> B[Coarsening Phase]
    B --> B1[Merge Highly Connected Cells]
    B1 --> B2[Level 1 Coarser Graph]
    B2 --> B3[Level 2 Even Coarser]
    B3 --> C[Smallest Level Initial Partition]
    C --> D[Apply FM or KL Here]
    D --> E[Uncoarsening Phase Projection Back]
    E --> E1[Level 1 Uncoarsened with FM Refinement]
    E1 --> E2[Level 0 Original with FM Refinement]
    E2 --> F[Final Partition Output]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between constructive and iterative improvement partitioning algorithms. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Constructive | Iterative Improvement |
|--------|--------------|----------------------|
| **Starting Point** | Begins from scratch (no prior solution) | Begins from an existing initial partition |
| **Approach** | Builds partition cell-by-cell using greedy/heuristic rules | Refines partition via local moves (swap/migrate) |
| **Quality** | Produces moderate-quality solutions quickly | Produces high-quality solutions, slower |
| **Examples** | (i) Random Partitioning, (ii) Cluster/Seed Growth | (i) Kernighan–Lin (KL), (ii) Fiduccia–Mattheyses (FM) |

Constructive methods are typically used as the **initial solution generator** for iterative methods.

---

#### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define cut size, ratio cut, and normalized cut. Explain why normalized cut is preferred for highly unbalanced partitions.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Cut Size** $C(E)$: Number of nets with at least one terminal in each partition.
- **Ratio Cut** $R_{cut} = \dfrac{C(E)}{|V_1| \cdot |V_2|}$: Normalizes cut by product of partition sizes.
- **Normalized Cut** $N_{cut} = C(E)\left(\dfrac{1}{d(V_1)} + \dfrac{1}{d(V_2)}\right)$: Normalizes by sum of node degrees (connectivity).

For highly unbalanced partitions (e.g., $|V_1| \ll |V_2|$), ratio cut can be artificially small because $|V_1| \cdot |V_2|$ is tiny. **Normalized cut** uses **degree sums** which better reflect the actual connectivity imbalance, preventing trivial solutions where one partition is nearly empty.

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Kernighan–Lin (KL) algorithm** in detail. State its objective function, the concept of gain, and the pairwise swap mechanism. Why does KL allow temporarily worsening moves?

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare the **Kernighan–Lin** and **Fiduccia–Mattheyses** algorithms in terms of: (i) graph type, (ii) move mechanism, (iii) data structure, (iv) time complexity, and (v) handling of imbalance.

##### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Kernighan–Lin Algorithm:**

**Objective:** Minimize the number of edges crossing the bipartition.

**Gain Definition:** For vertex $v \in V_1$, $g(v) = E_v - I_v$, where $E_v$ = edges to $V_2$, $I_v$ = edges within $V_1$.

**Pairwise Gain:**
$$
g_{\text{pair}}(v, u) = g(v) + g(u) - 2 \cdot c(v,u)
$$

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Start with initial partition $(V_1, V_2)$.
2. Compute gains for all vertices.
3. Repeatedly select the unlocked pair $(v^*, u^*)$ with maximum $g_{\text{pair}}$; swap and lock them.
4. Track cumulative gain $G(k)$ at each step.
5. Find $k^* = \arg\max_k G(k)$ and apply only the first $k^*$ swaps.
6. Repeat until $G^* \leq 0$.

**Why temporarily worsening moves?** KL's key insight is that by performing **all swaps** in a pass (even those with negative immediate gain) and then selecting the **optimal prefix**, it can escape local minima that greedy algorithms cannot. This "look-ahead" through the partial sequence is the algorithm's strength.

**(b) Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | KL Algorithm | FM Algorithm |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|
| (i) Graph type | Simple graph (edges between 2 vertices) | Hypergraph (nets with $>2$ terminals) |
| (ii) Move mechanism | Pairwise swap (2 vertices at once) | Single-vertex move |
| (iii) Data structure | Simple lists / arrays | Bucket lists indexed by gain |
| (iv) Time complexity | $O(n^2 \log n)$ per pass | $O(P_{\max})$ per pass |
| (v) Imbalance handling | Restricted (must swap pairs preserving balance) | Flexible (can move single vertex with balance adjustment) |

FM is the **industrial successor** of KL, designed specifically for **hypergraphs** (which VLSI nets inherently are) and offers **linear time complexity per pass** via the bucket list.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Describe the **Fiduccia–Mattheyses (FM) algorithm**. Explain the bucket list data structure and how incremental gain updates are performed when a cell is moved.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain **Simulated Annealing** as applied to partitioning. Derive the acceptance probability formula and discuss the role of the cooling schedule.

##### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Fiduccia–Mattheyses Algorithm:**

The FM algorithm is designed for **hypergraph** partitioning where nets can have more than two terminals. It uses **single-vertex moves** instead of pair swaps.

**Bucket List Data Structure:**
- Two doubly-linked lists (one per partition) are maintained.
- Each list is indexed by gain values from $-P_{\max}$ to $+P_{\max}$.
- Each bucket contains all currently **unlocked** cells with that gain value.
- Picking the highest-gain cell is $O(1)$: just take the head of the rightmost non-empty bucket.
- Updating a cell's gain (moving it to a different bucket) is also $O(1)$.

**Incremental Gain Update:**
When cell $c$ is moved from $V_1$ to $V_2$, only the **nets incident to $c$** need to be examined:

- **Critical net → Free net** (after $c$'s move, net has all terminals in same partition): For each unlocked cell $x$ in the source partition sharing this net, gain increases by $+1$. Update bucket: move $x$ from gain-$g$ bucket to gain-$(g+1)$ bucket.

- **Free net → Critical net** (after $c$'s move, net now spans both partitions): For each unlocked cell $x$ in the source partition sharing this net, gain decreases by $-1$. Update bucket accordingly.

- **Critical net → Still critical**: No gain change.

This incremental approach reduces complexity from $O(n^2)$ (KL) to $O(P_{\max})$ (FM).

**(b) Simulated Annealing:**

Simulated Annealing (SA) is a **probabilistic meta-heuristic** inspired by the annealing process in metallurgy. It allows the algorithm to **accept worse moves** with a controlled probability to escape local minima.

**Acceptance Probability (Metropolis Criterion):**
$$
P(\text{accept}) = 
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if } \Delta C \leq 0 \text{ (improving move)} \\
e^{-\Delta C / T}, & \text{if } \Delta C > 0 \text{ (worsening move)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Derivation Context:** From the Boltzmann distribution of statistical mechanics:
$$
P(s) \propto e^{-E(s)/k_B T}
$$
The ratio of probabilities of two states is $e^{-\Delta E / k_B T}$. As $T \to \infty$, all moves accepted (random walk). As $T \to 0$, only improving moves accepted (greedy descent).

**Cooling Schedule:**
A typical geometric cooling:
$$
T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k, \quad 0.9 \leq \alpha \leq 0.99
$$

**Role of Cooling:**
- **High $T$ initially:** Broad search of solution space; explores diverse regions.
- **Low $T$ at end:** Fine-tuning near the best found solution.
- **Slow cooling:** Better quality, longer runtime.
- **Fast cooling:** May get trapped in local minima.

**Application to Partitioning:**
1. Start with random partition; compute cost $C$.
2. Generate neighbor via random cell move or swap.
3. Compute $\Delta C$; accept with probability above.
4. Reduce $T$ according to schedule.
5. Repeat until $T \approx 0$ or time limit reached.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Evaluation:**
>
> 1. **Missing time complexity** — Always state $O(\cdot)$ for any algorithm discussed. Examiners allocate 1–2 marks explicitly for this.
> 2. **Confusing KL and FM** — A very common error: stating KL uses bucket lists (wrong — that's FM), or FM uses pair swaps (wrong — that's KL).
> 3. **Forgetting the "partial sequence" insight** — Both KL and FM apply only the prefix with maximum cumulative gain, not all swaps. Missing this loses **3 marks** on a 14-mark question.
> 4. **No mention of balance constraint** — A correct partition is one with $C$ minimized **subject to** balance. Forgetting the constraint loses 2 marks.
> 5. **SA derivation skipped** — Students often write $e^{-\Delta/T}$ without derivation. KTU 2024 expects at least the **Boltzmann context** (1–2 lines).
> 6. **Misnaming ratio cut vs normalized cut** — Ratio cut uses $|V_1| \cdot |V_2|$; normalized cut uses degree sums. Interchanging = **2-mark deduction**.
> 7. **Ignoring multi-level paradigm** — Modern KTU questions increasingly reference multi-level partitioning. Not mentioning hMETIS/ML-FM shows outdated understanding.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Partitioning = NP-hard** bipartitioning problem on hypergraphs.
- ✅ **Two major families**: Constructive (build) vs Iterative (refine).
- ✅ **KL Algorithm (1970)**: Pairwise swaps on graphs; $O(n^2 \log n)$; **partial prefix optimization** is the key innovation.
