The following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the physical & logical order of the mask layers as they are stacked in silicon, which is the typical fallback when a true cross-section cannot be drawn.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M2["Metal 2 M2"] --> VIA["Via cut 2x2 lambda"]
    M1["Metal 1 M1 horizontal interconnect"] --> CONT["Contact cut 2x2 lambda"]
    POLY["Polysilicon gate and local interconnect"]
    ACT["Active Thinox region where transistors form"]
    PSEL["p-Select masks p plus implant forms pMOS"]
    NSEL["n-Select masks n plus implant forms nMOS"]
    NWELL["n-Well deep p-region hosts NMOS"]
    SUB["p-Substrate host of pMOS in nWell process"]

    SUB --> NWELL
    NWELL --> PSEL
    NWELL --> NSEL
    PSEL --> ACT
    NSEL --> ACT
    ACT --> POLY
    POLY --> CONT
    CONT --> M1
    M1 --> VIA
```

## 4.2 CMOS Inverter Stick Diagram

A **stick diagram** is the *schematic* of a layout — each line is a "stick" of a specific color/layer. The inverter below is a KTU-favourite.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VDD["VDD rail Metal2 blue"] --> PACT["pMOS Active green under poly"]
    PACT --> OUT["Output node Y Metal1 blue"]
    OUT --> NACT["nMOS Active green under poly"]
    NACT --> GND["GND rail Metal2 blue"]
    IN["Input A Polysilicon red gates both transistors"]
    PACT -.->|shared diffusion| OUT
    NACT -.->|shared diffusion| OUT
    IN --> PACT
    IN --> NACT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - The *vertical* red line (Polysilicon) is the **gate** of both transistors.
> - The *vertical* green lines (Active) form the **source/drain** regions of pMOS (top) and nMOS (bottom).
> - The *horizontal* blue line (Metal-1) is the **output Y** — it ties the common diffusion of pMOS and nMOS.
> - The *horizontal* magenta lines (Metal-2) are the **VDD and GND rails** placed on a higher layer to avoid blocking Metal-1 routing.

## 4.3 Design-Rule Verification Flow

This is the **CAD-tool pipeline** the course PEEVT525 ultimately targets — the place-and-route tool must mechanically check every layout against the rule deck.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Schematic netlist"] --> B["Cell layout drawing per layer"]
    B --> C["Layer extraction polygons"]
    C --> D{"DRC Engine"}
    D -->|Width check| E["Compare polygon edge length to Wmin"]
    D -->|Spacing check| F["Measure distance between two same layer edges"]
    D -->|Enclosure check| G["Measure overlap between two different layers"]
    D -->|Extension check| H["Measure overhang of one layer over another"]
    E --> I{"All rules pass"}
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I -->|Yes| J["Clean layout for LVS and fabrication"]
    I -->|No| K["List of violations with coordinates and rule names"]
    K --> B
```

## 4.4 Minimum-Spacing Geometry

The following schematic shows the four families of intra-layer rules:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Intra["Same layer rules"]
        W["Min width W between two edges of the SAME shape"]
        S["Min spacing S between two edges of DIFFERENT shapes"]
    end
    subgraph Inter["Different layer rules"]
        E1["Enclosure of inner layer L1 by outer layer L2"]
        E2["Extension of layer L1 beyond layer L2 edge"]
    end
    Intra --> Inter
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "design rule" in VLSI layout. List any four important λ-based design rules with their typical values.**

**Model Answer:**

A *design rule* is a set of geometric constraints imposed on the IC layout to ensure that the pattern can be correctly transferred to silicon by the photolithographic and etching steps of the fabrication process, while maintaining acceptable yield.

Four λ-based rules:

| # | Rule | Value |
|:-:|------|:-----:|
| 1 | Minimum width of Poly / Metal / Diffusion | $2\lambda$ |
| 2 | Minimum spacing Poly-to-Poly (in same MOS) | $3\lambda$ |
| 3 | Minimum enclosure of contact by Metal-1 | $1\lambda$ |
| 4 | Minimum spacing between n-Select and p-Select | $4\lambda$ |

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the difference between intra-layer and inter-layer design rules? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Intra-layer rules** govern two shapes lying on the *same* mask. *Example:* Minimum width of a metal line = $2\lambda$, or minimum poly-to-poly spacing = $3\lambda$.
- **Inter-layer rules** govern the *relative positioning* of shapes on *two different* masks. *Example:* Polysilicon must extend at least $2\lambda$ beyond the Active edge to form a reliable MOS gate; Metal-1 must enclose a Contact by at least $1\lambda$ on every side.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Most students lose marks because they (a) forget the **units (λ vs µm)**, (b) miss the **directional cue** ("enclosure of A by B" vs "enclosure of B by A"), and (c) skip the **layer-color convention** in their stick diagrams. Always annotate the layer name and color, and state which layer encloses which.

### **Question A** — Layout Layers & Lambda Rules (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a neat stick diagram, draw the layout of a **CMOS 2-input NAND gate**. Identify every layer used and explain the role of each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a 0.6 µm CMOS process (minimum drawn gate length = 0.6 µm), calculate the value of λ and the physical dimensions of the following rules: minimum poly width, minimum poly-to-poly spacing, and minimum n-Well width. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution (a)**

The stick diagram of a 2-input NAND gate (nMOS in series, pMOS in parallel) is shown below. The output Y is taken from the common source of the pMOS pair and the common drain of the nMOS stack; VDD and GND run on Metal-2 to free Metal-1 for signals.

```
VDD (Metal-2, magenta) --------+
                               |
            +------ pMOS A ----+------+
   Input A --|                  |      |
            +------ pMOS B ----+      |---- Y (Metal-1, blue)
                                     |
            +------ nMOS A ----+      |
   Input A --|                  |      |
            +------ nMOS B ----+------+
                               |
GND (Metal-2, magenta) --------+
```

**Layer roles:**
- **Active (green):** forms pMOS (top) and nMOS (bottom) source/drain regions; covered by **n-Select / p-Select** to define doping.
- **Polysilicon (red):** two vertical lines forming the gates of A and B.
- **Metal-1 (blue):** horizontal output line Y, also the VDD bridge between pMOS sources and the GND bridge between nMOS drains.
- **Metal-2 (magenta):** power rails VDD and GND.
- **Contact (black X):** tie Metal-1 to diffusion/poly at the source, drain and output nodes.

### **Model Solution (b)**

**Step 1 — Compute λ:**

$$\lambda = \frac{L_{\min}}{2} = \frac{0.6\,\mu m}{2} = 0.30\,\mu m = 300\,nm$$

**Step 2 — Minimum poly width:**

$$W_{\text{poly}} = 2\lambda = 2 \times 0.30 = 0.60\,\mu m$$

**Step 3 — Minimum poly-to-poly spacing:**

$$S_{\text{poly-poly}} = 3\lambda = 3 \times 0.30 = 0.90\,\mu m$$

**Step 4 — Minimum n-Well width:**

$$W_{\text{nWell}} = 6\lambda = 6 \times 0.30 = 1.80\,\mu m$$

---

### **Question B** — Design-Rule Families & Micron Rules (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the **four families of design rules** (width, spacing, enclosure, extension) with one example and its typical λ-value for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Draw the stick diagram and the corresponding layout (using standard color convention) of a **CMOS transmission gate**. State the design rules that govern the spacing between the nMOS and pMOS Active regions. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution (a)**

| Family | Definition | Example | λ-value |
|--------|------------|---------|:-------:|
| **1. Minimum Width** | Narrowest line that can be drawn on a given layer. | Minimum poly width | $2\lambda$ |
| **2. Minimum Spacing** | Smallest distance between two *edges* on the same layer. | Poly-to-poly spacing | $3\lambda$ |
| **3. Minimum Enclosure** | One layer must completely surround another by a minimum amount. | Metal-1 enclosing a contact | $1\lambda$ |
| **4. Minimum Extension** | One layer must extend beyond the edge of another by a minimum amount. | Active extending beyond poly gate | $2\lambda$ |

### **Model Solution (b)**

A transmission gate consists of an nMOS and a pMOS in parallel, with **complementary control signals φ and φ̄** on their gates.

```
              +---------+---------+
   D --------+         |         +-------- D'
              |  nMOS   |  pMOS   |
              |  (φ)    |  (φ̄)    |
              +---------+---------+
```

In the stick diagram:
- The **horizontal green Active** is shared by both transistors (nMOS at top, pMOS at bottom, separated by the **n-Select / p-Select** boundary).
- The **two vertical red Polysilicon** lines are the two gates, driven by φ and φ̄.
- **Metal-1 (blue)** runs the input D to the source, the output D′ from the drain, and ties the body of nMOS to GND and the body of pMOS to VDD.
- The two transistors are *separated* by the **Select-to-Select spacing rule**:

$$S_{\text{nSel-pSel}} = 4\lambda \quad (\text{latch-up prevention})$$

Additionally, the **n-Well enclosing the pMOS** must be at least $6\lambda$ wide and the well edge must clear the pMOS Active by $3\lambda$ (or per foundry spec).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Layout = 2-D geometric description of an IC** drawn as polygons on stacked mask layers.
- **8 essential layers:** n-Well, Active, p-Select, n-Select, Polysilicon, Contact, Metal-1, Via, Metal-2.
- **Color convention:** Diffusion = green; Poly = red; n-Select = yellow dotted; p-Select = orange dotted; Metal-1 = blue; Metal-2 = magenta; Contact/Via = black ×.
- **A transistor forms wherever Polysilicon crosses Active** and the Active is *not* covered by Select (i.e., the area is exposed for doping).
- **λ (lambda) = half the minimum feature size** ⇒ layout is *scalable* across processes.
- **Four families of design rules:** Min Width, Min Spacing, Min Enclosure, Min Extension.
- **Critical λ-values to memorise:** $2\lambda$ (width, gate, extension), $3\lambda$ (spacing), $1\lambda$ (contact enclosure), $4\lambda$ (nSel–pSel), $6\lambda$ (well).
- **Effective channel length** $L_{\text{eff}} = L_{\text{drawn}} - 2L_D = 2\lambda - 1\lambda = 1\lambda$ (for $\lambda$-rule layout).
- **Contact triple enclosure:** Contact must be enclosed by at least $1\lambda$ of *Active, Polysilicon, and Metal-1* simultaneously — a classic KTU trick question.
- **LVS (Layout vs. Schematic)** comes *after* DRC (Design Rule Check) — both are mandatory before tape-out.
- **Scalable rules are technology-independent; micron-based rules are process-specific** — both must be checked by the CAD tool.
- **Sticks show topology, not exact dimensions** — a stick diagram is the bridge between schematic and full layout.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Design Optimizations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT525 - CAD BASED VLSI DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Physical Design Optimizations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — PHYSICAL DESIGN OPTIMIZATIONS

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Design Optimization** is the post-placement, post-routing phase of the VLSI design flow wherein automated CAD tools apply a suite of geometric, electrical, and topological transformations to a placed-and-routed netlist to **minimize a cost function** (typically a weighted sum of **delay**, **area**, **power**, **signal integrity**, and **manufacturability**) while **preserving logical correctness and functional timing closure**.

The principal objectives are:
1. **Timing Closure** — guaranteeing that all signal paths meet setup and hold constraints.
2. **Area Minimization** — reducing the silicon footprint and wirelength.
3. **Power Reduction** — dynamic and leakage power.
4. **Signal Integrity (SI)** — controlling crosstalk, noise, IR drop, and electromigration.
5. **Manufacturability (DFM/DFY)** — ensuring yield in deep-submicron nodes.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The City Traffic Engineer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you are a traffic engineer for a large city. After laying down the roads (routing), traffic signals (buffers), and intersections (vias), the city is *functionally* working. But bottlenecks remain — rush-hour congestion on one highway, accidents where two parallel lanes merge, intersections that waste fuel through idling. A **physical design optimizer** is exactly this city engineer: it places traffic buffers (buffers/repeaters), widens narrow streets (wire sizing), adds guard rails (shielding), and reroutes traffic (redundant via insertion) so the *same* road network runs *faster, cheaper, and more reliably*.

## 1.3 The Physical Design Optimization Loop

The optimizer operates as a **cost-driven, iterative refinement loop**:

$$
\text{Design} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Analysis} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Cost} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Transform} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Design}_{new}
$$

The **cost function** is universally written as:

$$
\Phi = \alpha \cdot \Phi_{timing} + \beta \cdot \Phi_{area} + \gamma \cdot \Phi_{power} + \delta \cdot \Phi_{SI}
$$

where $\alpha, \beta, \gamma, \delta \geq 0$ are **user-defined weights** that bias the tool toward the most critical design metric.

## 1.4 Categorization of Physical Design Optimizations

| Category | Stage of Application | Primary Knob |
|----------|---------------------|--------------|
| **Timing Optimization** | Post-CTS, Post-Route | Buffer, gate & wire sizing |
| **Power Optimization** | Post-CTS, Post-Route | Power gating, $V_{DD}$ scaling, cell swapping |
| **Area / Wirelength Opt.** | Placement, Post-Route | Cell sizing, pin assignment |
| **Crosstalk / SI Opt.** | Routing, Post-Route | Spacing, shielding, wire re-ordering |
| **Antenna Repair** | Post-Route | Diode insertion, jumper insertion |
| **DFM / Yield Opt.** | Sign-off | Redundant vias, fill metal, OPC |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Timing Optimization — The Core of Physical Optimization

Timing in modern VLSI is dominated by **interconnect delay** (often > 70 % of total path delay below 90 nm). Hence, physical optimization primarily targets **wire delay**, modeled as a distributed **RC network**.

### 2.1.1 RC Delay Model for a Single Wire

A wire of length $L$, width $w$, and thickness $t$ is approximated as a **lumped π-model** with total resistance $R_w$ and total capacitance $C_w$:

$$
R_w \;=\; R_{\square} \cdot \frac{L}{w} \qquad
C_w \;=\; C_{area} \cdot L \cdot w + 2 \, C_{fringe} \cdot L
$$

The **Elmore delay** from driver (resistance $R_s$) to the end of the wire is:

$$
T_{50\%} \;\approx\; 0.69 \, R_s \, C_w + 0.38 \, R_w \, C_w
$$

### 2.1.2 Distributed RC and the Elmore Model

For a wire of length $L$ with per-unit resistance $r$ ($\Omega/\mu m$) and per-unit capacitance $c$ ($fF/\mu m$), driven by a source resistance $R_s$ and loaded by load capacitance $C_L$:

$$
\boxed{\,T_D \;=\; R_s \,(cL + C_L) + r \, c \, \frac{L^2}{2} + r \, L \, C_L\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The quadratic term $\tfrac{1}{2} r c L^2$ is what makes **long wires inherently slow**. Physical optimization's central aim is to *break* this quadratic dependence by inserting buffers.

## 2.2 Buffer Insertion — The Van Ginneken Algorithm (Foundational Concept)

> [!NOTE]
> **Algorithm (LG-based Buffer Insertion):** A buffer with input cap $C_b$, output drive $R_b$ is placed at position $x$ along a wire of length $L$. Optimal buffer location $x^*$ is computed as the point that minimizes the quadratic delay contribution.

The **buffer-insertion condition** for a wire of length $l$ between two inverters with the same drive strength:

$$
\boxed{\,h \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{R_0 C_0 + R_0 C_1}{R_1 C_0}}\,}
$$

where $R_0, C_0$ are the wire (per-unit) parameters and $R_1, C_1$ are the buffer parameters. If the actual wire length $l > h$, a buffer must be inserted.

### 2.2.1 Optimal Number of Buffers (Closed Form)

For equal-strength buffers inserted along a wire:

$$
\boxed{\,k_{opt} \;=\; \ln\!\left(\frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right) \Big/ \ln(h)\,}
$$

giving a **linear (not quadratic)** delay:

$$
T_{D,buf} \;\approx\; 2 \cdot k \cdot h \cdot (R_b c + r C_b)
$$

## 2.3 Gate Sizing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Increase drive strength on critical path, decrease size (and cap) on non-critical branches to reduce load.

For a CMOS inverter, delay $\propto R_{inv} C_L \propto (1/W) \cdot C_L$ where $W$ is the channel width. Doubling the width halves the drive resistance but roughly doubles the input capacitance, so the **size-up is applied selectively** to high-fanout / critical nets.

The **Logical Effort** delay for a stage driving load $C_{L}$ through a buffer of size $x$ is:

$$
d \;=\; g \cdot h + p \;=\; g \cdot \frac{x \, C_{in}}{C_{in}} + p
$$

## 2.4 Wire Sizing

Optimal wire width along a tapered line follows a **continuous exponential taper** (Rosenstein & Wyatt, 1989):

$$
\boxed{\,w(x) \;=\; w_0 \cdot \exp(x / l_c)\,}
$$

where the **critical length** $l_c$ is:

$$
l_c \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2}{r \, c_w \, R_s \, C_g / C_w}}
$$

(Discrete equivalent uses **two-tier sizing**: thick on critical segment, thin elsewhere.)

## 2.5 Crosstalk / Signal Integrity Optimization

Two adjacent wires $i, j$ have coupled capacitance $C_c$ and grounded capacitance $C_g$. The **crosstalk-induced delay change** on victim $i$ is approximated by:

$$
\Delta T_i \;\approx\; \pm \; R_i \, C_c \quad \text{(sign depends on transition direction)}
$$

Mitigations:
- **Wire spacing** — increase $C_g / C_c$ ratio.
- **Shielding** — insert grounded wires between aggressors.
- **Wire ordering** — swap parallel segments to reduce worst-case aggressor count.

## 2.6 Antenna Effect Repair

During plasma etching, charge accumulates on long floating metal segments, stressing gate oxide. The **antenna ratio** must satisfy:

$$
AR \;=\; \frac{\text{Area of metal connected to gate}}{\text{Area of gate oxide}} \;\leq\; AR_{max}
$$

Fixes: **Diode insertion** (clamp), **jumper** to upper metal layer.

## 2.7 Power Optimization Knobs (Physical Layer)

$$
P_{total} \;=\; P_{dyn} + P_{leak} \;=\; \alpha \, C_L V_{DD}^2 f + I_{leak} V_{DD}
$$

Physical-level knobs: **clock gating** placement, **power-gate cell** insertion, **$V_{DD}$/GND** rail sizing for IR-drop, **multi- $V_{th}$** cell swapping.

## 2.8 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $T_D = R_s(C_L + cL) + rc\tfrac{L^2}{2} + rLC_L$ | Elmore delay of a wire | s |
| 2 | $h = \sqrt{(R_0 C_0 + R_0 C_1)/(R_1 C_0)}$ | Buffer-insertion critical length | m |
| 3 | $k_{opt} = \ln(C_{out}/C_{in}) / \ln(h)$ | Optimum buffer count | integer |
| 4 | $d = gh + p$ | Logical-effort stage delay | $\tau$ |
| 5 | $w(x) = w_0 \exp(x/l_c)$ | Tapered wire width | m |
| 6 | $l_c = \sqrt{2/(r c_w R_s C_g/C_w)}$ | Critical length of taper | m |
| 7 | $\Delta T = \pm R_v C_c$ | Crosstalk delay perturbation | s |
| 8 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ | Dynamic power | W |
| 9 | $\Phi = \alpha \Phi_T + \beta \Phi_A + \gamma \Phi_P + \delta \Phi_{SI}$ | Optimization cost function | — |
| 10 | $AR = A_{metal}/A_{gate} \le AR_{max}$ | Antenna rule | — |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **High-Performance CPUs (Intel, AMD):** Iterative timing-closure optimizations (buffering, sizing) account for 30–40 % of final timing slack recovery.
- **Mobile SoCs (Apple M-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon):** Power-driven gate sizing reduces leakage by 25–60 % without timing penalty.
- **Automotive ASICs:** DFM-driven redundant via insertion improves yield from ~70 % to > 95 %.
- **AI Accelerators (NVIDIA GPUs):** Critical-path repeater insertion is mandatory due to mm-scale interconnects across chiplets.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Distributed-RC Elmore Delay

> **Scenario:** A wire of total length $L$ with uniform per-unit resistance $r$ and per-unit capacitance $c$ is driven by a source resistance $R_s$ and loaded by $C_L$. We derive the 50 %-propagation delay using the **Elmore delay** (the first moment of the impulse response), which is the *standard KTU-referenced* metric.

### Step 1 — Discretize the wire
Split the wire into $N$ identical RC segments. Each segment has resistance $r L/N$ and capacitance $c L/N$. Index the nodes from $0$ (driver side) to $N$ (load side).

### Step 2 — Compute the path resistance from each node to ground
For node $k$ ($0 \le k \le N$), the resistance from $k$ to the load output is:

$$
R_{k \to N} \;=\; \left(\frac{rL}{N}\right)(N - k) + R_{s}\,\delta_{k,0}
$$

The capacitance at node $k$ is $C_k = (cL/N)$ for $1 \le k \le N-1$ and $C_N = C_L$.

### Step 3 — Apply the Elmore definition

$$
T_D \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{N} R_{k \to N} \cdot C_k
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the sum
Expand:

$$
T_D \;=\; R_s \,(C_L + cL) \;+\; \sum_{k=1}^{N} \left(\frac{rL}{N}\right)(N - k) \cdot \frac{cL}{N} \;+\; \frac{rL}{N}\,(0) \cdot C_L
$$

The middle term is a triangular summation:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{N} (N - k) \;=\; \sum_{j=0}^{N-1} j \;=\; \frac{(N-1)N}{2} \;\approx\; \frac{N^2}{2}
$$

So the term becomes:

$$
\frac{rL}{N} \cdot \frac{cL}{N} \cdot \frac{N^2}{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} r c L^2
$$

Combining all three terms:

$$
\boxed{\,T_D \;=\; R_s(C_L + cL) + \tfrac{1}{2}\, r c L^2 + r L C_L\,}
$$

### Step 5 — Converting to 50 % delay

The Elmore delay corresponds to the **63 % point** of the step response. The **50 % propagation delay** is obtained by the standard mapping factor **0.69** (for lumped RC), but the *quadratic* dependence on $L$ is preserved:

$$
T_{50\%} \;\approx\; 0.69\,R_s\,(C_L + cL) + 0.39 \, r c L^2 + 0.69\, r L\, C_L
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway for KTU:** *Why is the wire term quadratic?* Because both the *resistance* through which a charge must travel *and* the *capacitance* being charged scale linearly with $L$. This is precisely why **buffer insertion** (which breaks $L$ into $k$ pieces) reduces the order of delay from $\mathcal{O}(L^2)$ to $\mathcal{O}(L)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Buffer-Insertion Critical Length $h$

### Step 1 — Delay of a single segment of length $l$ (no buffer)

$$
T_1 \;=\; R_s c l + \tfrac{1}{2} r c l^2 + r l C_L
$$

### Step 2 — Delay of two segments of length $l/2$ separated by a buffer (drive $R_b$, input cap $C_b$)

$$
T_2 \;=\; R_s\,(c\tfrac{l}{2}+C_b) + \tfrac{1}{2}rc(\tfrac{l}{2})^2 + r\tfrac{l}{2}C_b \;+\; R_b(c\tfrac{l}{2}+C_L) + \tfrac{1}{2}rc(\tfrac{l}{2})^2 + r\tfrac{l}{2}C_L
$$

### Step 3 — Simplify
The two quadratic terms sum to $\tfrac{1}{4}rc l^2$ (half the original quadratic). Collecting linear and load terms:

$$
T_2 \;=\; R_s c \tfrac{l}{2} + R_s C_b + r \tfrac{l}{2} C_b + R_b c \tfrac{l}{2} + R_b C_L + r \tfrac{l}{2} C_L + \tfrac{1}{4}rc l^2
$$

### Step 4 — Find the breakeven length $h$ where $T_2 = T_1$
Set $T_2 = T_1$ and solve for $l$:

$$
R_s c \tfrac{l}{2} + R_s C_b + r\tfrac{l}{2}C_b + R_b c\tfrac{l}{2} + R_b C_L + r\tfrac{l}{2}C_L + \tfrac{1}{4}rc l^2
\;=\; R_s c l + \tfrac{1}{2}rc l^2 + r l C_L
$$

Rearranging, and in the limit $C_L \to 0$ (worst-case) and $R_s \to 0$ (rebuffered segment):

$$
\tfrac{1}{4}rc l^2 \;=\; R_s C_b + R_b c \tfrac{l}{2} + r \tfrac{l}{2} C_b
$$

The dominant term on the right is the constant $R_s C_b$. Solving:

$$
\boxed{\,l_{crit} \;=\; h \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{4\, R_0\,C_0}{rc} \left(1 + \frac{R_b c}{2R_s C_b}\right)} \;\approx\; \sqrt{\frac{R_0 C_0 + R_0 C_1}{R_1 C_0}}\,}
$$

where $R_0 = r$, $C_0 = c$, $R_1 = R_b$, $C_1 = C_b$ in KTU's standard notation.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Optimal Buffer Count

> **Problem:** A wire of length $L = 10\,$mm has $r = 0.075\,\Omega/\mu m$ and $c = 0.2\,fF/\mu m$. It is driven by a minimum-size inverter ($C_b = 2\,fF$, $R_b = 4\,k\Omega$) and feeds a load of $C_L = 50\,fF$.

### Step 1 — Compute the critical length $h$

$$
h \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{R_0 C_0 + R_0 C_1}{R_1 C_0}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{(0.075)(0.2) + (0.075)(2)}{(4000)(0.2)}} \;\;\mu m
$$

Numerator: $0.015 + 0.15 = 0.165$. Denominator: $800$. So:

$$
h \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{0.165}{800}} \;=\; \sqrt{2.06 \times 10^{-4}} \;\approx\; 0.01436\,mm \;=\; 14.36\,\mu m
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the optimum buffer count

$$
k_{opt} \;=\; \frac{\ln(C_{out}/C_{in})}{\ln(h)} \;=\; \frac{\ln(50/2)}{\ln(0.01436)} \;\approx\; \frac{3.218}{(-4.244)} \;\approx\; -0.76
$$

The magnitude gives a recommended **1 buffer** per ~14 µm segment. For 10 mm total:

$$
N_{buf} \;\approx\; \left\lceil \frac{10000\,\mu m}{14.36\,\mu m} \right\rceil \;=\; 697\;\text{buffers}
$$

### Step 3 — Resulting delay

$$
T_{D,buf} \;\approx\; 2 k h (R_b c + r C_b) \;=\; 2(697)(0.01436)(4000 \cdot 0.2 \times 10^{-3} + 0.075 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
= \; 20.0 \cdot (0.8 + 0.00015) \;\approx\; 16.0\,\text{ps}
$$

Compare to **no buffer** delay:

$$
T_{D,nobuf} \;\approx\; 0.39 \cdot 0.075 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot (10000)^2 \cdot 10^{-18} \;\approx\; 5.85\,\text{ns}
$$

> **Speed-up: ~ 365×** — demonstrates why buffer insertion is *the* most powerful physical optimization.

---

## 3.4 📐 Analytical Matrix — Optimization Transform vs. Knob

| Transform | Effect on Delay | Effect on Area | Effect on Power | Effect on Crosstalk |
|-----------|-----------------|----------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **Buffer insertion** | $\downarrow\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ (buf area) | $\uparrow$ (cap switched) | mild $\downarrow$ (shorter wires) |
| **Gate up-sizing** | $\downarrow$ on critical | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | neutral |
| **Gate down-sizing** | $\uparrow$ on critical | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ | neutral |
| **Wire up-sizing** | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ (Si) | $\uparrow$ (cap) | $\uparrow$ (coupling) |
| **Wire spacing** | mild $\downarrow$ | neutral | neutral | $\downarrow\downarrow$ |
| **Shielding** | neutral | $\uparrow$ | mild $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow\downarrow$ |
| **Vth swap (HVT→LVT)** | $\downarrow$ | neutral | $\uparrow$ (leakage) | neutral |
| **Redundant via** | neutral | $\uparrow$ (via) | neutral | neutral |
| **Antenna diode** | mild $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ (leak) | neutral |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Physical Design Optimization — End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Netlist + Constraints] --> A1[Initial Placement]
    A1 --> A2[Initial Routing]
    A2 --> A3{Static Timing Analysis STA}
    A3 -->|Violations Found| A4[Identify Critical Paths]
    A4 --> A5[Buffer Insertion on Critical Nets]
    A5 --> A6[Gate Sizing Fanout Optimization]
    A6 --> A7[Wire Sizing on Long Routes]
    A7 --> A8[Crosstalk Noise Analysis]
    A8 --> A9[Spacing and Shielding Fix]
    A9 --> A10[Antenna Repair]
    A10 --> A11[Power Optimization Vth Swap]
    A11 --> A3
    A3 -->|All Met| A12[Sign-off DRC LVS ERC]
    A12 --> A13[Tape-out Ready GDSII]
```

## 4.2 Buffer Insertion — Logical Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Driver Inverter] --> w1[Wire Segment L less than h]
    w1 --> ld1[Local Load]
    src2[Driver Inverter] --> w2[Wire Segment L greater than h]
    w2 --> buf[Buffer Repeater]
    buf --> w3[Next Wire Segment]
    w3 --> ld2[Far Load]
    style buf fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Optimization Cost-Function Engine

```mermaid
graph TD
    inp[Optimized Netlist] --> e1[Timing Cost Timer]
    inp --> e2[Area Cost Wirelength]
    inp --> e3[Power Cost Dynamic Leakage]
    inp --> e4[SI Cost Crosstalk IRdrop]
    e1 --> w1[Weight alpha]
    e2 --> w2[Weight beta]
    e3 --> w3[Weight gamma]
    e4 --> w4[Weight delta]
    w1 --> sum[Weighted Sum Phi]
    w2 --> sum
    w3 --> sum
    w4 --> sum
    sum --> opt[Optimizer Engine Gradient Descent]
    opt --> out[Transformed Netlist]
```

## 4.4 Crosstalk Mitigation Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Detected Coupling Cap] --> q1{Critical Path?}
    q1 -->|Yes| q2{Slack less than threshold?}
    q1 -->|No| endA[Ignore]
    q2 -->|Yes| fix1[Insert Shield Wire]
    q2 -->|No| fix2[Increase Spacing]
    fix1 --> check1{Crosstalk fixed?}
    fix1 -->|No| fix3[Reorder Wires]
    fix2 --> check1
    fix3 --> check1
    check1 -->|Yes| done[Pass]
    check1 -->|No| fix4[Add Buffer to Break Coupling]
    fix4 --> done
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the Elmore delay for a distributed RC interconnect. Why is it preferred over exact SPICE transient analysis in physical design optimization loops?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Elmore delay is the **first moment of the impulse response** of an RC tree, defined as $T_D = \sum_k R_{k\to sink} C_k$ (1 mark). For a uniform RC wire, this evaluates to $T_D = R_s(C_L + cL) + \tfrac{1}{2}rcL^2 + rLC_L$ (1 mark). It is preferred because it is **linear in circuit components**, has **closed-form expression**, and is **orders of magnitude faster** than SPICE, making it suitable for the millions of timing evaluations needed during iterative optimization (1 mark).

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any three physical design optimizations used to fix crosstalk noise violations. State the dominant effect of each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Wire spacing** — increases grounded-to-coupling capacitance ratio, reducing noise amplitude (1 mark).
> 2. **Shielding** — inserts a grounded wire between aggressor and victim, providing a low-impedance noise sink (1 mark).
> 3. **Buffer insertion** — breaks long coupled runs, isolating the coupling region (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Derive the closed-form Elmore delay expression for a uniform RC wire of length $L$, per-unit resistance $r$, per-unit capacitance $c$, driven by source resistance $R_s$ and terminated by load $C_L$. Show every summation step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5 mm wire has $r = 0.05\,\Omega/\mu m$, $c = 0.18\,fF/\mu m$, $R_s = 200\,\Omega$, $C_L = 100\,fF$. Compute (i) the Elmore delay without any buffer, and (ii) the number of equally-spaced buffers required if the critical length $h = 12\,\mu m$. Compare both delays. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
Follow the derivation in **Section 3.1** step-by-step:
- Discretize wire into $N$ segments, each with $R_i = rL/N$, $C_i = cL/N$ (1 mark).
- Identify path resistance to output: $R_{k\to N} = (rL/N)(N-k) + R_s \delta_{k,0}$ (1 mark).
- Apply $T_D = \sum_{k=0}^N R_{k\to N} C_k$ (1 mark).
- Split sum into driver term $R_s(C_L + cL)$, triangular wire term $\tfrac{1}{2}rcL^2$, load term $rLC_L$ (2 marks).
- State final boxed expression and explain the **quadratic dependence on $L$** (2 marks).

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Without buffer:**

$$
T_D = R_s(C_L + cL) + \tfrac{1}{2}rcL^2 + rLC_L
$$

Substituting:
- $R_s(C_L + cL) = 200 \times (100 \times 10^{-15} + 0.18 \times 10^{-15} \times 5000) = 200 \times (100 + 900) \times 10^{-15} = 200 \times 10^{-12} = 200\,ps$ (1.5 marks)
- $\tfrac{1}{2}rcL^2 = 0.5 \times 0.05 \times 0.18 \times 10^{-15} \times (5000)^2 = 0.5 \times 0.05 \times 0.18 \times 25 \times 10^{6} \times 10^{-15} = 0.1125\,ns$ (1.5 marks)
- $rLC_L = 0.05 \times 5000 \times 100 \times 10^{-15} = 25\,ps$ (1 mark)

**Total: $T_D \approx 200 + 112.5 + 25 = 337.5\,ps$** (1 mark)

**(ii) Number of buffers:**

$$
N_{buf} = \left\lceil \frac{5000\,\mu m}{12\,\mu m} \right\rceil = 417 \text{ buffers}
$$

(1 mark). Each buffered segment has delay $\approx 2 h (R_b c + r C_b) \approx 0.04\,ps$ per segment, giving a per-buffer-stage delay of order $\sim 4\,ps$ (2 marks). Total: $\sim 4\,ps$ vs. 337.5 ps — a **~ 84× speed-up** (1 mark).

---

### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the Van Ginneken / Logical-Effort based buffer insertion algorithm. Derive the critical length $h$ and the optimum buffer count $k_{opt}$ formulae. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the antenna effect in VLSI physical design. List two physical repair techniques, derive the antenna-ratio constraint, and explain why the effect becomes more severe in advanced (sub-28 nm) nodes. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Buffer Insertion (7 marks):**
- Introduce the concept: long wires have **quadratic delay**; inserting a buffer at the critical length $h$ converts the delay from $\mathcal{O}(L^2)$ to $\mathcal{O}(L)$ (1 mark).
- Derive $h$ by equating the delay of a single segment of length $l$ versus two buffered halves of length $l/2$ (2 marks), as in **Section 3.2**. State boxed formula.
- Derive $k_{opt} = \ln(C_{out}/C_{in}) / \ln(h)$ from equating input and output RC for the buffered chain (2 marks).
- Discuss practical considerations: discrete buffer library, slew-rate constraints, congestion cost (2 marks).

**(b) Antenna Effect (7 marks):**
- **Definition:** During reactive-ion etching, a floating metal segment accumulates charge proportional to its area; if this charge reaches the thin gate oxide, Fowler-Nordheim tunneling damages the oxide (1 mark).
- **Constraint derivation:** $AR = A_{metal}/A_{gate} \le AR_{max}$ (typically 400:1 for thin oxide) (2 marks).
- **Repair techniques:** (i) **Diode insertion** — anti-parallel diode to VDD/GND that clamps the charge (1 mark); (ii) **Jumper insertion** — break the offending metal segment and route to a higher metal layer using a via (1 mark).
- **Why worse in sub-28 nm:** thinner gate oxide (lower $V_{BD}$) and longer upper-metal wires for power delivery exacerbate both the **charge-collection area** and the **gate-oxide fragility** (2 marks).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the source-resistance term $R_s(C_L + cL)$** in Elmore derivations — 2 marks often lost.
> 2. **Mixing up 50 % delay (0.69 RC) with 63 % delay (1.0 RC)** — read the question carefully.
> 3. **In antenna-ratio problems, students omit the $A_{gate}$ term** — the ratio is *area of metal* ÷ *area of gate oxide*, not metal area alone.
> 4. **For $k_{opt}$ problems, not ceiling-rounding** — the number of buffers must be an integer, *always* round **up**, never down.
> 5. **In crosstalk questions, the sign of $\Delta T$** (positive for same-direction transitions, negative for opposite) is frequently ignored, losing 1 mark.
> 6. **Not justifying "why quadratic"** — the *derivation* in 3.1 explains it; examiners reward this conceptual link generously.

---

## 5.4 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 12 must-remember points for KTU 2024 Physical Design Optimization:**

1. ✅ Physical design optimization is a **cost-driven, iterative loop** (timing / area / power / SI weights).
2. ✅ **Elmore delay** $T_D = R_s(C_L + cL) + \tfrac{1}{2}rcL^2 + rLC_L$ is the workhorse metric.
3. ✅ Wire delay is **quadratic in $L$** — this is the *root cause* of all long-wire problems.
4. ✅ **Buffer insertion** breaks $\mathcal{O}(L^2)$ into $\mathcal{O}(L)$ — the single most powerful optimization.
5. ✅ **Critical length** $h = \sqrt{(R_0C_0 + R_0C_1)/(R_1C_0)}$ decides where to place buffers.
6. ✅ **Optimum buffer count** $k_{opt} = \ln(C_{out}/C_{in}) / \ln(h)$.
7. ✅ **Wire sizing** uses exponential taper $w(x) = w_0 e^{x/l_c}$ to balance resistance and capacitance.
8. ✅ **Gate sizing** uses logical effort $d = gh + p$ to size stages for minimum delay.
9. ✅ **Crosstalk** $\Delta T = \pm R_v C_c$ — mitigated by spacing, shielding, wire ordering, buffering.
10. ✅ **Antenna ratio** $AR = A_{metal}/A_{gate} \le AR_{max}$ — fixed by diodes or jumpers.
11. ✅ **Power optimization** is dynamic ($\alpha CV^2 f$) + leakage ($I_{leak} V_{DD}$); physical knobs include Vth-swapping, clock-gate placement, power-gate cells.
12. ✅ **DFM optimizations** (redundant vias, metal fill, OPC) become *more critical* as nodes scale to 7 nm, 5 nm, 3 nm.

> 🎯 **Final KTU Mantra:** *Quadratic wire delay → break with buffers → size for drive → space for SI → close timing at sign-off.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compaction: Applications of Compaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT525 - CAD BASED VLSI DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compaction: Applications of Compaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Layout Compaction** is a critical post-layout optimization phase in the VLSI physical design cycle where the geometric coordinates of every mask layer feature (polygons, wires, transistors, contacts) are systematically translated closer together in one or both planar dimensions, strictly subject to **Design Rule Constraints (DRC)**, electrical connectivity preservation, and functional correctness, in order to produce a minimum-area (and often minimum-wire-length) mask-ready layout.

In the formal mathematical sense applied to a 1-Dimensional (1D) X-direction compaction pass, let every circuit element (rectangle, vertex, or wire segment) be assigned a variable coordinate $x_i$ along the axis of compaction. The compaction engine solves a system of inequality constraints of the form:

$$x_j - x_i \ge d_{ij}$$

where $d_{ij}$ is the minimum required spacing dictated by the fabrication process design rules. The objective function is to minimize the total layout span $X_{max} - X_{min}$ over all feature variables, producing a DRC-clean and manufacturable mask description.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Compaction is treated as a Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP) solved via graph-theoretic methods, primarily the **Longest-Path Algorithm on a Constraint Graph**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Suitcase Packing Principle"

Imagine you have packed a suitcase for travel. You initially toss in clothes loosely, leaving wasted empty pockets and gaps. Just before zipping the suitcase, you push down on every item, slide the shoes against the jeans, and tuck the socks into every crevice until the suitcase is as small as physically possible — without ripping the clothes or breaking the zipper.

**Layout Compaction is exactly this process, but applied to silicon:**
- **The Suitcase** = The silicon die area (which costs real money per mm²).
- **The Clothes** = Transistors, contacts, and metal wires.
- **The Empty Pockets** = Unused whitespace between features.
- **The Zipper Limit** = Fabrication **Design Rules** (minimum spacing, width, and enclosure rules from the foundry).
- **You (the packer)** = The **Compaction Algorithm** running inside the EDA tool.

Just as you cannot crush the clothes beyond the point where they tear, the compactor cannot push two transistors closer than the **minimum spacing rule** specified by the semiconductor fab (e.g., 90nm node, 45nm node, etc.).

## 1.3 Why Compaction Exists — The Driving Forces

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Three engineering realities force every chip designer to use compaction:
>
> 1. **Cost Pressure:** Silicon area directly translates to cost per die. Reducing area by even **5–10%** can save millions of dollars in high-volume fabrication.
> 2. **Performance Pressure:** Shorter wires mean **lower parasitic capacitance**, which improves switching speed and reduces dynamic power dissipation ($P \propto C V^2 f$).
> 3. **Yield Pressure:** Smaller, denser layouts reduce the probability of random particle defects killing the die, directly improving manufacturing yield.

## 1.4 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Constraint Graph** | A directed acyclic graph where each node is a layout feature and each edge carries a minimum distance label $d_{ij}$. |
| **Critical Path** | The longest path in the constraint graph, which dictates the minimum achievable layout dimension. |
| **DRC** | Design Rule Checking — the verification of fab-process constraints (min width, min spacing, min area). |
| **Compaction Ratio** | The fractional area reduction achieved, typically $0.70$ to $0.90$ for production-quality layouts. |
| **Pitch** | The repetitive spacing unit of standard-cell rows, often the limiting factor in compaction. |
| **Symbolic Layout** | An abstract, grid-based representation of the circuit that pre-compaction tools operate on. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Architectural Variants of Compaction

### A. 1-Dimensional (1D) Compaction
In 1D compaction, features are slid along **only one axis at a time** (typically X first, then Y, or vice-versa). This decouples the optimization problem into two independent longest-path problems.

- **Advantage:** Polynomial-time solvable, deterministic, and guarantees convergence.
- **Disadvantage:** Sub-optimal because vertical movements in the X-pass and horizontal movements in the Y-pass are not co-optimized.

### B. 2-Dimensional (2D) Compaction
In 2D compaction, every feature is permitted to move in **both X and Y simultaneously**, while still respecting constraints. This is mathematically equivalent to a network flow / integer linear programming problem.

- **Advantage:** Achieves 10–25% extra area reduction over 1D.
- **Disadvantage:** **NP-hard** in the general case; requires heuristic solvers (simulated annealing, genetic algorithms).

## 2.2 The Constraint Graph Construction Logic

The construction proceeds in structured steps:

1. **Node Creation:** Each layout feature (rectangle corner, via, pin) becomes a graph node with its own coordinate variable.
2. **Edge Insertion (Geometric Constraints):** For every pair of features that are constrained by a design rule (e.g., poly-to-diffusion spacing), insert a directed edge labeled with the minimum distance $d_{ij}$.
3. **Edge Insertion (Connectivity Constraints):** For every wire that electrically connects two pins, insert an edge with label **0** so that the connected pins move together.
4. **Source and Sink Nodes:** Add a virtual source $x_s = 0$ and a virtual sink $x_t$, and compute the longest path from source to every node.

## 2.3 The Longest-Path Algorithm (Bellman-Ford / DAG Critical Path)

Because the design-rule constraint graph is a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** with non-negative edge weights, the optimal X-coordinate of every node is given by the length of the longest path from the source. This is computable in linear time $\mathcal{O}(V + E)$ via topological sort.

For each node $v$ in topological order:
$$x_v = \max_{(u \to v) \in E} \left( x_u + w(u,v) \right)$$

where $w(u,v)$ is the edge weight equal to the minimum spacing $d_{uv}$ between features $u$ and $v$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the exam-ready cheat sheet. Memorize the symbolic form, the meaning, and the units.

| Equation ID | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|-------------|---------|---------|---------------|
| F-1 | $x_j - x_i \ge d_{ij}$ | Geometric spacing constraint between features $i$ and $j$ | nanometers (nm) |
| F-2 | $x_v = \max_{u \in pred(v)} \left( x_u + w_{uv} \right)$ | Longest-path assignment for node coordinate | nm |
| F-3 | $A_{compacted} = (X_{max} - X_{min}) \times (Y_{max} - Y_{min})$ | Final compacted die area | $\text{mm}^2$ |
| F-4 | $\eta_{compaction} = 1 - \dfrac{A_{compacted}}{A_{original}}$ | Compaction efficiency (fractional area reduction) | dimensionless |
| F-5 | $C_{wire} = c_0 \cdot L_{wire}$ | Parasitic wire capacitance (impacts delay/power) | femtofarads (fF) |
| F-6 | $P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_{load} \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$ | Dynamic power dissipation after compaction | microwatts ($\mu$W) |
| F-7 | $t_{delay} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{wire} \cdot C_{wire}$ | RC propagation delay (Elmore approximation) | picoseconds (ps) |
| F-8 | $Y = e^{-D_0 \cdot A_{die}}$ | Yield model (Poisson defect distribution) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> The symbol `\vert` has been deliberately substituted in place of the vertical bar in tabular formulas above (F-8) to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The longest-path formulation for compaction is **not an academic curiosity**. It is the operational backbone of:
- **Cadence Virtuoso Layout Synthesizer**
- **Synopsys IC Compiler (post-route optimization pass)**
- **Mentor Graphics Calibre nmDRC (interactive compaction mode)**

Every time a chip company like Intel, TSMC, or Samsung ships a 3nm or 5nm chip, compaction runs in the dark hours of the tape-out cycle to squeeze the last few percent of area and yield out of the design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation — Computing Coordinates via the Longest-Path Algorithm

Consider a simple symbolic layout with four features A, B, C, D aligned along the X-axis with the following minimum spacing constraints (all in units of the technology grid, e.g., $\lambda = 45\,\text{nm}$):

- Feature A is connected to Feature C (electrical net, distance = $0$).
- Feature A must be at least $3\lambda$ left of Feature B.
- Feature B must be at least $2\lambda$ left of Feature D.
- Feature C must be at least $4\lambda$ left of Feature D.

**Step 1 — Identify Nodes and Edges:**
Nodes: $\{A, B, C, D\}$, with a virtual source node $S$ where $x_S = 0$.

**Step 2 — Build the Constraint Edge List:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Edge 1:} \quad A \to B, \quad w = 3\lambda \\
&\text{Edge 2:} \quad A \to C, \quad w = 0 \quad (\text{electrical connection}) \\
&\text{Edge 3:} \quad B \to D, \quad w = 2\lambda \\
&\text{Edge 4:} \quad C \to D, \quad w = 4\lambda \\
&\text{Edge 5:} \quad S \to A, \quad w = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Topological Sort:**
The DAG topological order is: $S \to A \to \{B, C\} \to D$.

**Step 4 — Longest-Path Coordinate Assignment:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_S &= 0 \\
x_A &= x_S + 0 = 0 \\
x_B &= x_A + 3\lambda = 3\lambda \\
x_C &= x_A + 0 = 0 \quad (\text{connected to A}) \\
x_D &= \max(x_B + 2\lambda,\; x_C + 4\lambda) = \max(5\lambda,\; 4\lambda) = 5\lambda
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Compaction Span:**
$X_{span} = x_D - x_S = 5\lambda$. If $\lambda = 45\,\text{nm}$, then the compacted width is $5 \times 45 = 225\,\text{nm}$.

> [!NOTE]
> The critical path was $S \to A \to B \to D$, not $S \to A \to C \to D$. This is precisely why the longest-path algorithm (not the shortest-path) is required — we must respect the **most demanding** constraint chain.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Yield Gain From Compaction

**Given:**
- Original layout area: $A_0 = 4.00\,\text{mm}^2$.
- Post-compaction area: $A_c = 3.52\,\text{mm}^2$.
- Defect density: $D_0 = 0.5\,\text{defects/cm}^2$.

**Find:** (a) Compaction efficiency. (b) Yield improvement.

**Step (a) — Compaction Efficiency $\eta$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{compaction} &= 1 - \frac{A_c}{A_0} \\
&= 1 - \frac{3.52}{4.00} \\
&= 1 - 0.88 \\
&= 0.12
\end{aligned}
$$

So compaction reduced area by **12%**.

**Step (b) — Yield Improvement:**

Original yield:
$$
Y_0 = e^{-D_0 \cdot A_0} = e^{-0.5 \times 4.00} = e^{-2.0} \approx 0.1353
$$

Compacted yield:
$$
Y_c = e^{-D_0 \cdot A_c} = e^{-0.5 \times 3.52} = e^{-1.76} \approx 0.1720
$$

Relative yield gain:
$$
\Delta Y = \frac{Y_c - Y_0}{Y_0} \times 100\% = \frac{0.1720 - 0.1353}{0.1353} \times 100\% \approx 27.1\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **12%** area reduction yielded a **27.1%** yield improvement — illustrating the non-linear (exponential) sensitivity of yield to die area.

## 3.3 Analytical Trade-off Matrix

| Compaction Intensity | Area Reduction | Wire Delay | Risk of DRC Violation | Mask Cost |
|---------------------|----------------|------------|----------------------|-----------|
| Light (initial pass) | 5–10% | Modest gain | Very low | Low |
| Moderate (standard EDA) | 10–20% | Noticeable gain | Low (clean DRC) | Medium |
| Aggressive (2D heuristic) | 20–35% | High gain | Medium | High |
| Over-compaction (illegal) | >35% | Theoretically max | Severe — breaks fab rules | Not manufacturable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Compaction Pipeline Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Symbolic Layout Input] --> nodeB[Constraint Extraction Module]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Connectivity Graph Builder]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Design Rule Parser]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Constraint Graph DAG]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF[Longest Path Solver]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Coordinate Assignment Table]
    nodeG --> nodeH[DRC Verification Pass]
    nodeH --> nodeI{DRC Clean?}
    nodeI -- Yes --> nodeJ[Final Mask Layout Output]
    nodeI -- No --> nodeK[Constraint Relaxation Loop]
    nodeK --> nodeF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The flow moves left-to-right. The right-side feedback loop (nodeK) is critical — it represents the iterative nature of industrial-grade compaction where DRC violations trigger constraint refinement rather than scrapping the design.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 2D Compaction Engine

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph inputStage["INPUT STAGE"]
        node1[Symbolic Layout Database]
        node2[Fab Process Design Rules]
        node3[Netlist Connectivity]
    end

    subgraph coreEngine["COMPACTION CORE"]
        node4[Constraint Graph Constructor]
        node5[ILP Formulation Module]
        node6[Longest Path Solver X axis]
        node7[Longest Path Solver Y axis]
        node8[Conflict Resolution Unit]
    end

    subgraph outputStage["OUTPUT STAGE"]
        node9[Compacted Geometry]
        node10[DRC Report]
        node11[Yield and Timing Estimator]
    end

    node1 --> node4
    node2 --> node4
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node5 --> node7
    node6 --> node8
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9
    node8 --> node10
    node9 --> node11
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Before/After Visualization

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph beforeCompaction["BEFORE COMPACTION"]
        nodeB1[Transistor A]
        nodeB2[Empty Whitespace]
        nodeB3[Wire Segment]
        nodeB4[Empty Whitespace]
        nodeB5[Transistor B]
        nodeB1 --- nodeB2
        nodeB2 --- nodeB3
        nodeB3 --- nodeB4
        nodeB4 --- nodeB5
    end

    subgraph afterCompaction["AFTER COMPACTION"]
        nodeA1[Transistor A]
        nodeA2[Wire Segment]
        nodeA3[Transistor B]
        nodeA1 --- nodeA2
        nodeA2 --- nodeA3
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Q: Define layout compaction. Why is it considered a necessary step in the physical design flow of VLSI circuits?**

**Model Answer:**
Layout compaction is the process of reducing the geometric dimensions (area) of a VLSI layout by systematically translating circuit features closer to one another, while strictly preserving all design-rule constraints (minimum spacing, width, enclosure) and electrical connectivity.

It is necessary because:
1. **Cost:** Every mm² of silicon costs money; reducing die area directly cuts wafer cost.
2. **Performance:** Shorter wires reduce parasitic resistance and capacitance, improving switching speed.
3. **Yield:** Smaller dies have a lower probability of containing a fatal random defect, raising manufacturing yield.
4. **Mask Economy:** Compact layouts enable more die per wafer, improving fab throughput.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Q: Distinguish between 1D and 2D compaction. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | 1D Compaction | 2D Compaction |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| Direction | One axis at a time (X or Y) | Both X and Y simultaneously |
| Algorithmic Complexity | Polynomial, deterministic | NP-hard (heuristic) |
| Area Reduction | Moderate (10–20%) | Higher (20–35%) |
| Advantage | Guaranteed convergence, fast runtime | Better area optimization |
| Disadvantage | Sub-optimal final area | Long runtime, may not converge |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
**Q: (a)** Explain the constraint-graph formulation of 1D compaction in detail. Construct the constraint graph for a symbolic layout with the following minimum spacing rules and compute the optimal X-coordinates using the longest-path algorithm. **(7 Marks)**
- Features: $P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$.
- Spacing: $P_1 \to P_2$ needs $2\lambda$, $P_2 \to P_3$ needs $3\lambda$, $P_1 \to P_3$ needs $0\lambda$ (connected), $P_3 \to P_4$ needs $1\lambda$.

**(b)** Describe four real-world applications of compaction in modern VLSI design with engineering justification. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Step 1 — Build the edge list:
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Source } S \to P_1: w = 0 \\
&P_1 \to P_2: w = 2\lambda \\
&P_1 \to P_3: w = 0 \quad (\text{electrical net}) \\
&P_2 \to P_3: w = 3\lambda \\
&P_3 \to P_4: w = 1\lambda
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Topological sort yields: $S, P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$.

Step 3 — Longest path computation:
$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{P_1} &= 0 \\
x_{P_2} &= 0 + 2\lambda = 2\lambda \\
x_{P_3} &= \max(x_{P_1} + 0,\; x_{P_2} + 3\lambda) = \max(0,\; 5\lambda) = 5\lambda \\
x_{P_4} &= x_{P_3} + 1\lambda = 6\lambda
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Final span: $X_{span} = 6\lambda$.

**Model Solution for (b) — Four Real-World Applications:**

1. **Silicon Area Reduction & Cost Optimization:** Compaction reduces die size, allowing more dies per wafer. For a 300mm wafer, even a 10% area reduction can yield 20–25% more working chips.

2. **Performance Enhancement via Wire-Length Minimization:** Shorter interconnects reduce RC delay. Per Equation F-7, $t_{delay} \propto R_{wire} C_{wire} \propto L^2$, so halving wire length yields roughly $4\times$ speedup on long routes.

3. **Yield Improvement:** Per Equation F-8, yield is exponentially sensitive to die area. Compaction directly improves the binomial/Poisson yield model.

4. **Power Reduction:** Per Equation F-6, $P_{dynamic} \propto C_{load}$. Shorter wires mean lower capacitance, which reduces dynamic power — critical for mobile and IoT chips.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete pipeline of a 2D compaction engine. Mention the role of the ILP (Integer Linear Programming) formulation module. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A layout has original area $A_0 = 5.0\,\text{mm}^2$. After compaction, area becomes $A_c = 4.2\,\text{mm}^2$. If the defect density $D_0 = 0.4\,\text{defects/cm}^2$, compute (i) compaction efficiency, (ii) original yield, (iii) compacted yield, (iv) absolute yield improvement. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The 2D compaction engine pipeline consists of:
1. **Input Stage:** Receives symbolic layout, fab design rules, and netlist connectivity.
2. **Constraint Graph Constructor:** Builds a unified graph containing both geometric and electrical edges.
3. **ILP Formulation Module:** Converts the CSP into an Integer Linear Program:
   - Objective: minimize total span in both dimensions.
   - Variables: integer coordinates $x_i, y_i$ for every feature.
   - Constraints: $x_j - x_i \ge d_{ij}^{x}$ and $y_j - y_i \ge d_{ij}^{y}$.
   The ILP module is the mathematical heart that allows simultaneous X and Y optimization.
4. **Solver:** Branches on X first, then Y, propagating constraints back via a conflict resolution unit.
5. **Output Stage:** Generates compacted geometry, runs DRC, and reports yield/timing estimates.

**Model Solution for (b):**

(i) Compaction efficiency:
$$
\eta = 1 - \frac{4.2}{5.0} = 0.16 \quad \text{or} \quad 16\%
$$

(ii) Original yield (noting $A_0 = 5.0\,\text{mm}^2 = 0.05\,\text{cm}^2$):
$$
Y_0 = e^{-0.4 \times 0.05} = e^{-0.02} \approx 0.9802
$$

(iii) Compacted yield:
$$
Y_c = e^{-0.4 \times 0.042} = e^{-0.0168} \approx 0.9833
$$

(iv) Absolute yield improvement:
$$
\Delta Y = Y_c - Y_0 = 0.9833 - 0.9802 = 0.0031 \approx 0.31\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Unit Conversion Trap (Part b):** Students frequently forget to convert $\text{mm}^2 \to \text{cm}^2$ when using $D_0$ in $\text{cm}^{-2}$. One square millimeter is $0.01\,\text{cm}^2$. Always write out the unit conversion explicitly to earn the working-step marks.
> - **Longest-Path vs Shortest-Path (Part a):** Students often use Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm by mistake. The compaction problem requires the **longest path**, because every constraint chain must satisfy its **maximum** demand. Writing the wrong algorithm costs 2 marks.
> - **Connectivity Edges (Part a):** Forgetting that electrically connected pins must have edge weight **zero** (so they move together) is the most common 1-mark loss.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam Day:**
>
> - Compaction = Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP) solved on a DAG.
> - Core inequality: $x_j - x_i \ge d_{ij}$ (Equation F-1).
> - Solve via **longest path** from a virtual source (NOT shortest path).
> - Time complexity is linear: $\mathcal{O}(V + E)$.
> - 1D compaction is polynomial; 2D compaction is NP-hard.
> - Main engineering benefits: area reduction, yield gain, speed boost, power drop.
> - Yield follows $Y = e^{-D_0 \cdot A}$ — exponential sensitivity to area.
> - Dynamic power $P \propto C V^2 f$ — shorter wires mean lower $C$.
> - RC delay scales as $L^2$ for long interconnects — compaction directly helps timing closure.
> - Always convert $\text{mm}^2 \to \text{cm}^2$ before plugging into yield formulas.
> - Connectivity edges have weight $0$; design-rule edges have weight = minimum spacing.
> - Modern EDA tools (Cadence, Synopsys, Mentor) run compaction in the final mask-prep stage.
> - Over-compaction breaks DRC and renders the chip unmanufacturable.
> - Critical path in the constraint graph dictates the minimum achievable layout dimension.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Informal Problem Formulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT525 - CAD BASED VLSI DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Informal Problem Formulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Informal Problem Formulation** is the preliminary, descriptive, and non-mathematical stage of defining a VLSI design optimization problem where the designer qualitatively identifies and articulates the **design variables**, **design objectives**, **performance constraints**, and the **design space** of interest, prior to constructing a rigorous mathematical optimization model.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Course Outcome CO3 of PEEVT525), this topic forms the conceptual bridge between a raw engineering design requirement (e.g., "design a low-power 32-bit adder") and its formal mathematical representation as an optimization problem (e.g., a constrained non-linear program).

The four canonical pillars of informal problem formulation in VLSI CAD are:

| Pillar | Description | VLSI Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Design Variables** | Free parameters the designer can manipulate | Transistor width ($W$), length ($L$), supply voltage ($V_{DD}$), placement coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$ |
| **Design Objectives** | Quantitative goals to be minimized or maximized | Power dissipation, propagation delay, silicon area |
| **Constraints** | Hard physical, electrical, or performance limits | Timing slack $\geq 0$, maximum area budget, DRC rules |
| **Performance Metrics** | Measurable figures of merit | PDP (Power-Delay Product), EDP (Energy-Delay Product), FoM (Figure of Merit) |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cooking Recipe" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Cooking a Biryani for 50 Guests**
>
> Imagine you must cook a perfect biryani for 50 guests, but you have never written a formal recipe. Before you start, you informally decide:
> - **Variables:** How much rice? How much spice? Which cooking method?
> - **Objectives:** Make it taste amazing (maximize taste), serve all 50 (maximize quantity).
> - **Constraints:** Budget = ₹2000, time = 2 hours, stove has only 3 burners.
> - **Metrics:** Cost per plate, spice level rating, leftover quantity.
>
> This **informal thinking** (no equations, no numbers pinned down yet) is exactly what "Informal Problem Formulation" means in VLSI design. You are *thinking through* the problem before you commit to a formal mathematical model.

In VLSI, before any optimizer (e.g., a gate-sizer, placer, or router) is invoked, the human designer or CAD engineer must perform this informal framing to ensure the optimizer receives a **well-posed problem**.

### 1.3 Why Informal Formulation is Critical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Without a correctly formulated informal problem, the subsequent mathematical formulation will lead to *spurious optimal solutions* — designs that are mathematically optimal but practically meaningless or infeasible in silicon.

A poorly formulated VLSI optimization problem may suffer from:
- **Conflicting objectives** not explicitly declared (e.g., minimize power vs. maximize speed).
- **Hidden constraints** (e.g., thermal limits, electromigration) not modeled.
- **Disconnected variables** that the optimizer cannot actually tune.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Silicon resistivity** ($\rho_{Si} \approx 1 - 100 \text{ }\Omega\cdot\text{cm}$ — process dependent).
- **Dielectric constant of SiO₂**: $\kappa \approx 3.9$.
- **Electron mobility**: $\mu_n \approx 1350 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V·s}$ (NMOS, lightly doped).
- **Hole mobility**: $\mu_p \approx 480 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V·s}$ (PMOS, lightly doped).
- **Supply voltage range for modern nodes**: $V_{DD} \in [0.6 \text{ V}, 1.2 \text{ V}]$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Step Informal Problem Formulation Framework

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU board examiner expects students to demonstrate mastery over the **five logical steps** below when answering 14-mark design questions on this topic.

**Step 1 — Identify the Design Context and Hierarchy Level**
Determine *which level* of the VLSI design hierarchy the problem operates at:
- **System level** (architecture, partitioning)
- **Logic / RTL level** (gate sizing, logic synthesis)
- **Circuit level** (transistor sizing, biasing)
- **Physical level** (placement, routing, floorplanning)

The choice of level determines what variables and objectives are meaningful.

**Step 2 — Enumerate the Candidate Design Variables**
Variables are the *knobs* the optimizer is allowed to turn. Categorize them:
- **Continuous variables**: $W, L, V_{DD}, V_{th}$.
- **Discrete variables**: layer assignments, standard cell selection.
- **Topological variables**: connectivity, netlist structure.

**Step 3 — Declare the Objective Function(s)**
Decide whether the problem is:
- **Single-objective** (one goal — e.g., minimize delay only).
- **Multi-objective** (several competing goals — e.g., minimize power AND maximize speed).
- **Weighted aggregate** (combine multiple goals into a single scalar via weighting).

**Step 4 — List the Hard and Soft Constraints**
- **Hard constraints** (must satisfy): DRC rules, timing closure, max area.
- **Soft constraints** (preferred but relaxable): preferred aspect ratio, symmetry.

**Step 5 — Define the Design Space and Stopping Criteria**
The *design space* is the Cartesian product of all variable domains. The optimizer searches inside this space. Stopping criteria may include maximum iterations, convergence tolerance, or wall-clock time.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **core high-yield reference** for all derivation and numerical questions on this topic. Memorize the symbolic structure — KTU examiners reward students who write formulas in this exact disciplined format.

| # | Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Generic Optimization Problem | $\min_{x \in \mathcal{X}} f(x)$ subject to $g_i(x) \leq 0, \ h_j(x) = 0$ | Universal form; $f$ is objective, $g_i$ are inequality constraints, $h_j$ are equality constraints | — |
| 2 | Design Vector | $x = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | All tunable parameters stacked as a column | — |
| 3 | Design Space | $\mathcal{X} = \mathcal{D}_1 \times \mathcal{D}_2 \times \cdots \times \mathcal{D}_n$ | Set of all admissible designs (Cartesian product of domains) | — |
| 4 | Multi-Objective Aggregation (Weighted Sum) | $F(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k f_k(x), \quad \sum w_k = 1$ | Scalarizes multiple objectives into one scalar | — |
| 5 | Transistor Sizing Power (Dynamic) | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$ | Power to be minimized in low-power designs | W (watts) |
| 6 | Propagation Delay (Elmore approx.) | $t_d = 0.69 R_{eq} C_L$ | Delay to be minimized in high-speed paths | s (seconds) |
| 7 | Power-Delay Product (PDP) | $PDP = P_{dyn} \cdot t_d$ | Combined energy metric for a switching event | J (joules) |
| 8 | Energy-Delay Product (EDP) | $EDP = PDP \cdot t_d = P_{dyn} \cdot t_d^2$ | Trade-off metric favoring speed without energy bloat | J·s |
| 9 | Area Constraint (bounding box) | $A_{total} = W_{chip} \times H_{chip} \leq A_{budget}$ | Hard constraint from die-size economics | m² |
| 10 | Timing Slack | $slack_i = T_{required,i} - T_{actual,i} \geq 0$ | Hard constraint — negative slack violates timing closure | s |
| 11 | Process Variation Bound (3-sigma) | $\vert P_{actual} - P_{nominal} \vert \leq 3\sigma_P$ | Yield-related soft constraint | — |
| 12 | Figure of Merit (FoM) | $FoM = \frac{1}{PDP \cdot A \cdot \sigma_{delay}}$ | Composite metric often used in analog sizing | — |

> **Note on notation:** In the table above, the absolute-value / magnitude notation is written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ using `\vert` so the markdown table parser is not disrupted.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

Informal problem formulation is the foundation for every commercial EDA tool:
- **Synopsys Design Compiler** uses formulation inputs (timing libraries, area constraints) generated by informal problem framing.
- **Cadence Innovus** placement engine requires the designer to declare congestion vs. timing trade-offs informally before invoking the optimizer.
- **Magic / VLSI placement tools** used in academic labs rely on correctly declared design spaces.

Without a clean informal formulation, even the most sophisticated optimizer (simulated annealing, convex programming, gradient descent) will return a useless answer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Transition: From Informal Statement to Formal Mathematical Model

The central derivation of this module is showing **how** an informal verbal design statement is converted into a rigorous optimization problem. We will work through this transition step-by-step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** *Informal Statement:* "Design a CMOS inverter chain to drive a large load capacitance $C_L = 5$ pF with the minimum possible delay, using a fixed number of stages and supply voltage."

**Step 1 — Identify the Design Context**
This is a **circuit-level** optimization problem (transistor sizing within an inverter chain). The hierarchy level is the *logical effort* domain.

**Step 2 — Enumerate the Design Variables**
The variables are the **stage sizing ratios** $\gamma_i = C_{g,i+1}/C_{g,i}$ for each of the $N$ stages. Since the total ratio is fixed:

$$
\gamma = \left(\frac{C_L}{C_{g,1}}\right)^{1/N}
$$

we have only one *effective* design variable per stage, namely the gate capacitance $C_{g,i}$.

**Step 3 — Declare the Objective Function**
The objective is to **minimize the total propagation delay**:

$$
f(x) = t_d(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} 0.69 \cdot R_i \cdot (C_{g,i+1} + C_{wire,i})
$$

where $R_i$ is the output resistance of stage $i$ and $C_{wire,i}$ is the parasitic wiring capacitance.

**Step 4 — List the Constraints**
- Total stage ratio constraint: $\prod_{i=1}^{N} \gamma_i = C_L / C_{g,1}$.
- Minimum transistor size: $C_{g,i} \geq C_{g,min}$ (process-limited).
- Maximum transistor size: $C_{g,i} \leq C_{g,max}$ (area-limited).

$$
g_1(x) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \gamma_i - \frac{C_L}{C_{g,1}} = 0
$$

$$
g_2(x) = C_{g,min} - C_{g,i} \leq 0 \quad \forall i
$$

$$
g_3(x) = C_{g,i} - C_{g,max} \leq 0 \quad \forall i
$$

**Step 5 — Define the Design Space**
Each $C_{g,i} \in [C_{g,min}, C_{g,max}]$, and the design space is:

$$
\mathcal{X} = [C_{g,min}, C_{g,max}]^N
$$

**Final Formal Model:**

$$
\min_{x \in \mathcal{X}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} 0.69 \cdot R_i \cdot (C_{g,i+1} + C_{wire,i})
$$

subject to the three constraint groups above. This is the **formal problem formulation** derived from the **informal** description.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Single-Stage Optimization of a CMOS Inverter

Consider an informal problem: *"Minimize the delay of a single CMOS inverter driving load $C_L$, varying only the transistor width ratio $W_n/W_p$."*

**Step 1 — Identify the variables**

$$
x = \frac{W_n}{W_p}, \quad x \in [0.5, 4.0]
$$

**Step 2 — Objective function (delay minimization)**

The delay of a CMOS inverter under the Elmore model is:

$$
t_d(x) = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq}(x) \cdot C_L
$$

The equivalent resistance depends on which transistor is slower. For a falling transition (NMOS discharges), and assuming $R_n \propto 1/W_n$, $R_p \propto 1/W_p$:

$$
R_{eq,fall} = R_n = \frac{K_n}{W_n}
$$

where $K_n$ is the process transconductance parameter for NMOS. Setting $W_p = W_n / x$, we obtain:

$$
R_{eq,fall}(x) = \frac{K_n}{W_n} \quad \text{(independent of } x)
$$

For the rising transition (PMOS charges):

$$
R_{eq,rise}(x) = \frac{K_p}{W_p} = \frac{K_p \cdot x}{W_n}
$$

The **symmetric delay** (averaged over both edges) becomes:

$$
t_d(x) = 0.69 \cdot \frac{W_n}{2} \left( \frac{K_n}{W_n} + \frac{K_p \cdot x}{W_n} \right) \cdot C_L
$$

Simplifying:

$$
t_d(x) = 0.345 \cdot C_L \cdot (K_n + K_p \cdot x)
$$

**Step 3 — Constrained optimization**

For typical CMOS, $K_p \approx 2.5 K_n$ (since hole mobility is lower). The unconstrained minimum of $t_d(x)$ over $x \in [0.5, 4.0]$ occurs at $x = 0.5$ (the lower bound), but this degrades rise time significantly.

To balance rise and fall times, we impose the **symmetry constraint**:

$$
R_{eq,fall} = R_{eq,rise} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_n = K_p \cdot x
$$

Solving for $x$:

$$
x^* = \frac{K_n}{K_p}
$$

Substituting $K_p \approx 2.5 K_n$:

$$
x^* = \frac{K_n}{2.5 K_n} = 0.4
$$

Since this is outside the lower bound $x = 0.5$, the **constrained optimum** is at the boundary $x^* = 0.5$.

**Step 4 — Final informal problem statement, now quantified:**

> *"Find $W_n, W_p$ such that the average delay of a CMOS inverter driving $C_L = 1$ pF is minimized, subject to:*
> *- Symmetry of rise/fall times,*
> *- $W_n/W_p \in [0.5, 4.0]$,*
> *- Total width budget $W_n + W_p \leq 10 \text{ }\mu m$."*

This is the **complete informal problem formulation**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Informal Elements to Formal Math

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This mapping matrix is the single most exam-relevant artifact of Module 3.** Examiners frequently ask students to "convert the following informal problem into a formal optimization statement." Memorize this table.

| Informal Element | Formal Symbol | Mathematical Role |
|---|---|---|
| "Minimize the delay" | $f(x) = t_d(x)$ | Objective function |
| "Vary the widths" | $x = [W_n, W_p]^T$ | Design vector |
| "Width budget" | $W_n + W_p \leq W_{max}$ | Inequality constraint |
| "Symmetric rise/fall" | $t_{rise} = t_{fall}$ | Equality constraint |
| "Width ratio limit" | $0.5 \leq W_n/W_p \leq 4.0$ | Bound on design space |
| "Don't exceed $C_L$" | $C_L \leq C_{max}$ | Hard constraint |
| "Try to keep area low" | $A(x) \leq A_{budget}$ | Soft (preferred) constraint |
| "PDP should be low" | $PDP \leq \epsilon$ | Performance bound |

### 3.4 Multi-Objective Formulation: Pareto Trade-off Derivation

When the informal problem lists **two competing objectives** (e.g., delay *and* power), the formal formulation becomes a **multi-objective optimization**:

$$
\min_{x \in \mathcal{X}} \left[ f_1(x), f_2(x) \right]^T
$$

Using the **weighted-sum scalarization**:

$$
F(x; w) = w_1 f_1(x) + w_2 f_2(x), \quad w_1 + w_2 = 1
$$

The Pareto front is obtained by sweeping $w_1 \in [0, 1]$ and recording the optimal $x$ for each weight combination.

For a transistor-sizing problem with $f_1 = P_{dyn}$ and $f_2 = t_d$:

$$
F(x; w) = w_1 \cdot \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk} + w_2 \cdot 0.69 R_{eq}(x) C_L
$$

The optimal transistor widths as a function of $w_1$ trace out the **Pareto-optimal curve**, illustrating the inherent speed-power trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Five-Stage Informal Formulation Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block renders the canonical five-step workflow every KTU student should be able to sketch for full marks in a 14-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Raw Engineering Need] --> B[Step 1: Identify Design Context and Hierarchy Level]
    B --> C[Step 2: Enumerate Design Variables]
    C --> D[Step 3: Declare Objective Functions]
    D --> E[Step 4: List Hard and Soft Constraints]
    E --> F[Step 5: Define Design Space and Stopping Criteria]
    F --> G{Is Problem Well Posed?}
    G -- No --> H[Refine Variables and Constraints]
    H --> B
    G -- Yes --> I[Formal Mathematical Formulation]
    I --> J[Solve via Optimizer]
    J --> K[Post Layout Verification]
    K --> L[End: Validated VLSI Design]

    nodeA[Start]:::startNode
    nodeL[End]:::endNode

    classDef startNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef endNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Formulation Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Informal Problem Inputs]
        A1[Verbal Design Brief]
        A2[Process Design Kit PDK]
        A3[Library Characterization Data]
    end

    subgraph FORM[Formulation Engine]
        B1[Variable Extractor]
        B2[Objective Declarator]
        B3[Constraint Classifier: Hard vs Soft]
        B4[Design Space Constructor]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Formal Output]
        C1[Design Vector x]
        C2[Objective Function f x]
        C3[Constraint Set G H]
        C4[Design Space Script Chi]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C2
    B1 --> B3
    B3 --> C3
    B1 --> B4
    B4 --> C4
    C1 --> OUT[Ready for Optimizer Invocation]
    C2 --> OUT
    C3 --> OUT
    C4 --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing the Optimization Class

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Informal Problem Statement Received] --> B{Number of Objectives?}
    B -- One --> C[Single Objective Optimization]
    B -- Many --> D[Multi Objective Optimization]
    C --> E{Variables Continuous or Discrete?}
    D --> F{Variables Continuous or Discrete?}
    E -- Continuous --> G[Convex or Gradient Based NLP]
    E -- Discrete --> H[Mixed Integer Programming or Simulated Annealing]
    F -- Continuous --> I[Weighted Sum or Pareto Front Sweep]
    F -- Discrete --> J[Evolutionary Algorithms: GA NSGA II]
    G --> K[Solution: Optimal x star]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

### 4.4 Informal-to-Formal Mapping Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INFORMAL[Informal Verbal Layer]
        I1[Minimize Delay]
        I2[Vary Widths]
        I3[Stay Within Area Budget]
    end

    subgraph FORMAL[Formal Mathematical Layer]
        F1[f of x equals t d of x]
        F2[x equals vector Wn Wp]
        F3[Constraint: Wn plus Wp leq Wmax]
    end

    subgraph OPTIMIZER[Optimizer Layer]
        O1[Invoke Simulated Annealing or Gradient Descent]
    end

    I1 --> F1
    I2 --> F2
    I3 --> F3
    F1 --> O1
    F2 --> O1
    F3 --> O1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
> *Define "Informal Problem Formulation" in the context of VLSI design optimization. List its four essential elements.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Informal Problem Formulation is the qualitative, non-mathematical preliminary stage of an optimization task where the engineer articulates *what* must be optimized, *which parameters* may be varied, and *what limits* must be respected, before writing any equations.

Four essential elements:
1. **Design Variables** — free parameters (e.g., transistor widths).
2. **Design Objectives** — goals to minimize or maximize (e.g., delay).
3. **Design Constraints** — physical/performance limits (e.g., area budget).
4. **Design Space** — set of all admissible variable combinations.

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**  
> *Differentiate between hard and soft constraints with two VLSI examples each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Hard constraints** *must* be strictly satisfied; violation means the design is invalid. *Examples:* DRC minimum width rule (e.g., $W \geq 0.1 \text{ }\mu m$), timing slack $\geq 0$.
- **Soft constraints** are preferred but relaxable; the optimizer tries to satisfy them but may trade them off against the objective. *Examples:* preferred aspect ratio = 1.0, symmetry between P and N trees, target PDP $\leq 50$ fJ.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Note on KTU pattern:** Each Part B question carries two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Both choices A and B below are *complete alternative questions* (not sub-parts of the same Q).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

> *"You are tasked with sizing the transistors of a 4-stage CMOS buffer chain driving a load capacitance of 2 pF. The informal design brief states: minimize total delay, allow up to 50 µm total transistor width, and keep the width of each stage within $[1, 20] \text{ }\mu m$. The process offers: $K_n = 50 \text{ }\mu m \cdot \Omega^{-1}$ and $K_p = 25 \text{ }\mu m \cdot \Omega^{-1}$."*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Formally state the optimization problem. Identify the design vector, objective function, hard constraints, soft constraints, and the design space.

**Model Solution:**

- **Design Vector:** $x = [W_1, W_2, W_3, W_4]^T$, where $W_i$ is the effective width of the $i$-th inverter stage (NMOS width; PMOS width = $2.5 W_i$ for symmetric rise/fall).
- **Objective Function:** Minimize total delay. Using the logical-effort approximation:

$$
