### 4.1 Combinational Logic Circuit — Schematic Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["A : Primary Input"] -->|delay 3| nodeC["C : AND Gate"]
    nodeA -->|delay 2| nodeD["D : OR Gate"]
    nodeB["B : Primary Input"] -->|delay 4| nodeC
    nodeB -->|delay 1| nodeD
    nodeC -->|delay 2| nodeE["E : Primary Output"]
    nodeD -->|delay 5| nodeE
```

> **Figure 1:** Gate-level DAG representation. Edge labels denote intrinsic gate propagation delays in nanoseconds.

### 4.2 Algorithm Flow — Longest Path on DAG

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Start: Read DAG G = V,E"] --> stepB["Compute Indegree of all vertices"]
    stepB --> stepC{"Indegree equals 0?"}
    stepC -->|Yes| stepD["Enqueue vertex to TopoQueue"]
    stepC -->|No| stepE["Wait for predecessor processing"]
    stepE --> stepC
    stepD --> stepF{"Queue empty?"}
    stepF -->|No| stepG["Dequeue vertex u"]
    stepG --> stepH["For each out-edge u to v"]
    stepH --> stepI["dist v = max dist v, dist u + w u v"]
    stepI --> stepJ["Update predecessor v = u"]
    stepJ --> stepK["Decrement indegree v"]
    stepK --> stepL["Re-check indegree v equals 0"]
    stepL --> stepC
    stepF -->|Yes| stepM["Identify PO with max dist v"]
    stepM --> stepN["Backtrack via predecessor array"]
    stepN --> stepO["Output: Critical path and delay"]
```

> **Figure 2:** Block-level functional architecture of the Longest Path Algorithm showing the interplay between Kahn's topological sort, DP-based relaxation, and path reconstruction.

### 4.3 Distance Propagation Snapshot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initA["A : dist = 0"] --> procA
    initB["B : dist = 0"] --> procB
    procA["Process A"] --> updC1["C : dist = max -inf, 0+3 = 3"]
    procA --> updD1["D : dist = max -inf, 0+2 = 2"]
    procB["Process B"] --> updC2["C : dist = max 3, 0+4 = 4"]
    procB --> updD2["D : dist = max 2, 0+1 = 2"]
    updC2 --> procC["Process C"]
    updD2 --> procD["Process D"]
    procC --> updE1["E : dist = max -inf, 4+2 = 6"]
    procD --> updE2["E : dist = max 6, 2+5 = 7"]
    updE1 --> finalE["E : dist = 7 : Critical Path Found"]
    updE2 --> finalE
```

> **Figure 3:** Stepwise state evolution of the `dist[]` array during algorithm execution, showing how the dynamic programming relaxation propagates the longest distances from sources to sinks.

### 4.4 Critical Path Highlight (Result Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["A : 0 ns"] ==> nodeD["D : 2 ns"]
    nodeD ==> nodeE["E : 7 ns : CRITICAL"]
    nodeB["B : 0 ns"] -.-> nodeC["C : 4 ns"]
    nodeC -.-> nodeE
```

> **Figure 4:** Bold arrows represent the critical path $A \to D \to E$ with cumulative delay of **7 ns**. Dashed arrows show non-critical paths contributing to the DAG topology but not governing the clock period.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEEVT525]`

**Define a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG). Why is the longest path problem polynomial-time solvable on a DAG but NP-hard on a general directed graph?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** is a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ with no directed cycles, i.e., there is no path $v_1 \to v_2 \to \cdots \to v_1$ that returns to its starting vertex. (1 mark)

The longest path problem is polynomial-time solvable on a DAG because the **absence of cycles** permits a **topological ordering** of the vertices. Once ordered, dynamic programming can compute the longest path in $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ time via the recurrence $dist[v] = \max_{(u,v) \in E}\{dist[u] + w(u,v)\}$. (1 mark)

On a general directed graph, cycles permit the existence of arbitrarily long paths through repeated traversal, making the optimization problem computationally intractable (NP-hard). (1 mark)

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEEVT525]`

**What is meant by the "critical path" in a combinational VLSI circuit? How does it determine the maximum operating frequency of the circuit?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **critical path** in a combinational circuit is the topological path from any primary input (PI) to any primary output (PO) that has the **maximum cumulative gate delay**. (1 mark)

It is found by computing the longest path in the gate-level DAG, where each edge weight is the propagation delay of the corresponding gate. (1 mark)

The **maximum operating frequency** is given by $f_{max} = 1 / t_{pd, max}$, where $t_{pd, max}$ is the critical path delay. The clock period must satisfy $T_{clk} \geq t_{pd, max} + t_{setup} + t_{hold}$, so the slowest path dictates the chip's clock speed. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEEVT525, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the **Longest Path Algorithm for a DAG** with reference to its use in VLSI static timing analysis. Discuss the role of **topological sorting** and **dynamic programming relaxation** in achieving linear time complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the DAG shown below, compute the **longest path (critical path)** from primary inputs to primary outputs using the Longest Path Algorithm. Show all intermediate distance values, identify the critical path, and state the worst-case propagation delay.

| **Edge** | **Weight (ns)** |
|---|---|
| $S_1 \to N_1$ | 2 |
| $S_1 \to N_2$ | 5 |
| $S_2 \to N_2$ | 3 |
| $S_2 \to N_3$ | 4 |
| $N_1 \to N_3$ | 1 |
| $N_1 \to T_1$ | 6 |
| $N_2 \to T_1$ | 2 |
| $N_3 \to T_1$ | 4 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Algorithm Explanation (7 marks):**

The Longest Path Algorithm for a DAG is the **computational core of Static Timing Analysis (STA)** in VLSI design. The algorithm operates in three phases:

**Phase 1: Topological Sorting (Kahn's Algorithm)** — Computes a linear ordering of vertices such that every directed edge points from an earlier to a later vertex. This is achieved using a queue initialized with all zero-indegree source nodes (primary inputs). Each dequeued vertex's outgoing neighbors have their indegree decremented, and any that reach zero indegree are enqueued. Time: $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$. (2 marks)

**Phase 2: Dynamic Programming Relaxation** — Vertices are processed in topological order. For each vertex $u$, every outgoing edge $(u, v)$ with weight $w(u, v)$ triggers the update:
$$dist[v] = \max(dist[v],\; dist[u] + w(u, v))$$
This is correct because by the time $u$ is processed, all predecessor paths to $u$ have already been finalized. A parallel **predecessor array** $\pi[v]$ is updated to record which predecessor gave the maximum, enabling path reconstruction. (3 marks)

**Phase 3: Result Extraction** — The maximum of $dist[v]$ over all primary outputs (sinks) yields the critical path delay $t_{pd, max}$. Backtracking from this sink via $\pi$ reconstructs the actual critical path. Total time complexity: $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$, space: $O(\vert V \vert)$. (2 marks)

**Engineering Significance:** In STA tools like Synopsys PrimeTime, this algorithm is invoked iteratively across all timing corners (PVT — Process, Voltage, Temperature) to verify timing closure. The **linear complexity** is critical because modern SoCs have millions of gates.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute Indegrees**

$$
\begin{aligned}
indeg[S_1] &= 0,\quad indeg[S_2] = 0 \\
indeg[N_1] &= 1,\quad indeg[N_2] = 2,\quad indeg[N_3] = 2 \\
indeg[T_1] &= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Topological Sort**

Queue starts with $\{S_1, S_2\}$. Process in order: $S_1, S_2, N_1, N_2, N_3, T_1$. (1 mark)

**Step 3: Initialize and Relax**

$$
\begin{aligned}
dist[S_1] &= 0,\; dist[S_2] = 0 \\
dist[N_1] &= dist[N_2] = dist[N_3] = dist[T_1] = -\infty
\end{aligned}
$$

Process $S_1$ (edges $S_1 \to N_1, S_1 \to N_2$):
$$
dist[N_1] = 0 + 2 = 2,\quad dist[N_2] = 0 + 5 = 5
$$

Process $S_2$ (edges $S_2 \to N_2, S_2 \to N_3$):
$$
dist[N_2] = \max(5, 0+3) = 5,\quad dist[N_3] = 0 + 4 = 4
$$

Process $N_1$ (edges $N_1 \to N_3, N_1 \to T_1$):
$$
dist[N_3] = \max(4, 2+1) = 4,\quad dist[T_1] = 2 + 6 = 8
$$

Process $N_2$ (edge $N_2 \to T_1$):
$$
dist[T_1] = \max(8, 5+2) = 8
$$

Process $N_3$ (edge $N_3 \to T_1$):
$$
dist[T_1] = \max(8, 4+4) = 8
$$

**Step 4: Extract Critical Path**

$$
t_{pd, max} = dist[T_1] = \mathbf{8 \text{ ns}}
$$

Backtrack from $T_1$: predecessor is $N_1$ (since $2+6=8$ beat $5+2=7$ and $4+4=8$ — tie at $N_3$ also yields $8$; pick $N_1$).

Predecessor of $N_1$ is $S_1$.

**Critical Path:** $S_1 \to N_1 \to T_1$ with cumulative delay $2 + 6 = 8$ ns. (2 marks for final answer)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEEVT525]`

**(a)** Discuss the **role of the Longest Path Algorithm in VLSI design optimization**. Explain how critical path information guides **gate sizing, retiming, and pipelining** decisions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a combinational circuit represented as a DAG with 7 vertices: sources $A, B$, internal nodes $C, D, E$, and primary output $F$. The edge list with delays is: $A \to C(3)$, $A \to D(2)$, $B \to C(4)$, $B \to D(1)$, $B \to E(5)$, $C \to E(2)$, $C \to F(6)$, $D \to F(4)$, $E \to F(3)$. Compute the **longest path and critical delay** using the algorithm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Role in VLSI Design Optimization (7 marks):**

The Longest Path Algorithm is foundational to three major VLSI optimization strategies:

**1. Gate Sizing (2 marks):** The critical path delay is a function of gate sizes. By identifying which gates lie on the critical path, the designer can selectively upsize (increase $W/L$ ratio) those gates to reduce their delay. Gates on non-critical paths can be downsized to reduce area and power. The algorithm must be **re-run iteratively** after each sizing change because upsizing one gate may shift the critical path.

**2. Retiming (2 marks):** Retiming relocates flip-flops across combinational logic to balance path delays. The longest path algorithm identifies "bottleneck" regions where the combinational delay exceeds the target clock period. The minimum feasible clock period equals the maximum, over all cuts of the DAG, of the longest path delay across that cut — a generalization of the basic longest path.

**3. Pipelining (2 marks):** When $t_{pd, max} > T_{target}$, the designer inserts **pipeline registers** along the critical path to break it into $k$ stages, each with delay $\leq T_{target}$. The algorithm's path enumeration identifies optimal insertion points to maximize throughput. The new clock period becomes $T_{clk}' = t_{pd, max} / k$ (approximately).

**Synthesis:** Without the longest path algorithm, none of these optimizations could be performed systematically. It transforms timing closure from an ad-hoc art into a rigorous, algorithmic science. (1 mark)

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Indegrees**

$$
indeg[A]=0,\; indeg[B]=0,\; indeg[C]=2,\; indeg[D]=2,\; indeg[E]=2,\; indeg[F]=3
$$

**Step 2: Topological Order:** $A, B, C, D, E, F$. (1 mark)

**Step 3: Initialize**

$$
dist[A]=0,\; dist[B]=0,\; dist[C]=dist[D]=dist[E]=dist[F]=-\infty
$$

**Step 4: Relax (2 marks)**

Process $A$: $dist[C] = 0+3 = 3$; $dist[D] = 0+2 = 2$.

Process $B$: $dist[C] = \max(3, 0+4) = 4$; $dist[D] = \max(2, 0+1) = 2$; $dist[E] = 0+5 = 5$.

Process $C$: $dist[E] = \max(5, 4+2) = 6$; $dist[F] = 4+6 = 10$.

Process $D$: $dist[F] = \max(10, 2+4) = 10$.

Process $E$: $dist[F] = \max(10, 6+3) = 10$.

**Step 5: Result**

$$
t_{pd, max} = dist[F] = \mathbf{10 \text{ ns}}
$$

**Critical Path Reconstruction:** $F$'s predecessor giving max is $C$ (since $4+6=10$ ties with $D$'s contribution $2+4=6$ and $E$'s $6+3=9$ — the max is $C$).

Predecessor of $C$ is $B$ (since $0+4=4$ beat $0+3=3$).

**Critical Path:** $B \to C \to F$ with delay $4 + 6 = 10$ ns. (2 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse shortest path with longest path** — KTU examiners frequently test whether students use $\min$ vs $\max$ in the relaxation step. Using $\min$ here gives the **fastest** signal arrival, NOT the critical path.
> 2. **Always show topological order explicitly** — Skipping this step costs 1–2 marks even if the final answer is correct. Validators check for a well-defined linear ordering.
> 3. **Forgetting to handle $-\infty$ initialization properly** — Multiple-source DAGs require ALL PIs to be initialized to $0$, not just one. A common error is initializing only a single source.
> 4. **Path reconstruction requires the predecessor array** — Stating the distance value alone (without the actual path) loses 1–2 marks in the "result extraction" step.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DAG Definition**: Directed graph with no cycles — the canonical model for combinational logic.
- **Longest Path on DAG** = **Critical Path** in VLSI timing analysis.
- **Time complexity**: $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ — **linear**, not exponential.
- **Algorithm skeleton**: (1) Topological sort → (2) Initialize $dist[\text{PIs}] = 0$ → (3) Relax in order → (4) Extract max over POs → (5) Backtrack via $\pi[]$.
- **Recurrence**: $dist[v] = \max_{(u,v) \in E} \{ dist[u] + w(u, v) \}$.
- **Engineering impact**: Determines $T_{clk}$ and $f_{max}$; guides gate sizing, retiming, pipelining.
- **Slack formula**: $Slack[v] = T_{clk} - dist[v]$ — must be $\geq 0$ for timing closure.
- **NP-Hard on general digraphs**: Why cycle-free assumption is essential.
- **Predecessor array $\pi[v]$** is mandatory for reconstructing the actual critical path.
- **Kahn's algorithm vs DFS-based sort** — both achieve topological order; Kahn's is preferred for explicit cycle detection (queue empty prematurely $\Rightarrow$ cycle exists).
- **KTB 2024 Hot Topic**: Expect 14-mark questions combining algorithm explanation (7 marks) with numerical computation on a 5–7 vertex DAG (7 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Liao-Wong Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT525 - CAD BASED VLSI DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Liao-Wong Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Liao–Wong Partitioning Algorithm** is an iterative-improvement heuristic introduced by Yuh-Sheng Liao and C. K. Wong (1991) for the **bipartitioning of hypergraphs** that model VLSI circuits. It is used during the **Partitioning stage** of the VLSI physical design flow to divide a netlist into two near-equal halves such that the number of inter-partition (cut) nets is minimized.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEEVT525 – Module 3):**
> The Liao–Wong algorithm is studied as an *enhanced extension of the classical Kernighan–Lin (KL) algorithm*. Its novelty is the introduction of a **"free set"** of cells and the corresponding **free-set-aware gain function**, which prevents the algorithm from getting trapped in poor local minima when the initial partition is highly unbalanced or the netlist is dense.

Formally, given a hypergraph $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ is the set of circuit cells and $E$ is the set of multi-terminal nets, the objective is to find disjoint subsets $A$ and $B$ (with $A \cup B = V$) such that:

$$\text{Minimize } \; \text{Cut}(A,B) = \bigl\vert \{ e \in E \;:\; e \cap A \neq \emptyset \;\text{and}\; e \cap B \neq \emptyset \} \bigr\vert$$

subject to a **balance constraint**:

$$\bigl\vert \; \vert A \vert - \vert B \vert \; \bigr\vert \;\leq\; \delta$$

where $\delta$ is the maximum allowable imbalance (commonly $\delta = 1$ for near-perfect balance).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are the **zoning commissioner of a growing town** that must be split into exactly two wards of nearly equal population:

- **Cells / Nodes** = households
- **Nets / Hyperedges** = shared utilities (a single water pipeline serving 3 or 4 houses is a multi-terminal net)
- **Cut Nets** = pipes/wires that cross from Ward 1 into Ward 2 — every crossing costs money and reliability

You start by roughly dividing the town, but many pipes cross the boundary. You now want to **swap families** between wards to reduce crossings, while keeping the populations balanced.

The standard **Kernighan–Lin** method is like blindly swapping a family and hoping for the best. The **Liao–Wong** method is smarter — it puts *undecided* families in a neutral zone (the **"free set"**) and carefully evaluates how each swap affects:
1. Families already locked into a ward,
2. Families in the opposite ward, **and**
3. Families still sitting in the neutral zone.

This triple-awareness is what makes Liao–Wong superior for dense, multi-terminal VLSI nets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Liao–Wong = "Partitioning with a Neutral Buffer Zone." The free set acts as a triage area that prevents premature, irreversible commitment of cells to a partition.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology (KTU Board Favourites)

| Metric | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Hypergraph** | $G(V,E)$ | Netlist model |
| **Cells / Vertices** | $V$ | Circuit modules |
| **Nets / Hyperedges** | $E$ | Electrical connections |
| **Partition sizes** | $\vert A \vert, \vert B \vert$ | Number of cells in each partition |
| **Cut size** | $\text{Cut}(A,B)$ | Nets crossing the partition |
| **Balance tolerance** | $\delta$ | Allowed size difference |
| **Free set** | $F$ | Cells not yet committed to a partition |
| **Lock status** | $\text{locked}(v)$ | Whether cell $v$ has been finalized |

> [!TIP]
> Examiner's Eye: Always quote the **balance constraint** $\vert A \vert \approx \vert B \vert$ when defining a partitioning problem. Missing it costs 1–2 marks in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Sets: $A$, $B$, and $F$

Unlike the classic KL algorithm (which only uses $A$ and $B$), Liao–Wong maintains **three disjoint sets** throughout execution:

- **$A$** — cells currently committed to Partition $A$
- **$B$** — cells currently committed to Partition $B$
- **$F$** — the **free set** (cells not yet assigned to any partition)

Initially, the algorithm places a subset of cells in $F$ (e.g., $F$ may be empty initially and grow as some cells are left unassigned, or a small set is reserved as $F$). During each iteration, exactly one cell moves out of $F$ into $A$ or $B$, ensuring every cell is eventually committed.

---

## 2.2 The Liao–Wong Gain Function (The Heart of the Algorithm)

For any cell $v \in A$, define three integer quantities based on the nets incident to $v$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_A(v) &= \text{number of nets incident to } v \text{ whose OTHER endpoints are all in } A \\
C_A(v) &= \text{number of nets incident to } v \text{ that have at least one endpoint in } B \\
F_A(v) &= \text{number of nets incident to } v \text{ that have at least one endpoint in } F
\end{aligned}
$$

The **Liao–Wong gain** of moving $v$ from $A$ to $B$ is then defined as:

$$
g_{A \to B}(v) \;=\; D_A(v) \;-\; C_A(v) \;-\; F_A(v)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the $-F_A(v)$ term?**
> If $v$ is moved out of $A$, every net touching $v$ that also touches the free set $F$ will *become a new cut net* (because the other endpoint is no longer in $A$). Subtracting $F_A(v)$ penalizes such moves, preventing the algorithm from aggressively displacing cells that are well-connected to the unassigned free pool.

Symmetrically, for a cell $u \in B$ being moved to $A$:

$$
g_{B \to A}(u) \;=\; D_B(u) \;-\; C_B(u) \;-\; F_B(u)
$$

For a cell $w \in F$ being moved to $A$ (or $B$):

$$
g_{F \to A}(w) \;=\; -\,C_F^{A}(w)
$$

where $C_F^{A}(w)$ is the number of nets incident to $w$ that already have at least one endpoint in $A$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table collects **every formula** a student must memorize for the KTU board exam on this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cut Size** | $\text{Cut}(A,B) = \bigl\vert \{ e \in E : e \cap A \neq \emptyset \;\wedge\; e \cap B \neq \emptyset \} \bigr\vert$ | Objective to minimize |
| 2 | **Balance Constraint** | $\bigl\vert \;\vert A \vert - \vert B \vert \; \bigr\vert \leq \delta$ | Usually $\delta = 1$ |
| 3 | **Gain $A \to B$** | $g_{A \to B}(v) = D_A(v) - C_A(v) - F_A(v)$ | Liao–Wong signature |
| 4 | **Gain $B \to A$** | $g_{B \to A}(u) = D_B(u) - C_B(u) - F_B(u)$ | Symmetric |
| 5 | **Gain $F \to A$** | $g_{F \to A}(w) = -\,C_F^{A}(w)$ | Free-set gain |
| 6 | **Cumulative Gain** | $G_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} g_i$ | After $k$ swaps |
| 7 | **Best Partial Sum** | $G^{*} = \max_{1 \leq k \leq n} G_k$ | Defines rollback point |
| 8 | **Time Complexity** | $O(\vert V \vert^{2} \cdot \vert E \vert)$ per pass | Polynomial heuristic |

---

## 2.4 Why Liao–Wong Outperforms Kernighan–Lin

| Property | Kernighan–Lin (1970) | Liao–Wong (1991) |
|---|---|---|
| **State sets** | Only $A$, $B$ | $A$, $B$, and **free set $F$** |
| **Gain definition** | $D - C$ only | $D - C - F$ (penalty-aware) |
| **Initial balance** | Requires pre-balancing | Self-corrects via free set |
| **Multi-terminal nets** | Biased towards 2-terminal | **Native hypergraph handling** |
| **Local minima escape** | Weak | Strong (free set provides buffer) |
| **Best for** | Sparse graphs | **Dense VLSI netlists** |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Favourite Question:** *"How does Liao–Wong improve over KL?"* — Answer: by introducing the free set $F$ and the $F_A(v)$ penalty term in the gain function, allowing the algorithm to make informed, balanced moves even when the initial partition is poor.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern VLSI design flows (Synopsys ICC, Cadence Innovus), the Liao–Wong algorithm is embedded inside:
- **Top-down multi-level partitioning** (e.g., hMETIS, MLPart)
- **FPGA clustering and placement pre-processing**
- **Multi-FPGA prototyping** where the design must be split across chips
- **SoC IP partitioning** where hard IPs and soft logic must be balanced across dies in a chiplet package

The algorithm's ability to handle **multi-terminal nets** (a single net connecting 5+ cells) makes it especially suited to modern designs where average net fanout often exceeds 4.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Complete Liao–Wong Algorithm (Derivation of Procedure)

The algorithm is derived from first principles by extending the **Kernighan–Lin local-search framework** with a tri-state representation.

### Algorithm 1: Liao–Wong Bipartitioning

**Input:** Hypergraph $G = (V, E)$, target balance $\delta$.

**Output:** Partition $(A, B)$ with minimum cut subject to balance.

---

**Step 1 — Initialization**

Construct an initial partition $(A_0, B_0)$ and a free set $F_0$ such that:

$$
A_0 \cup B_0 \cup F_0 \;=\; V, \qquad A_0 \cap B_0 = A_0 \cap F_0 = B_0 \cap F_0 = \emptyset
$$

A common heuristic is to randomly assign $\vert V \vert / 2$ cells to $A$, $\vert V \vert / 2$ cells to $B$, and leave the rest in $F$. All cells are marked **unlocked**.

---

**Step 2 — Gain Computation**

For every unlocked cell $v$, compute:
- If $v \in A$: $g_{A \to B}(v) = D_A(v) - C_A(v) - F_A(v)$
- If $v \in B$: $g_{B \to A}(v) = D_B(u) - C_B(u) - F_B(u)$
- If $v \in F$: $g_{F \to A}(v) = -C_F^A(v)$ and $g_{F \to B}(v) = -C_F^B(v)$

This requires examining every net incident to $v$ — done in $O(\deg(v))$ time.

---

**Step 3 — Best Pair Selection**

Find the pair $(v^{*}, u^{*})$ with $v^{*} \in A$, $u^{*} \in B$ that maximizes the combined swap gain:

$$
\Delta(v, u) \;=\; g_{A \to B}(v) \;+\; g_{B \to A}(u)
$$

subject to the balance constraint being maintained after the swap.

---

**Step 4 — Lock and Update**

- Mark $v^{*}$ and $u^{*}$ as **locked** (they cannot move again in this pass).
- Physically swap them: $v^{*}$ joins $B$, $u^{*}$ joins $A$.
- Recompute gains for all **unlocked neighbours** of $v^{*}$ and $u^{*}$ (only those affected nets need updating).
- Record the cumulative gain $G_k$ after this $k$-th swap.

---

**Step 5 — Rollback Check**

After all cells are locked (one full pass), find the index $k^{*}$ that maximizes $G_k$:

$$
k^{*} \;=\; \arg\max_{1 \leq k \leq \vert V \vert} \; G_k
$$

Then **undo all swaps performed after $k^{*}$**, retaining only the swaps $1, 2, \ldots, k^{*}$. This is the famous **"cliff-climbing with rollback"** trick inherited from KL.

---

**Step 6 — Iterate or Terminate**

If the cut size improved, restart the algorithm from the new partition. Otherwise, **terminate** — the current $(A, B)$ is a local minimum.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Apply Liao–Wong to a hypergraph with 4 cells $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and 3 nets:

- $e_1 = \{1, 2, 3\}$
- $e_2 = \{2, 3, 4\}$
- $e_3 = \{1, 4\}$

**Initial partition:** $A = \{1, 2\}$, $B = \{3, 4\}$, $F = \emptyset$.

---

**Step A — Compute Initial Cut Size**

Check each net:
- $e_1 = \{1, 2, 3\}$ → has cells in both $A$ and $B$ → **CUT** ✂️
- $e_2 = \{2, 3, 4\}$ → has cells in both $A$ and $B$ → **CUT** ✂️
- $e_3 = \{1, 4\}$ → has cells in both $A$ and $B$ → **CUT** ✂️

$$
\text{Cut}(A, B) \;=\; 3
$$

---

**Step B — Compute Gains**

Since $F = \emptyset$, the free-set terms vanish: $F_A(v) = 0$ for all $v$.

For cell $1 \in A$:
- Nets incident: $e_1 = \{1,2,3\}$, $e_3 = \{1,4\}$
- $D_A(1)$: nets whose other endpoints are all in $A = \{2\}$ → $e_1$ has $\{2\} \subset A$ (yes, also 3 in B) ✗; $e_3$ has $\{4\} \subset B$ ✗ → $D_A(1) = 0$
- $C_A(1)$: nets with at least one endpoint in $B$ → $e_1$ (via 3), $e_3$ (via 4) → $C_A(1) = 2$

$$
g_{A \to B}(1) \;=\; 0 - 2 - 0 \;=\; -2
$$

For cell $2 \in A$:
- Nets incident: $e_1 = \{1,2,3\}$, $e_2 = \{2,3,4\}$
- $D_A(2)$: $e_1$ has $\{1\} \subset A$ ✓ → contributes 1; $e_2$ has $\{3,4\} \subset B$ ✗ → $D_A(2) = 1$
- $C_A(2)$: $e_1$ via 3, $e_2$ via 3,4 → $C_A(2) = 2$

$$
g_{A \to B}(2) \;=\; 1 - 2 - 0 \;=\; -1
$$

For cell $3 \in B$:
- $D_B(3)$: $e_1$ has $\{1,2\} \subset A$ ✗; $e_2$ has $\{4\} \subset B$ ✓ → $D_B(3) = 1$
- $C_B(3)$: $e_1$ via 1,2; $e_2$ via 2 → $C_B(3) = 2$

$$
g_{B \to A}(3) \;=\; 1 - 2 - 0 \;=\; -1
$$

For cell $4 \in B$:
- $D_B(4)$: $e_2$ has $\{2,3\}$ in mixed state ✗; $e_3$ has $\{1\} \subset A$ ✗ → $D_B(4) = 0$
- $C_B(4)$: $e_2$ via 2,3; $e_3$ via 1 → $C_B(4) = 2$

$$
g_{B \to A}(4) \;=\; 0 - 2 - 0 \;=\; -2
$$

---

**Step C — Best Pair Selection**

Pair candidates: $(1,3), (1,4), (2,3), (2,4)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta(1,3) &= -2 + (-1) = -3 \\
\Delta(1,4) &= -2 + (-2) = -4 \\
\Delta(2,3) &= -1 + (-1) = -2 \\
\Delta(2,4) &= -1 + (-2) = -3
\end{aligned}
$$

Best pair: $(v^*, u^*) = (2, 3)$ with $\Delta = -2$.

---

**Step D — Swap and Recompute**

Swap: new $A = \{1, 3\}$, new $B = \{2, 4\}$.

Re-evaluate cut:
- $e_1 = \{1, 2, 3\}$ → CUT ✂️
- $e_2 = \{2, 3, 4\}$ → CUT ✂️
- $e_3 = \{1, 4\}$ → CUT ✂️

Cut remains 3. The greedy first move fails. The Liao–Wong **rollback mechanism** will then test the next best pair $(1, 3)$ with $\Delta = -3$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** When ALL gains are negative, it means the initial partition is at or near a local minimum. The algorithm will perform the swap with the *least bad* gain and rely on the cumulative-gain rollback to escape.

---

## 3.3 Gain Recomputation — Efficient Update Rule

After swapping $v$ from $A$ to $B$, only neighbours of $v$ in the **net adjacency graph** need updating. For each net $e$ incident to $v$:

| Net State Before | Net State After | Update Effect on Neighbours |
|---|---|---|
| $e$ had all endpoints in $A$ except $v$ | $e$ now becomes cut | All former co-residents of $v$ in $A$: $D \to D-1$, $C \to C+1$ |
| $e$ was already a cut net | $e$ may become internal to $B$ | All former co-residents in $B$: $C \to C-1$, $D \to D+1$ |
| $e$ had endpoint in $F$ | $e$ now connects to $B$ | All $F$-residents: $F_A \to F_A - 1$ |

This incremental update is what gives Liao–Wong its $O(\vert V \vert^2 \cdot \vert E \vert)$ complexity per pass.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison with Contemporary Algorithms

| Algorithm | Year | Handles Multi-Terminal Nets | Free Set | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Kernighan–Lin (KL)** | 1970 | ✗ (approximated) | ✗ | Sparse 2-terminal graphs |
| **Fiduccia–Mattheyses (FM)** | 1982 | ✓ (single-cell moves) | ✗ | Faster, single-pass |
| **Liao–Wong** | **1991** | **✓** | **✓** | **Dense, unbalanced netlists** |
| **hMETIS** | 1998 | ✓ | ✗ (multi-level) | Large-scale, modern VLSI |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — The Liao–Wong Engine

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete operational pipeline** of the Liao–Wong algorithm. This is the exact block-flow a student should sketch in the board exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Hypergraph G V E] --> initB[Initial Partition A B F]
    initB --> gainComp[Compute Initial Gains D C F]
    gainComp --> pairSel{Best Pair Selection}
    pairSel -->|max delta found| swapExec[Swap and Lock Pair]
    swapExec --> gainUpdate[Incremental Gain Update]
    gainUpdate --> allLocked{All Cells Locked}
    allLocked -->|no| pairSel
    allLocked -->|yes| rollback[Find k star maximizing G k]
    rollback --> undoSwaps[Undo Swaps After k star]
    undoSwaps --> cutCheck{Cut Improved}
    cutCheck -->|yes| gainComp
    cutCheck -->|no| finalOut[Output Final Partition A B]
```

---

## 4.2 Three-Set State Transition Diagram

This diagram shows how a cell $v$ transitions between the sets $A$, $B$, and $F$ during algorithm execution.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stateA[Set A committed] -->|swap with u in B| stateB[Set B committed]
    stateB -->|swap with v in A| stateA
    stateF[Set F free set] -->|g F to A| stateA
    stateF -->|g F to B| stateB
    stateA -->|demote if high F penalty| stateF
    stateB -->|demote if high F penalty| stateF
```

---

## 4.3 Gain Computation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cellV[Cell v in A] --> incidentNets[Enumerate Incident Nets e in E]
    incidentNets --> classify{Net Endpoint Classification}
    classify -->|all in A| dCount[D A v increment]
    classify -->|at least one in B| cCount[C A v increment]
    classify -->|at least one in F| fCount[F A v increment]
    dCount --> gainFormula
    cCount --> gainFormula
    fCount --> gainFormula
    gainFormula[Gain equals D minus C minus F] --> outputG[Output g A to B of v]
```

---

## 4.4 Multi-Pass Iteration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pass1[Pass 1] --> check1{Cut improved}
    check1 -->|yes| pass2[Pass 2]
    check1 -->|no| terminate[Terminate at Local Minimum]
    pass2 --> check2{Cut improved}
    check2 -->|yes| pass3[Pass 3]
    check2 -->|no| terminate
    pass3 --> ellipsis[Dots up to Pass P]
    ellipsis --> finalTerminate[Convergence Reached]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "draw the flowchart," always include the **rollback step** — it is the signature feature of KL-family algorithms and KTU examiners explicitly check for it.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the Liao–Wong partitioning algorithm. What is the role of the "free set" $F$ in this algorithm?**

**Model Answer:**

The Liao–Wong algorithm is an iterative-improvement heuristic used to bipartition a hypergraph $G = (V, E)$ representing a VLSI circuit into two balanced subsets $A$ and $B$ while minimizing the cut size.

The **free set $F$** is a third partition holding cells not yet committed to either $A$ or $B$. Its role is to:

1. Provide a **buffer zone** for cells whose optimal placement is uncertain.
2. Actively influence the **gain function** via the $F_A(v)$ penalty term: $g_{A \to B}(v) = D_A(v) - C_A(v) - F_A(v)$, discouraging moves that would create new cut nets connected to unassigned cells.
3. Enable the algorithm to **escape local minima** by allowing cells to temporarily reside outside both partitions, preventing premature commitment.

**(3 marks — full marks if all three points and the gain formula are given.)**

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: Distinguish between the gain function used in the Kernighan–Lin algorithm and the Liao–Wong algorithm.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Kernighan–Lin | Liao–Wong |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $g(v) = D(v) - C(v)$ | $g(v) = D(v) - C(v) - F(v)$ |
| Free set | Absent | Present |
| Multi-terminal nets | Approximated as pairs | Native hypergraph support |
| Penalty for new cuts | None | $F(v)$ discourages risky moves |

The Liao–Wong gain includes an extra $-F(v)$ term that penalizes moving cells whose incident nets connect to the free set, thereby producing more balanced and lower-cut partitions for dense VLSI netlists.

**(3 marks — distinction table with the formula difference is mandatory.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question B1 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the Liao–Wong bipartitioning algorithm in detail. Describe the role of the free set and how it differs from the classical Kernighan–Lin approach. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a hypergraph with cells $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ and nets $E = \{e_1, e_2, e_3, e_4\}$ defined as:
- $e_1 = \{1, 2, 3\}$
- $e_2 = \{3, 4, 5\}$
- $e_3 = \{1, 5, 6\}$
- $e_4 = \{2, 4, 6\}$

Starting from initial partition $A = \{1, 2, 3\}$, $B = \{4, 5, 6\}$, $F = \emptyset$, compute the Liao–Wong gains for all cells and identify the best swap pair. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for B1(a):**

1. **Introduction:** Liao–Wong is a heuristic for hypergraph bipartitioning (KL family, 1991). It introduces a third set $F$ (free set) alongside $A$ and $B$.

2. **Initialization:** Place $\vert V \vert/2$ cells in $A$, $\vert V \vert/2$ in $B$, and remaining cells in $F$. All cells unlocked.

3. **Gain Definition:**
   $g_{A \to B}(v) = D_A(v) - C_A(v) - F_A(v)$ — where $D_A$ = nets internal to $A$, $C_A$ = cut nets, $F_A$ = nets touching $F$.

4. **Iteration:**
   - Select best pair $(v^*, u^*)$ maximizing $\Delta = g_{A \to B}(v^*) + g_{B \to A}(u^*)$.
   - Lock and swap them.
   - Incrementally update gains of affected neighbours.

5. **Rollback:** After all cells locked, find $k^* = \arg\max_k G_k$ and undo swaps beyond $k^*$.

6. **Termination:** Repeat passes until cut stops improving.

7. **Difference from KL:** KL uses only $A$ and $B$ with gain $D - C$. Liao–Wong's free set $F$ and penalty term $F(v)$ prevent premature locking and yield better hypergraph cuts.

---

#### **Model Solution for B1(b):**

**Initial Partition:** $A = \{1,2,3\}$, $B = \{4,5,6\}$, $F = \emptyset$.

Since $F = \emptyset$, all $F$-terms vanish.

**Cut size initially:**
- $e_1 = \{1,2,3\}$ → all in $A$ → internal ✓
- $e_2 = \{3,4,5\}$ → 3 in $A$, 4,5 in $B$ → **CUT**
- $e_3 = \{1,5,6\}$ → 1 in $A$, 5,6 in $B$ → **CUT**
- $e_4 = \{2,4,6\}$ → 2 in $A$, 4,6 in $B$ → **CUT**

$$
\text{Cut}(A,B) = 3
$$

**Gains for cells in $A$:**

| Cell | Incident Nets | $D_A$ | $C_A$ | $g_{A\to B} = D - C$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e_1, e_3$ | $e_1$ has $\{2,3\}\subset A$ → 1 | $e_3$ via 5,6 → 1 | $1 - 1 = 0$ |
| 2 | $e_1, e_4$ | $e_1$ has $\{1,3\}\subset A$ → 1 | $e_4$ via 4,6 → 1 | $1 - 1 = 0$ |
| 3 | $e_1, e_2$ | $e_1$ has $\{1,2\}\subset A$ → 1 | $e_2$ via 4,5 → 1 | $1 - 1 = 0$ |

**Gains for cells in $B$:**

| Cell | Incident Nets | $D_B$ | $C_B$ | $g_{B\to A} = D - C$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | $e_2, e_4$ | none (rest in $A$) → 0 | $e_2$ via 3, $e_4$ via 2 → 2 | $0 - 2 = -2$ |
| 5 | $e_2, e_3$ | none → 0 | $e_2$ via 3, $e_3$ via 1 → 2 | $0 - 2 = -2$ |
| 6 | $e_3, e_4$ | none → 0 | $e_3$ via 1, $e_4$ via 2 → 2 | $0 - 2 = -2$ |

**Best Pair Combinations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta(1,4) &= 0 + (-2) = -2 \\
\Delta(1,5) &= 0 + (-2) = -2 \\
\Delta(1,6) &= 0 + (-2) = -2 \\
\Delta(2,4) &= 0 + (-2) = -2 \\
\Delta(2,5) &= 0 + (-2) = -2 \\
\Delta(2,6) &= 0 + (-2) = -2 \\
\Delta(3,4) &= 0 + (-2) = -2 \\
\Delta(3,5) &= 0 + (-2) = -2 \\
\Delta(3,6) &= 0 + (-2) = -2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Best swap pair (tie, any one acceptable):** $(v^*, u^*) = (1, 4)$ with $\Delta = -2$.

After swap: $A = \{4, 2, 3\}$, $B = \{1, 5, 6\}$. Cut size remains 3 — algorithm continues to next pass. **All tie-breaking pairs yield the same combined gain.**

---

### **Question B2 (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**(a)** With neat flowcharts and gain equations, describe the Liao–Wong algorithm for circuit partitioning. Compare its complexity with the Fiduccia–Mattheyses algorithm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a hypergraph with nets $e_1 = \{1,2\}$, $e_2 = \{2,3,4\}$, $e_3 = \{1,4\}$, $e_4 = \{3\}$, starting from $A = \{1,2\}$, $B = \{3,4\}$, $F = \{5\}$ (assume cell 5 has no incident nets), compute all Liao–Wong gains and identify the swap that maximally reduces the cut. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for B2(a):**

**Flowchart:** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the Mermaid flow — translate to handwritten block diagram in the exam.

**Key Equations:**

$$
g_{A \to B}(v) = D_A(v) - C_A(v) - F_A(v)
$$

$$
\Delta(v, u) = g_{A \to B}(v) + g_{B \to A}(u)
$$

$$
k^{*} = \arg\max_{k} G_k, \quad \text{where } G_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} g_i
$$

**Complexity Comparison:**

| Algorithm | Per Pass Complexity | Key Bottleneck |
|---|---|---|
| **Fiduccia–Mattheyses (FM)** | $O(\vert E \vert)$ | Single-cell moves |
| **Liao–Wong (LW)** | $O(\vert V \vert^{2} \cdot \vert E \vert)$ | Pair-wise swap evaluation |

Liao–Wong is **slower per pass** but achieves **lower cut sizes** because pair-wise swaps explore a richer neighbourhood than FM's single-cell moves.

---

#### **Model Solution for B2(b):**

**Initial:** $A = \{1,2\}$, $B = \{3,4\}$, $F = \{5\}$ (isolated).

**Incident net listings:**
- Cell 1: $e_1, e_3$
- Cell 2: $e_1, e_2$
- Cell 3: $e_2, e_4$
- Cell 4: $e_2, e_3$
- Cell 5: (none)

**Cut size:**
- $e_1 = \{1,2\}$ → internal to $A$ ✓
- $e_2 = \{2,3,4\}$ → **CUT** (2 in A, 3,4 in B)
- $e_3 = \{1,4\}$ → **CUT**
- $e_4 = \{3\}$ → internal to $B$ ✓

$$
\text{Cut}(A,B) = 2
$$

**Gains for cells in $A$ (with free-set terms):**

For cell 1: $e_1$ has other endpoints $\{2\} \subset A$ → $D_A(1) = 1$. $e_3$ has $\{4\} \subset B$ → $C_A(1) = 1$. No net touches $F$. So $F_A(1) = 0$.

$$
g_{A \to B}(1) = 1 - 1 - 0 = 0
$$

For cell 2: $e_1$ has $\{1\} \subset A$ → contributes to $D_A$. $e_2$ has $\{3,4\} \subset B$ → $C_A(2)$ includes $e_2$. So $D_A(2) = 1$, $C_A(2) = 1$, $F_A(2) = 0$.

$$
g_{A \to B}(2) = 1 - 1 - 0 = 0
$$

**Gains for cells in $B$:**

For cell 3: $e_4$ has $\{3\} \subset B$ → $D_B(3) = 1$. $e_2$ has $\{2\} \subset A$ → $C_B(3) = 1$. $F_B(3) = 0$.

$$
g_{B \to A}(3) = 1 - 1 - 0 = 0
$$

For cell 4: $e_2$ has $\{2\} \subset A$, $\{3\} \subset B$ → both $D$ and $C$ contribute. $e_3$ has $\{1\} \subset A$ → cut. $D_B(4)$: no net has all other endpoints in $B$ (since $e_2$ has 2 in $A$, $e_3$ has 1 in $A$). So $D_B(4) = 0$, $C_B(4) = 2$ ($e_2$ via 2, $e_3$ via 1).

$$
g_{B \to A}(4) = 0 - 2 - 0 = -2
$$

**Best Pair Combinations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta(1,3) &= 0 + 0 = 0 \\
\Delta(1,4) &= 0 + (-2) = -2 \\
\Delta(2,3) &= 0 + 0 = 0 \\
\Delta(2,4) &= 0 + (-2) = -2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Best swap pairs (multiple ties with $\Delta = 0$):** $(1,3)$ or $(2,3)$.

Choosing $(1, 3)$: After swap, $A = \{3, 2\}$, $B = \{1, 4\}$. New cut: $e_1 = \{1,2\}$ has 1 in B, 2 in A → **CUT**; $e_2 = \{2,3,4\}$ has all in mixed → **CUT**; $e_3 = \{1,4\}$ all in B ✓; $e_4 = \{3\}$ in A ✓. Cut size = 2 — same as before. The algorithm must continue.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the balance constraint** when swapping — students often swap cells without checking $|\vert A \vert - \vert B \vert| \leq \delta$. **−2 marks.**
> 2. **Miscounting $D$ vs $C$** — a net with endpoints in BOTH $A$ and $F$ (but not in $B$) must be counted as $F_A$, not $D_A$. **−1 mark per error.**
> 3. **Skipping the rollback step** in flowcharts — the rollback is the *defining feature* of KL-family algorithms. **−2 marks** if missing.
> 4. **Confusing Liao–Wong with FM** — Fiduccia–Mattheyses uses single-cell moves, Liao–Wong uses **pair-wise swaps**. Examiners deduct **−1 to −2 marks** for this confusion.
> 5. **Omitting the initial cut-size computation** — every Liao–Wong problem should begin with $\text{Cut}(A_0, B_0)$ to establish the baseline.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Final 60-Second Recall List for KTU Board Exam:**

- [x] **Algorithm Class:** Iterative-improvement hypergraph bipartitioning heuristic (KL family).
- [x] **Inventors:** Y. Liao & C. K. Wong (1991).
- [x] **Three Sets:** $A$, $B$, and **free set $F$** — the signature innovation.
- [x] **Gain Formula:** $g_{A \to B}(v) = D_A(v) - C_A(v) - F_A(v)$.
- [x] **Free-set Penalty:** $-F_A(v)$ discourages moves that would create new cut nets touching unassigned cells.
- [x] **Objective:** Minimize $\text{Cut}(A, B)$ subject to $\bigl\vert \;\vert A \vert - \vert B \vert \; \bigr\vert \leq \delta$.
- [x] **Swap Rule:** Choose pair $(v^*, u^*)$ maximizing $\Delta = g_{A \to B}(v) + g_{B \to A}(u)$.
- [x] **Locking:** Swapped cells are locked for the rest of the pass.
- [x] **Rollback:** Find $k^* = \arg\max_k G_k$, undo swaps beyond $k^*$.
- [x] **Termination:** Stop when a pass produces no cut improvement.
- [x] **Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert^{2} \cdot \vert E \vert)$ per pass.
- [x] **vs KL:** Adds free set and $F(v)$ penalty — better for dense VLSI netlists.
- [x] **vs FM:** Slower per pass (pair-wise) but yields lower cuts.
- [x] **Real-World Use:** Multi-level VLSI partitioning, FPGA prototyping, chiplet die balancing.
- [x] **Always Quote:** Balance constraint, rollback step, and three-set model in your answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Bellman-Ford Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT525 - CAD BASED VLSI DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Bellman-Ford Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — THE BELLMAN-FORD ALGORITHM

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Bellman-Ford Algorithm** is a single-source shortest-path algorithm defined over a weighted, directed graph $G = (V, E)$ with a real-valued weight function $w : E \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$. Given a source vertex $s \in V$, the algorithm computes for every vertex $v \in V$ the minimum total weight $\delta(s, v)$ of any path from $s$ to $v$. It is uniquely distinguished from Dijkstra's method by its ability to operate correctly on graphs containing **negative-weight edges** and to **report the presence of any negative-weight cycle** reachable from the source.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Source Shortest Path (SSSP) Theorem (Bellman, 1958; Ford, 1956):**
> If a graph $G$ contains no negative-weight cycle reachable from $s$, then the relaxation process terminates with $d[v] = \delta(s, v)$ for every $v \in V$. If such a cycle exists, no finite shortest path exists and the algorithm signals failure.

### 1.2 Notation Used Throughout the Notes

Let the directed graph contain $\vert V \vert = n$ vertices and $\vert E \vert = m$ edges. The following quantities are maintained by the algorithm during execution:

- $d[v]$ — current best estimate (upper bound) of the shortest-path distance from source $s$ to vertex $v$.
- $\pi[v]$ — predecessor pointer of $v$ on the best known path, used for path reconstruction.
- $w(u, v)$ — weight of the directed edge $(u \rightarrow v)$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a delivery driver planning a route across a city, where every road segment carries a **time cost**, but a few roads give the driver an **unconventional advantage** (e.g., electric-vehicle charging lanes that *reduce* total time, modelled as negative weights). Ordinary greedy navigation (Dijkstra) would refuse to take such "discounted" roads, getting stuck on a longer path. The Bellman-Ford approach is like a planner that **re-evaluates the entire route book (V − 1) times**, gradually allowing the discount credit to propagate forward through the network. If, after all passes, a road still keeps claiming to make the driver "richer with each loop," the planner knows a **fraudulent negative cycle** exists and refuses to output any route.

> [!NOTE]
> In VLSI CAD, the "delivery network" is the **interconnect / timing graph** of a placed-and-routed chip. Positive edge weights represent gate delays and wire delays; negative weights can model credit-style optimization objectives (e.g., clock-skew budgeting, slack recovery), and **negative cycles correspond to timing violations** that the optimizer must flag.

### 1.4 Why Bellman-Ford Matters in VLSI CAD-Based Optimization

The algorithm is a workhorse in **Static Timing Analysis (STA)**, **clock-tree synthesis (CTS)**, **global routing**, and **graph-based floorplanning**, wherever weighted directed graphs with potentially negative slack edges arise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics in KTU Questions:** $n = \vert V \vert$, $m = \vert E \vert$, relaxation count = $(n-1)$ passes, total edge relaxations = $(n-1) \cdot m$, time complexity = $O(nm)$, space complexity = $O(n)$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Logic — Structured Steps

The Bellman-Ford procedure decomposes into three strictly separated phases:

### Phase A — INITIALIZE-SINGLE-SOURCE$(G, s)$
1. For every vertex $v \in V$ set $d[v] \leftarrow \infty$.
2. For every vertex $v \in V$ set $\pi[v] \leftarrow \text{NIL}$.
3. Set $d[s] \leftarrow 0$.

### Phase B — RELAX$(u, v, w)$
A single edge $(u \rightarrow v)$ is examined. The "relaxation" operation is defined as:

$$
\text{if } d[v] > d[u] + w(u, v) \text{ then }
\begin{cases}
d[v] \leftarrow d[u] + w(u, v) \\
\pi[v] \leftarrow u
\end{cases}
$$

If the current estimate of $v$ can be improved by going through $u$, the estimate is tightened and the predecessor updated. Relaxation is the *atomic operation* of the algorithm.

### Phase C — MAIN BELLMAN-FORD LOOP
1. Execute the relaxation step for **every edge** of the graph, exactly once per outer iteration.
2. Repeat the pass for a total of $\vert V \vert - 1$ iterations.
3. **Negative-Cycle Test:** After the $\vert V \vert - 1$ passes, perform one additional pass. If any edge can still be relaxed, a **negative-weight cycle** is reachable from $s$.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind $(n - 1)$ Iterations

Any simple (cycle-free) path in a graph with $n$ vertices contains **at most $n - 1$ edges**. Hence, the true shortest path (when finite) will be fully discovered within $n - 1$ outer passes. This is the cornerstone of the algorithm's correctness proof.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cycle vs. Simple Path Distinction:** A "simple path" has no repeated vertex. If a shortest path repeated a vertex, it would also repeat an edge, forming a cycle. Cycles with positive weight are *avoided*; cycles with zero weight are *indifferent*; only **negative-weight cycles** make shortest paths *undefined* (since traversing them indefinitely drives the path weight to $-\infty$).

## 2.3 The Three Foundational Properties (Lemmas)

1. **Triangle Inequality (Path-Weight Inequality):** For any edge $(u, v) \in E$ and any source $s$,
   $$\delta(s, v) \leq \delta(s, u) + w(u, v)$$

2. **Upper-Bound Property:** $d[v] \geq \delta(s, v)$ always holds after initialization, and is preserved by every relaxation step. The estimate is *always an upper bound* on the true optimum.

3. **Convergence Property:** If the shortest path $p = \langle v_0, v_1, \dots, v_k \rangle$ (with $v_0 = s$) is relaxed in order $v_0, v_1, \dots, v_{k-1}$, then after $k$ relaxations we obtain $d[v_k] = \delta(s, v_k)$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table consolidates every formula, condition, and metric that may appear in a KTU ESE question on Bellman-Ford.**

| **Quantity / Property** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Value / Bound** | **Engineering Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of outer iterations | $i_{\max}$ | $n - 1$ | Sufficient for any simple shortest path |
| Total edge relaxations attempted | $(n-1) \cdot m$ | exact count | Worst-case work performed |
| Time complexity (worst case) | $O(nm)$ | $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$ | Acceptable for sparse timing graphs |
| Space complexity | $O(n)$ | linear in vertices | Stores $d[\cdot]$ and $\pi[\cdot]$ arrays |
| Relaxation update rule | $d[v] = \min(d[v],\, d[u] + w(u,v))$ | greedy local fix | Applied to every edge |
| Negative-cycle detection | $\exists (u,v) : d[v] > d[u] + w(u,v)$ | boolean | True ⇒ no SSSP exists |
| Predecessor traceback | $v \rightarrow \pi[v] \rightarrow \pi[\pi[v]] \dots \rightarrow s$ | unique path | Reconstructs optimal path |
| Triangle inequality | $\delta(s, v) \leq \delta(s, u) + w(u, v)$ | invariant | Bound on all-pairs shortest paths |
| No-negative-cycle corollary | $d[v] = \delta(s, v) \; \forall v$ | post-condition | Algorithm success criterion |
| Path-weight recursion | $d(s, v_k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w(v_{i-1}, v_i)$ | additive | Total cost of traversed edges |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Static Timing Analysis (STA) in VLSI:** Each timing arc is a directed edge, gate delay + wire delay is the weight, and the longest path (or shortest in dual-graph formulation) is computed using Bellman-Ford-style relaxation.
- **Network Routing Protocols:** RIP (Routing Information Protocol) and distance-vector routing historically use Bellman-Ford with split-horizon and poison-reverse refinements.
- **Arbitrage Detection in Currency Exchange:** Edge weights are $-\log(\text{rate})$; negative cycle ⇒ profitable arbitrage opportunity.
- **Robotics and Autonomous Systems:** Path planners handle signed reward/cost terrains.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation — Why Relaxation is Correct

### Derivation 1 — Optimality of the Update Rule

We wish to find, for every $v \in V$, the value

$$
\delta(s, v) = \min_{p \in P_{sv}} \left\{ w(p) \right\}, \quad \text{where} \quad w(p) = \sum_{e \in p} w(e)
$$

and $P_{sv}$ is the set of all paths from $s$ to $v$.

Consider an arbitrary edge $(u, v)$. Suppose the algorithm has already correctly computed $d[u] = \delta(s, u)$. Then a candidate path from $s$ to $v$ through $u$ has total weight $d[u] + w(u, v)$. Any path to $v$ must end with some incoming edge $(x, v)$, so by the triangle inequality:

$$
\delta(s, v) \leq \delta(s, x) + w(x, v) = d[x] + w(x, v)
$$

Taking the minimum over all possible predecessors $x$:

$$
\delta(s, v) \leq \min_{x : (x,v) \in E} \big[ d[x] + w(x, v) \big]
$$

Since $d[x] \geq \delta(s, x)$ at all times, the *best* candidate path is the one with the smallest $d[x] + w(x, v)$, which is exactly what the relaxation computes. Therefore, after every edge is examined once, the estimate $d[v]$ becomes the best possible upper bound derivable from one-edge extensions. Repeating this $n - 1$ times extends the search depth to a full simple path. $\blacksquare$

### Derivation 2 — The $(n-1)$-Pass Sufficiency

**Claim:** After $i$ outer passes of Bellman-Ford, $d[v]$ equals the shortest path from $s$ to $v$ using **at most $i$ edges**.

**Proof by Mathematical Induction:**

- **Base Case ($i = 0$):** No edges used. Only $d[s] = 0$ is correct; all other $d[v] = \infty$. ✓
- **Inductive Step:** Assume the claim holds after $i$ passes. In pass $i+1$, the relaxation examines every edge $(u, v)$. If the true shortest path using $\leq i+1$ edges reaches $v$ as $s \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow u \rightarrow v$, the prefix to $u$ uses $\leq i$ edges and by the induction hypothesis equals $d[u]$. The relaxation then sets $d[v] \leq d[u] + w(u, v)$, capturing the full path weight.

Since any simple path has $\leq n-1$ edges, the claim is proven with $i = n-1$. $\blacksquare$

### Derivation 3 — Negative-Cycle Detection Criterion

**Claim:** A negative-weight cycle is reachable from $s$ **iff** some edge can still be relaxed after $n-1$ passes.

**Forward Direction:** Suppose a negative-weight cycle $C = \langle c_0, c_1, \dots, c_k = c_0 \rangle$ is reachable from $s$. Then there is a finite simple path $p_0$ from $s$ to $c_0$ with weight $w(p_0)$. The total weight of traversing $C$ once is $w(C) < 0$. After $n-1$ passes, $d[c_0] \leq w(p_0)$. Traversing the cycle once more produces the path $p_0 \oplus C$ with weight $w(p_0) + w(C) < w(p_0)$, so the edge entering $c_0$ in the cycle can still be relaxed — contradiction unless we allow the cycle. Hence a relaxation still fires.

**Reverse Direction:** If some edge $(u, v)$ is relaxable after $n-1$ passes, then $d[v] > d[u] + w(u, v)$. By the upper-bound property, $d[u] \geq \delta(s, u)$, and similarly for $d[v]$. However, repeated relaxation would generate a sequence of strictly decreasing distance values for some vertex, which can only happen on a cycle of total negative weight. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Complete Solution

Consider the directed graph $G = (V, E)$ with $V = \{s, a, b, c, t\}$ and the following edge list with weights:

| Edge | $s \to a$ | $s \to b$ | $a \to b$ | $a \to c$ | $b \to a$ | $b \to t$ | $c \to t$ | $t \to c$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Weight | 6 | 7 | 5 | $-4$ | 8 | $-3$ | 9 | 7 |

**Initial State (after Phase A):**

$$
d[s] = 0, \quad d[a] = d[b] = d[c] = d[t] = \infty, \quad \pi[\cdot] = \text{NIL for all}
$$

**Iteration 1** (relax all edges in order):

- $s \to a$: $d[a] > 0 + 6 \Rightarrow d[a] = 6, \pi[a] = s$
- $s \to b$: $d[b] > 0 + 7 \Rightarrow d[b] = 7, \pi[b] = s$
- $a \to b$: $d[b] > 6 + 5 = 11$? No. Skip.
- $a \to c$: $d[c] > 6 + (-4) = 2 \Rightarrow d[c] = 2, \pi[c] = a$
- $b \to a$: $d[a] > 7 + 8 = 15$? No. Skip.
- $b \to t$: $d[t] > 7 + (-3) = 4 \Rightarrow d[t] = 4, \pi[t] = b$
- $c \to t$: $d[t] > 2 + 9 = 11$? No. Skip.
- $t \to c$: $d[c] > 4 + 7 = 11$? No. Skip.

**After Iteration 1:** $d = \{0, 6, 7, 2, 4\}$, $\pi = \{-, s, s, a, b\}$

**Iteration 2:**

- $s \to a$: $6 > 0 + 6 = 6$? No (strict inequality required). Skip.
- $s \to b$: $7 > 0 + 7$? No. Skip.
- $a \to b$: $7 > 6 + 5 = 11$? No. Skip.
- $a \to c$: $2 > 6 + (-4) = 2$? No. Skip.
- $b \to a$: $6 > 7 + 8 = 15$? No. Skip.
- $b \to t$: $4 > 7 + (-3) = 4$? No. Skip.
- $c \to t$: $4 > 2 + 9 = 11$? No. Skip.
- $t \to c$: $2 > 4 + 7 = 11$? No. Skip.

**No changes — algorithm converges.**

**Negative-Cycle Test (Iteration 5):** All edges $d[v] \leq d[u] + w(u, v)$? Verified above — none relaxable. **No negative cycle reachable from $s$.**

**Final Shortest Distances from $s$:**

$$
\delta(s, s) = 0, \quad \delta(s, a) = 6, \quad \delta(s, b) = 7, \quad \delta(s, c) = 2, \quad \delta(s, t) = 4
$$

**Path Reconstruction to $t$:** $t \leftarrow \pi[t] = b \leftarrow \pi[b] = s$, so path is $\langle s, b, t \rangle$ with total weight $7 + (-3) = 4$. ✓

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — KTU-Ready Convergence Behaviour

| **Pass $i$** | **$d[s]$** | **$d[a]$** | **$d[b]$** | **$d[c]$** | **$d[t]$** | **Edges Updated** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 (Init) | 0 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | none |
| 1 | 0 | 6 | 7 | 2 | 4 | $s \to a$, $s \to b$, $a \to c$, $b \to t$ |
| 2 | 0 | 6 | 7 | 2 | 4 | none |
| 3 | 0 | 6 | 7 | 2 | 4 | none |
| 4 | 0 | 6 | 7 | 2 | 4 | none |
| Test | 0 | 6 | 7 | 2 | 4 | **No negative cycle detected** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence in Practice:** Although the theoretical bound is $n - 1 = 4$ passes, the algorithm often stabilizes in 1–2 passes for sparse acyclic-like subgraphs. Early-termination variants check whether a pass produces *no updates* and exit.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flow — Mermaid Compilation

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid flowchart implements the full Bellman-Ford control flow. Node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes to comply with Mermaid parser safeguards; quoted labels are kept raw (no markdown) to ensure rendering fidelity.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1([START BELLMAN FORD])
    n2[INIT: dist s = 0]
    n3[INIT: dist all others = infinity]
    n4[INIT: predecessor all = NIL]
    n5[SET pass counter i = 1]
    n6[ITERATE every edge u to v with weight w]
    n7{DECISION: dist u plus w less than dist v}
    n8[UPDATE dist v = dist u + w]
    n9[SET predecessor v = u]
    n10{END of edge list for this pass}
    n11[INCREMENT pass counter i]
    n12{DECISION: i greater than V minus 1}
    n13[PERFORM negative cycle test pass]
    n14{DECISION: any edge still relaxable}
    n15[REPORT negative cycle exists]
    n16[REPORT no negative cycle]
    n17[OUTPUT shortest path distances and predecessor tree]
    n18([END])

    n1 --> n2
    n2 --> n3
    n3 --> n4
    n4 --> n5
    n5 --> n6
    n6 --> n7
    n7 -- YES --> n8
    n7 -- NO --> n10
    n8 --> n9
    n9 --> n10
    n10 -- NOT YET --> n6
    n10 -- YES --> n11
    n11 --> n12
    n12 -- NO --> n6
    n12 -- YES --> n13
    n13 --> n14
    n14 -- YES --> n15
    n14 -- NO --> n16
    n15 --> n18
    n16 --> n17
    n17 --> n18
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — VLSI CAD Application

The diagram below maps the Bellman-Ford algorithm to a **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** pipeline used in placement-and-routing CAD tools.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    b1[Netlist and SPEF Database]
    b2[Graph Constructor Module]
    b3[Directed Timing Graph: Vertices = Pins, Edges = Delay Arcs]
    b4[Bellman Ford Solver Engine]
    b5[Relaxation Kernel with n minus 1 Passes]
    b6[Negative Slack Cycle Detector]
    b7[Path Traceback and Critical Path Builder]
    b8[Slack Report and Timing Closure Output]

    b1 --> b2
    b2 --> b3
    b3 --> b4
    b4 --> b5
    b5 --> b6
    b5 --> b7
    b6 --> b8
    b7 --> b8
```

**Mapping of Components to Algorithm Phases:**

- **Graph Constructor Module ↔ Phase A (INITIALIZE):** Builds the directed weighted graph from the gate-level netlist; sets all node slack estimates to $+\infty$ except the primary inputs at $0$.
- **Relaxation Kernel ↔ Phase B (RELAX):** Iteratively propagates arrival-time and required-time slacks along every delay arc.
- **Negative Slack Cycle Detector ↔ Phase C (Cycle Test):** Flags combinational loops or incorrect constraint specifications as **timing-violation cycles**.
- **Critical Path Builder ↔ Path Reconstruction:** Traces back via the $\pi[\cdot]$ predecessor array to recover the **longest combinational path** (the critical path).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In the dual graph, finding the *longest* path is equivalent to finding the *shortest* path with negated weights.** This is precisely why Bellman-Ford's tolerance to negative weights makes it indispensable in VLSI timing analysis.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEEVT525]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**"State the Bellman-Ford algorithm and list two key differences between it and Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm."**

**Model Answer:**
The Bellman-Ford algorithm is a single-source shortest-path algorithm that computes the minimum-weight paths from a source vertex $s$ to every other vertex $v \in V$ in a weighted directed graph $G = (V, E)$ by iteratively relaxing **all** edges exactly $\vert V \vert - 1$ times, and then performing a final test pass to detect negative-weight cycles.

| **Differentiator** | **Dijkstra** | **Bellman-Ford** |
|---|---|---|
| Negative edge weights | Forbidden | Permitted |
| Negative cycle detection | Not supported | Explicitly reported |
| Underlying principle | Greedy (min-heap) | Dynamic programming (relaxation) |

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEEVT525]**

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**"What is a negative-weight cycle? Explain in one paragraph how the Bellman-Ford algorithm detects its presence in a graph."**

**Model Answer:**
A *negative-weight cycle* is a directed cycle $C = \langle c_0, c_1, \dots, c_k = c_0 \rangle$ such that the sum of edge weights satisfies $w(C) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w(c_{i-1}, c_i) < 0$. Traversing such a cycle repeatedly drives the path cost to $-\infty$, so the shortest-path weight $\delta(s, v)$ is **undefined** for any vertex $v$ reachable through the cycle.

The Bellman-Ford algorithm detects a negative-weight cycle by exploiting the **upper-bound convergence** property. After exactly $n - 1$ outer passes, any finite shortest simple path is fully discovered, and the distance estimates satisfy $d[v] = \delta(s, v)$ for all $v$. If, upon performing **one extra pass** (the $n$-th relaxation), an edge $(u, v)$ is found to still satisfy $d[v] > d[u] + w(u, v)$, then the algorithm concludes that a negative-weight cycle is reachable from $s$ and reports failure.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEEVT525]** (Model Paper Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**"Explain the Bellman-Ford algorithm in detail with its mathematical formulation. Using the graph given below, compute the shortest path from vertex $s$ to all other vertices. Discuss its time complexity and one application in VLSI design."**

Vertices: $V = \{s, a, b, c, d\}$, with edges $s \to a = 4$, $s \to b = 2$, $b \to a = 3$, $a \to c = 5$, $a \to d = -2$, $b \to d = 6$, $c \to d = 1$, $d \to b = 1$.

#### Part (a) — Algorithm Explanation + Worked Example (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Initialization:**

$$
d[s] = 0, \quad d[a] = d[b] = d[c] = d[d] = \infty
$$

**Step 2 — Iterative Relaxation (Pass 1):**

- $s \to a$: $d[a] = \min(\infty, 0 + 4) = 4$, $\pi[a] = s$
- $s \to b$: $d[b] = \min(\infty, 0 + 2) = 2$, $\pi[b] = s$
- $b \to a$: $d[a] = \min(4, 2 + 3) = 4$ — no improvement
- $a \to c$: $d[c] = \min(\infty, 4 + 5) = 9$, $\pi[c] = a$
- $a \to d$: $d[d] = \min(\infty, 4 + (-2)) = 2$, $\pi[d] = a$
- $b \to d$: $d[d] = \min(2, 2 + 6) = 2$ — no improvement
- $c \to d$: $d[d] = \min(2, 9 + 1) = 2$ — no improvement
- $d \to b$: $d[b] = \min(2, 2 + 1) = 2$ — no improvement

**State after Pass 1:** $d = [0, 4, 2, 9, 2]$, $\pi = [-, s, s, a, a]$

**Step 3 — Pass 2:** No further improvement on any edge (all candidate relaxations are non-strict). Algorithm **converges**.

**Step 4 — Negative-Cycle Test:** No edge is further relaxable ⇒ **no negative cycle reachable from $s$**.

**Final Shortest Distances:** $\delta(s, s) = 0$, $\delta(s, a) = 4$, $\delta(s, b) = 2$, $\delta(s, c) = 9$, $\delta(s, d) = 2$.

**Path Reconstruction to $d$:** $d \leftarrow \pi[d] = a \leftarrow \pi[a] = s$, so path is $\langle s, a, d \rangle$ with total weight $4 + (-2) = 2$. ✓

#### Part (b) — Time Complexity + VLSI Application (7 Marks)

**Time Complexity:** Each of the $(n-1)$ passes relaxes all $m$ edges, yielding $O(nm)$ total edge operations. With $n = 5$ and $m = 8$ in this example, the algorithm performs at most $4 \times 8 = 32$ relaxations.

**Space Complexity:** $O(n)$ to store the $d[\cdot]$ and $\pi[\cdot]$ arrays.

**VLSI CAD Application — Static Timing Analysis (STA):** In modern chip design, the placed-and-routed netlist is represented as a directed timing graph where each gate output and primary input/output is a node, and each delay arc is a weighted edge. The critical path (longest combinational delay) is found by negating the edge weights and invoking Bellman-Ford to compute the **shortest path** in the dual graph. The algorithm's tolerance of negative weights makes it especially suitable for slack-propagation analysis and clock-skew optimization in clock-tree synthesis.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE

**"Derive the optimality condition of the Bellman-Ford algorithm. Show with a worked example how it handles a graph with one negative-weight edge and no negative cycle. Also explain how the algorithm would report a negative cycle, using a different small graph."**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Optimality + Worked Example (7 Marks)

**Optimality Derivation (sketch):**

Let $p = \langle v_0, v_1, \dots, v_k \rangle$ be the shortest path from $s = v_0$ to $v_k$. We prove by induction on $i$ that after the $i$-th outer pass, $d[v_i] = \delta(s, v_i)$.

- **Base:** After initialization, $d[v_0] = 0 = \delta(s, v_0)$ and $d[v_i] = \infty \geq \delta(s, v_i)$ for $i \geq 1$.
- **Inductive Step:** Assume $d[v_{i-1}] = \delta(s, v_{i-1})$ after pass $i-1$. During pass $i$, the edge $(v_{i-1}, v_i)$ is examined. Since $\delta(s, v_i) = \delta(s, v_{i-1}) + w(v_{i-1}, v_i)$, the relaxation sets $d[v_i] = d[v_{i-1}] + w(v_{i-1}, v_i) = \delta(s, v_i)$. By the upper-bound property, no later operation can lower $d[v_i]$ further, so $d[v_i]$ remains optimal.

This proves the **path-optimality theorem** of Bellman-Ford. $\blacksquare$

**Worked Example with a Single Negative Edge:** Consider $V = \{s, a, t\}$ with edges $s \to a = 6$, $a \to t = -4$, $s \to t = 5$. Initialize $d = [0, \infty, \infty]$. Pass 1: $d[a] = 6$, $d[t] = 5$. Then $a \to t$: $d[t] = \min(5, 6 + (-4)) = 2$. Final $d = [0, 6, 2]$, $\pi = [-, s, a]$, shortest path to $t$ is $\langle s, a, t \rangle$ with weight $2$.

#### Part (b) — Negative-Cycle Reporting on a Small Graph (7 Marks)

Consider $V = \{s, a, b\}$ with edges $s \to a = 1$, $a \to b = -3$, $b \to a = 1$. Note the cycle $a \to b \to a$ has total weight $-3 + 1 = -2 < 0$.

**Iteration 1:** $d = [0, \infty, \infty]$ → $d[a] = 1$, $d[b] = -2$ (via $s \to a \to b$).

**Iteration 2:** $b \to a$ gives $d[a] = \min(1, -2 + 1) = -1$. Improvement!

**Iteration 3:** $a \to b$ gives $d[b] = \min(-2, -1 + (-3)) = -4$. Improvement!

This continues indefinitely — the distance estimates keep shrinking. After the $\vert V \vert - 1 = 2$ prescribed passes, the algorithm performs the **test pass** and finds the edge $b \to a$ still relaxable (since $d[a] = -1$ and $d[b] + w(b, a) = -4 + 1 = -3 < -1$). It therefore **reports the existence of a negative-weight cycle reachable from $s$** and terminates with a failure indication.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Bellman-Ford Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing strict vs. non-strict inequality in the relaxation:** The update fires *only* when $d[v] > d[u] + w(u, v)$ is **strictly** true. Writing $\geq$ (non-strict) skips valid updates.
> 2. **Forgetting the final negative-cycle pass:** Students often stop after $n-1$ iterations and forget the $n$-th verification pass — this costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Mis-stating the iteration count:** Some students write "iterate $n$ times" instead of "$n-1$ times" — the algorithm is defined over $n-1$ passes, with the $n$-th being the cycle test.
> 4. **Path reconstruction mistakes:** The predecessor traceback must follow $\pi[v] \rightarrow \pi[\pi[v]] \dots$ *backwards* from destination to source. Forward reconstruction gives a *broken* path.
> 5. **Missing the VLSI application context:** In PEEVT525, a pure CS-only answer is incomplete. At least **one VLSI-specific use-case** (STA, CTS, or routing) is mandatory for full marks in Part B.
> 6. **Time complexity units:** Always write $O(VE)$ or $O(nm)$ — never $O(V + E)$ (that is the storage complexity of an adjacency list, not Bellman-Ford's running time).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- The Bellman-Ford algorithm is a **single-source shortest-path** algorithm for **directed, weighted** graphs that **tolerates negative edge weights** and **detects negative cycles**.
- It runs **$n - 1$ outer passes**, each relaxing **all $m$ edges**, giving worst-case **time complexity $O(nm)$** and **space complexity $O(n)$**.
- The **core atomic operation** is the *relaxation*: $d[v] \leftarrow \min(d[v],\, d[u] + w(u, v))$, paired with $\pi[v] \leftarrow u$.
- The **$\pi[\cdot]$ predecessor array** is updated **only when** $d[v]$ strictly improves, and is essential for **path reconstruction**.
- The **upper-bound property** guarantees $d[v] \geq \delta(s, v)$ at all times; the **convergence property** guarantees $d[v] = \delta(s, v)$ after the optimal pass count.
- The **$(n - 1)$ bound** is tight because any *simple* (cycle-free) path has at most $n - 1$ edges.
- A **negative-weight cycle** is detected when *any* edge remains relaxable after the $(n-1)$-th pass.
- **Dijkstra is faster** ($O((n+m) \log n)$ with a heap) but **forbids negative edges** — choose Bellman-Ford when the graph has signed weights.
- In **VLSI CAD**, the algorithm powers **Static Timing Analysis**, **critical-path extraction**, and **clock-skew optimization** in clock-tree synthesis.
- In the **dual graph** formulation, finding the *longest* (critical) path is equivalent to finding the *shortest* path with **negated weights** — exactly the regime in which Bellman-Ford excels.
- **Bellman-Ford is the foundation** of the *distance-vector* routing paradigm (e.g., RIP) in computer networks.
- For **arbitrage detection** in finance/currency exchange, edge weights are set to $-\log(\text{exchange rate})$; a negative cycle ⇒ a profitable arbitrage loop.
- The **early-termination heuristic** (exit when a pass makes no updates) often yields substantial speedup in practice, though the worst-case bound remains $O(nm)$.
- **Path reconstruction rule:** Start at destination $t$, follow $\pi[t], \pi[\pi[t]], \dots$ until reaching $s$, then **reverse** the sequence.
- The algorithm is **distributed-friendly** (relaxation can be done locally on each edge), which is why it scaled naturally to early internet routing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Placement: Optimization Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT525 - CAD BASED VLSI DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Placement: Optimization Objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Placement** is the critical step in the VLSI physical design flow that determines the **legal positions** of all standard cells, macros, and I/O pads on the chip canvas (layout surface), subject to design rule constraints, with the goal of optimizing one or more design metrics such as **wirelength, timing, congestion, area, and power**.

The **Placement Optimization Objective** is the mathematically formulated **cost function** that the placement engine (placer) attempts to **minimize (or maximize)** while satisfying geometric, electrical, and manufacturing constraints. It is the heart of any modern placement algorithm (simulated annealing, quadratic placement, analytical placement, etc.).

Mathematically, the placement problem is expressed as:

$$\min_{x_i, y_i} \; \Phi(x, y) \quad \text{subject to } \mathcal{C}$$

Where:
- $(x_i, y_i)$ is the coordinate of cell $i$ on the placement canvas.
- $\Phi(x, y)$ is the **objective function** capturing the design metric.
- $\mathcal{C}$ is the constraint set (density, fixed objects, row boundaries).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** Examiners frequently test the **distinction between wirelength-driven, timing-driven, and congestion-driven placement**. Always relate the objective back to the overall **physical design flow** (Floorplan → Placement → CTS → Routing).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are organizing a **large wedding reception** in a banquet hall:
- The **guests (cells)** must be seated at **tables (rows of the placement region)**.
- The **hosts' family (timing-critical paths)** must be seated **close to the stage** so they can be called quickly (short critical-path delay).
- Guests who **talk a lot with each other (highly-connected nets)** should be placed at **adjacent tables** to minimize the shouting distance (wirelength).
- However, the **aisles (routing channels)** must not be overcrowded with people crossing the hall (routing congestion).
- A balance is needed — that's exactly what the **placement optimizer** does.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Placement is NOT just about squeezing cells together. It is a **constrained multi-objective optimization** where multiple competing goals must be balanced.

## 1.3 Primary Optimization Metrics in Placement

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following core objectives:

| # | Optimization Objective | Physical Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Wirelength (WL)** | Minimize total interconnection length | **µm** |
| 2 | **Critical Path Delay** | Minimize longest path timing | **ns (picoseconds)** |
| 3 | **Routing Congestion** | Evenly distribute routing demand | **Overflow edges** |
| 4 | **Area / Density** | Fit cells within row capacity | **Utilization %** |
| 5 | **Dynamic Power** | Minimize $P \propto \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ | **µW** |
| 6 | **Thermal / Hotspots** | Spread high-activity cells | **°C** |

> [!TIP]
> The **wirelength** objective is the **backbone** of almost every placer. Timing, congestion, and power objectives are layered on top as **weighted penalty terms** or **constraints**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of the Placement Objective

A modern placer's objective function $\Phi(x, y)$ is typically composed of **three layered components**:

$$\Phi(x, y) = \underbrace{\alpha \cdot W(x, y)}_{\text{Wirelength Term}} + \underbrace{\beta \cdot T(x, y)}_{\text{Timing Term}} + \underbrace{\gamma \cdot G(x, y)}_{\text{Congestion Term}}$$

Where $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are **user-defined weighting coefficients** (knobs) that allow the designer to bias the optimization.

### 2.1.1 The Wirelength Objective (Primary)

The most widely used **HPWL (Half-Perimeter Wire Length)** is:

$$W_{\text{HPWL}}(n) = \left( \max_{i \in n} x_i - \min_{i \in n} x_i \right) + \left( \max_{i \in n} y_i - \min_{i \in n} y_i \right)$$

Total placement wirelength:

$$W_{\text{total}} = \sum_{n \in N} w_n \cdot W_{\text{HPWL}}(n)$$

Where:
- $N$ = set of all nets.
- $w_n$ = weight (criticality) of net $n$.
- $x_i, y_i$ = pin coordinates of cell $i$ on net $n$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why HPWL?** It is a **convex, differentiable lower bound** on the true Steiner Minimal Tree (SMT) wirelength. It correlates strongly with actual routed length while being computationally cheap to evaluate.

### 2.1.2 The Timing Objective

The timing term penalizes excessive delay on **critical paths**:

$$T(x, y) = \sum_{p \in \mathcal{P}} \text{slack}_p \cdot D_p(x, y)$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{P}$ = set of timing paths.
- $D_p$ = delay of path $p$ as a function of cell positions.
- $\text{slack}_p$ = timing slack (negative for critical paths).

The delay is modeled using the **Elmore delay model**:

$$D_p = \sum_{i \in p} R_i \cdot \left( \frac{C_{out,i}}{2} + C_{wire,i}(x, y) \right)$$

Where $C_{wire,i}$ is the **wire capacitance**, which depends directly on **net wirelength** — this is the **fundamental link** between wirelength minimization and timing closure.

### 2.1.3 The Congestion Objective

Congestion is estimated using a **global routing model** (e.g., probabilistic or box-router):

$$G(x, y) = \sum_{e \in E_{\text{routing}}} \max(0, \; d_e - c_e)$$

Where:
- $d_e$ = routing demand of edge $e$ (number of nets crossing it).
- $c_e$ = routing capacity of edge $e$.

The term $\max(0, d_e - c_e)$ is the **congestion overflow** — only positive overflows are penalized.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following table. These formulas appear in **Part A (3 marks)** and frequently anchor **Part B (14 marks)** sub-questions.

| Formula | Name | Application |
|---|---|---|
| $W_{\text{HPWL}} = (x_{\max} - x_{\min}) + (y_{\max} - y_{\min})$ | Half-Perimeter Wire Length | Per-net length lower bound |
| $W_{\text{total}} = \sum_n w_n \cdot \text{HPWL}_n$ | Weighted Total Wirelength | Global placement cost |
| $D_{\text{Elmore}} = R \cdot C$ | Elmore Delay | First-order RC delay |
| $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$ | Dynamic Power | Power-driven placement |
| $\text{Utilization} = \frac{A_{\text{cells}}}{A_{\text{core}}}$ | Core Utilization | Density constraint |
| $\Phi = \alpha W + \beta T + \gamma G$ | Composite Objective | Multi-objective placement |
| $C_{\text{wire}} = c_0 \cdot L + c_{\text{coupler}}$ | Wire Capacitance | Routing resource estimation |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX/Markdown Pitfall:** When writing HPWL inline, **never** use `$|x|$` (the pipe breaks markdown tables). Use `$\lvert x \rvert$` or `$\mid x \mid$` instead.

## 2.3 Why These Objectives Matter in Engineering

| Objective | Engineering Impact |
|---|---|
| **Wirelength ↓** | Lower interconnect delay, less routing congestion, lower dynamic power |
| **Timing ↑** | Higher operating frequency, reliable chip operation |
| **Congestion ↓** | Successful routing closure, fewer DRC violations |
| **Power ↓** | Longer battery life (mobile), less thermal stress |
| **Area ↓** | More chips per wafer → lower unit cost |

## 2.4 Wirelength Models — A Comparative Study

Three dominant wirelength models are used in placement:

### A) HPWL (Half-Perimeter Wire Length)
- **Complexity:** Linear in net size.
- **Differentiability:** Piecewise-linear (not smooth).
- **Accuracy:** Good lower-bound estimate.

### B) Log-Sum-Exp (LSE) Smoothed HPWL
Used in **analytical placement** to obtain a smooth, differentiable approximation:

$$W_{\text{LSE}}(n) = \gamma \left[ \ln \left( \sum_{i \in n} e^{x_i / \gamma} \right) + \ln \left( \sum_{i \in n} e^{-x_i / \gamma} \right) \right] + \text{(same for y)}$$

As $\gamma \to 0$, $W_{\text{LSE}} \to W_{\text{HPWL}}$; for large $\gamma$, it is smooth and convex.

### C) Quadratic Wirelength (Bound-to-Bound)
Used in **quadratic placement** (e.g., Kraftwerk2, FastPlace):

$$W_{\text{quad}} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j \in n} w_{ij} \left[ (x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2 \right]$$

Yields a sparse linear system $Ax = b$, solved in $O(n^{1.5})$ time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the HPWL Bound

**Starting Point:** Consider a 2-pin net $n$ connecting cells at $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$.

The **Manhattan (L1) length** is:
$$L_{\text{Manh}} = \lvert x_1 - x_2 \rvert + \lvert y_1 - y_2 \rvert$$

**Step 1: Define bounding box**
The bounding box of net $n$ is:
$$B_n = \left[ \min_i x_i, \max_i x_i \right] \times \left[ \min_i y_i, \max_i y_i \right]$$

**Step 2: Express HPWL directly**
$$W_{\text{HPWL}}(n) = \left( \max_i x_i - \min_i x_i \right) + \left( \max_i y_i - \min_i y_i \right)$$

**Step 3: Prove HPWL ≤ true routed length (Steiner tree)**
For any routing tree $T$ connecting all pins of $n$:
$$\text{Length}(T) \geq W_{\text{HPWL}}(n)$$

This is because:
- The tree must enter the bounding box from outside and visit all interior pins.
- Minimum traversal = half-perimeter traversal (the L-shaped Steiner tree).
