\frac{\partial \Phi_{\text{cong}}}{\partial x_m} = \sum_{i,j} \mathbb{1}_{D_{i,j} > C_{i,j}} \cdot \frac{\partial D_{i,j}}{\partial x_m}
$$

where $x_m$ is the $x$-coordinate of movable cell $m$. This gradient **pushes** cells out of overcongested regions.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice Style)

> **Example:** Consider a chip divided into $2 \times 2$ GCs. The design has 3 nets:
> - Net 1: 2-pin, occupies GCs $(1,1)$ and $(1,2)$ → 1 horizontal crossing.
> - Net 2: 2-pin, occupies GCs $(1,1)$ and $(2,1)$ → 1 vertical crossing.
> - Net 3: 3-pin Steiner, crosses GCs $(1,1)$, $(2,1)$, $(2,2)$.

Compute $D_{i,j}$ assuming each crossing contributes **1 wire-track**.

**Step 1 — Tabulate crossings.**

| Net | GC (1,1) | GC (1,2) | GC (2,1) | GC (2,2) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 3 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| **Total $D_{i,j}$** | **3** | **1** | **2** | **1** |

**Step 2 — Apply capacity $C_{i,j} = 2$ for all GCs.**

$$
OF_{i,j} = \max(0, D_{i,j} - 2) \Rightarrow
\begin{cases}
OF_{1,1} = 1 \\
OF_{1,2} = 0 \\
OF_{2,1} = 0 \\
OF_{2,2} = 0
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Total overflow penalty.**

$$
\Phi_{\text{cong}} = \sum_{i,j} OF_{i,j} = 1
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** GC $(1,1)$ is a **congestion hotspot** with density 3 vs. capacity 2. The placer must spread out the cells in this region.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wire Density Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Netlist N] --> B[Extract Pin Coordinates]
    B --> C[Compute Bounding Box B_n per net]
    C --> D[Choose Density Model]
    D --> E1[Discrete Bin Model]
    D --> E2[Continuous L-Route Model]
    D --> E3[Gaussian Smooth Model]
    E1 --> F1[D_ij = Sum over nets of crossings]
    E2 --> F2[g_n x y = delta-line contributions]
    E3 --> F3[g_n x y = 2D Gaussian bell]
    F1 --> G[Aggregate D x,y over grid]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Compare to Capacity C x,y]
    H --> I[Compute Overflow OF x,y]
    I --> J[Feedback to Placement Engine]
    J --> K{Converged?}
    K -- No --> D
    K -- Yes --> L[Final Routable Placement]
```

## 4.2 Wire Density Distribution Map (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Chip[CHIP SURFACE Region R]
        A1[GC Row 1] --- A2[GC Row 2] --- A3[GC Row 3]
        B1[GC Col 1] --- B2[GC Col 2] --- B3[GC Col 3]
    end
    H1[Hotspot Zone D=8] -->|exceeds C=5| X1[Overflow +3]
    C1[Comfortable Zone D=3] --> Y1[Within capacity]
    L1[Empty Zone D=0] --> Z1[Underutilized]
    X1 --> SOL[Move cells out of hotspot]
    Z1 --> SOL
    SOL --> BAL[Balanced Density Map]
```

## 4.3 Relationship Between Wire Length, Wire Density, and Routability

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WL[Total Wire Length HPWL] --> WLD[Wire Length Distribution]
    WD[Wire Density D x,y] --> CONG[Congestion Map]
    WLD --> CONG
    CONG --> CAP[Compare to Routing Capacity C x,y]
    CAP --> OF[Overflow OF x,y]
    OF --> ROUTE{Routable?}
    ROUTE -- Yes --> DONE[Valid Layout]
    ROUTE -- No --> REPLACE[Iterate Placement with Density Penalty]
    REPLACE --> WD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (`A`, `B`, `C`, `WL`, `WLD`, etc.) — no reserved keywords used as standalone IDs. Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting.

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture of a Congestion-Driven Placer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INP[Netlist + Library] --> GP[Global Placer]
    GP --> COST[Cost Function Evaluator]
    COST --> WLCOST[HPWL Term]
    COST --> DCOST[Density Term]
    COST --> TCOST[Timing Term]
    WLCOST --> OBJ[Combined Objective Phi]
    DCOST --> OBJ
    TCOST --> OBJ
    OBJ --> OPT[Quadratic Programming Solver]
    OPT --> MOV[Cell Movements dx_i, dy_i]
    MOV --> GP
    GP --> END{Convergence Met}
    END -- Yes --> DP[Detailed Placement]
    END -- No --> GP
    DP --> OUT[Final Layout GDSII]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define wire density in the context of VLSI placement. Why is it used as a congestion metric?**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question, Module 3]**

**Model Answer:**
Wire density $D(x,y)$ is a scalar field that represents the **expected concentration of routing wires per unit area** at any point $(x,y)$ on the chip. It is computed as the sum of individual net contributions $g_n(x,y)$ over all nets. It serves as a congestion metric because:
1. It is computed **before detailed routing**, giving an early warning of hotspots.
2. It correlates directly with the **number of routing tracks** required in each region.
3. It allows the placement engine to **redistribute cells** to regions with spare capacity, improving overall routability.
*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for three congestion-metric justifications.)*

### **Q2. State the formula for wire density contribution of a 2-pin net in the L-route model. What is its normalization?**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question, Module 3]**

**Model Answer:**
For a 2-pin net from $A = (x_1, y_1)$ to $B = (x_2, y_2)$ (with $x_1 < x_2$, $y_1 < y_2$), the L-route density contribution is:

$$
g_n(x,y) = \frac{1}{x_2 - x_1}\delta(y - y_1)\mathbb{1}_{[x_1,x_2]}(x) + \frac{1}{y_2 - y_1}\delta(x - x_2)\mathbb{1}_{[y_1,y_2]}(y)
$$

The integral over the bounding box equals **2** (one for each L-segment), which equals the HPWL contribution of the net under proper normalization.
*(3 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for explanation, 1 for normalization.)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Wire Density Questions**
> - **Pitfall 1 (–2 marks):** Forgetting to specify the **routing model** (L-route, Steiner, Gaussian) before writing the density formula. Always state the assumption.
> - **Pitfall 2 (–2 marks):** Confusing **wire density** (per unit area) with **wire length** (per net). They are linked by integration but are distinct quantities.
> - **Pitfall 3 (–1 mark):** Missing the **normalization check** $\int g_n = 1$ (or 2 for L-routes). Examiners award a separate mark for this verification.
> - **Pitfall 4 (–2 marks):** Writing overflow as $D - C$ without the $\max(0, \cdot)$ operator. Negative overflow is **physically meaningless**.

---

### **PART B – OPTION A (14 Marks)**

**Question A:** *Explain in detail the concept of wire density in VLSI placement. Derive the density contribution for a 2-pin net using the bounding-box model. Discuss the discrete bin-based overflow metric used in global routing with a suitable example. [14 Marks]*

#### **Part (a) — Concept and 2-Pin Density Derivation [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept [2 marks]:**
Wire density $D(x,y)$ is the spatial distribution of expected wiring concentration over the chip surface. It is constructed by summing per-net contributions $g_n(x,y)$ for all nets in the design. It is used during global placement to predict and avoid routing congestion.

**Step 2 — Bounding Box [1 mark]:**
For a 2-pin net with pins $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$, the bounding box has dimensions $\Delta x = \vert x_2 - x_1 \vert$ and $\Delta y = \vert y_2 - y_1 \vert$.

**Step 3 — Density Contribution [3 marks]:**
Assuming uniform probability within the bounding box, the 2D density contribution is:

$$
g_n(x,y) = \frac{1}{\Delta x \cdot \Delta y} \quad \text{for } (x,y) \in B_n, \quad \text{else } 0
$$

**Step 4 — Verification [1 mark]:**

$$
\int_{B_n} g_n(x,y) \, dA = 1
$$

This confirms $g_n$ is a valid probability density over the bounding box.

#### **Part (b) — Discrete Bin Overflow Metric with Example [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Grid Setup [1 mark]:**
Divide the chip into $H \times V$ global routing cells (GCs). Each GC has a horizontal capacity $C^h_{i,j}$ and vertical capacity $C^v_{i,j}$.

**Step 2 — Density per GC [2 marks]:**
For each net $n$, find the set of GCs it crosses. The horizontal density in GC $(i,j)$ is:

$$
D^h_{i,j} = \sum_{n} \mathbb{1}_{[n \text{ uses horizontal track in } (i,j)]}
$$

**Step 3 — Overflow Definition [2 marks]:**

$$
OF^h_{i,j} = \max(0, \; D^h_{i,j} - C^h_{i,j})
$$

Total overflow penalty: $\Phi = \sum_{i,j} OF^h_{i,j} + OF^v_{i,j}$.

**Step 4 — Worked Example [2 marks]:**
For a 3-net design (as in Section 3.4) with capacity $C = 2$ uniformly, compute $D_{i,j} = [3, 1; 2, 1]$ giving $\Phi = 1$, and identify GC $(1,1)$ as the hotspot requiring cell redistribution.

---

### **PART B – OPTION B (14 Marks)**

**Question B:** *With the help of a block diagram, describe the role of wire density in congestion-driven placement. Derive the Gaussian smooth density model for a multi-pin net and explain how its gradient is used to move cells out of overcongested regions. [14 Marks]*

#### **Part (a) — Role of Wire Density & Block Diagram [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Motivation [2 marks]:**
Traditional placement optimizes HPWL only, which can produce **unroutable** layouts due to local congestion. Wire density adds a **congestion term** to the objective, balancing wire length and routability.

**Step 2 — Block Diagram Description [3 marks]:**
Referring to Section 4.4: The cost function evaluator receives the current placement, computes three terms — wire length (HPWL), density penalty, and timing penalty — combines them into a single objective $\Phi$, and feeds the gradient to a quadratic programming solver which moves cells. Iterations continue until density hotspots are eliminated.

**Step 3 — Quantitative Role [2 marks]:**
Density reduces the maximum routing demand below capacity, i.e., ensures $D_{i,j} \leq C_{i,j}$ for all $(i,j)$, thereby **guaranteeing a feasible global routing solution**.

#### **Part (b) — Gaussian Density Model & Gradient [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Centroid & Spread [2 marks]:**
For a $k$-pin net with pins $\{p_1, \ldots, p_k\}$:

$$
\bar{x}_n = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i, \quad \sigma_{n,x}^{2} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k}(x_i - \bar{x}_n)^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Gaussian Density [3 marks]:**

$$
g_n(x,y) = \frac{1}{2\pi \sigma_{n,x}\sigma_{n,y}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(x - \bar{x}_n)^{2}}{2\sigma_{n,x}^{2}} - \frac{(y - \bar{y}_n)^{2}}{2\sigma_{n,y}^{2}} \right)
$$

This produces a smooth "bell" over the chip, whose height is large where pins are clustered.

**Step 3 — Gradient Push-Out [2 marks]:**
The gradient with respect to a cell position $x_m$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial D(x,y)}{\partial x_m} = -\frac{(x_m - \bar{x}_n)}{\sigma_{n,x}^{2}} \cdot g_n(x_m, y_m)
$$

This derivative is **negative** if the cell is to the right of the centroid, **pushing the cell leftward** (away from the density peak), thereby spreading pins and reducing local congestion.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Wire density $D(x,y)$** is a **scalar field** measuring wires per unit area; it is the *sum* of per-net contributions $g_n(x,y)$.
- ✅ **Three common density models**: (1) Discrete bin / GC grid, (2) Continuous L-route 1D, (3) Gaussian smooth.
- ✅ **2-pin L-route** density uses **delta functions** along horizontal and vertical segments; integrates to 2 over the bounding box.
- ✅ **Gaussian model** uses **centroid and spread** of pins; its integral over $\mathbb{R}^2$ equals 1 per net.
- ✅ **Overflow** is **$OF = \max(0, D - C)$** — never allow negative values.
- ✅ **Wire density and wire length are linked by integration**: $\int_{\mathcal{R}} D \, dA \propto \sum_n L_n^{HPWL}$.
- ✅ **Peak density $D_{peak}$** is the **KTU-favoured** metric for hotspot detection.
- ✅ Always **state the routing model** (L / Steiner / Gaussian) before writing the density formula in exams.
- ✅ **Normalization check** $\int g_n = 1$ (or 2 for L-routes) is a **scoring point**.
- ✅ **Congestion-driven placers** add a density penalty to the HPWL objective: $\Phi = \alpha \cdot \text{HPWL} + \beta \cdot \sum_{i,j} \max(0, D_{i,j} - C_{i,j})$.
- ✅ **Industrial relevance**: ISPD 2007/2008 contest benchmarks, modern ePlace/RePlAce, and the open-source OpenROAD flow all use density-driven placement.
- ✅ **Bottleneck relationship**: $D_{peak} \leq C_{uniform}$ is a *necessary* (not sufficient) condition for routability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🟢 Final KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "define and explain wire density," always structure your answer as **Definition → Formula → Routing model → Example → Application**. This 5-step structure has consistently scored full marks in KTU 2024 Scheme valuation patterns.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Placement Algorithms: Constructive Placement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT525 - CAD BASED VLSI DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Placement Algorithms: Constructive Placement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Placement** in the VLSI physical design flow is the process of determining the optimal positions of pre-designed cells (standard cells, macros, or I/O pads) on a chip's die surface to minimize a chosen cost function. The cost function typically combines **wirelength**, **routability**, **timing (delay)**, and **area utilization**.

**Constructive Placement** is a class of placement algorithms that build a complete, non-overlapping initial placement of all circuit elements from scratch — usually by **iteratively partitioning**, **clustering**, or **applying physical analogies (force-based heuristics)** — without any need for a starting configuration. The output of a constructive algorithm is typically a *reasonable initial placement* which may later be refined by iterative improvement methods (e.g., simulated annealing, genetic algorithms, or gradient descent).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PEEVT525, Module 3):**
> Constructive placement is the *first* sub-topic of placement. It deals with techniques that *construct* a placement directly from the netlist — **as opposed to iterative placement which starts from a random/initial solution and perturbs it**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you are furnishing a new house. You have a list of furniture (sofa, dining table, TV, bed, fridge, stove) and a "wish-list" of which pieces must be close to each other (sofa near TV, fridge near stove, bed near wardrobe).

- **Constructive placement** is like *planning the house on an empty plot from scratch*: you first divide the plot into rooms (partitioning), then place the most "needy" item first (e.g., the TV in the living room), and gradually slot the remaining items into the most-suitable nearby slots (cluster growth) — you never start with furniture in random places and shuffle it.
- **Iterative placement** would be like *throwing all furniture randomly and then swapping pairs that are in the wrong rooms* (simulated annealing / pairwise exchange).

The "from-scratch" approach is faster per run but usually produces a *good-but-not-optimal* solution.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics Used During Constructive Placement

| Metric | Notation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Half-Perimeter Wire Length | **HPWL** | Cheap surrogate for total wire length |
| Net Cut Cost | $C_{cut}$ | Penalizes nets crossing partitions |
| Cell Connectivity | $w_{ij}$ | Strength of connection between cells $i$ and $j$ |
| Total Estimated Wire Length | **TEWL** | Aggregate of bounding-box wire lengths |
| Chip Aspect Ratio | $\alpha$ | Width-to-height ratio of the die |

> [!NOTE]
> Constructive placement algorithms generally **minimize HPWL** because exact Steiner-tree wire length is NP-hard to evaluate for every candidate placement.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Placement Problem — Mathematical Formulation

Given a netlist $\mathcal{N} = \{N_1, N_2, \ldots, N_m\}$ with cells $\mathcal{C} = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n\}$, the placement problem is to find cell coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$ for every cell such that

$$
\Phi \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{m} W_k \cdot L(N_k) \;\;\text{is minimized}
$$

subject to **non-overlap constraints** and **boundary constraints**.

Where $L(N_k)$ is the wire-length estimator of net $N_k$ and $W_k$ is its weight (often **1** for ordinary nets, **2** for clock nets).

## 2.2 Core Cost Functions (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Sheet — Constructive Placement**

| $\#$ | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Half-Perimeter Wire Length of net $N_k$ | $L_{HPWL}(N_k) \;=\; (x_{\max} - x_{\min}) + (y_{\max} - y_{\min})$ | Computed on the bounding box of the net's pins |
| 2 | Total Wire Length | $TEWL \;=\; \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{m} L_{HPWL}(N_k)$ | Most common objective in constructive placement |
| 3 | Net Cut Cost of a partition $(P, \overline{P})$ | $C_{cut} \;=\; \displaystyle\sum_{k \,\in\, cut(P)} w_k$ | Sum of weights of nets with pins in **both** sides |
| 4 | Gain of moving cell $c$ from $P$ to $\overline{P}$ | $\Delta(c) \;=\; T(c) - C(c)$ | $T$ = nets becoming *internal*, $C$ = nets becoming *external* |
| 5 | Pairwise Swap Gain (Kernighan–Lin) | $\Delta(a,b) \;=\; \Delta_{ab} \;=\; g(a) + g(b) - 2 c_{a,b}$ | $c_{a,b}$ = connection cost between the two swapped cells |
| 6 | Attractive force (quadratic model) | $F_{ij}^{attr} \;=\; k_1 \cdot w_{ij} \cdot (d_{ij} - L_{ij})$ | Spring-like force along net connection $i\!-\!j$ |
| 7 | Repulsive force (inverse-square) | $F_{ij}^{rep} \;=\; \dfrac{k_2}{d_{ij}^{2}}$ | Pushes unrelated cells apart |
| 8 | Total potential energy | $E \;=\; \displaystyle\sum_{i<j}\! w_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}^{2} \;+\; \sum_{i<j}\! \dfrac{k_2}{d_{ij}}$ | Minimized by force-directed placement |
| 9 | Chip area | $A_{chip} \;=\; W \cdot H$ | Must accommodate all cell areas + routing area |
| 10 | Aspect ratio (typical) | $\alpha \;\in\; [1, 2]$ | Constrained by package and pad-frame |

> [!WARNING]
> The vertical-bar symbol has been replaced with $\mid$ / `\vert` inside the table above. **Never** type `|x|` inside a markdown table cell — it breaks the KTU-friendly markdown renderer.

## 2.3 Why Use HPWL Instead of True Wire Length?

True minimum rectilinear Steiner tree length $L_{MST}$ is computationally expensive. HPWL satisfies two important properties:

$$
L_{MST}(N_k) \;\le\; L_{HPWL}(N_k) \;\le\; 2 \cdot L_{MST}(N_k)
$$

Hence HPWL is a **monotonic lower-bound** and a **bounded upper-bound** estimator. It is differentiable and trivially incremental — moving one cell re-evaluates at most **O(pins per net)**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Constructive placement** is the *backbone* of the floorplan step in modern commercial EDA tools (Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC2) — they all use **multi-level min-cut** as their constructive engine.
- Cluster-growth techniques are still used to generate **seed layouts** for FPGAs and large macro blocks.
- Force-directed placement forms the basis of the **global placement** phase in many academic tools (ePLACE, mPL).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

In this section we walk through **two cornerstone constructive algorithms** — **Cluster Growth** and **Min-Cut (Kernighan–Lin) Placement** — with full derivations and worked examples.

---

## 3.1 Cluster Growth Placement — Worked Example

### 3.1.1 Input

Consider a small netlist with 5 cells $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$ and 4 nets:

$$
N_1=\{A,B\},\;\; N_2=\{A,C\},\;\; N_3=\{A,D\},\;\; N_4=\{B,C\},\;\; N_5=\{C,D,E\}
$$

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Compute the Connectivity Matrix $W$

The connectivity weight $w_{ij}$ is the number of nets shared by cells $i$ and $j$.

$$
W \;=\; \begin{bmatrix}
 & A & B & C & D & E \\
A & 0 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 0 \\
B & 1 & 0 & 2 & 0 & 0 \\
C & 1 & 2 & 0 & 1 & 1 \\
D & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 \\
E & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

For example, $w_{BC} = 2$ because cells $B$ and $C$ share nets $N_1$ and $N_4$.

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Choose the Seed Cell

Pick the cell with the **highest net-degree** (most pin connections). Here:

$$
deg(A)=3,\;\; deg(B)=2,\;\; deg(C)=4,\;\; deg(D)=3,\;\; deg(E)=2
$$

**Seed = C** (maximum degree = 4).

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Compute Seed-to-Other-Cell Weights

$$
w(C,A)=1,\;\; w(C,B)=2,\;\; w(C,D)=1,\;\; w(C,E)=1
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Add the Strongest Connected Neighbor

The strongest neighbor of $C$ is $B$ (weight 2). Add $B$ to the cluster.

$$
\mathcal{K}_1 \;=\; \{C, B\},\quad \text{cluster-size} = 2
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Re-evaluate Cluster Connectivity

After adding $B$, recompute the cluster's combined connectivity (sum of each cell's connection to outsiders, avoiding double-counting):

$$
\sigma(A) = w(A,C) + w(A,B) = 1+1 = 2
$$

$$
\sigma(D) = w(D,C) + w(D,B) = 1+0 = 1
$$

$$
\sigma(E) = w(E,C) + w(E,B) = 1+0 = 1
$$

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Add Next Strongest Neighbor

Add $A$ (max $\sigma=2$). Cluster: $\mathcal{K}_2=\{C,B,A\}$.

### 3.1.8 Step 7 — Continue

Suppose target cluster size is 4. Continue:

$$
\sigma(D) = w(D,C)+w(D,B)+w(D,A) = 1+0+1 = 2
$$

$$
\sigma(E) = w(E,C)+w(E,B)+w(E,A) = 1+0+0 = 1
$$

Add $D$. Cluster: $\mathcal{K}_3 = \{C, B, A, D\}$.

Remaining cell $E$ becomes a separate cluster.

### 3.1.9 Result

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Clusters: } \{A,B,C,D\},\;\;\{E\}\;}
$$

These clusters are then placed together in adjacent slots to **minimize inter-cluster wire length**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Algorithmic Complexity** of cluster growth: $O(n^2)$ per cluster; the standard 2-way growing needs $O(n \log n)$ with a priority queue.

---

## 3.2 Min-Cut (Kernighan–Lin) Bisection — Worked Example

### 3.2.1 Input

Consider 6 cells $V = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ with nets:

$$
N_1=\{A,B,C\},\;\; N_2=\{B,C,D\},\;\; N_3=\{A,D\},\;\; N_4=\{C,E,F\},\;\; N_5=\{D,E\}
$$

### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Initial 2-Way Partition

$$
P_0 \;=\; \{\,A,\,B,\,C\,\} \;\big\vert\; \{\,D,\,E,\,F\,\}
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 2 — Compute Initial Cut Cost

| Net | Pins in $P_0$ | Pins in $\overline{P_0}$ | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| $N_1$ | $A,B,C$ | $\varnothing$ | Internal |
| $N_2$ | $B,C$ | $D$ | **Cross** |
| $N_3$ | $A$ | $D$ | **Cross** |
| $N_4$ | $C$ | $E,F$ | **Cross** |
| $N_5$ | $\varnothing$ | $D,E$ | Internal |

$$
C_{cut}(P_0) \;=\; \vert\{N_2,N_3,N_4\}\vert \;=\; 3
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 3 — Compute Move-Gain for Each Cell

For a cell $c$, the move-gain is

$$
\Delta(c) \;=\; T(c) - C(c)
$$

where $T(c)$ = number of nets that would become **internal** if $c$ moves, and $C(c)$ = number of nets that would become **cut** if $c$ moves.

**Move D to Left (L):**

- $N_2 = \{B,C,D\}$ → all in $L$ → becomes internal → $T$ += 1
- $N_3 = \{A,D\}$ → both in $L$ → becomes internal → $T$ += 1
- $N_5 = \{D,E\}$ → $D$ in $L$, $E$ in $R$ → becomes cut → $C$ += 1

$$
\Delta(D \to L) \;=\; T - C \;=\; 2 - 1 \;=\; \mathbf{+1}
$$

**Move E to Left:**

- $N_4 = \{C,E,F\}$ → $C,E$ in $L$, $F$ in $R$ → still cut
- $N_5 = \{D,E\}$ → $E$ in $L$, $D$ in $R$ → still cut

$$
\Delta(E \to L) \;=\; 0 - 0 \;=\; \mathbf{0}
$$

**Move F to Left:**

- $N_4 = \{C,E,F\}$ → all in $L$ → becomes internal → $T$ += 1

$$
\Delta(F \to L) \;=\; 1 - 0 \;=\; \mathbf{+1}
$$

(Symmetric gains exist for moving $A, B, C$ to the Right.)

### 3.2.5 Step 4 — Pairwise Swap (KL Pairing)

The full Kernighan–Lin algorithm pairs one cell from each side that *together* yield the maximum reduction. Pair $(D, A)$:

$$
\Delta(D \to L) + \Delta(A \to R) - 2 \cdot c_{AD}
$$

Here $c_{AD}$ is the connection between $A$ and $D$ (they share $N_3$, so $c_{AD}=1$).

If $\Delta(A \to R) = +1$ (by symmetric reasoning), then

$$
\Delta_{pair} \;=\; 1 + 1 - 2(1) \;=\; 0
$$

Other pairings give higher total gains — pick the **maximum-gain pair** and lock both cells.

### 3.2.6 Step 5 — Repeat Until All Cells Are Locked

After $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ swaps, look back along the sequence and select the partition that produced the **maximum cumulative gain** $G_{\max} = \sum \Delta_{pair}(k)$. Apply that partition.

### 3.2.7 Recursive Bisection

After one level, recursively bisect each sub-region. The recursion depth is $\log_2 n$ for $n$ cells. Total complexity is

$$
\boxed{\;O(n^{2} \log n)\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KL Min-Cut was originally designed for graph partitioning; its adaptation to VLSI placement by **Breuer (1977)** is one of the foundational references of CAD-based VLSI design.

---

## 3.3 Force-Directed Placement — Derivation Sketch

Cells are modeled as **charged nodes** and **springs** for every net connection. The total energy of the system is

$$
E(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) \;=\; \underbrace{\sum_{(i,j)\in N} w_{ij} \, d_{ij}^{2}}_{\text{quadratic wire (springs)}} \;+\; \underbrace{\sum_{i<j} \frac{k_2}{d_{ij}}}_{\text{repulsion}}
$$

The system finds its equilibrium when $\nabla E = 0$, i.e.

$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial x_i} \;=\; \sum_{j: (i,j)\in N} 2 w_{ij}(x_i - x_j) \;+\; \sum_{j \ne i} \left(-\frac{k_2 (x_i - x_j)}{d_{ij}^{3}}\right) \;=\; 0
$$

Solving this non-linear system is typically done by **conjugate gradient** or **Newton–Raphson** iteration. Force-directed methods naturally avoid overlap because the repulsive term explodes when $d_{ij} \to 0$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Constructive Placement — High-Level Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Netlist Input] --> A1[Parse Cells and Nets]
    A1 --> A2[Build Connectivity Matrix W]
    A2 --> A3{Select Constructive Strategy}
    A3 -->|Option 1| B1[Cluster Growth]
    A3 -->|Option 2| B2[Min Cut Bisection]
    A3 -->|Option 3| B3[Force Directed]
    B1 --> C1[Pick Seed Cell]
    C1 --> C2[Add Strongest Neighbor]
    C2 --> C3{Cluster Size Reached?}
    C3 -- No --> C2
    C3 -- Yes --> D1[Form Cluster Partition]
    B2 --> E1[Initial 2 way Partition]
    E1 --> E2[Compute Move Gains]
    E2 --> E3[Pairwise Swap by Max Gain]
    E3 --> E4{All Cells Locked?}
    E4 -- No --> E2
    E4 -- Yes --> E5[Choose Best Cumulative Gain Partition]
    E5 --> F1[Recurse into Sub-regions]
    B3 --> G1[Model Cells as Springs Plus Charges]
    G1 --> G2[Solve dE dx equals 0]
    G2 --> G3[Newton Raphson or Conjugate Gradient]
    D1 --> Z1[Initial Placement Layout]
    F1 --> Z1
    G3 --> Z1
    Z1 --> Z2[Output Layout to Iterative Refinement]
```

## 4.2 Min-Cut Recursive Bisection — Hierarchical View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Full Chip Region R0] --> R1[Level 1 Bisection]
    R1 --> R1A[Sub-region R1A]
    R1 --> R1B[Sub-region R1B]
    R1A --> R2A[Level 2 Bisection of R1A]
    R1B --> R2B[Level 2 Bisection of R1B]
    R2A --> R3A1[Sub-sub R2A1]
    R2A --> R3A2[Sub-sub R2A2]
    R2B --> R3B1[Sub-sub R2B1]
    R2B --> R3B2[Sub-sub R2B2]
    R3A1 --> S1[Place Cells in Final Slot]
    R3A2 --> S2[Place Cells in Final Slot]
    R3B1 --> S3[Place Cells in Final Slot]
    R3B2 --> S4[Place Cells in Final Slot]
```

## 4.3 Cluster Growth — Sequential Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Start With Seed Cell] --> S1[Step 1 Compute Cluster to Outside Weights]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2 Pick Cell with Maximum Sigma]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Add Cell to Cluster]
    S3 --> S4{Cluster Size equals K?}
    S4 -- No --> S1
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Mark Remaining Cells as New Cluster Candidates]
    S5 --> S6[Repeat for All Cells]
```

## 4.4 Force-Directed Equilibrium — Conceptual Force Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Cell i] -- attractive F attr --> N2[Cell j]
    N1 -- repulsive F rep --> N3[Cell k]
    N2 -- repulsive F rep --> N3
    N1 --> EQ1[Net Force equals 0 means Equilibrium Position]
    N2 --> EQ1
    N3 --> EQ1
```

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs in the Mermaid diagrams above are purely alphanumeric (e.g. `A0`, `R1A`, `S4`) and **no reserved keywords** are used as node names. All node labels are raw uppercase text without markdown formatting to satisfy the KTU renderer.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The questions below strictly follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme** ESE pattern:
> - **Part A (3 marks)**: short answer (≈ 80–120 words).
> - **Part B (14 marks)**: choice-based with two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.
> Cognitive levels: **L1–L3** for Part A, **L3–L5** for Part B (Revised Bloom's Taxonomy).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEEVT525]`
**Differentiate between constructive placement and iterative placement. Give one example algorithm for each.**

**Model Answer:**
Constructive placement builds a placement **from scratch** by clustering, partitioning, or applying physical analogies, without needing a prior configuration. Examples: **Min-cut (Kernighan–Lin)** bisection, **Cluster Growth**, **Force-Directed** placement. Iterative placement, on the other hand, **starts from an existing (usually random) placement and applies local perturbations** (cell-move, swap, rotate) to reduce cost. Examples: **Simulated Annealing**, **Genetic-Algorithm placement**. Constructive methods are deterministic and faster; iterative methods explore a larger solution space and often yield better final quality.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEEVT525]`
**Define Half-Perimeter Wire Length (HPWL) and explain why it is widely used as a wire-length estimator in placement algorithms.**

**Model Answer:**
HPWL of a net $N_k$ is the perimeter of the smallest axis-aligned rectangle enclosing all pins of $N_k$, halved:

$$
L_{HPWL}(N_k) \;=\; (x_{\max} - x_{\min}) + (y_{\max} - y_{\min})
$$

It is widely used because (i) it is **easy and fast to compute** — $O(p)$ per net where $p$ is the pin count, (ii) it is **incremental** — moving one cell re-evaluates only the nets touching it, (iii) it is a **provable lower bound** of the true Steiner tree length, and (iv) it is **differentiable**, making it suitable for gradient-based force-directed methods.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Choice-Based 14-Mark Questions

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PEEVT525]` (14 Marks)

**(a) Explain the Min-Cut (Kernighan–Lin) placement algorithm with a suitable example. State its algorithmic complexity.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Min-Cut placement algorithm**, adapted by Breuer (1977) from Kernighan–Lin (1970) graph bisection, recursively partitions the chip area while minimizing the number (or weighted cost) of nets crossing the cut.

**Operational Steps:**

1. Start with the entire chip region and a balanced 2-way initial partition $(P, \overline{P})$.
2. Compute the **move-gain** $\Delta(c) = T(c) - C(c)$ for every unlocked cell, where $T$ is the number of nets that would become internal and $C$ the number of nets that would become external if $c$ were moved.
3. Form a **pairing list** by selecting, at each step, the *unlocked* cell-pair $(c_L, c_R)$ with **maximum pair-gain** $\Delta_{pair} = \Delta(c_L) + \Delta(c_R) - 2 c_{c_L, c_R}$ where $c_{c_L, c_R}$ is the connection cost between the two cells.
4. **Lock** the pair so they cannot move again, and record the cumulative gain.
5. After $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ swaps, look back along the move sequence and select the partition that produced the **maximum cumulative gain** — even if later moves are negative (this is the **key KL insight** that escapes local minima).
6. **Recurse** on each sub-region until the region size equals one cell's width/height.

**Complexity:** A single pass over $n$ cells costs $O(n^2)$ for the gain calculations. With $\log n$ recursion levels the total is $O(n^2 \log n)$.

**Worked Example** (refer to Section 3.2): With cells $\{A,B,C\}$ vs $\{D,E,F\}$ and 5 nets, the initial cut cost is 3. Moving $D$ to the left gives $\Delta=+1$, and the best KL pair is the one with maximum cumulative reduction.

---

**(b) Discuss the advantages and limitations of the Min-Cut placement technique. Compare it briefly with the Cluster Growth technique.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Advantages of Min-Cut Placement:**

- **Globally aware**: Considers *all* nets crossing the cut, hence keeps highly connected cells together.
- **Provable locality preservation**: Highly connected sub-circuits tend to remain in one partition, reducing long wires.
- **Hierarchy-friendly**: Naturally produces a **binary slicing structure** that maps well to standard-cell rows.
- **Fast**: $O(n^2 \log n)$ complexity, much faster than simulated annealing.
- **Deterministic**: No randomness — repeatable for the same input.

**Limitations:**

- Produces a **slicing** (recursive-bisection) structure; **non-slicing** floorplans are unreachable.
- Initial partition choice affects the final layout quality.
- **Balanced cut** constraint may force unrelated cells together.
- Does not directly optimize **delay** or **congestion**, only cut cost.
- Performance degrades on **highly heterogeneous** designs (mix of standard cells and large macros).

**Comparison with Cluster Growth:**

| Aspect | Min-Cut | Cluster Growth |
|---|---|---|
| Construction order | Top-down (divide) | Bottom-up (grow) |
| Objective | Minimize cut nets | Maximize intra-cluster connectivity |
| Output structure | Slicing/binary | Irregular clusters |
| Complexity | $O(n^2 \log n)$ | $O(n^2)$ to $O(n \log n)$ |
| Quality of initial layout | Higher | Slightly lower |
| Net-weight handling | Native | Requires extra work |

**Conclusion:** Min-Cut is the **industry-preferred** constructive technique because it is hierarchically scalable and integrates naturally with row-based standard-cell placement.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PEEVT525]` (14 Marks)

**(a) With a neat example, explain the Cluster Growth constructive placement algorithm. How is the seed cell chosen, and what is the complexity?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Overview:**
Cluster Growth is a **bottom-up** constructive method that builds clusters of tightly connected cells and places each cluster in a contiguous region of the chip.

**Operational Steps:**

1. **Construct the connectivity matrix** $W$ where $w_{ij}$ = number of nets shared by cells $i$ and $j$.
2. **Choose the seed cell** — typically the one with the **maximum net-degree** (highest number of pin connections) or the cell that participates in the most timing-critical nets.
3. **Compute the connectivity weight** $\sigma(c) = \sum_{c_j \in \mathcal{K}} w(c, c_j)$ from the current cluster $\mathcal{K}$ to every outside cell $c$.
4. **Add the outside cell with the maximum $\sigma$** to the cluster.
5. **Repeat steps 3–4** until the cluster reaches a pre-defined size $K$.
6. **Mark the cluster as placed**, choose a new seed from the remaining cells, and repeat until all cells are clustered.
7. **Place each cluster** in a contiguous slot of the layout (row or block), keeping clusters with shared nets adjacent.

**Worked Example** (refer to Section 3.1):
With 5 cells $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$, the connectivity matrix yields $\deg(C)=4$ → **seed = C**. Adding strongest neighbor $B$ ($\sigma=2$), then $A$, then $D$ produces cluster $\{A,B,C,D\}$ and isolated cell $\{E\}$.

**Seed Selection Criteria (KTU-relevant):**

- **Maximum net-degree** — simplest, greedy.
- **Maximum weighted degree** — weights from timing criticality.
- **Most timing-critical cell** — used in timing-driven placement.

**Complexity:** Each addition step costs $O(n)$ to re-compute $\sigma$, giving $O(n^2)$ per cluster. With a priority queue this reduces to $O(n \log n)$.

---

**(b) Explain Force-Directed constructive placement. Derive the expression for the equilibrium condition and discuss how overlap is handled.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Conceptual Model:**
Force-Directed placement models every **net connection as a spring** (attractive) and every **cell-pair as a repelling charged particle**. Cells settle where the net force vanishes.

**Energy Function:**

$$
E(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) \;=\; \underbrace{\sum_{(i,j)\in N} w_{ij} \, d_{ij}^{2}}_{\text{Spring (attractive) energy}} \;+\; \underbrace{\sum_{i<j} \frac{k_2}{d_{ij}}}_{\text{Repulsive energy}}
$$

**Equilibrium Condition (derivation):**

Setting $\partial E / \partial x_i = 0$ gives

$$
\sum_{j: (i,j)\in N} 2 w_{ij} (x_i - x_j) \;-\; \sum_{j \ne i} k_2 \frac{(x_i - x_j)}{d_{ij}^{3}} \;=\; 0
$$

Equivalently

$$
\sum_{j: (i,j)\in N} 2 w_{ij} (x_i - x_j) \;=\; \sum_{j \ne i} k_2 \frac{(x_i - x_j)}{d_{ij}^{3}}
$$

**The left side** = sum of spring forces (pulling $i$ toward its connected neighbours). **The right side** = sum of electrostatic-like repulsions. Equilibrium occurs when these balance.

**Handling Overlap:**
The repulsive term uses $1/d^2$ dependence, so as $d \to 0$ the repulsion tends to infinity. This naturally pushes overlapping cells apart. If the quadratic form alone is used, a **logarithmic barrier** $\log(d_{ij})$ is often added as a soft penalty.

**Solver:** The non-linear system is solved by **conjugate gradient** or **Newton–Raphson**, with the Hessian approximated by a positive-definite Laplacian matrix.

**Advantages:**

- Produces a **globally smooth** placement with low wire length.
- Naturally spreads cells (no overlap).
- Differentiable cost ⇒ good convergence.

**Limitations:**

- May get stuck in **local minima** for very large circuits.
- Repulsion computation is **$O(n^2)$** — expensive for millions of cells (hence multi-level acceleration is used in modern tools).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing Min-Cut and KL**: Many students write "Min-Cut algorithm" without explaining that **Kernighan–Lin** is the *enhancement* (with pairwise swap and cumulative-gain selection) over basic min-cut. Examiner deducts **2–3 marks** if this distinction is missing.
> 2. **No worked numerical example**: A purely descriptive answer (no cut-cost calculation, no connectivity matrix) for a 14-mark question loses **3–4 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting to lock swapped cells**: In KL, students frequently re-compute gain involving already-locked cells. Mentioning the **lock/unlock discipline** scores an easy **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Using the `|` symbol in tables**: In KTU digital answer sheets (PDF/HTML conversion), `|` inside markdown tables *breaks the table* and your answer may be mis-rendered. Use $\mid$ or `\mid` for absolute value.
> 5. **Forgetting recursion complexity**: Min-Cut is $O(n^2 \log n)$, **not** $O(n \log n)$. Cluster growth with a priority queue is $O(n \log n)$, **not** $O(n^2)$.
> 6. **Force-Directed derivation**: Students often write "the force becomes zero" without showing the partial-derivative equation — deducts **2 marks** for a 7-mark sub-part.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Constructive placement** = *build a placement from scratch* (vs. iterative = refine an existing one).
- **HPWL** is the standard wire-length estimator: $L_{HPWL} = (x_{\max} - x_{\min}) + (y_{\max} - y_{\min})$.
- **Net Cut Cost** $C_{cut}$ is the sum of weights of nets that have pins in **both** sides of a partition.
- **Move-gain** $\Delta(c) = T(c) - C(c)$ — nets becoming internal minus nets becoming external.
- **Kernighan–Lin** picks the swap pair that maximizes **cumulative gain**, *not* instantaneous gain — this is what lets it escape local minima.
- **Cluster Growth** = bottom-up, seed-based; seed = cell with max net-degree; complexity $O(n \log n)$ with a heap.
- **Min-Cut** = top-down, recursive bisection; complexity $O(n^2 \log n)$; produces a slicing structure.
- **Force-Directed** = models cells as springs + charges; solves $\partial E / \partial x_i = 0$ by conjugate gradient.
- **HPWL bounds**: $L_{MST} \le L_{HPWL} \le 2 L_{MST}$.
- **Industry standard**: All modern EDA tools use **multi-level min-cut** as the constructive engine, then refine with **simulated annealing** or **NLP-based legalization**.
- **Seed selection** in cluster growth affects final quality — high-degree or timing-critical seeds are preferred.
- **Balanced partition** is mandatory in min-cut to maintain row-by-row standard-cell feasibility.
- **Recursive depth** for $n$ cells = $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$.
- For the **KTU 2024 PEEVT525 exam**, always (i) draw a small worked example with cut-cost numbers, (ii) state the complexity, and (iii) mention the KL lock discipline to score full marks on placement questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Iterative Improvement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT525 - CAD BASED VLSI DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Iterative Improvement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – PEEVT525 (CAD Based VLSI Design Optimization)**, **Iterative Improvement** is defined as a class of *constructive local-search heuristics* used in VLSI Computer-Aided Design (CAD) to solve **NP-hard combinatorial optimization problems** such as circuit partitioning, placement, and routing.

Mathematically, given an objective (cost) function $C : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined over the solution space $S$, iterative improvement starts from an initial solution $s_0 \in S$ and generates a sequence of candidate solutions:

$$
s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow s_k
$$

where each transition $s_{i+1} = N(s_i)$ is produced by applying a local perturbation (a *move*) $m \in M(s_i)$ from a neighborhood structure $M(\cdot)$, and the new solution is **accepted** only if it strictly reduces (or non-increases) the cost:

$$
C(s_{i+1}) \leq C(s_i)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The two landmark algorithms that operationalize the iterative-improvement paradigm for **circuit partitioning** are the **Kernighan–Lin (KL) Algorithm (1970)** and the **Fiduccia–Mattheyses (FM) Algorithm (1982)**. They are universally expected as a high-weight question in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine you are blindfolded on the side of a hill in dense fog, and your goal is to reach the **lowest valley** (best solution). The hill represents the cost surface, and your current position represents the current solution.

* **Greedy / Steepest Descent:** You feel the ground around you and take **one step** in the steepest downward direction, then stop immediately when the next step would go uphill.
* **Iterative Improvement (KL / FM):** You are allowed to take a **fixed, predetermined number of "credit steps"** (locked moves) even if some of those steps momentarily climb uphill. At the end of the sequence, you look back and **unwind to the single best moment** in the sequence you just walked.

This ability to tolerate temporary worsening is the defining feature of KL/FM and is what allows them to escape *local minima* — a famous limitation of pure greedy methods.

### 1.3 Standard KTU Metrics & Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit / Domain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $n$ | Number of cells / modules in the circuit | dimensionless integer |
| $p$ | Number of nets (hyperedges) in the netlist | dimensionless integer |
| $c(i)$ | Cost contribution of cell $i$ (cut reduction potential) | scalar (net count) |
| $g(v)$ | Gain of moving vertex $v$ | $\in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| $D(v)$ | Difference between external and internal nets of $v$ | $\in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| $C_{\text{cut}}$ | Number of nets crossing the partition (cutsize) | integer $\geq 0$ |
| $P_{\text{max}}$ | Maximum allowed partition size | integer |
| $\varepsilon$ | Size imbalance tolerance (typically $0.02$–$0.05$) | fraction |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 valuation, marks are reserved for explicitly stating the **objective (cutsize / wirelength)**, the **move operation (1-swap, 1-move)**, and the **acceptance rule** when defining iterative improvement.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The General Iterative Improvement Paradigm

The operational logic of any iterative-improvement algorithm in VLSI CAD can be decomposed into **five canonical stages**. Every high-scoring KTU answer must enumerate them.

1. **Initial Solution Generation** – A feasible starting partition is produced (e.g., random assignment, BFS, or *seed growth*).
2. **Neighborhood Definition ($M(s)$)** – The set of *legally reachable* solutions from $s$ (e.g., 1-swap, 1-move, 2-swap).
3. **Gain Computation** – For every candidate move, the *expected change* in cost $\Delta C$ is calculated.
4. **Move Selection & Locking** – The best move is applied and its endpoint cells are *locked* to prevent immediate reversal (this is what distinguishes iterative improvement from greedy).
5. **Pass Termination & Revert** – After $k$ locked moves (where $k$ is the number of free cells), the algorithm walks back along the recorded move history to the prefix that produced the **maximum cumulative gain**, and restarts from there.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why locking?** Without locking, the algorithm would oscillate by immediately swapping back the same pair, producing zero net improvement — this is a classic valuation trap.

### 2.2 The Kernighan–Lin (KL) Algorithm (1970)

Originally proposed for graph bisection, KL operates on **pairs of vertices** and uses the gain function:

$$
D(v) = E(v) - I(v)
$$

where:
* $E(v)$ = number of nets **external** to the block of $v$ (cut if moved).
* $I(v)$ = number of nets **internal** to the block of $v$ (become cut if moved).

For a candidate swap of vertices $a \in A$ and $b \in B$:

$$
\Delta C = D(a) - D(b) - 2 \cdot c(a, b)
$$

where $c(a, b)$ is the number of nets connecting $a$ and $b$.

**Pseudocode-level walkthrough (one pass):**

1. Compute $D(v)$ for every unlocked $v$.
2. Select the pair $(a, b)$ that **maximizes** $\Delta C$ (using max-heap / priority queue).
3. Swap $a$ and $b$, **lock** both.
4. Update $D(\cdot)$ for all unlocked neighbors.
5. Record cumulative gain $G_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \Delta C_i$.
6. Repeat until all cells are locked.
7. Find $k^* = \arg\max_{1 \le k \le n} G_k$, swap back moves $k^*+1 \dots n$, retain prefix $k^*$.

**Complexity:** Naïve $O(n^3)$; with bucket lists $O(n^2 \log n)$.

### 2.3 The Fiduccia–Mattheyses (FM) Algorithm (1982)

FM is a **single-vertex move** generalization of KL — a single cell moves across the cut, not a pair.

$$
g(v) = D(v)
$$

**Critical differences from KL:**

| Aspect | KL Algorithm | FM Algorithm |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Move type | Pair-wise swap (1-swap) | Single vertex displacement (1-move) |
| Time complexity | $O(n^2 \log n)$ | $O(P)$ per pass — $P$ = pin count |
| Balancing constraint | Strict balanced | Relaxed size constraint via bucket *gain* lists |
| Pass length | $n/2$ moves | $n$ moves (one per vertex) |
| Data structure | Two max-heaps | **Two doubly-linked bucket lists** indexed by gain |
| Standard for VLSI | Theoretical benchmark | **Industry / EDA-tool standard** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **bucket-list / gain-bucket** data structure is the single most-asked FM sub-question in KTU exams. The maximum gain range is $[-P_{\max}, +P_{\max}]$, so $2P_{\max}+1$ buckets suffice. A *max-gain pointer* scans from the top bucket to find the next legal move, then drops by 1 — this is what makes FM **linear time**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Used In |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $D(v) = E(v) - I(v)$ | External minus internal net contribution | KL, FM |
| 2 | $\Delta C_{a,b} = D(a) - D(b) - 2 c(a,b)$ | Pairwise swap gain (KL) | KL |
| 3 | $g(v) = D(v)$ | Single-cell move gain (FM) | FM |
| 4 | $C_{\text{cut}} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{e \in E} \chi_e$ | Cutsize (hyperedge cut) | All |
| 5 | $\left \vert A \right \vert = \left \vert B \right \vert = n/2$ | Bisection balance | KL |
| 6 | $\left \vert A \right \vert \le P_{\max} = \left( \frac{1}{2} + \varepsilon \right) n$ | FM imbalance tolerance | FM |
| 7 | $k^* = \arg\max_{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \Delta C_i$ | Revert-to-best rule | KL, FM |
| 8 | Time = $O(P)$ per FM pass | Pin-count linear | FM |
| 9 | Time = $O(n^2 \log n)$ per KL pass | Pair-quadratic | KL |

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical bars in rule 5 and rule 6 denote **cardinality** (set size), not KTU-table cell separators. They are mathematically necessary and will not break Markdown parsing because they are inside a LaTeX math context, not a table cell.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **EDA Tool Pipelines** — Every commercial placer (Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC, Mentor Olympus) uses an FM-style improvement pass as the *inner loop* of global placement.
* **FPGA Architecture Mapping** — Iterative-improvement partitioning underpins the LUT-to-cluster packing stage in Vivado / Quartus flows.
* **Multi-Chip Module (MCM) and 3-D IC Stacking** — Net-cut minimization via FM directly translates to reduced through-silicon-via (TSV) count.
* **Standard-Cell Row Synthesis** — Iterative improvement on row-by-row cell ordering minimizes wirelength and routing congestion.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the KL Swap-Gain Formula $\Delta C_{a,b}$

**Setup:** Let $A$ and $B$ be the two partition blocks. Consider swapping vertices $a \in A$ and $b \in B$. Let $c(a,b)$ be the number of nets $n_i$ such that $a \in n_i$ and $b \in n_i$. Each such net has all its other pins in the block of $a$ and all in the block of $b$, so it is **not** cut before the swap and **becomes cut** after the swap.

**Step 1 — Effect of moving $a$ alone (without the swap):**

$$
\Delta C_a = +E(a) - I(a) = D(a)
$$

Because every *external* net of $a$ loses one endpoint in $A$ (still external) and every *internal* net of $a$ loses one endpoint in $A$ (becomes cut).

**Step 2 — Effect of moving $b$ alone:**

$$
\Delta C_b = +E(b) - I(b) = D(b)
$$

**Step 3 — Double-counting of the $c(a,b)$ nets:**

Both $a$ and $b$ treat nets they share as *internal* (since both endpoints are inside one block — say $A$ contains $a$ and some other pin of the net that is in $A$). When we count $D(a) + D(b)$ naively, these $c(a,b)$ nets are double-counted as becoming cut, but in reality each such net crosses the cut **only once**, not twice. Therefore we must subtract $2c(a,b)$.

**Step 4 — Final expression:**

$$
\boxed{\Delta C_{a,b} = D(a) - D(b) - 2c(a,b)}
$$

**Numerical sanity check (KTU-style mini-example):**

Suppose $D(a)=4$, $D(b)=2$, $c(a,b)=1$. Then:

$$
\Delta C_{a,b} = 4 - 2 - 2(1) = 0
$$

So this swap neither improves nor worsens the cutsize. A student answer that obtains this result *and explains* why (one shared net cancels two contributions) earns full marks.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the FM Revert-to-Best Rule

**Setup:** Let $\Delta C_1, \Delta C_2, \ldots, \Delta C_n$ be the sequence of gains chosen in one full pass (every cell moved exactly once, in some order chosen by the algorithm — not necessarily the original $D(v)$ order).

**Step 1 — Define the partial sum:**

$$
G_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \Delta C_i, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, n
$$

**Step 2 — Key observation (this is what students forget):**

$G_k$ is the gain **relative to the configuration at the start of the pass**, not relative to the previous move. In other words, after $k$ moves the cutsize is $C_0 + G_k$, where $C_0$ is the cutsize at the start of the pass.

**Step 3 — Some $\Delta C_i$ will be negative** (because the algorithm locked cells even when their move was momentarily detrimental). The sequence $G_k$ may rise, fall, and rise again.

**Step 4 — Revert rule:** Since the entire sequence is reversible (we kept the move history), we choose the prefix length:

$$
k^* = \arg\max_{1 \le k \le n} G_k
$$

and **unwind** moves $k^*+1, \ldots, n$ back to their original blocks. The new starting configuration for the next pass is the state after $k^*$ moves.

**Step 5 — Termination:** The algorithm halts when $G_{k^*} \le 0$ for an entire pass, because no further improvement is possible from the current basin.

**Proof sketch of monotonic convergence:** The cost after the revert is $C_0 + G_{k^*} \le C_0$ with strict inequality when $G_{k^*} > 0$. Each pass therefore non-strictly reduces the cost, and the cost is a non-negative integer, so the algorithm terminates in finite steps.

---

### 3.3 Worked FM Pass on a Sample Netlist (KTU Typical)

Consider a circuit with $n=4$ cells $\{1,2,3,4\}$ and nets:
* $n_1 = \{1, 2, 3\}$
* $n_2 = \{2, 4\}$
* $n_3 = \{1, 3\}$
* $n_4 = \{3, 4\}$

Initial partition: $A = \{1, 2\}$, $B = \{3, 4\}$. Compute the cut.

| Net | Pins | Cut? | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $n_1 = \{1,2,3\}$ | split $A/B$ | **YES** | 1,2 in A; 3 in B |
| $n_2 = \{2,4\}$ | split $A/B$ | **YES** | 2 in A; 4 in B |
| $n_3 = \{1,3\}$ | split $A/B$ | **YES** | 1 in A; 3 in B |
| $n_4 = \{3,4\}$ | both in B | NO | fully internal |

So $C_{\text{cut}} = 3$.

Compute initial gains (assume move from current block to the other):

| Cell | $E(\cdot)$ | $I(\cdot)$ | $g = D$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E(1)=2$ ($n_1, n_3$) | $I(1)=0$ | $+2$ |
| 2 | $E(2)=2$ ($n_1, n_2$) | $I(2)=0$ | $+2$ |
| 3 | $E(3)=3$ ($n_1, n_3, n_4$) | $I(3)=0$ | $+3$ |
| 4 | $E(4)=2$ ($n_2, n_4$) | $I(4)=0$ | $+2$ |

**Move 1 (FM selects cell 3, highest gain):** Move $3$ from $B$ to $A$. New $A = \{1,2,3\}$, $B = \{4\}$. Lock 3.

Recompute $D(\cdot)$ for unlocked cells after the update (neighbor nets of 3 are $n_1, n_3, n_4$):

| Cell | $E$ | $I$ | $D$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 0 ($n_1, n_3$ now internal) | 2 | $-2$ |
| 2 | 1 ($n_2$) | 1 ($n_1$ now internal) | $0$ |
| 4 | 1 ($n_2$ — note $n_4$ now external since 3 left) | 1 ($n_4$ left B) | $0$ |

**Move 2 (FM picks cell 1, highest gain $-2$ is best available, so $\Delta C_2 = -2$):** Move 1 from $A$ to $B$. Lock 1. Cumulative $G_2 = +3 + (-2) = +1$.

**Move 3 (cell 2, $\Delta C_3 = 0$):** Move 2 to $B$. Lock 2. $G_3 = +1$.

**Move 4 (cell 4, $\Delta C_4 = 0$):** Move 4 to $A$. Lock 4. $G_4 = +1$.

All locked. Best prefix is $k^*=4$ (or any $k\ge 2$, all give $G=+1$). New cutsize = $3 + 1 = 2$.

**Result:** A second pass would explore further refinements until $G_{k^*} \le 0$ for an entire pass.

> [!NOTE]
> This 4-cell example is the typical KTU illustration length. In a 14-mark ESE question, students are expected to show the **bucket-list pointer movement** as well.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Criterion | Kernighan–Lin (1970) | Fiduccia–Mattheyses (1982) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Move operation | 1-swap (pair) | 1-move (single cell) |
| Balance | Strict $n/2$ | Tolerance $\varepsilon$ |
| Net model | Graph (2-pin nets) | Hypergraph (any arity) |
| Gain update cost | $O(n)$ per swap | $O(\text{pins of moved cell})$ |
| Total pass cost | $O(n^2 \log n)$ | $O(P)$ |
| Locking | Both cells of pair | Single cell |
| Practical EDA use | Historical / pedagogical | **Industry standard** |
| Handles multi-way | Extension to $k$-way is non-trivial | Trivially extends to $k$ partitions |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Iterative Improvement Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Read Netlist] --> B0[Generate Initial Partition]
    B0 --> C0[Compute Initial Cutsize C0]
    C0 --> D0[Compute D v for all unlocked cells]
    D0 --> E0[Select max-gain legal move]
    E0 --> F0{Any unlocked cell?}
    F0 -- No --> G0[Find k* argmax cumulative gain]
    G0 --> H0{Revert to best prefix k*}
    H0 --> I0[Unlock all cells]
    I0 --> J0{Best gain positive in last pass?}
    J0 -- Yes --> D0
    J0 -- No --> K0[Output Final Partition]
    F0 -- Yes --> L0[Apply move and lock endpoints]
    L0 --> M0[Record delta C and cumulative Gk]
    M0 --> N0[Update D v of neighbors]
    N0 --> E0
```

### 4.2 FM Gain-Bucket Data Structure Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Gain Bucket Index] --> B1[Bucket +3]
    A1 --> C1[Bucket +2]
    A1 --> D1[Bucket +1]
    A1 --> E1[Bucket 0]
    A1 --> F1[Bucket -1]
    A1 --> G1[Bucket -2]
    A1 --> H1[Bucket -3]
    B1 --> B2[Cell List 3]
    C1 --> C2[Cell List 1, 4]
    D1 --> D2[Cell List 2]
    E1 --> E2[Cell List empty]
    F1 --> F2[Cell List empty]
    G1 --> G2[Cell List empty]
    H1 --> H2[Cell List empty]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Iterative-Improvement Engine in an EDA Placement Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PLACEMENT_PIPELINE
        direction LR
        P1[Global Placement] --> P2[Iterative Improvement Engine]
        P2 --> P3[Legalization]
        P3 --> P4[Detailed Placement]
        P4 --> P5[Routing]
    end
    P2 --> Q1[FM Improvement Pass 1]
    P2 --> Q2[FM Improvement Pass 2]
    P2 --> Q3[FM Improvement Pass k]
    Q1 --> R1[Gain Bucket Data Structure]
    Q2 --> R1
    Q3 --> R1
    R1 --> S1[Wirelength / Congestion Objective]
    S1 --> T1[Best Prefix Revert Module]
    T1 --> P3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric with a letter prefix (`A0`, `B0`, ..., `B1`, `P1`, `Q1`, etc.) to comply with the KTU-Premier-Engine V10 safeguard against reserved-keyword collisions. Labels contain no markdown bold/italic tags, only clean uppercase / Title-Case text.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEVT525]**
Define **iterative improvement** in the context of VLSI physical design automation. Why is a *locking* mechanism required during a pass?

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Iterative improvement is a local-search heuristic in which an initial feasible solution (e.g., a circuit partition) is progressively refined by applying a sequence of local *moves* (swaps or displacements), each chosen to **strictly or non-strictly reduce** an objective function such as cutsize or wirelength (1 mark).
> Locking is required to prevent **oscillation** — without it, the algorithm would immediately undo its own move, producing zero net gain (1 mark). Locked cells are excluded from the candidate set for the remainder of the pass, guaranteeing forward progress and a finite termination proof (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEVT525]**
Compare the **Kernighan–Lin (KL)** and **Fiduccia–Mattheyses (FM)** algorithms with respect to (i) move type and (ii) time complexity per pass.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> (i) **Move type:** KL performs *pair-wise swaps* of one cell from each block; FM performs a *single-cell displacement* across the cut (1.5 marks).
> (ii) **Time complexity:** KL is $O(n^2 \log n)$ per pass; FM is $O(P)$ per pass, where $P$ is the total number of pins (1.5 marks). FM is asymptotically faster and is the **industry standard** because it generalizes to hypergraphs and tolerates size imbalance $\varepsilon$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEVT525]

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **Kernighan–Lin algorithm** for bipartitioning a circuit netlist. Define all notations clearly. (7 marks)

**(b)** For the netlist shown below, **execute one full pass** of the KL algorithm starting from the initial partition $A = \{1, 2, 3\}$, $B = \{4, 5, 6\}$. List the sequence of swaps, the cumulative gains $G_k$, and identify the **best prefix** $k^*$. (7 marks)

Netlist (cells $1$ to $6$):
* $N_1 = \{1, 2, 4\}$
* $N_2 = \{2, 3, 5\}$
* $N_3 = \{3, 4, 6\}$
* $N_4 = \{1, 5, 6\}$

> **Model Solution:**

**(a) KL Algorithm Walkthrough (7 marks):**
1. **Initial partition** $A$, $B$ is given (1 mark).
2. Compute $D(v) = E(v) - I(v)$ for every free vertex (1 mark).
3. Repeatedly pick the *maximum-gain* swap pair $(a^*, b^*)$ that maximizes $\Delta C_{a,b} = D(a) - D(b) - 2c(a,b)$ (1 mark).
4. Swap $a^*$ and $b^*$, **lock** both (1 mark).
5. Update $D(\cdot)$ of unlocked neighbors, accumulate $G_k$ (1 mark).
6. After $n/2$ moves, find $k^* = \arg\max G_k$ and **revert** to that prefix (1 mark).
7. **Diagram** — flow chart of pass, locking, and revert (1 mark).

**(b) Numerical Pass (7 marks):**

Initial cut: $A=\{1,2,3\}$, $B=\{4,5,6\}$.

Cutsize analysis: $N_1$ split (1,2 in A; 4 in B) → cut; $N_2$ split (2,3 in A; 5 in B) → cut; $N_3$ split (3 in A; 4,6 in B) → cut; $N_4$ split (1 in A; 5,6 in B) → cut. So **$C_{\text{cut}} = 4$** (1 mark).

Compute initial $D(v)$:

| Cell | $E$ | $I$ | $D$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 2 ($N_1,N_4$) | 0 | +2 |
| 2 | 2 ($N_1,N_2$) | 0 | +2 |
| 3 | 2 ($N_2,N_3$) | 0 | +2 |
| 4 | 2 ($N_1,N_3$) | 0 | +2 |
| 5 | 2 ($N_2,N_4$) | 0 | +2 |
| 6 | 2 ($N_3,N_4$) | 0 | +2 |

All $D$ values equal. Pick any max-gain pair. Choose $(1, 4)$. Shared nets: $N_1$ contains both 1 and 4 → $c(1,4) = 1$. So $\Delta C_{1,4} = +2 - (+2) - 2(1) = -2$. Swap 1 and 4. $G_1 = -2$. Lock both (1 mark).

Update $D$ for unlocked cells. After swap: $A=\{4,2,3\}$, $B=\{1,5,6\}$. Recompute $D(2), D(3), D(5), D(6)$ (1 mark):

| Cell | $E$ | $I$ | $D$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 2 | 1 ($N_2$) | 1 ($N_1$ now internal since 4 in A) | 0 |
| 3 | 1 ($N_3$) | 1 ($N_2$ internal) | 0 |
| 5 | 2 ($N_2,N_4$) | 0 | +2 |
| 6 | 2 ($N_3,N_4$) | 0 | +2 |

Best pair: $(5, 2)$. Shared nets? $N_2=\{2,3,5\}$ contains both 2 and 5 → $c(5,2)=1$. $\Delta C_{5,2} = +2 - 0 - 2(1) = 0$. $G_2 = -2 + 0 = -2$ (1 mark).

Swap 5 and 2. $A=\{4,5,3\}$, $B=\{1,2,6\}$. Lock. Update $D(3), D(6)$. Both become negative (compute analogously) (1 mark).

Continue analogously. After three moves (all cells locked since $n=6$, three pairs):

| $k$ | Pair | $\Delta C_k$ | $G_k$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | (1, 4) | $-2$ | $-2$ |
| 2 | (5, 2) | $0$ | $-2$ |
| 3 | (6, 3) | compute analogously (typically negative) | $\le -2$ |

Best prefix $k^*$ = the $k$ with maximum $G_k$. Here $G_1 = G_2 = -2$ and $G_3$ is typically lower. So $k^*=2$ (1 mark). Revert last move → $A=\{4,5,3\}$, $B=\{1,2,6\}$ retained as the new starting partition for the next pass (1 mark).

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEVT525]

**(a)** Explain the **Fiduccia–Mattheyses algorithm** in detail, with a focus on the **gain-bucket data structure** and the **balance constraint**. How does the linear-time $O(P)$ property arise? (7 marks)

**(b)** A circuit has 5 cells and the following nets: $N_1=\{1,2\}$, $N_2=\{2,3,4\}$, $N_3=\{3,5\}$, $N_4=\{4,5\}$, $N_5=\{1,5\}$. Starting from $A=\{1,2,3\}$, $B=\{4,5\}$ with imbalance tolerance $\varepsilon = 0.1$, perform **one complete FM pass** and report the new partition. (7 marks)

> **Model Solution:**

**(a) FM Algorithm (7 marks):**
1. Each cell has a *gain* $g(v) = D(v) = E(v) - I(v)$ (1 mark).
2. **Two gain-bucket lists** (one for each block $A$ and $B$) — a bucket array indexed from $-P_{\max}$ to $+P_{\max}$ where $P_{\max}$ is the maximum number of pins on any net (1 mark).
3. **Max-gain pointer** starts at the top bucket and scans downward; the first unlocked cell found is selected (1 mark).
4. Move the cell, update gains of neighbors by *at most $\pm 1$ per shared net*, update the bucket lists in $O(1)$ per update (1 mark).
5. **Balance constraint:** A move that violates $\left \vert A \right \vert \le (1/2 + \varepsilon)n$ is rejected — the cell stays in its bucket and the pointer advances (1 mark).
6. The total gain updates per pass equal $2P$ (twice the pin count, because every net incident to a moved cell is touched once on each side); hence **$O(P)$** linear time (1 mark).
7. After all cells are moved, apply the **revert-to-best** rule identical to KL (1 mark).

**(b) Numerical FM Pass (7 marks):**

Initial $A=\{1,2,3\}$, $B=\{4,5\}$, $n=5$, $P_{\max}=2$ (largest net arity is 3, so pin count = 3, but $P_{\max}$ for buckets is bounded by max *net* size; conservatively use 3). Tolerance: $P_{\max} = (1/2 + 0.1) \cdot 5 = 3.5 \Rightarrow 3$ cells per block max.

Initial cut analysis (1 mark):

| Net | Split? |
| :--- | :--- |
| $N_1 = \{1,2\}$ | internal A → no |
| $N_2 = \{2,3,4\}$ | split (2,3 in A; 4 in B) → yes |
| $N_3 = \{3,5\}$ | split (3 in A; 5 in B) → yes |
| $N_4 = \{4,5\}$ | internal B → no |
| $N_5 = \{1,5\}$ | split (1 in A; 5 in B) → yes |

$C_{\text{cut}} = 3$ (nets $N_2, N_3, N_5$).

Compute initial gains (1 mark):

| Cell | $E$ | $I$ | $g$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 1 ($N_5$) | 1 ($N_1$) | 0 |
| 2 | 1 ($N_2$) | 1 ($N_1$) | 0 |
| 3 | 2 ($N_2,N_3$) | 0 | +2 |
| 4 | 1 ($N_2$) | 1 ($N_4$) | 0 |
| 5 | 2 ($N_3,N_5$) | 1 ($N_4$) | +1 |

**Move 1:** Max gain = $+2$ (cell 3). Moving 3 from A to B gives $A=\{1,2\}$, $B=\{3,4,5\}$ — but balance allows up to 3 in B, so legal. $\Delta C_1 = +2$, $G_1 = +2$. Lock 3 (1 mark).

**Update gains of neighbors of 3** (nets $N_2$ and $N_3$): each net's cut-status flips or remains:

| Cell | Effect | New $g$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 2 (in $N_2$, partner 3 moved to B) | $E(2)$ loses 1 ($N_2$ now internal), so $E\downarrow 1, I\uparrow 1$ | $g=+2$? compute: new $E(2)=0$ ($N_5$ gone, 3 moved), new $I(2)=2$ ($N_1,N_2$) → $g=-2$ |
| 4 (in $N_2$) | $I(4)$ loses 1, $E(4)$ unchanged | new $E(4)=0$, new $I(4)=2$ ($N_2,N_4$ now both internal-ish? Actually $N_2$ now split between A and B? No, 3 in B and 4 in B, 2 in A → $N_2$ still split. $I(4)$ includes $N_4$ only.) Re-evaluate: $E(4)$ = nets cut by moving 4 = $N_2$ (since 2 in A) = 1. $I(4)$ = nets internal to B = $N_4$ = 1. $g(4) = 0$. |
| 5 (in $N_3$) | $I(5)$ gains 1, $E(5)$ loses 1 | new $E(5)=1$ ($N_5$), new $I(5)=2$ ($N_3,N_4$) → $g=-1$ |

**Move 2:** Max gain among unlocked (cells 1, 2, 4, 5) is cell 2 with $g=+2$? Wait, recompute carefully: $g(2) = E(2) - I(2)$. After move of 3, $E(2) = 1$ (only $N_5$? no, 2 is not in $N_5$). Actually $E(2) = 0$ because $N_1$ now contains 1,2 both in A (internal), and $N_2$ contains 2 in A, 3 in B, 4 in B → split. So $E(2) = 1$ ($N_2$). $I(2) = 1$ ($N_1$). $g(2) = 0$. Similarly recompute all (1 mark):

| Cell | $E$ | $I$ | $g$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 1 ($N_5$) | 1 ($N_1$) | 0 |
| 2 | 1 ($N_2$) | 1 ($N_1$) | 0 |
| 4 | 1 ($N_2$) | 1 ($N_4$) | 0 |
| 5 | 1 ($N_5$) | 2 ($N_3,N_4$) | $-1$ |

**Move 2:** Tie at $g=0$ between cells 1, 2, 4. Suppose FM picks cell 4. Moving 4 from B to A: $A=\{1,2,4\}$ — legal ($|A|=3 \le 3.5$). $\Delta C_2 = 0$, $G_2 = +2$. Lock 4 (1 mark).

**Move 3, 4, 5:** Continue for cells 1, 2, 5. Typical pattern: $\Delta C$ values become 0 or negative as the high-gain cells have been moved. Final $G_5$ after all moves is some value $\le G_2 = +2$. The best prefix is $k^*=2$ (1 mark).

**Revert moves 3, 4, 5:** Return to the configuration after 2 moves. Final partition: $A=\{1,2,4\}$, $B=\{3,5\}$ (1 mark).

**Verification:** New cutsize — recompute the net-cut for this final partition:

| Net | Status |
| :--- | :--- |
| $N_1=\{1,2\}$ | both A → internal |
| $N_2=\{2,3,4\}$ | split (2,4 in A; 3 in B) → **cut** |
| $N_3=\{3,5\}$ | both B → internal |
| $N_4=\{4,5\}$ | split (4 in A; 5 in B) → **cut** |
| $N_5=\{1,5\}$ | split (1 in A; 5 in B) → **cut** |

$C_{\text{cut}} = 3$. (Note: in this small example the cutsize did not decrease; the algorithm continues to a second pass from the new starting point and may improve further, or terminate if no positive prefix is found.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — How Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting the revert step** (2–3 marks lost): Many students perform $n$ moves and *stop*, reporting the final configuration. The correct procedure is to **revert to the prefix $k^*$ with maximum cumulative gain**.
> 2. **Ignoring the balance constraint** (1–2 marks): In FM, a move that violates $\left \vert A \right \vert \le P_{\max}$ must be *rejected*, not silently applied.
> 3. **Confusing $D(v)$ and $c(a,b)$**: The term $2c(a,b)$ in the KL formula is the most-frequently omitted factor. Without it, the gain is wrong by an even integer.
> 4. **Stating the time complexity of FM as $O(n^2)$**: It is **$O(P)$ per pass** — pin-count linear — and this distinction is worth 1 mark.
> 5. **Not explaining the locking mechanism**: This is the conceptual core of iterative improvement and is awarded 1–2 marks separately from numerical computation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **Iterative improvement** = local-search heuristic that progressively refines an initial solution via *locked moves* and a *revert-to-best* rule.
* **Objective (Cost) Functions**: cutsize (partitioning), wirelength (placement), congestion (routing).
* **Kernighan–Lin (1970)**: 1-swap, strict balance, $D(v) = E(v) - I(v)$, $\Delta C_{a,b} = D(a) - D(b) - 2c(a,b)$, $O(n^2 \log n)$.
* **Fiduccia–Mattheyses (1982)**: 1-move, hypergraph, gain-bucket lists, balance tolerance $\varepsilon$, $O(P)$ per pass.
* **Revert-to-Best Rule**: $k^* = \arg\max_{k} G_k$ where $G_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \Delta C_i$. **Always revert, never stop at the final locked state.**
* **Locking** is what distinguishes iterative improvement from greedy: it prevents oscillation and guarantees forward progress.
* **Termination** is guaranteed because the cost is a non-negative integer and strictly decreases across passes when $G_{k^*} > 0$.
* **Industry relevance**: FM is the *de facto* standard inner loop in modern commercial placers (Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC).
* **Common KTU traps**: omitting the $2c(a,b)$ term, ignoring balance constraints, conflating KL and FM, stating FM complexity as $O(n^2)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Partitioning: The Kernighan-Lin Partitioning Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT525 - CAD BASED VLSI DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Partitioning: The Kernighan-Lin Partitioning Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **VLSI Physical Design Automation**, *partitioning* is the process of dividing a large circuit (represented as a hypergraph or graph of cells/nets) into smaller, more manageable sub-circuits such that the number of inter-partition connections (the **cut-size**) is minimized and the sizes of the resulting partitions are balanced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kernighan-Lin (KL) Partitioning Algorithm** (Kernighan & Lin, 1970) is an iterative, graph-bisection heuristic used to partition a graph $G = (V, E)$ with an *even* number of vertices into two disjoint, equal-sized subsets $A$ and $B$ such that the number of edges crossing the cut (i.e., edges with one endpoint in $A$ and the other in $B$) is **minimized**.

The KL algorithm is classified as a **min-cut bipartitioning heuristic** and operates on a *graph* (single net-pair connectivity), forming the foundation for later hypergraph extensions like **Fiduccia-Mattheyses (FM)**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a college with **2N students** who must be divided into **two equal project teams (Team A and Team B)**. Some pairs of students are *friends* and collaborate well, others *dislike* each other. The faculty wants:

1. **Equal team sizes** (balanced partition).
2. **Minimum friction** between the two teams (minimum number of "disliked" cross-team pairs).

The naïve approach is to randomly assign students and pray. But what if students could be **swapped in carefully selected pairs** to reduce friction at each step? Furthermore, what if we allowed *temporary* swaps that increase friction, because they unlock a much better configuration two swaps later? This is exactly the genius of Kernighan-Lin:

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition**: KL does NOT greedily pick the best single swap at each step. Instead, it performs a *fixed-length sequence* of **N swaps** (locking one pair per iteration), measures the **cumulative gain** of every prefix of this sequence, and finally applies only the prefix that yielded the **maximum partial cumulative gain**. This escapes local minima.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used

| Metric | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **External cost** | $E(v)$ | Number of edges from $v$ to vertices in the *opposite* partition |
| **Internal cost** | $I(v)$ | Number of edges from $v$ to vertices in the *same* partition |
| **Cost difference (D-value)** | $D(v) = E(v) - I(v)$ | Net "pull" of $v$ toward the opposite side |
| **Cut-size** | $C(A,B)$ | Total number of edges crossing between $A$ and $B$ |
| **Gain of a swap** | $g_i$ | Reduction in cut-size produced by the $i^{\text{th}}$ pair-swap |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Algorithmic Philosophy (Step-by-Step Logic)

The KL algorithm treats partitioning as a **combinatorial optimization** problem. Rather than chasing the local optimum greedily, it uses a strategy inspired by **2-opt local search with backtracking-free partial undo**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Operational Loop**:
> 1. Start from any balanced bisection $(A, B)$ with $\vert A \vert = \vert B \vert = n/2$.
> 2. Compute the *cost difference* $D(v)$ for every un-locked vertex.
> 3. Repeatedly select the *highest-gain unlocked pair* $(a, b)$ with $a \in A$, $b \in B$, swap them, and **lock** them.
> 4. Record the **cumulative gain** $G_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} g_i$ after each of the $k = 1, 2, \ldots, n/2$ swaps.
> 5. Find $k^*$ = the index that maximizes $G_k$. **Permanently apply** the first $k^*$ swaps; revert the rest.
> 6. Repeat the entire procedure until no further improvement is obtained.

---

### 2.2 Why It Works: The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why compute $D(v)$?** It tells us, for a single vertex, the immediate *benefit* of moving it to the other side. A vertex with high $D$ is "unhappy" in its current partition.
- **Why consider pairs $(a,b)$?** Swapping a single vertex breaks balance; pair-swaps preserve $\vert A \vert = \vert B \vert$.
- **Why subtract $2 \cdot c(a,b)$ in the gain?** If $a$ and $b$ are connected to each other, the edge $(a,b)$ is *internal* before the swap and becomes a *cut edge* after — adding $1$ to cut-size, hence subtracted from the gain.
- **Why allow temporary worsening of cut-size?** A locally bad swap may unlock a much better configuration several moves later. The cumulative-sum technique captures this.
- **Why lock vertices?** To prevent the same vertex from being swapped back and forth, ensuring finite convergence.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | LaTeX Expression | Meaning / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cost Difference | $D(v) = E(v) - I(v)$ | External minus internal edges at $v$ |
| 2 | Pair-wise Gain | $g(a,b) = D(a) + D(b) - 2 \cdot c(a,b)$ | $c(a,b) \in \{0, 1\}$ for graph |
| 3 | Cumulative Gain | $G_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} g_i$ | Sum of first $k$ swap gains |
| 4 | Best Prefix Index | $k^* = \arg\max_k \, G_k$ | $k \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n/2\}$ |
| 5 | Final Cut-size | $C'(A,B) = C(A,B) - G_{k^*}$ | Reduced cut-size after pass |
| 6 | Termination | $G_{k^*} \leq 0$ | Stop; current partition is stable |
| 7 | Edge indicator | $c(a,b) = 1$ if edge $(a,b) \in E$, else $0$ | Connectivity between pair |
| 8 | D-value update | $D_{\text{new}}(x) = D_{\text{old}}(x) + \text{sign}(x)$ | After swapping, for neighbor $x$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exam answers, **always write the D-value update rule explicitly**: a vertex $x$ connected to $a$ or $b$ has its $D(x)$ updated by $+1$ or $-1$ depending on whether $x$ and the swapped vertex are now in the same or opposite partition.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in VLSI Engineering

- **Logic partitioning** of large gate-netlists into sub-circuits that fit into individual FPGA regions or ASIC blocks.
- **Pre-placement step** in standard-cell and gate-array design flows to minimize inter-block routing congestion.
- **Foundational heuristic** that led directly to the FM algorithm (used in nearly every industrial EDA tool) and to multilevel partitioners (hMETIS, MLPart, KaHyPar).
- Enables **hierarchical design**, where each block can be optimized independently before global assembly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Pair-Gain Formula $g(a,b)$

Let the current partition be $(A, B)$ with $a \in A$ and $b \in B$. Define:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(a) &= \text{edges from } a \text{ to } B \setminus \{b\} \\
I(a) &= \text{edges from } a \text{ to } A \setminus \{a\} \\
E(b) &= \text{edges from } b \text{ to } A \setminus \{a\} \\
I(b) &= \text{edges from } b \text{ to } B \setminus \{b\} \\
c(a,b) &= \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } (a,b) \in E \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

The *D-values* are:

$$
D(a) = E(a) - I(a), \qquad D(b) = E(b) - I(b)
$$

If we swap $a$ and $b$ (placing $a$ into $B$ and $b$ into $A$), the change in cut-size $\Delta C$ equals (new cut edges) − (old cut edges). The original contribution of $a$ to the cut was $E(a)$; its new contribution becomes $I(a)$. The original contribution of $b$ was $E(b)$; its new contribution becomes $I(b)$. Additionally, if the edge $(a,b)$ existed and was internal to neither side, it becomes a new cut edge (adding $+1$), and conversely if it had been a cross-edge it becomes internal (removing $-1$). Combining:

$$
\Delta C = \bigl[I(a) + I(b) + 2 c(a,b)\bigr] - \bigl[E(a) + E(b)\bigr]
$$

Since the *gain* is defined as $g = -\Delta C$, after simplification:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(a,b) &= E(a) + E(b) - I(a) - I(b) - 2c(a,b) \\
       &= D(a) + D(b) - 2 \cdot c(a,b)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the canonical KL pair-gain formula used in every textbook and EDA reference.

---

### 3.2 D-Value Update Rule After a Swap

After $a$ and $b$ are swapped and locked, every *un-locked* neighbor $x$ of $a$ or $b$ must update its $D(x)$. The update depends on whether $x$ is in the same or opposite partition as the moved vertex:

$$
D_{\text{new}}(x) = D_{\text{old}}(x) + \begin{cases}
+1, & \text{if } x \text{ and } a \text{ move into the SAME partition (both } A \text{ or both } B\text{)} \\
-1, & \text{if } x \text{ and } a \text{ move into OPPOSITE partitions} \\
\end{cases}
$$

> The same rule applies symmetrically for $x$ adjacent to $b$. This is why each pass of KL can update $D$-values in $O(\vert E \vert)$ overall.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

Consider the following 8-vertex graph (undirected, unweighted) and the initial partition:

$$
A = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}, \qquad B = \{5, 6, 7, 8\}
$$

Edges (cut edges are underlined):
$$
E = \{(1,5),\, (1,6),\, (2,5),\, (2,7),\, (3,6),\, (3,8),\, (4,7),\, (4,8),\, (1,2),\, (3,4),\, (5,6),\, (7,8)\}
$$

Initial cut-edges: $(1,5), (1,6), (2,5), (2,7), (3,6), (3,8), (4,7), (4,8)$ → $C = 8$.

**Step 1: Compute initial D-values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D(1) &= E(1) - I(1) = 2 - 1 = +1 \\
D(2) &= E(2) - I(2) = 2 - 1 = +1 \\
D(3) &= E(3) - I(3) = 2 - 1 = +1 \\
D(4) &= E(4) - I(4) = 2 - 1 = +1 \\
D(5) &= E(5) - I(5) = 2 - 1 = +1 \\
D(6) &= E(6) - I(6) = 2 - 1 = +1 \\
D(7) &= E(7) - I(7) = 2 - 1 = +1 \\
D(8) &= E(8) - I(8) = 2 - 1 = +1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute all pair-gains** for $a \in A$, $b \in B$. By symmetry, only unique pairs matter:

$$
g(a,b) = D(a) + D(b) - 2 c(a,b)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(1,5) &= 1 + 1 - 2(1) = 0 \\
g(1,6) &= 1 + 1 - 2(1) = 0 \\
g(2,5) &= 1 + 1 - 2(1) = 0 \\
g(2,7) &= 1 + 1 - 2(1) = 0 \\
