g(3,6) &= 1 + 1 - 2(1) = 0 \\
g(3,8) &= 1 + 1 - 2(1) = 0 \\
g(4,7) &= 1 + 1 - 2(1) = 0 \\
g(4,8) &= 1 + 1 - 2(1) = 0 \\
g(1,7) &= 1 + 1 - 2(0) = 2 \\
g(2,6) &= 1 + 1 - 2(0) = 2 \\
g(3,5) &= 1 + 1 - 2(0) = 2 \\
g(4,5) &= 1 + 1 - 2(0) = 2 \\
\text{(and remaining 0-edge pairs)} &\rightarrow g = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Pick the maximum-gain pair**, e.g., $(1, 7)$ with $g_1 = 2$. Swap and lock.

**Step 4: Update D-values** of unlocked neighbors. Vertex 2 is adjacent to 1 (now in B); vertices 5, 6, 8 are adjacent to 1 (now in B, so they're now in the same partition). After the swap, $A' = \{2,3,4,7\}$ and $B' = \{5,6,8,1\}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D(2) &= E(2) - I(2) = [1 \text{ (edge to 5)}] - [1 \text{ (edge to 7)}] = 0 \\
D(3) &= 2 - 1 = 1 \\
D(4) &= 2 - 1 = 1 \\
D(5) &= [2 \text{ (edges to 2,6)}] - [1 \text{ (edge to 1, now same side)}] = 1 \\
D(6) &= [2 \text{ (edges to 1,3)}] - [1 \text{ (edge to 5)}] = 1 \\
D(8) &= [2 \text{ (edges to 3,4)}] - [1 \text{ (edge to 7, now same side? 7 is in A, 8 in B → opposite)}] = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Continue** until $n/2 = 4$ pairs are locked. Record $G_k$ after each swap. Find $k^*$ that maximizes $G_k$, and commit only the prefix up to $k^*$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU exam, when asked to "demonstrate one KL pass on a small graph," show **only the first 2–3 swaps** plus the cumulative-gain column. Do not get bogged down with all 4 swaps unless marks are explicitly allotted for it.

---

### 3.4 Convergence and Complexity

- **Time per pass**: $O(\vert E \vert \cdot \log \vert V \vert)$ using a max-priority-queue keyed on gain.
- **Number of passes**: Empirically 1–10 for typical VLSI graphs.
- **Convergence**: Guaranteed because each pass either reduces cut-size or terminates (when $G_{k^*} \leq 0$).
- **Local optimum, NOT global**: KL is a heuristic and can get stuck in local minima; **multistart** with random initial partitions is used in practice.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Top-Level KL Algorithm Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Start: Read graph G and balanced initial partition A, B"] --> B0["Compute D(v) for all v in V"]
    B0 --> C0["Lock all vertices as UNLOCKED"]
    C0 --> D0{"All vertices locked?"}
    D0 -- No --> E0["Select pair (a, b) with max gain g = D(a)+D(b)-2c(a,b)"]
    E0 --> F0["Record cumulative gain G_k += g"]
    F0 --> G0["Swap a and b, LOCK both"]
    G0 --> H0["Update D(x) for all unlocked neighbors x of a and b"]
    H0 --> D0
    D0 -- Yes --> I0["Find k* = argmax G_k"]
    I0 --> J0{"Is G_k* > 0?"}
    J0 -- Yes --> K0["Commit first k* swaps permanently"]
    K0 --> L0["Unlock all vertices and update A, B"]
    L0 --> B0
    J0 -- No --> M0["Terminate: Return current A, B as final partition"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a KL Pass

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Block"]
        I1["Graph G = V, E"]
        I2["Initial bisection A, B with equal size"]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE["Computation Block"]
        C1["D-value Calculator\nD(v) = E(v) - I(v)"]
        C2["Pair-Gain Engine\ng(a,b) = D(a)+D(b)-2c(a,b)"]
        C3["Priority Queue\nkeyed on max gain"]
    end

    subgraph SWAP["Swap and Update Block"]
        S1["Swap selected a in A with b in B"]
        S2["Lock a and b"]
        S3["Incremental D-value Updater\nfor all unlocked neighbors"]
    end

    subgraph TRACK["Tracking Block"]
        T1["Cumulative Gain Array G_k"]
        T2["Best Prefix Selector k*"]
    end

    I1 --> C1
    I2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> C1
    S1 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> C1
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic: Locked vs Unlocked State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Unlocked
    Unlocked --> Locked: Pair (a,b) chosen, swapped, locked
    Locked --> Unlocked: Pass ends AND k > k* (revert)
    Locked --> PermanentLocked: Pass ends AND k <= k* (commit)
    PermanentLocked --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The state diagram illustrates that only the first $k^*$ pairs (those preceding the maximum cumulative gain) become *permanent*. All other locked vertices return to the un-locked pool at the start of the next pass.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the cost-difference $D(v)$ for a vertex $v$ in the Kernighan-Lin partitioning algorithm. Why is it called a "cost difference" and how is it used in computing the pair-wise gain?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The cost difference is defined as

$$
D(v) = E(v) - I(v)
$$

where $E(v)$ is the number of edges from $v$ to vertices in the opposite partition (external cost) and $I(v)$ is the number of edges from $v$ to vertices in the same partition (internal cost). It is called a "difference" because it measures the *net number of edges that would be removed from the cut* if $v$ were moved to the other partition. It is used in the pair-gain formula as $g(a,b) = D(a) + D(b) - 2 c(a,b)$, where the $2 c(a,b)$ term corrects for the double-counting of the edge between $a$ and $b$. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why does the Kernighan-Lin algorithm perform a fixed-length sequence of $n/2$ pair-swaps in each pass and then revert to the prefix with maximum cumulative gain, instead of greedily accepting only improving swaps?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Performing only $n/2$ pair-swaps but recording cumulative gains allows the algorithm to **escape local minima**. Greedy acceptance of every improving swap can trap the solution in a suboptimal configuration because a swap that locally worsens the cut-size may unlock a far better configuration several swaps later. By tracking cumulative gain and finally selecting $k^* = \arg\max_k G_k$, the algorithm can commit to a *prefix* of swaps including some temporarily worsening ones, achieving a globally superior partition. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**: Students frequently lose marks by **(i)** forgetting the $2 c(a,b)$ correction in the gain formula, **(ii)** writing the D-value update rule incorrectly (sign convention reversal is the most common pitfall), and **(iii)** not showing the cumulative-gain column explicitly. Always show $G_k$ values in a tabular form for full credit.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]*

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain in detail the Kernighan-Lin (KL) partitioning algorithm. Clearly state the input, output, the D-value computation, the pair-gain formula, the locking mechanism, and the convergence condition.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* For the graph shown below with initial partition $A = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and $B = \{5, 6, 7, 8\}$, perform **one complete pass** of the KL algorithm. List the D-values, the chosen pair sequence with their individual gains, the cumulative gains $G_k$, the optimal $k^*$, and the new partition after applying the first $k^*$ swaps.

Edges: $(1,5), (1,6), (2,5), (2,7), (3,6), (3,8), (4,7), (4,8), (1,2), (3,4), (5,6), (7,8)$. (Same graph as Section 3.3.)

---

**Model Solution for Q-A:**

**(a) Explanation (7 marks):**

- **Input**: A graph $G = (V, E)$ with $\vert V \vert = 2n$ (even). An initial balanced bisection $A, B$ with $\vert A \vert = \vert B \vert = n$. **(1 mark)**
- **Output**: An improved partition $(A', B')$ with cut-size $\leq$ original. **(0.5 mark)**
- **D-value Computation**: $D(v) = E(v) - I(v)$ for all unlocked $v$. **(1 mark)**
- **Pair-gain Formula**: $g(a, b) = D(a) + D(b) - 2 c(a, b)$, $a \in A$, $b \in B$. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Locking Mechanism**: After each swap, $a$ and $b$ are locked (removed from future consideration in the same pass) and $D$-values of neighbors are updated. **(1 mark)**
- **Cumulative Gain Tracking**: $G_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} g_i$; choose $k^* = \arg\max_k G_k$; commit first $k^*$ swaps. **(1 mark)**
- **Convergence**: Terminate when $G_{k^*} \leq 0$. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Worked Pass (7 marks):** (Using the example from Section 3.3)

| Step | Pair (a, b) | Gain $g_i$ | Cumulative $G_k$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (1, 7) | +2 | 2 |
| 2 | (2, 5) | (after D-update) −1 | 1 |
| 3 | (3, 6) | +2 | 3 |
| 4 | (4, 8) | +1 | 4 |

$k^* = 4$ (max $G_k = 4$). All four swaps are committed. Final partition:

$$
A' = \{4, 5, 6, 7\}, \quad B' = \{1, 2, 3, 8\}
$$

New cut-edges: $(2,5), (2,7), (4,5), (4,7)$ → $C' = 4$ (reduced from 8). **(7 marks — distribute 1 mark per row + 1 mark for the final partition + 1 mark for the new cut-size)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Derive the Kernighan-Lin pair-wise gain formula $g(a,b) = D(a) + D(b) - 2 c(a, b)$ from first principles. Clearly show the contribution of the edge $(a,b)$ if it exists.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Compare the Kernighan-Lin (KL) algorithm with the Fiduccia-Mattheyses (FM) algorithm in terms of (i) representation (graph vs hypergraph), (ii) movement (pair-wise vs single-vertex), (iii) time complexity, and (iv) handling of imbalance. State which one is more suitable for modern VLSI netlists and justify your answer.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from the definition of $D(v)$:

$$
D(a) = E(a) - I(a), \qquad D(b) = E(b) - I(b)
$$

The change in cut-size when $a \in A$, $b \in B$ are swapped is:

$$
\Delta C = \bigl[I(a) + I(b) + 2c(a,b)\bigr] - \bigl[E(a) + E(b)\bigr]
$$

*(Explanation: after the swap, $a$'s former internal edges become cut edges contributing $I(a)$, similarly $I(b)$ for $b$. The term $2c(a,b)$ accounts for the double-effect of the $(a,b)$ edge: if it existed, it flips twice — once from cut to internal, once from internal to cut — net contribution $+2$. The original cut contribution from $a$ was $E(a)$, from $b$ was $E(b)$.)* **(4 marks)**

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(a,b) &= -\Delta C = E(a) + E(b) - I(a) - I(b) - 2c(a,b) \\
       &= \bigl[E(a) - I(a)\bigr] + \bigl[E(b) - I(b)\bigr] - 2c(a,b) \\
       &= D(a) + D(b) - 2c(a,b)
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3 marks for final simplification and stating the role of $2c(a,b)$.)**

---

**(b) Comparison (7 marks — 1.5 marks per row + 1 mark for justification):**

| Feature | KL Algorithm | FM Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Representation | Graph (edges, 2-pin nets) | Hypergraph (nets of any pin count) |
| (ii) Movement | Pair-wise swap of two vertices | Single-vertex move with balance constraint |
| (iii) Time Complexity | $O(\vert E \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ per pass | $O(\vert P \vert)$ per pass, linear |
| (iv) Imbalance Handling | Requires equal-size partitions | Allows tolerance $\varepsilon$ for area imbalance |

**Suitability**: **FM is more suitable for modern VLSI netlists** because real circuits have multi-pin nets (a single net can connect 3+ cells), which hypergraphs represent natively. FM is also linear-time per pass and supports area-based balance constraints (cells have unequal areas), both critical for industrial designs. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Goal of KL**: Minimize cut-size for a *balanced* bipartition of a graph. **(Always state both objectives.)**
- **D-value**: $D(v) = E(v) - I(v)$; large positive $D$ ⇒ $v$ "wants" to move.
- **Pair-gain**: $g(a,b) = D(a) + D(b) - 2 c(a,b)$; the $2 c(a,b)$ term is the most-missed KTU point.
- **One pass** = exactly $n/2$ pair-swaps, regardless of whether intermediate swaps improve the cut.
- **Cumulative gain** $G_k$ must be tabulated explicitly; the maximum partial sum $G_{k^*}$ determines the commit prefix.
- **Locking** prevents a vertex from being moved twice in the same pass.
- **Convergence**: $G_{k^*} \leq 0$ ⇒ stop. KL produces a *local* minimum, not necessarily global.
- **Complexity**: $O(\vert E \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ per pass using a max-heap on gain.
- **Limitation**: Graph-based; ignores multi-pin nets ⇒ extended by FM (1982) and multilevel methods (hMETIS).
- **Practical tip for KTU answers**: Always show (1) D-values, (2) the chosen pair + its gain, (3) the cumulative gain column, (4) the $k^*$ selection, (5) the final partition + new cut-size. This structure fetches full marks consistently.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimization Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT525 - CAD BASED VLSI DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Optimization Objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Computer-Aided Design (CAD) based VLSI Design**, an **Optimization Objective** is a mathematically defined **scalar or vector performance metric** that a CAD tool seeks to minimize or maximize during the synthesis, placement, routing, or physical design stages of an integrated circuit. These objectives are formally expressed through a **Cost Function** (or **Objective Function**) $\Phi(\mathbf{x})$, where $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ represents the vector of design parameters (e.g., transistor sizing, supply voltage, threshold voltage, wire width, buffer insertion locations, gate sizing).

The general constrained optimization problem in VLSI CAD is formally stated as:

$$\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{S}} \; \Phi(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad g_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \;\; h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$$

where $\mathcal{S}$ is the feasible design space, and $g_i(\mathbf{x})$, $h_j(\mathbf{x})$ are the inequality and equality constraints (e.g., maximum delay bound, minimum drive strength, layout geometry rules).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In VLSI design optimization, the term "objective" is **not** equivalent to a single metric. Modern CAD frameworks treat design closure as a **multi-objective optimization (MOO)** problem in which Area, Power, and Delay form the canonical three-axis trade-off surface, often visualized as the **Pareto Front**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vacation Planner" Intuition

Imagine you are planning a vacation with three competing desires:
1. **Minimize cost** (cheaper is better)
2. **Minimize travel time** (faster is better)
3. **Maximize scenic beauty** (better views are better)

You cannot have all three at once. If you cut cost, you fly economy (slow + boring). If you want speed, you fly first-class (expensive). This is exactly the dilemma in VLSI design:
- **Smaller area** $\rightarrow$ transistors are tightly packed $\rightarrow$ **higher capacitance & slower speeds + more heat**.
- **Lower power** $\rightarrow$ reduced supply voltage $\rightarrow$ **slower switching speeds + timing failures**.
- **Higher speed** $\rightarrow$ bigger drivers, wider wires $\rightarrow$ **more area + more power**.

A **CAD optimization objective** is the mathematical way of saying: *"Among all possible chip designs, find the one that gives me the best balance (or extreme) of these competing wishes."* The CAD tool acts as the travel agent that finds the best compromise.

### 1.3 Canonical VLSI Optimization Objectives

| Objective | Symbol | Direction | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Area | $A$ | Minimize | Silicon real estate occupied |
| Delay (Critical Path) | $T_{cp}$ or $\tau$ | Minimize | Speed / clock frequency limit |
| Dynamic Power | $P_{dyn}$ | Minimize | Switching power dissipation |
| Static / Leakage Power | $P_{leak}$ | Minimize | Subthreshold + gate leakage |
| Total Power | $P_{tot}$ | Minimize | $P_{dyn} + P_{leak}$ |
| Energy per Operation | $E$ | Minimize | Battery life metric |
| Power-Delay Product | $PDP$ | Minimize | Combined speed-power figure of merit |
| Energy-Delay Product | $EDP$ | Minimize | Higher-order FOM |
| Yield | $Y$ | Maximize | % of functional dies after fab |
| Signal Integrity (Noise) | $N$ | Minimize | Crosstalk, IR drop |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition Box — Cost Function:** A cost function $\Phi(\mathbf{x})$ in VLSI CAD is a closed-form expression that maps a design-parameter vector $\mathbf{x}$ to a scalar value representing "how good" or "how bad" that design choice is. Lower $\Phi$ = better design (for minimization problems).

### 1.4 Classification of Optimization Objectives

**1. Single-Objective Optimization (SOO):**
Only one metric is optimized (e.g., minimize area only). CAD tools internally reduce multi-constraint problems to this form using **Lagrangian methods** or **weighted penalties**.

**2. Multi-Objective Optimization (MOO):**
Two or more conflicting objectives are optimized simultaneously. Solution is a **Pareto-optimal set** rather than a single point.

**3. Constrained Optimization:**
Objective is optimized while satisfying hard constraints (e.g., *minimize power subject to $T_{cp} \leq T_{spec}$*).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Formulation of the VLSI Optimization Problem

A typical **sizing + biasing** optimization problem in CMOS logic design is:

$$\min_{W, L, V_{DD}, V_T} \; \Phi(\mathbf{x}) = \alpha_1 A(\mathbf{x}) + \alpha_2 P_{tot}(\mathbf{x}) + \alpha_3 T_{cp}(\mathbf{x})$$

subject to functional and geometric constraints, where $\alpha_i \geq 0$ are user-defined weights indicating relative importance.

### 2.2 Why Are These Objectives "Competing"? — The Deep "Why"

The competition arises from **fundamental CMOS physics** that ties all three objectives to a common set of device parameters:

- **Increasing drive current** $I_D$ requires **larger $W/L$** $\Rightarrow$ both **larger area** AND **larger capacitance to charge** (paradoxically slowing the gate on the next stage).
- **Reducing $V_{DD}$** reduces dynamic power quadratically ($\propto V_{DD}^2$) but increases delay roughly as $1/V_{DD}$ once $V_T$ is approached.
- **Reducing $V_T$** increases subthreshold leakage exponentially (sub-threshold slope $\sim 60$ mV/decade at room temperature).

This triad of physical coupling is the *root cause* of the trade-off surface, and CAD optimization algorithms (TILOS, gate sizing, Lagrangian relaxation) navigate it.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dynamic Power | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$ | Switching power of CMOS gate |
| 2 | Short-Circuit Power | $P_{sc} = I_{sc} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Power during gate transition |
| 3 | Static (Leakage) Power | $P_{leak} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Sub-threshold + gate leakage |
| 4 | Total Power | $P_{tot} = P_{dyn} + P_{sc} + P_{leak}$ | Total chip power dissipation |
| 5 | CMOS Gate Delay (alpha-power) | $\tau = \frac{K \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{(V_{DD} - V_T)^{\alpha}}$ | Propagation delay |
| 6 | Energy per Switching Event | $E = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | Energy to charge output node |
| 7 | Power-Delay Product (PDP) | $PDP = P_{dyn} \cdot \tau$ | Combined power-speed FOM |
| 8 | Energy-Delay Product (EDP) | $EDP = E \cdot \tau = PDP \cdot \tau$ | Higher-order speed-energy FOM |
| 9 | Sub-threshold Leakage Current | $I_{leak} = I_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{V_{GS} - V_T}{S}}$ | Exponential in $V_T$ |
| 10 | Weighted Aggregate Cost | $\Phi = w_1 \tilde{A} + w_2 \tilde{P} + w_3 \tilde{\tau}$ | Normalized MOO scalar reduction |
| 11 | Pareto Dominance | $\mathbf{x}_1 \prec \mathbf{x}_2$ if $\forall i: f_i(\mathbf{x}_1) \leq f_i(\mathbf{x}_2)$ AND $\exists j: f_j(\mathbf{x}_1) < f_j(\mathbf{x}_2)$ | Definition of dominance |
| 12 | Lagrangian Form | $\mathcal{L} = \Phi(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_k \lambda_k g_k(\mathbf{x})$ | Constrained SOO reduction |
| 13 | Yield Objective (Poisson model) | $Y = e^{-A \cdot D}$ | $D$ = defect density |
| 14 | Area-Delay Product (ADP) | $ADP = A \cdot T_{cp}$ | Layout-speed trade-off FOM |
| 15 | Energy-Area Product | $EAP = E \cdot A$ | Battery + die-cost FOM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Pearl:** The alpha-power law $\tau \propto V_{DD} / (V_{DD} - V_T)^{\alpha}$ is a **must-know** for KTU ESE. For long-channel devices, $\alpha = 1$; for short-channel, $\alpha \approx 1.3$–$1.5$.

### 2.4 Pareto Optimality — The Heart of MOO

A design point $\mathbf{x}^*$ is **Pareto optimal** if no other feasible point improves one objective without worsening another. The set of all such points forms the **Pareto Front** in the objective space.

For a 2-objective problem ($\min$ Area, $\min$ Power):

$$\mathcal{P} = \{\mathbf{x}^* \in \mathcal{S} \mid \nexists \, \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{S} : A(\mathbf{x}) \leq A(\mathbf{x}^*) \;\text{and}\; P(\mathbf{x}) < P(\mathbf{x}^*)\}$$

### 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in Industry

- **Mobile/IoT chips:** Designers use EDP minimization to maximize battery life at acceptable speed.
- **High-performance CPUs:** Critical-path delay is the primary objective; power is bounded by a thermal design power (TDP) constraint.
- **FPGA fabrics:** Area is a hard constraint (fixed LUT count); the CAD tool optimizes delay and power.
- **Yield optimization** is critical at 7nm, 5nm, and below, where process variations dominate.
- **Design for Test (DFT) objectives** add test-coverage as a fourth axis in modern CAD flows.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Power-Delay Product (PDP) as a Composite Objective

**Goal:** Show that the Power-Delay Product is a legitimate composite objective that captures both switching energy and speed in a single scalar.

**Starting point:** Dynamic power and gate delay expressions for a CMOS inverter driving a load $C_L$ with activity factor $\alpha$:

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$

$$\tau = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \, \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{DD} - V_T)^2} \quad \text{(long-channel, first-order)}$$

**Step 1 — Multiply $P_{dyn}$ and $\tau$:**

$$PDP = P_{dyn} \cdot \tau = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk} \cdot \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{DD} - V_T)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Group terms:**

$$PDP = \frac{\alpha \cdot f_{clk} \cdot C_L^2 \cdot V_{DD}^3}{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{DD} - V_T)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

$$\boxed{PDP = \frac{\alpha \cdot f_{clk} \cdot C_L^2 \cdot V_{DD}^3}{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{DD} - V_T)^2}}$$

The PDP is a meaningful composite objective because:
- A design with **low power but very slow** has moderate PDP.
- A design with **high power but very fast** also has moderate PDP.
- The **optimal PDP point** corresponds to the best energy-per-operation *at a given speed*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** Notice that PDP $\propto C_L^2$. This means halving load capacitance cuts PDP by $4\times$, which is the foundation of why **buffer insertion** and **wire sizing** are so powerful as optimization moves in CAD.

### 3.2 Derivation: Weighted Scalarization of Multi-Objective Problem

**Goal:** Convert a multi-objective VLSI optimization problem into a tractable single-objective form using **weighted sum scalarization**.

**Multi-objective problem:**

$$\min_{\mathbf{x}} \; \big[ f_1(\mathbf{x}), \, f_2(\mathbf{x}), \, f_3(\mathbf{x}) \big]$$

where $f_1 = A$, $f_2 = P_{tot}$, $f_3 = T_{cp}$.

**Step 1 — Assign non-negative weights:**

Choose $w_1, w_2, w_3 \geq 0$ with at least one $w_i > 0$.

**Step 2 — Form the scalar aggregate:**

$$\Phi(\mathbf{x}) = w_1 \cdot \frac{A(\mathbf{x})}{A_{ref}} + w_2 \cdot \frac{P_{tot}(\mathbf{x})}{P_{ref}} + w_3 \cdot \frac{T_{cp}(\mathbf{x})}{T_{ref}}$$

where $A_{ref}, P_{ref}, T_{ref}$ are normalization constants (typical values or specification limits) that make the terms dimensionless and comparable.

**Step 3 — Solve the SOO:**

$$\mathbf{x}^* = \arg\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{S}} \Phi(\mathbf{x})$$

**Step 4 — Vary weights to trace the Pareto Front:**

By varying $(w_1, w_2, w_3)$ systematically and re-solving, we obtain a discrete approximation of the Pareto front $\mathcal{P}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation of Weighted Sum:** This method **cannot find non-convex Pareto fronts**. For those, $\epsilon$-constraint or evolutionary algorithms (NSGA-II) are needed in advanced CAD tools.

### 3.3 Derivation: Lagrangian Formulation for a Constrained Sizing Problem

**Problem:** Minimize power of a chain of $N$ inverters subject to a maximum total delay.

$$\min_{W_1, \ldots, W_N} \; \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i(W_i) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tau_i(W_i) \leq T_{spec}$$

**Step 1 — Form the Lagrangian:**

$$\mathcal{L}(W_1, \ldots, W_N, \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i(W_i) + \lambda \left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tau_i(W_i) - T_{spec} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Stationarity conditions (KKT):**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_i} = \frac{dP_i}{dW_i} + \lambda \cdot \frac{d\tau_i}{dW_i} = 0, \quad i = 1, \ldots, N$$

$$\lambda \geq 0, \quad \lambda \left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tau_i - T_{spec} \right) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for optimal size:**

The Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$ acts as the **"price of delay"**: a positive $\lambda$ forces the optimizer to spend more power to meet the timing budget. This formulation is the analytical heart of the classical **TILOS** algorithm for transistor sizing in VLSI CAD.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity of Delay to Design Variables

| Variable $x$ | $\partial \tau / \partial x$ | Sign | Design Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{DD}$ ↑ | $\tau$ decreases | $-$ | Faster but higher $V_{DD}^2$ power |
| $V_T$ ↑ | $\tau$ increases | $+$ | Slower but lower leakage |
| $W$ ↑ | $\tau$ decreases | $-$ | Faster but more area + more gate cap |
| $L$ ↑ | $\tau$ increases | $+$ | Slower, less short-channel effects |
| $C_L$ ↑ | $\tau$ increases | $+$ | Slower, drives more wire load |
| $T$ (temperature) ↑ | $\tau$ increases | $+$ | Mobility degrades |
| $V_{DD}$ (very low, $<2V_T$) | $\tau$ skyrockets | Strongly $+$ | Below $V_T$, transistor shuts off |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — VLSI Multi-Objective Optimization Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Netlist and Constraints] --> B[Parse Design Parameters x]
    B --> C{Optimization Mode}
    C -->|SOO| D[Single Objective Cost Function Phi]
    C -->|MOO| E[Vector Objective f1, f2, f3]
    D --> F[Apply Optimization Engine]
    E --> G[Weighted Sum Scalarization]
    E --> H[Epsilon Constraint Method]
    E --> I[NSGA-II Evolutionary Solver]
    G --> F
    H --> F
    I --> F
    F --> J[Evaluate Phi x at Each Iteration]
    J --> K[Check Convergence Criteria]
    K -->|Not Converged| L[Update Design Variables x]
    L --> F
    K -->|Converged| M[Extract Optimal Design Point x star]
    M --> N[Generate Output Reports]
    N --> O[Area Report]
    N --> P[Power Report]
    N --> Q[Delay Report]
    N --> R[Pareto Front Visualization]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Trade-off Relationships Between Canonical Objectives

```mermaid
graph LR
    AREA[Area Minimization] -->|Reduce W/L| SPEED
    SPEED[Speed Maximization] -->|Increase Drive| AREA
    AREA -->|Denser Layout| HEAT[Higher Power Density]
    HEAT --> PWR[Power Dissipation]
    PWR -->|Thermal Limit| SPEED
    SPEED -->|Lower Vdd to save P| DELAY[Delay Degradation]
    DELAY --> SPEED
    PWR -->|Reduce Vdd| DELAY
    DELAY -->|Larger Buffer| AREA
    LEAK[Leakage Reduction] -->|Higher Vt| DELAY
    DELAY --> LEAK
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Pareto Front in Area-Power Space

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ParetoFront[Pareto Front in Objective Space]
    P1[Point A: Small Area, High Power] --> P2[Point B: Balanced]
    P2 --> P3[Point C: Large Area, Low Power]
    end
    subgraph InfeasibleZone[Infeasible Region]
    I1[Cannot achieve small area AND low power simultaneously]
    end
    subgraph DominatedZone[Dominated by Pareto Front]
    D1[Sub-optimal Designs]
    end
    ParetoFront --> EngineeringDecision
    EngineeringDecision[Designer Picks Final Point Based on Application] --> FinalSpec
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Flow of a CAD Sizing Optimizer (TILOS-style)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Initial Sizing All Transistors at Min Width] --> B0[Critical Path Identification]
    B0 --> C0[Compute Sensitivities dPhi over dW for Each Gate]
    C0 --> D0[Apply TILOS Sensitivity Update Rule]
    D0 --> E0{Spec Met or Budget Exhausted}
    E0 -->|No| F0[Update W of Most Sensitive Gate]
    F0 --> C0
    E0 -->|Yes| G0[Report Optimal Sizes and Objective Value]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
*Define the term "optimization objective" in the context of VLSI CAD. List any four canonical objectives used in modern design flows.*

**Model Answer:**

An **optimization objective** in VLSI CAD is a formally defined scalar function $\Phi(\mathbf{x})$ of the design-parameter vector $\mathbf{x}$ that the CAD tool attempts to minimize (or maximize) during synthesis, placement, or routing.

**Four canonical objectives:**

1. **Area** ($A$) — silicon area occupied by the layout.
2. **Delay** ($T_{cp}$) — critical-path propagation delay.
3. **Power dissipation** ($P_{tot}$) — sum of dynamic + static power.
4. **Energy per operation** ($E$) — battery-life metric.

*(Auxiliary objectives often included: yield, signal integrity, testability, cost.)* — **[3 Marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]**
*What is a Pareto-optimal solution in multi-objective VLSI design? State the condition for Pareto dominance.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Pareto-optimal** design point $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a feasible design such that **no other feasible point can improve one objective without worsening at least one other objective**. The set of all such non-dominated points forms the **Pareto Front** $\mathcal{P}$ in the objective space.

**Mathematical condition (Pareto Dominance):** A point $\mathbf{x}_1$ *dominates* $\mathbf{x}_2$ (denoted $\mathbf{x}_1 \prec \mathbf{x}_2$) if:

$$\forall i \in \{1,\ldots,k\}: f_i(\mathbf{x}_1) \leq f_i(\mathbf{x}_2) \quad \text{AND} \quad \exists \, j : f_j(\mathbf{x}_1) < f_j(\mathbf{x}_2)$$

where $f_i$ are the objective functions (e.g., Area, Power, Delay). A point not dominated by any other is Pareto-optimal. — **[3 Marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 Style]**

**(a)** Formulate the general multi-objective VLSI design optimization problem. Discuss the canonical objectives (Area, Power, Delay) and the physical reasons why they are **mutually conflicting** in CMOS design. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the Power-Delay Product (PDP) for a CMOS inverter. Show that PDP $\propto C_L^2$ and explain why this is the **key motivator** for buffer insertion and wire sizing as optimization techniques in CAD. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**General Mathematical Formulation:**

The canonical VLSI multi-objective optimization is:

$$\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{S}} \; \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}) = \big[ f_1(\mathbf{x}), f_2(\mathbf{x}), f_3(\mathbf{x}) \big]^T$$

with $\mathbf{x} = (W, L, V_{DD}, V_T, \ldots)^T$ and $f_1 = A$, $f_2 = P_{tot}$, $f_3 = T_{cp}$.

**Physical conflict reasoning (one mark per explanation):**

1. **Area vs. Delay:** Increasing transistor width $W$ boosts drive current, reducing delay, but the wider transistor also occupies more silicon area and presents higher gate capacitance to its driver, paradoxically slowing upstream stages. Hence "minimize area" and "minimize delay" conflict.

2. **Power vs. Delay:** Lowering $V_{DD}$ reduces dynamic power quadratically ($\propto V_{DD}^2$), but for $V_{DD} \to V_T$, the delay rises sharply per $\tau \propto V_{DD}/(V_{DD} - V_T)^\alpha$. Hence "minimize power" and "minimize delay" conflict.

3. **Area vs. Power:** Smaller geometry increases wire resistance and per-unit leakage, but static power per unit area grows exponentially with process scaling. Hence "minimize area" and "minimize power" also conflict.

**Conclusion:** No single point is universally optimal. The CAD tool must navigate this **Pareto surface** to find a design best matching the application. — **[7 Marks: 2 for formulation + 5 for conflict analysis]**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Step 1 — Start with delay and dynamic power expressions:**

$$\tau = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) (V_{DD} - V_T)^2}$$

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply them:**

$$PDP = P_{dyn} \cdot \tau = \alpha f_{clk} C_L V_{DD}^2 \cdot \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)(V_{DD} - V_T)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$PDP = \frac{\alpha f_{clk} C_L^2 V_{DD}^3}{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)(V_{DD} - V_T)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Note the $C_L^2$ dependence:**

The quadratic dependence on load capacitance is the central observation. **Halving $C_L$ reduces PDP by 4×.** Therefore, optimization techniques that reduce load capacitance — such as **buffer insertion** (to break long wires into driven segments) and **wire sizing** (thicker wires reduce resistance, but at area cost) — are extremely high-leverage optimization moves in CAD.

**Engineering context:** This is why placement-and-routing CAD tools have a dedicated *buffer-insertion* stage and a *wire-sizing* stage post-route; PDP minimization is a major driver. — **[7 Marks: 4 for derivation + 3 for engineering interpretation]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4 Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a clear mathematical formulation, explain the **weighted-sum scalarization** method for converting a multi-objective VLSI problem into a single-objective problem. State one key limitation. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Formulate the Lagrangian for a **constrained power-minimization problem with a delay constraint** in a chain of $N$ inverters. State the KKT conditions and explain the role of the Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Step 1 — Original MOO problem:**

$$\min_{\mathbf{x}} \big[ f_1(\mathbf{x}), f_2(\mathbf{x}), \ldots, f_k(\mathbf{x}) \big]$$

**Step 2 — Introduce weights and normalizations:**

Choose $w_i \geq 0$, with $\sum w_i = 1$ (sometimes), and reference values $f_i^{ref}$:

$$\Phi(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot \frac{f_i(\mathbf{x})}{f_i^{ref}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve SOO:**

$$\mathbf{x}^* = \arg\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{S}} \Phi(\mathbf{x})$$

**Step 4 — Trace Pareto front:** Vary weights $(w_1, \ldots, w_k)$ and re-solve.

**Limitation:** The weighted-sum method can **only produce Pareto-optimal points on convex regions** of the Pareto front. If the true Pareto front is non-convex, this method completely **misses** the non-convex portions. Alternatives include the $\epsilon$-constraint method or NSGA-II. — **[7 Marks: 5 for formulation + 2 for limitation]**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Step 1 — Primal problem:**

$$\min_{W_1,\ldots,W_N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i(W_i) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tau_i(W_i) \leq T_{spec}$$

**Step 2 — Lagrangian:**

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i(W_i) + \lambda \left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tau_i(W_i) - T_{spec} \right)$$

**Step 3 — KKT stationarity:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_i} = \frac{dP_i}{dW_i} + \lambda \frac{d\tau_i}{dW_i} = 0 \quad \forall i$$

**Step 4 — Complementary slackness:**

$$\lambda \geq 0, \qquad \lambda \left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tau_i - T_{spec} \right) = 0$$

**Interpretation of $\lambda$:** The Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$ is the **"price of delay"** — it measures the *marginal power increase per unit reduction in delay*. If the unconstrained optimum already satisfies $\sum \tau_i \leq T_{spec}$, then $\lambda = 0$. Otherwise, $\lambda > 0$ and the optimizer trades off power for timing. This dual variable is used in the classical **TILOS** algorithm. — **[7 Marks: 4 for KKT + 3 for interpretation]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "minimize" direction:** Students often write "maximize delay" instead of "minimize delay". Always phrase the objective as a minimization; the CAD tool convention is minimization.
> 2. **Skipping normalization in weighted sum:** Without $f_i^{ref}$ in the denominator, a 1 cm² area is treated equal to 1 mW power, which is dimensionally meaningless and numerically catastrophic. **Always normalize.**
> 3. **Forgetting constraints in Lagrangian:** The constraint $\sum \tau_i \leq T_{spec}$ MUST be brought into the Lagrangian with a multiplier; many students write the Lagrangian with no constraint term, earning a -3 mark penalty.
> 4. **Calling NSGA-II "a kind of weighted-sum":** It is not. NSGA-II is a population-based evolutionary algorithm that handles non-convex Pareto fronts. Mixing them loses marks.
> 5. **Missing units in PDP derivation:** Final PDP has units of Joules (energy, not power × time-as-seconds). State it explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Optimization objective** = scalar or vector cost function $\Phi(\mathbf{x})$ that a CAD tool minimizes.
- [ ] **Canonical VLSI objectives:** Area $A$, Power $P_{tot}$, Delay $T_{cp}$ — the **APD triangle**.
- [ ] **Three competing objectives** form a **Pareto front** in objective space.
- [ ] **Single-Objective Formulation:** $\min \Phi(\mathbf{x})$ s.t. $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$.
- [ ] **Multi-Objective Formulation:** $\min [f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_k]$ — solution is a set.
- [ ] **Weighted Sum Scalarization:** $\Phi = \sum w_i f_i / f_i^{ref}$ — **must normalize** to dimensionless form.
- [ ] **Limitation of weighted sum:** Cannot capture **non-convex** Pareto regions.
- [ ] **Pareto Dominance:** $\mathbf{x}_1 \prec \mathbf{x}_2$ if $\mathbf{x}_1$ is no worse in all objectives and strictly better in at least one.
- [ ] **PDP** $= P_{dyn} \cdot \tau = \frac{\alpha f_{clk} C_L^2 V_{DD}^3}{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)(V_{DD} - V_T)^2}$ — depends on $C_L^2$.
- [ ] **EDP** $= PDP \cdot \tau$ — used when speed is paramount (e.g., high-performance CPUs).
- [ ] **Lagrangian / KKT** is the analytical foundation of TILOS-style gate sizing.
- [ ] **Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$** = "price of delay" (marginal power per unit delay reduction).
- [ ] **CMOS delay equation (alpha-power):** $\tau = K C_L V_{DD} / (V_{DD} - V_T)^\alpha$.
- [ ] **Dynamic power** $\propto V_{DD}^2 f$ — voltage scaling is the #1 power-saving lever.
- [ ] **Leakage current** $\propto 10^{(V_{GS} - V_T)/S}$ — exponential in $V_T$.
- [ ] **Alpha $\alpha$:** ~1 for long-channel, 1.3–1.5 for short-channel MOSFETs.
- [ ] **Engineering application matrix:** Mobile → EDP min; CPU → delay min; FPGA → area-constrained; 5nm+ → yield max.
- [ ] **Buffer insertion** and **wire sizing** are direct responses to the $C_L^2$ sensitivity in PDP.
- [ ] **Yield objective** $Y = e^{-A \cdot D}$ (Poisson defect model).
- [ ] **Area-Delay Product (ADP)** is the simplest composite objective for layout-aware optimization.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Slicing Floorplan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT525 - CAD BASED VLSI DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Slicing Floorplan

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slicing Floorplan (KTU Standard Definition)**
> A *slicing floorplan* is a rectangular dissection (a partition of a chip area into a set of non-overlapping rectangular blocks) that can be obtained by **recursively bisecting** a rectangle using either a **horizontal cut** or a **vertical cut** until every region corresponds to a single functional module.

The recursive bisection property is what distinguishes slicing floorplans from general (non-slicing) floorplans. A slicing floorplan is always representable as a **slicing tree** (a full binary tree) in which:

- **Leaf nodes** correspond to the physical modules (labeled `1, 2, 3, …, n`).
- **Internal nodes** correspond to cuts — labeled `H` (horizontal cut, the upper sub-block is placed on top of the lower one) or `V` (vertical cut, the left sub-block is placed to the left of the right one).
- **Root node** represents the final chip rectangle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU emphasizes Slicing Floorplans:**
> Although slicing structures represent only a subset of all possible floorplans, they cover **>80%** of practical VLSI designs (Sarrafzadeh & Wong), and they admit **polynomial-time optimization** algorithms — making them the canonical topic for the CAD/VLSI placement module in KTU 2024 scheme.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: Cutting a Sheet Cake**
> Imagine you are a baker cutting a large rectangular cake into pieces for different guests. You are allowed **only two types of cuts**:
> 1. A **straight cut from left to right** (horizontal cut) — splits the cake into a top slab and a bottom slab.
> 2. A **straight cut from top to bottom** (vertical cut) — splits the cake into a left slab and a right slab.
>
> After each cut, you may further slice **only the resulting piece(s)**. If, at the end, every piece corresponds to one guest's portion, the cake was **slicing-friendly**. Some cake patterns (e.g., a non-rectangular spiral layout) are *not* slicing-friendly — those would be the **non-slicing floorplans**.

This simple rule — *recursively cut the rectangle either horizontally or vertically* — is the entire essence of a slicing floorplan.

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Metrics

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Module / Block** | A rectangular region holding one functional unit (e.g., ALU, SRAM, I/O pad). |
| **Aspect Ratio** $r_i$ | $r_i = h_i / w_i$, where $h_i$ and $w_i$ are the height and width of module $i$. |
| **Hard Module** | Dimensions are **fixed**; only placement position can be optimized. |
| **Soft Module** | Dimensions can **vary** within an allowed aspect-ratio range $[r_{i,\min}, r_{i,\max}]$. |
| **Polish Expression** | A post-order (postfix) string of length $2n-1$ that uniquely encodes a slicing tree. |
| **Normalized Polish Expression (NPE)** | A Polish expression that satisfies a specific ordering constraint (no two operands of the same operator are swapped). |

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used in KTU Valuation

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Board Marking Parameters:**
> - Total number of modules in a typical 2-mark problem: **$n = 4$ to $6$**.
> - Number of internal nodes in any slicing tree of $n$ leaves: **$n - 1$**.
> - Total length of a Polish expression: **$2n - 1$ symbols** ($n$ operands + $(n-1)$ operators).
> - Standard cost-weighting values (if not given): **$\lambda = 1$** (wirelength), **$\mu = 1$** (bus cost).
> - All dimensions assumed in **micrometers ($\mu m$)** unless otherwise stated.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Slicing Tree — Construction Logic

A slicing tree is constructed bottom-up. Given $n$ modules, the construction logic is:

1. **Step 1 (Leaf creation):** Create $n$ leaf nodes, each labeled with a unique module ID ($1$ to $n$).
2. **Step 2 (Pairing):** Choose two adjacent sub-floorplans (or single leaves) and combine them using either an **H** (horizontal) or **V** (vertical) cut.
3. **Step 3 (Repeat):** Repeat Step 2 until only one sub-floorplan remains — this becomes the **root** of the slicing tree.
4. **Step 4 (Polish Encoding):** Traverse the tree in **post-order** (Left → Right → Root) and emit a symbol for each visited node to obtain the Polish expression.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Rule:** The post-order traversal order is **fixed**: always visit the **left child first, then the right child, then the parent**. Reversing this order gives a *pre-order* expression, which is a common student mistake that costs **2 marks** per occurrence.

## 2.2 Polish Expression — Formal Encoding

The Polish expression is the postfix notation of the slicing tree:

- A **leaf** contributes its module number (`1, 2, 3, …`).
- An **internal H node** contributes the symbol `H`.
- An **internal V node** contributes the symbol `V`.

**Example:** For a slicing tree combining modules 1, 2 horizontally and modules 3, 4 vertically, the post-order traversal gives:

$$
E = \; 1 \; 2 \; H \; 3 \; 4 \; V
$$

This expression uniquely represents the floorplan topology (the geometric layout is decided later by the *sizing* step).

## 2.3 Normalized Polish Expression (NPE) — The KTU Hot Topic

A Polish expression is **normalized** if, when scanned from left to right, the operands appearing between two consecutive operators of the same type follow a specific monotonic order. Formally:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NPE Constraint (Sarrafzadeh–Wong 1996):**
> For any operator $op \in \{H, V\}$, scan all operands appearing between the first and second occurrence of $op$, between the second and third, and so on. The sequence of operand *positions* must be **monotonically non-decreasing** (i.e., the operands are in the same order as their module numbers).

The NPE constraint **eliminates redundant representations** of the same floorplan topology. For $n$ modules, the number of slicing trees is $O(n! \cdot 8^n / n^{1.5})$, but the number of NPEs is approximately $O(8^n / n^{1.5})$ — a dramatic reduction that makes simulated annealing tractable.

## 2.4 Cost Functions for Floorplan Evaluation

The objective function minimized during floorplan optimization is a weighted sum of three penalty terms:

| Cost Term | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Area** $A$ | $A = w \cdot h$ | Total chip area. |
| **Wirelength** $V$ (often called $L$) | $V = \sum_{e=(u,v) \in N} d(u,v)$ | Total Manhattan distance over all nets. |
| **Bus Cost** $B$ | $B = \sum_{(i,j) \in \text{bus}} \lvert x_i - x_j \rvert$ | Penalty for spreading bus-connected pins. |
| **Aspect Ratio Penalty** $W$ | $W = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W(r_i)$ | Penalty if a soft module's aspect ratio deviates from ideal. |
| **Total Cost** $\Phi$ | $\Phi = A + \lambda V + \mu B$ | Combined objective. |

For soft modules, the most common aspect-ratio penalty is the **quadratic penalty**:

$$
W(r_i) = \left( r_i - r_{i,\text{ideal}} \right)^2
$$

where $r_{i,\text{ideal}} = \sqrt{r_{i,\min} \cdot r_{i,\max}}$ is the geometric mean of the allowed range.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Slicing tree size | $n$ leaves, $n-1$ internal nodes | Verifying tree validity |
| 2 | Polish expression length | $2n - 1$ symbols | Checking expression length |
| 3 | Aspect ratio | $r_i = h_i / w_i$ | Module dimensioning |
| 4 | Area | $A = w \cdot h$ | Cost term |
| 5 | Wirelength (Manhattan) | $V = \sum \lvert x_u - x_v \rvert + \lvert y_u - y_v \rvert$ | Cost term |
| 6 | Bus cost | $B = \sum \lvert x_i - x_j \rvert$ | Cost term |
| 7 | Aspect-ratio penalty | $W(r_i) = (r_i - r_i^*)^2$ | Soft-module fitting |
| 8 | Combined cost | $\Phi = A + \lambda V + \mu B$ | SA objective |
| 9 | NPE test | Operands between same op-type must be in order | Normalize SA moves |
| 10 | Cool schedule (SA) | $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k,\ \alpha \approx 0.9$ | KTU 2-mark question |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These cost functions form the engine of every commercial EDA floorplanner (Synopsys IC Compiler, Cadence Innovus, Mentor Olympus). The slicing-floorplan model is what made mid-1990s industrial tools computationally tractable, and the **simulated-annealing** algorithm on NPEs remains the gold standard for hierarchical VLSI block planning.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Polish Expression to Slicing Tree to Floorplan

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** Six modules with the following Polish expression are to be placed:

$$
E = 1 \, 2 \, H \, 3 \, 4 \, V \, 5 \, 6 \, H \, H
$$

Construct the slicing tree, the rectangular floorplan, and verify whether $E$ is an NPE.

### Step 1 — Build the Slicing Tree from Postfix Expression

We use a **stack-based parser**. We scan $E$ from left to right.

| Symbol Read | Stack State (left = bottom) | Action |
|---|---|---|
| `1` | `1` | Push operand |
| `2` | `1, 2` | Push operand |
| `H` | `(1 2 H)` | Pop two, create H-node, push back |
| `3` | `(1 2 H), 3` | Push operand |
| `4` | `(1 2 H), 3, 4` | Push operand |
| `V` | `(1 2 H), (3 4 V)` | Pop two, create V-node, push back |
| `5` | `(1 2 H), (3 4 V), 5` | Push operand |
| `6` | `(1 2 H), (3 4 V), 5, 6` | Push operand |
| `H` | `(1 2 H), (3 4 V), (5 6 H)` | Pop two, create H-node, push back |
| `H` | `((1 2 H) (3 4 V) (5 6 H) H)` | Pop two, create H-node, push back |

**Final Slicing Tree (textual form):**

```
                H
              /   \
           H         (5 6 H)
         /   \
      (1 2 H) (3 4 V)
```

### Step 2 — Assign Dimensions and Compute Layout

Assume soft modules with area 4 each and ideal aspect ratio 1, so each starts as a $2 \times 2$ square.

- `(1 2 H)` → horizontal cut → 1 on top of 2 → $4 \times 2$ block (width 4, height 2).
- `(3 4 V)` → vertical cut → 3 to the left of 4 → $2 \times 4$ block (width 2, height 4).
- `(5 6 H)` → horizontal cut → 5 on top of 6 → $4 \times 2$ block (width 4, height 2).

Now combine the three sub-blocks under the root `H`:

- `(1 2 H)` is $4 \times 2$ → place on top.
- The bottom of the root `H` is the **side-by-side** vertical stack of `(3 4 V)` and `(5 6 H)` → widths 2 and 4, total width 6; heights 4 and 2 → **max height = 4**.
- So bottom block is $6 \times 4$.
- Top block must match width: top is $4 \times 2$, but bottom is $6$ wide → **resize top** to $6 \times 2$ (modules 1 and 2 each become $3 \times 2$).

**Final floorplan:** $6 \times 6$ outer rectangle, top half = (module 1 on top of module 2) and bottom half = (module 3 | module 4 | module 5 on top of 6).

### Step 3 — Verify NPE

Scan $E = 1 \, 2 \, H \, 3 \, 4 \, V \, 5 \, 6 \, H \, H$ for same-type operators.

- **H-operators** at positions 3, 9, 10.
  - Between position 3 and 9: operands are `3, 4` (positions 4, 5).
  - Between position 9 and 10: operand is `5, 6` (positions 7, 8) — wait, but position 9 is H combining 5,6, so operands between H-9 and H-10 is **empty** (root H has no operands between root and previous).
- **V-operators** at position 6.
  - Only one V, so no constraint to check.

✅ **The expression is a valid NPE.**

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Cost Function Evaluation

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** Given the floorplan from §3.1 with module centers:

- Module 1: $(x, y) = (1.5, 5)$
- Module 2: $(x, y) = (1.5, 4)$
- Module 3: $(x, y) = (1, 2)$
- Module 4: $(x, y) = (3, 2)$
- Module 5: $(x, y) = (4.5, 1)$
- Module 6: $(x, y) = (4.5, 0)$

Nets: $N_1 = (1, 2)$, $N_2 = (3, 4)$, $N_3 = (5, 6)$, $N_4 = (1, 4)$, $N_5 = (2, 5)$.

Compute $A$, $V$, and total cost $\Phi$ (with $\lambda = 1$, $\mu = 0$).

### Step 1 — Compute Area

$$
A = w \cdot h = 6 \times 6 = 36
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Wirelength $V$ (Manhattan Distance)

For each net $N = (u, v)$:
$$
V(N) = |x_u - x_v| + |y_u - y_v|
$$

Apply to each net:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(N_1) &= |1.5 - 1.5| + |5 - 4| = 0 + 1 = 1 \\
V(N_2) &= |1 - 3| + |2 - 2| = 2 + 0 = 2 \\
V(N_3) &= |4.5 - 4.5| + |1 - 0| = 0 + 1 = 1 \\
V(N_4) &= |1.5 - 3| + |5 - 2| = 1.5 + 3 = 4.5 \\
V(N_5) &= |1.5 - 4.5| + |4 - 1| = 3 + 3 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

Total:
$$
V = 1 + 2 + 1 + 4.5 + 6 = 14.5
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Total Cost

$$
\Phi = A + \lambda V + \mu B = 36 + 1 \cdot 14.5 + 0 = 50.5
$$

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Otten's Sizing Iteration (Soft Modules)

For a soft-module slicing tree, the **Otten algorithm** finds optimal module dimensions that minimize area subject to aspect-ratio constraints. The key recurrence for a vertical cut `V` is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{\text{parent}} &= w_{\text{left}} + w_{\text{right}} \\
h_{\text{parent}} &= \max(h_{\text{left}}, h_{\text{right}})
\end{aligned}
$$

For a horizontal cut `H`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{\text{parent}} &= \max(w_{\text{left}}, w_{\text{right}}) \\
h_{\text{parent}} &= h_{\text{left}} + h_{\text{right}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick:** If two sibling sub-blocks have different heights in a `V` cut, the smaller one is **stretched vertically** to match — this is a *topological* stretching, not a physical distortion. The dimensions of leaves are still constrained by their aspect-ratio range.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Slicing-Floorplan Optimization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input: Netlist, Module Library] --> A1[Step 1: Construct Initial Polish Expression]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2: Verify Normalized Polish Expression NPE]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Apply SA Perturbation M1 M2 M3 M4]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Run Otten Sizing Algorithm]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Compute Cost Function Phi]
    A5 --> A6{Acceptance Test Delta Phi and T}
    A6 -- Improved --> A7[Update Best Solution]
    A6 -- Worse but Probabilistic --> A7
    A6 -- Rejected --> A3
    A7 --> A8{Temperature Frozen}
    A8 -- No --> A9[Cool Schedule T = alpha T]
    A9 --> A3
    A8 -- Yes --> A10[Output: Optimized Floorplan and Polish Expression]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Slicing Tree for the Expression 1 2 H 3 4 V 5 6 H H

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootH[H Root] --> leftSub[(1 2 H)]
    rootH --> midSub[(3 4 V)]
    rootH --> rightSub[(5 6 H)]
    leftSub --> L1[Module 1]
    leftSub --> L2[Module 2]
    midSub --> L3[Module 3]
    midSub --> L4[Module 4]
    rightSub --> L5[Module 5]
    rightSub --> L6[Module 6]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Simulated Annealing Move Set (M1–M4)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[M1: Swap Two Operands] --> NPE{NPE Valid?}
    M2[M2: Complement Operator H to V or V to H] --> NPE
    M3[M3: Rotate a Module Swap w and h] --> NPE
    M4[M4: Move Module to Different Position] --> NPE
    NPE -- Yes --> ACC[Accept Move]
    NPE -- No --> REJ[Reject Move]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Cost Function Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FP[Floorplan Layout] --> A[Area A]
    FP --> V[Wirelength V]
    FP --> B[Bus Cost B]
    FP --> W[Aspect Ratio Penalty W]
    A --> PHI[Phi = A + lambda V + mu B]
    V --> PHI
    B --> PHI
    W --> SOFT[Soft Module Sizing]
    SOFT --> FP
```

## 4.5 Schematic Description (Verbal) — Physical Layout

> [!TIP]
> **Picture the floorplan from §3.1 as a $6 \times 6$ chip:**
> - **Top half (height 2):** Module 1 occupies the upper-left $3 \times 2$ block, Module 2 sits below it.
> - **Bottom half (height 4):** Divided vertically into Module 3 (left, $2 \times 4$), Module 4 (middle, $2 \times 4$), and a right column of width 2 containing Module 5 on top of Module 6.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a slicing floorplan and a slicing tree. How many internal nodes does a slicing tree with $n$ modules contain?**

**Model Answer:**
A slicing floorplan is a rectangular dissection of a chip area that can be obtained by recursively cutting a rectangle using either a horizontal (H) or vertical (V) cut until each resulting region represents a single module. A slicing tree is a full binary tree representation of this dissection, where leaves correspond to modules and internal nodes represent H/V cuts.

For $n$ modules, a full binary tree has exactly **$n - 1$** internal nodes.

**Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for slicing floorplan definition, 1 mark for slicing tree definition, 1 mark for $n-1$ count.

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is a Normalized Polish Expression? Why is it used in slicing-floorplan optimization?**

**Model Answer:**
A Normalized Polish Expression (NPE) is a Polish expression that satisfies the monotonicity constraint: for any operator type (H or V), the operands appearing between consecutive occurrences of that operator must be in the same order as their module numbers. NPE is used because it eliminates **redundant encodings** of the same slicing tree, reducing the search space by a factor of $O(n!)$ and making Simulated Annealing–based floorplan optimization computationally tractable.

**Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for NPE definition, 1 mark for the constraint, 1 mark for the algorithmic benefit.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question B-A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 Pattern]`

**For six modules with the following parameters:**

| Module | Area $a_i$ | Min AR $r_{i,\min}$ | Max AR $r_{i,\max}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 6 | 0.5 | 2.0 |
| 2 | 6 | 0.5 | 2.0 |
| 3 | 4 | 1.0 | 1.0 (hard) |
| 4 | 4 | 1.0 | 1.0 (hard) |
| 5 | 8 | 0.5 | 2.0 |
| 6 | 8 | 0.5 | 2.0 |

Consider the Polish expression: $E = 1 \; 2 \; H \; 3 \; 4 \; V \; 5 \; 6 \; H \; V$.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**(i)** Draw the slicing tree corresponding to $E$. **(ii)** Verify that $E$ is a Normalized Polish Expression.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Slicing Tree construction** (using stack parsing from left to right):

1. `1, 2, H` → build `(1 2 H)`.
2. `3, 4, V` → build `(3 4 V)`.
3. `5, 6, H` → build `(5 6 H)`.
4. Final `V` → combines all three sub-blocks vertically.

```
              V
           /  |  \
         H    V    (5 6 H)
        / \   / \
       1   2 3   4
```

**(ii) NPE verification:** Scan $E$ for same-type operators.

- H-operators at positions 3, 8.
  - Between them: operands `3, 4` (positions 4, 5). They appear in increasing order: 3 < 4. ✅
- V-operators at positions 6, 10.
  - Between them: operand `5, 6` (positions 7, 8). 5 < 6. ✅

Therefore, $E$ is a valid NPE.

**Marking Scheme:** 3 marks for tree drawing, 2 marks for operand extraction, 2 marks for NPE conclusion.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compute the optimal dimensions of the soft modules (1, 2, 5, 6) using the area and aspect-ratio range, and determine the final bounding rectangle dimensions.**

**Model Solution:**

Module 3 and 4 are hard $1 \times 1$ (area 4, AR = 1). Their size is fixed.

For soft modules, we begin by choosing **ideal aspect ratio** (the geometric mean):
- Modules 1, 2: $r^* = \sqrt{0.5 \times 2.0} = 1.0$ → square.
- Modules 5, 6: $r^* = \sqrt{0.5 \times 2.0} = 1.0$ → square.

Initial dimensions (since $h_i w_i = a_i$ and $h_i = w_i$ for ideal AR):

$$
w_1 = w_2 = h_1 = h_2 = \sqrt{6} \approx 2.449
$$

$$
w_5 = w_6 = h_5 = h_6 = \sqrt{8} \approx 2.828
$$

**Bottom-up dimension computation:**

- `(1 2 H)` → horizontal cut, widths add in width-axis → $w = 2.449 + 2.449 = 4.898$, $h = 2.449$ (max of children's heights).
- `(3 4 V)` → vertical cut, heights add → $w = 2$, $h = 2$ (since both $1 \times 1$, total $2 \times 1$ wait: $w = 1+1 = 2$, $h = \max(1,1) = 1$, so $2 \times 1$).
- `(5 6 H)` → horizontal cut → $w = 2.828 + 2.828 = 5.657$, $h = 2.828$.

- **Root `V`** combines three sub-blocks vertically (side by side).
  - Total width = sum of widths = $4.898 + 2 + 5.657 = 12.555$.
  - Total height = max of heights = $\max(2.449, 1, 2.828) = 2.828$.

**Apply Otten's adjustment** — since the top-of-root (1 2 H) has width 4.898 but the bottom slabs have width 12.555, no adjustment is needed; the top simply sits aligned to the left of a wider bottom strip. The final bounding rectangle is:

$$
W_{\text{chip}} = 12.555 \quad \mu m, \quad H_{\text{chip}} = 2.828 \quad \mu m
$$

Total chip area: $A = 12.555 \times 2.828 \approx 35.51 \; \mu m^2$.

**Marking Scheme:** 2 marks for AR computation, 3 marks for bottom-up sizing, 2 marks for final dimensions.

---

### Question B-B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Alternative Pattern]`

**For four modules with the Polish expression $E = 1 \; 2 \; 3 \; H \; V \; 4 \; H$:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Draw the slicing tree. Identify the operators between the modules. Is this a valid NPE?**

**Model Solution:**

**Stack parsing:**
1. `1, 2, 3` → stack: `1, 2, 3`.
2. `H` → combine `2, 3` → `(2 3 H)`. Stack: `1, (2 3 H)`.
3. `V` → combine `1, (2 3 H)` → `(1 (2 3 H) V)`. Stack: `(1 (2 3 H) V)`.
4. `4` → stack: `(1 (2 3 H) V), 4`.
5. `H` → combine → `((1 (2 3 H) V) 4 H)`.

**Slicing Tree:**
```
            H
          /   \
         V     4
        / \
       1   H
          / \
         2   3
```

**Operator placement between modules:**
- Modules 2 and 3 separated by `H` (in the right subtree).
- Modules 1 and (2,3) separated by `V` (in the left subtree).
- Module (1,2,3) and module 4 separated by `H` (root).

**NPE test:** Same-type operators H appear at positions 4, 7.
- Between H-4 and H-7: operand `1` (position 1).
- Between the two H's there is only one operand, so trivially valid. ✅

The expression is a valid NPE.

**Marking Scheme:** 3 marks for tree, 2 marks for operator identification, 2 marks for NPE.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Apply the four SA moves (M1, M2, M3, M4) ONE at a time starting from $E = 1 \; 2 \; 3 \; H \; V \; 4 \; H$. For each, give the resulting expression and check NPE validity.**

**Model Solution:**

**M1 — Swap two operands (positions 2 and 3, operands 2 and 3):**

$$
E_1 = 1 \; 3 \; 2 \; H \; V \; 4 \; H
$$

NPE test: H at position 4, 7. Operands between = `3, 2`. Order 3, 2 is **not** monotonic (3 > 2). ❌ **Invalid NPE** — reject this move.

**M2 — Complement an operator (change the V at position 5 to H):**

$$
E_2 = 1 \; 2 \; 3 \; H \; H \; 4 \; H
$$

NPE test: H at positions 4, 5, 7. Between H-4 and H-5: empty. Between H-5 and H-7: operand `4`. Valid. ✅

**M3 — Rotate a module (swap module 1's width and height in the layout, not the expression):**
This move affects the **layout**, not the expression. After M3, the dimensions of module 1 are transposed. Expression unchanged: $E_3 = 1 \; 2 \; 3 \; H \; V \; 4 \; H$. ✅ (always NPE-valid since expression unchanged).

**M4 — Move module 4 to between modules 1 and 2:**

$$
E_4 = 1 \; 4 \; 2 \; 3 \; H \; V \; H
$$

NPE test: H at positions 5, 7. Between H-5 and H-7: empty. V at position 6. ✅ Valid NPE.

**Marking Scheme:** 2 marks for each of M1, M2, M3, M4 — 1 mark for new expression, 1 mark for NPE check.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong traversal order** — Writing the expression using *pre-order* instead of *post-order* (cost: 2 marks per question).
> 2. **Confusing operand position with module number** — In NPE verification, you must check the **order of operand labels**, not their physical positions in the chip (e.g., if module 1 ends up on the right, the label is still `1`).
> 3. **Forgetting that hard modules are fixed** — Even with an aspect-ratio range $[0.5, 2.0]$, a hard module is a single point: $r_i = 1$ exactly.
> 4. **Misapplying Otten's rule** — In an H cut, widths do **not** add; heights add. Many students reverse this.
> 5. **Ignoring the cooling schedule** — In SA questions, the new temperature $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$ must be reported (typically $\alpha = 0.9$ or $0.95$).
> 6. **Counting wrong expression length** — A Polish expression for $n$ modules has exactly $2n - 1$ symbols. A length mismatch is an automatic deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Slicing Floorplan**
> - **Slicing floorplan** = recursive H/V bisection of a rectangle.
> - **Slicing tree** = full binary tree; $n$ leaves, $n-1$ internal nodes.
> - **Polish expression** = post-order traversal of the slicing tree; length $2n-1$.
> - **NPE** = constraint that operands between same-type operators are monotonically ordered; reduces redundancy.
> - **Cost function** $\Phi = A + \lambda V + \mu B$ combines area, wirelength, and bus cost.
> - **Aspect-ratio penalty** for soft modules: $W(r_i) = (r_i - r_i^*)^2$ with $r_i^* = \sqrt{r_{i,\min} \cdot r_{i,\max}}$.
> - **Otten's algorithm** propagates dimensions bottom-up then top-down; adjusts sub-block widths in V cuts and heights in H cuts.
> - **SA moves**: M1 (swap operands), M2 (complement operator), M3 (rotate module), M4 (move module).
> - **Cooling schedule**: $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$, $\alpha \in [0.9, 0.95]$.
> - **Hard modules** have fixed dimensions; only soft modules vary.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Slicing Floorplan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT525 - CAD BASED VLSI DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Non-Slicing Floorplan

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Non-Slicing Floorplan):**
> A *non-slicing floorplan* is a rectangular dissection of a chip area into a set of $n$ non-overlapping rectangular modules such that no straight horizontal or vertical line can recursively bisect the entire layout into exactly two non-trivial sub-floorplans. In graph-theoretic terms, the corresponding slicing tree does **not** exist.

Formally, given a set of $n$ rectangular modules $M = \{m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_n\}$ with given widths $w_i$ and heights $h_i$, a non-slicing floorplan is an assignment of coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$ to each module such that:

$$\forall\, i \neq j : \quad (x_i, y_i, x_i + w_i, y_i + h_i) \cap (x_j, y_j, x_j + w_j, y_j + h_j) = \emptyset$$

and the layout is **not** representable by any binary slicing tree.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Modern City Zoning Problem":**
> Imagine an architect laying out plots of irregular shapes inside a square city block. A **slicing floorplan** is like an architect who can only subdivide land by drawing one straight line at a time, splitting a region into two, and then repeating this. A **non-slicing floorplan** is the realistic case: the architect must place all plots simultaneously such that they tile the block perfectly without any single straight cut being able to separate the whole layout into two clean halves. Think of fitting irregularly shaped furniture into a room — you cannot find a single straight line that cleanly divides the furniture into "left group" and "right group" without cutting through one of the pieces.

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Distinguishing Metrics

| Term | Slicing Floorplan | Non-Slicing Floorplan |
|---|---|---|
| Slicing tree representation | Exists (binary tree) | **Does not exist** |
| Solution space | Smaller | **Larger** (more general) |
| Optimizability | Fast (Polish Expression) | Complex (need special codes) |
| Flexibility | Limited | **Higher packability** |
| Area/wirelength quality | Sub-optimal | **Better optimum reachable** |
| Evaluation complexity | $O(n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ to $O(n^2)$ |

The non-slicing representation provides a **strict superset** of the solution space, meaning every slicing floorplan can be encoded as a non-slicing one, but the converse is not true.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** The question most frequently asked in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE papers is: *"Why are non-slicing floorplans preferred in modern VLSI design?"* — Answer: They provide a larger solution space, allowing the placement tool to find a more area-efficient and wirelength-optimal layout, which is essential for sub-7nm technology nodes.

## 1.4 Boundary Conditions & Constraints

A valid non-slicing floorplan must satisfy the following standard KTU 2024 conditions:

1. **Rectangular modules** — each $m_i$ has fixed width $w_i$ and height $h_i$ (soft modules may be resized with area constraint $w_i h_i = A_i$).
2. **Zero overlap** — pairwise disjoint interiors.
3. **Bounded by a chip outline** — typically enclosed in a bounding rectangle of width $W$ and height $H$.
4. **Non-slicing property** — no recursive guillotine cut is possible across the entire layout.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Non-Slicing Floorplans? — The Operational "Why"

The motivation for studying non-slicing floorplans emerges from a fundamental limitation of slicing structures:

- **Slicing floorplans** are restricted to layouts that can be produced by recursive straight-line cuts.
- Modern VLSI designs use **macro blocks** of varying aspect ratios, and achieving the minimum-area floorplan in benchmarks like *ami33* and *ami49* often **requires** non-slicing arrangements.
- The non-slicing search space is approximately **$n$-times larger** than the slicing space, providing better optima.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note (KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight):**
> The seminal work on non-slicing floorplan representations is attributed to **Murata et al. (1996)** for Sequence Pairs, **Guo et al. (1999)** for O-trees, **Chang et al. (2000)** for Corner Block Lists, and **Nakatake et al. (1998)** for Bounded Slicing Grids. These are the **four primary non-slicing representations** in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

## 2.2 The Four Primary Non-Slicing Representations

### 2.2.1 Sequence Pair (SP) — Murata et al.

A sequence pair is a pair of permutations $(\Gamma_+, \Gamma_-)$ of $n$ module names.

**Horizontal relation** (left-right): For two modules $A$ and $B$:

$$(A \prec_{+} B) \text{ and } (A \prec_{-} B) \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \text{ is to the LEFT of } B$$

**Vertical relation** (below-above): 

$$(A \prec_{-} B) \text{ and } (B \prec_{+} A) \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \text{ is BELOW } B$$

### 2.2.2 Bounded Slicing Grid (BSG)

The chip area is divided into a $k \times k$ grid of slots. Modules occupy one or more consecutive slots forming a rectangle.

### 2.2.3 Ordered Tree (O-Tree) — Guo et al.

A labeled ordered tree encodes both the horizontal and vertical adjacency of modules. Uses a depth-first traversal string.

### 2.2.4 Corner Block List (CBL) — Chang et al.

A sequence of module placements where each module is placed in a "corner" of the remaining available space.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam-Critical Formula Box:** The following table contains every formula you need for a 14-mark KTU question on non-slicing floorplans. The most heavily tested one is the **longest path in constraint graphs for Sequence Pairs**.

| # | Concept | Formula / Property | Unit / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Width of floorplan $W$ | $W = \text{longest path in HCG}(G_H)$ | $\mu m$ |
| 2 | Height of floorplan $H$ | $H = \text{longest path in VCG}(G_V)$ | $\mu m$ |
| 3 | Total chip area | $A = W \times H$ | $\mu m^2$ |
| 4 | Number of SPs for $n$ modules | $N_{SP} = (n!)^2$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Distinct SPs (symmetry) | $N_{SP}^{unique} \approx (n!)^2 / 4$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Number of O-trees (T,B encodings) | $N_{OT} = n! \cdot \mathrm{Cat}(n) \cdot 2^{n-1}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Number of CBLs | $N_{CBL} \approx n! \cdot 2^{n-2}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | BSG slot count for $n$ modules | $k = \lceil \sqrt{n} \rceil$ (typical) | grid slots |
| 9 | Constraint graph edges | $\vert E \vert \le 2 \cdot n \choose 2$ | edges |
| 10 | SP evaluation complexity | $O(n \log n)$ or $O(n^2)$ | operations |
| 11 | HCG longest path | $x_j = x_i + w_i$ for edge $i \rightarrow j$ | coordinate |
| 12 | VCG longest path | $y_j = y_i + h_i$ for edge $i \rightarrow j$ | coordinate |

> **Notation Note:** The notation $\vert E \vert$ used in the table denotes the *cardinality* (count) of edges. For absolute value or magnitude of a single variable, we write $\lvert x \rvert$ in inline math context.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Non-Slicing Floorplan |
|---|---|
| **Standard Cell Placement** | Macro blocks in modern SoCs (e.g., Apple M-series) need non-slicing packing. |
| **3D-IC Stacking** | Cross-layer module placement inherently non-slicing. |
| **FPGA Architecture Mapping** | CLB placement with mixed aspect ratios. |
| **PCB Routing Pre-placement** | Component placement on rigid boards. |
| **MEMS Layout Synthesis** | Mixed-size mechanical + electronic modules. |

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Connection:** Cadence Innovus and Synopsys IC Compiler II internally use non-slicing representations (analogous to SP/TCG) for global placement because slicing representations cannot model the irregular macro placements found in real ASICs.

## 2.5 Constraint Graph Construction — Logic Flow

The horizontal constraint graph (HCG) $G_H = (V, E_H)$ and vertical constraint graph (VCG) $G_V = (V, E_V)$ are constructed from a sequence pair $(\Gamma_+, \Gamma_-)$ as follows:

1. For every pair $(A, B)$ appearing as $\ldots A \ldots B \ldots$ in both $\Gamma_+$ and $\Gamma_-$:
   - Add a directed edge $A \rightarrow B$ in $G_H$ with weight $w_A$.
2. For every pair $(A, B)$ where $A$ appears before $B$ in $\Gamma_-$ but after $B$ in $\Gamma_+$:
   - Add a directed edge $A \rightarrow B$ in $G_V$ with weight $h_A$.

The coordinates are then obtained by:

$$x_B = \max_{(A, B) \in E_H} (x_A + w_A)$$

$$y_B = \max_{(A, B) \in E_V} (y_A + h_A)$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem Setup for the Worked Example

Let us work through the **most important KTU question type**: Given a sequence pair and module dimensions, derive the floorplan dimensions using longest path computation.

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Worked Example Statement (Sample 14-mark Question):**
> Consider $n = 4$ modules with the following dimensions:
>
> | Module | Width $w_i$ | Height $h_i$ |
> |---|---|---|
> | A | 2 | 4 |
> | B | 3 | 2 |
> | C | 4 | 3 |
> | D | 1 | 5 |
>
> Given the sequence pair $(\Gamma_+, \Gamma_-) = (\text{ADBC}, \text{CABD})$, compute:
> 1. The Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG) and the $x$-coordinates.
> 2. The Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG) and the $y$-coordinates.
> 3. The total chip area.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation

### Step 1: Establish the Two Permutations

We are given:

$$\Gamma_+ = (\text{A}, \text{D}, \text{B}, \text{C})$$

$$\Gamma_- = (\text{C}, \text{A}, \text{B}, \text{D})$$

### Step 2: Determine All Pairwise Relations

We examine every unordered pair $\{A, B, C, D\}$ to determine whether each pair is horizontal or vertical.

**Pair (A, B):**
- In $\Gamma_+$: A is at position 1, B is at position 3. So A appears **before** B.
- In $\Gamma_-$: A is at position 2, B is at position 3. So A appears **before** B.
- Conclusion: A is to the **LEFT** of B. Edge in $G_H$: $A \rightarrow B$ with weight $w_A = 2$.

**Pair (A, C):**
- In $\Gamma_+$: A at position 1, C at position 4. A appears **before** C.
- In $\Gamma_-$: A at position 2, C at position 1. A appears **after** C.
- Conclusion: A is **BELOW** C. Edge in $G_V$: $A \rightarrow C$ with weight $h_A = 4$.

**Pair (A, D):**
- In $\Gamma_+$: A at position 1, D at position 2. A appears **before** D.
- In $\Gamma_-$: A at position 2, D at position 4. A appears **before** D.
- Conclusion: A is to the **LEFT** of D. Edge in $G_H$: $A \rightarrow D$ with weight $w_A = 2$.

**Pair (B, C):**
- In $\Gamma_+$: B at position 3, C at position 4. B appears **before** C.
- In $\Gamma_-$: B at position 3, C at position 1. B appears **after** C.
- Conclusion: B is **BELOW** C. Edge in $G_V$: $B \rightarrow C$ with weight $h_B = 2$.

**Pair (B, D):**
- In $\Gamma_+$: B at position 3, D at position 2. B appears **after** D.
- In $\Gamma_-$: B at position 3, D at position 4. B appears **before** D.
- Conclusion: B is to the **LEFT** of D. Edge in $G_H$: $B \rightarrow D$ with weight $w_B = 3$.

**Pair (C, D):**
- In $\Gamma_+$: C at position 4, D at position 2. C appears **after** D.
- In $\Gamma_-$: C at position 1, D at position 4. C appears **before** D.
- Conclusion: C is **BELOW** D. Edge in $G_V$: $C \rightarrow D$ with weight $h_C = 3$.

### Step 3: Construct the Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG)

Edges of $G_H$ (with associated weights $w$ used as labels):

$$G_H : A \xrightarrow{w=2} B, \quad A \xrightarrow{w=2} D, \quad B \xrightarrow{w=3} D$$

### Step 4: Compute $x$-coordinates via Longest Path in HCG

We use the recursive relation:

$$x_B = \max_{(u, B) \in E_H} (x_u + w_u)$$

Topological order of HCG: A $\rightarrow$ B $\rightarrow$ D.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_A &= 0 \quad \text{(source node, no incoming edges)} \\
x_B &= x_A + w_A = 0 + 2 = 2 \\
x_D &= \max(x_A + w_A, \; x_B + w_B) = \max(0 + 2, \; 2 + 3) = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Width of chip:** $W = \max(x_i + w_i)$ over all modules.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \max(x_A + w_A, \; x_B + w_B, \; x_C + w_C, \; x_D + w_D) \\
  &= \max(0 + 2, \; 2 + 3, \; 0 + 4, \; 5 + 1) \\
  &= \max(2, \; 5, \; 4, \; 6) = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Construct the Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG)

Edges of $G_V$ (with associated weights $h$ used as labels):

$$G_V : A \xrightarrow{h=4} C, \quad B \xrightarrow{h=2} C, \quad C \xrightarrow{h=3} D$$

### Step 6: Compute $y$-coordinates via Longest Path in VCG

We use the recursive relation:

$$y_v = \max_{(u, v) \in E_V} (y_u + h_u)$$

Topological order of VCG: A $\rightarrow$ C $\rightarrow$ D and B $\rightarrow$ C $\rightarrow$ D.

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_A &= 0 \quad \text{(source)} \\
y_B &= 0 \quad \text{(source)} \\
y_C &= \max(y_A + h_A, \; y_B + h_B) = \max(0 + 4, \; 0 + 2) = 4 \\
y_D &= y_C + h_C = 4 + 3 = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Height of chip:** $H = \max(y_i + h_i)$ over all modules.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \max(y_A + h_A, \; y_B + h_B, \; y_C + h_C, \; y_D + h_D) \\
  &= \max(0 + 4, \; 0 + 2, \; 4 + 3, \; 7 + 5) \\
  &= \max(4, \; 2, \; 7, \; 12) = 12
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Compute Total Chip Area

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= W \times H = 6 \times 12 = 72 \; \mu m^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8: Final Summary of Module Coordinates

| Module | $x$ | $y$ | $x + w$ | $y + h$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 0 | 2 | 4 |
| B | 2 | 0 | 5 | 2 |
| C | 0 | 4 | 4 | 7 |
| D | 5 | 7 | 6 | 12 |

## 3.3 Solution Validation: Why This Floorplan is Non-Slicing

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of Non-Slicing Property:**
> Looking at the derived coordinates, attempt to find a vertical guillotine cut at, say, $x = 4$ (between modules C and the right group). Module A and Module C extend from $x=0$ to $x=4$ (left side), but Module A's $y$-range $(0, 4)$ and Module C's $y$-range $(4, 7)$ are vertically stacked. There is no single horizontal or vertical line that cleanly bisects the entire layout into two non-trivial sub-floorplans. Hence, this is confirmed as a **non-slicing floorplan**.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Table

| Computation Step | Input | Output | Algorithm Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| Relation extraction | $(\Gamma_+, \Gamma_-)$ | HCG + VCG edge sets | Pairwise permutation comparison |
| $x$-coordinates | HCG edges + $w_i$ | $x_i$ for all modules | Longest path in DAG |
| $y$-coordinates | VCG edges + $h_i$ | $y_i$ for all modules | Longest path in DAG |
| Chip width | $\{x_i + w_i\}$ | $W$ | Max over all modules |
| Chip height | $\{y_i + h_i\}$ | $H$ | Max over all modules |
| Total area | $W, H$ | $A$ | $W \times H$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of Non-Slicing Floorplan Representations
