The following Mermaid block diagram maps the four primary non-slicing representation families, their construction flow, and evaluation pipeline — exactly as students are expected to understand for the KTU 2024 scheme.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputNode[Input: n Modules with Width and Height]
    inputNode --> spNode[Sequence Pair SP]
    inputNode --> bsgNode[Bounded Slicing Grid BSG]
    inputNode --> otreeNode[Ordered Tree O-Tree]
    inputNode --> cblNode[Corner Block List CBL]

    spNode --> hcgNode[Build Horizontal Constraint Graph HCG]
    spNode --> vcgNode[Build Vertical Constraint Graph VCG]

    hcgNode --> xcalcNode[Longest Path for X Coordinates]
    vcgNode --> ycalcNode[Longest Path for Y Coordinates]

    bsgNode --> gridNode[Assign Modules to Grid Slots]
    otreeNode --> dfsNode[DFS Traversal and Tree Encoding]
    cblNode --> cornerNode[Place Modules at Corner Positions]

    xcalcNode --> placeNode[Generate Floorplan Placement]
    ycalcNode --> placeNode
    gridNode --> placeNode
    dfsNode --> placeNode
    cornerNode --> placeNode

    placeNode --> areaNode[Compute Total Area A equals W times H]
    placeNode --> wireNode[Compute Wirelength Cost]
    placeNode --> costNode[Cost Function Evaluation]

    costNode --> optNode[Optimization Simulated Annealing]
    optNode --> finalNode[Final Non-Slicing Floorplan Output]
```

## 4.2 Algorithm Flow: Sequence Pair Evaluation Pipeline

The following diagram illustrates the **end-to-end sequence pair evaluation flow** — the highest-yield topic for KTU 14-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Start: Receive SP Gamma Plus and Gamma Minus]
    stepB[Initialize empty HCG and VCG]
    stepC[For every module pair P Q do]
    stepD[Check order of P and Q in Gamma Plus]
    stepE[Check order of P and Q in Gamma Minus]
    stepF{Same order in both?}
    stepF -->|YES| stepG[Add edge P to Q in HCG with weight wP]
    stepF -->|NO| stepH[Add edge P to Q in VCG with weight hP]
    stepG --> stepI[Continue loop]
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ{All pairs processed?}
    stepJ -->|NO| stepC
    stepJ -->|YES| stepK[Topological sort of HCG and VCG]
    stepK --> stepL[Compute x via longest path in HCG]
    stepK --> stepM[Compute y via longest path in VCG]
    stepL --> stepN[Compute W as max of x plus w]
    stepM --> stepO[Compute H as max of y plus h]
    stepN --> stepP[Compute Area A equals W times H]
    stepO --> stepP
    stepP --> stepQ[Return placement and area]
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical Classification of Floorplan Representations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootNode[Floorplan Representations]
    rootNode --> slicingNode[Slicing Floorplans]
    rootNode --> nonslicingNode[Non-Slicing Floorplans]

    slicingNode --> polishNode[Polish Expression Normalized Slicing Tree]

    nonslicingNode --> spNode2[Sequence Pair]
    nonslicingNode --> bsgNode2[BSG]
    nonslicingNode --> otreeNode2[O-Tree]
    nonslicingNode --> cblNode2[Corner Block List]
    nonslicingNode --> tcgNode[TCG Transitive Closure Graph]

    spNode2 --> advSp[Advantage: Simple SP evaluation]
    bsgNode2 --> advBsg[Advantage: Bounded slot packing]
    otreeNode2 --> advOtree[Advantage: Reduced redundancy]
    cblNode2 --> advCbl[Advantage: Compact encoding]
    tcgNode --> advTcg[Advantage: Transitive edges]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a computational stage. Solid arrows indicate data flow direction. The decision blocks (diamonds) represent branching logic — typically the order-comparison step that decides whether a constraint edge belongs to the HCG or VCG.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Differentiate between slicing and non-slicing floorplan representations. Give one example representation for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Slicing Floorplan | Non-Slicing Floorplan |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Recursively bisectable by straight lines | Not recursively bisectable by straight lines |
| Representation | Slicing tree / Polish expression | Sequence pair, BSG, O-tree, CBL |
| Solution space | Smaller (subset of all floorplans) | Larger (superset) |
| Example representation | Polish expression `12V34H` | Sequence pair `(ADBC, CABD)` |
| Evaluation | $O(n)$ via stack-based parsing | $O(n \log n)$ to $O(n^2)$ |
| Optimality | Sometimes sub-optimal | **Closer to global optimum** |

**(1 mark per row: definition + example representation, 1 mark difference, 1 mark optimality/solution space.)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**What is a sequence pair? Explain the horizontal and vertical relations used in sequence pair representation with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **sequence pair (SP)** is an ordered pair of permutations $(\Gamma_+, \Gamma_-)$ of $n$ module names (Murata et al., 1996) that uniquely encodes a rectangular floorplan.

- **Horizontal Relation (A is LEFT of B):** If A appears before B in *both* $\Gamma_+$ and $\Gamma_-$, i.e., $A \prec_+ B$ and $A \prec_- B$. **(1 mark)**
- **Vertical Relation (A is BELOW B):** If A appears after B in $\Gamma_+$ but before B in $\Gamma_-$, i.e., $B \prec_+ A$ and $A \prec_- B$. **(1 mark)**
- **Example:** For SP $(ADBC, CABD)$: A is left of D (A before D in both), and C is below D (C before D in $\Gamma_-$, D before C in $\Gamma_+$). **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]**

*Given the following four modules with dimensions:*
*Module A: w=2, h=4; Module B: w=3, h=2; Module C: w=4, h=3; Module D: w=1, h=5.*

*For the sequence pair $(\Gamma_+, \Gamma_-) = (ADBC, CABD)$:*
*(i) Construct the Horizontal Constraint Graph HCG. (ii) Construct the Vertical Constraint Graph VCG. (iii) Determine all module coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$ using the longest path method. (iv) Compute the total chip area. (14 marks divided as 2+2+2+1 in sub-parts)*

**Model Solution:**

*(See Section 3 of these notes for the full exhaustive derivation.)*

**Final Answer Box:**

$$W = 6, \quad H = 12, \quad A = W \times H = 72 \; \mu m^2$$

Coordinates: A(0, 0), B(2, 0), C(0, 4), D(5, 7).

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]**

*Explain the Bounded Slicing Grid (BSG) representation for non-slicing floorplans. Mention the slot assignment rules and the time complexity of placement. Also, state one advantage and one limitation of BSG compared to Sequence Pairs.*

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** The **Bounded Slicing Grid (BSG)** representation (Nakatake et al., 1998) discretizes the chip area into a $k \times k$ grid of equally-sized slots. Each module occupies a rectangular region formed by 1 or more consecutive grid slots.

**Slot Assignment Rules (3 marks):**
1. Each module must be placed in a **rectangular block** of slots.
2. No two modules may overlap in slot assignments.
3. Modules are typically placed starting from the **bottom-left** corner.
4. Slot merging is allowed when both horizontally and vertically adjacent slots are empty and form a rectangle.

**Time Complexity (1 mark):** $O(k^2)$ for slot enumeration, $O(n)$ for module placement where $n$ is the number of modules. The grid size $k = \lceil \sqrt{n} \rceil$ is typically chosen.

**Comparison (1 mark):**
- **Advantage over SP:** BSG provides a bounded solution space, allowing faster convergence in simulated annealing.
- **Limitation vs SP:** BSG cannot represent all possible floorplans (some layouts cannot be packed into the bounded grid), while SP has a larger search space.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

*For a floorplan with $n=4$ modules $\{A, B, C, D\}$ with dimensions:*
*$A(3,2), B(2,3), C(4,1), D(1,4)$.*

*Given the sequence pair $(\Gamma_+, \Gamma_-) = (ABCD, BDAC)$:*
*(i) Identify all horizontal relations between module pairs. (ii) Identify all vertical relations. (iii) Construct both HCG and VCG. (iv) Compute the chip width and height.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Permutations given**

$$\Gamma_+ = (A, B, C, D), \quad \Gamma_- = (B, D, A, C)$$

**Step 2: Pairwise relation analysis (i & ii)**

| Pair | Position in $\Gamma_+$ | Position in $\Gamma_-$ | Relation | Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A, B | A: 1, B: 2 (A before B) | A: 3, B: 1 (A after B) | A below B | Vertical |
| A, C | A: 1, C: 3 (A before C) | A: 3, C: 4 (A before C) | A left of C | Horizontal |
| A, D | A: 1, D: 4 (A before D) | A: 3, D: 2 (A after D) | A below D | Vertical |
| B, C | B: 2, C: 3 (B before C) | B: 1, C: 4 (B before C) | B left of C | Horizontal |
| B, D | B: 2, D: 4 (B before D) | B: 1, D: 2 (B before D) | B left of D | Horizontal |
| C, D | C: 3, D: 4 (C before D) | C: 4, D: 2 (C after D) | C below D | Vertical |

**Step 3: Build constraint graphs (iii)**

HCG edges (with weight $w$):
- A $\rightarrow$ C (weight $w_A = 3$)
- B $\rightarrow$ C (weight $w_B = 2$)
- B $\rightarrow$ D (weight $w_B = 2$)

VCG edges (with weight $h$):
- A $\rightarrow$ B (weight $h_A = 2$)
- A $\rightarrow$ D (weight $h_A = 2$)
- C $\rightarrow$ D (weight $h_C = 1$)

**Step 4: Compute coordinates (iv)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_A &= 0, \quad x_B = 0 \\
x_C &= \max(x_A + w_A, \; x_B + w_B) = \max(3, 2) = 3 \\
x_D &= x_B + w_B = 0 + 2 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \max(x_A + w_A, \; x_B + w_B, \; x_C + w_C, \; x_D + w_D) \\
  &= \max(3, 2, 7, 3) = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_A &= 0, \quad y_C = 0 \\
y_B &= y_A + h_A = 0 + 2 = 2 \\
y_D &= \max(y_A + h_A, \; y_C + h_C) = \max(0 + 2, \; 0 + 1) = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \max(y_A + h_A, \; y_B + h_B, \; y_C + h_C, \; y_D + h_D) \\
  &= \max(2, 5, 1, 6) = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $W = 7, \quad H = 6, \quad A = 42 \; \mu m^2$.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

*Describe the O-Tree representation for non-slicing floorplans. With a suitable diagram representation, explain how the DFS traversal of an O-tree uniquely encodes a floorplan. Compare O-tree with Sequence Pair in terms of solution space size and computation time.*

**Model Solution:**

**O-Tree Definition (2 marks):** An **O-tree** (Guo et al., 1999) is a labeled ordered tree where each node corresponds to a module, and the **horizontal** placement is determined by the tree structure itself (sibling order = horizontal order), while the **vertical** placement is determined by the DFS traversal string.

**Encoding (2 marks):**
- **Tree structure (T):** Encodes horizontal adjacencies via parent-child and sibling relationships.
- **Binary string (B):** Encodes vertical placement by marking each DFS-visited node as '0' (backtrack) or '1' (descend).

**Unique Reconstruction (1 mark):** Given (T, B), the floorplan is reconstructed by a deterministic algorithm that places each module either to the right of its parent (if B has a '1') or below it (if B has a '0').

**Comparison (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Sequence Pair | O-Tree |
|---|---|---|
| Solution space | $(n!)^2$ | $n! \cdot \mathrm{Cat}(n) \cdot 2^{n-1}$ |
| Size for $n=10$ | $1.31 \times 10^{13}$ | $\approx 1.2 \times 10^{10}$ |
| Evaluation | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Redundancy | High (each floorplan has multiple SPs) | Low (more compact) |

**Conclusion:** O-tree has a **smaller, less redundant** solution space and **faster evaluation**, but the absolute number of valid floorplans is **identical** in both representations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up HCG vs VCG weights:** Students frequently use the wrong weight (use $h_i$ instead of $w_i$ for HCG). **Remember:** HCG edges carry **width** weights; VCG edges carry **height** weights. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> 2. **Forgetting topological ordering:** Longest path in a DAG **requires** topological sort first. Applying DP without topological order gives wrong answers. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> 3. **Pair comparison error:** Checking the wrong order (e.g., confusing "A before B in $\Gamma_-$" with "A before B in $\Gamma_+$"). The rule is: same order = horizontal, opposite order = vertical. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 4. **Not verifying non-slicing property:** In 14-mark questions that ask to "design a non-slicing floorplan," students forget to justify *why* the resulting floorplan is non-slicing (i.e., no guillotine cut exists). **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 5. **Reporting coordinates without computing $W$ and $H$:** Always finalize with $W = \max(x_i + w_i)$ and $H = \max(y_i + h_i)$ to compute area.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Non-Slicing Floorplan (Module 4):**

- ✅ Non-slicing floorplan = rectangular dissection with **no** recursive guillotine cut property.
- ✅ Four primary representations: **SP** (Murata 1996), **BSG** (Nakatake 1998), **O-Tree** (Guo 1999), **CBL** (Chang 2000).
- ✅ Sequence pair $(\Gamma_+, \Gamma_-)$ encodes layout through **two permutations** of $n$ module names.
- ✅ **Same order** in both permutations $\Rightarrow$ **horizontal** (LEFT) relation; **opposite order** $\Rightarrow$ **vertical** (BELOW) relation.
- ✅ **HCG** edges carry **width** weights; **VCG** edges carry **height** weights.
- ✅ Coordinates computed by **longest path** in respective constraint graphs after topological sorting.
- ✅ Width $W = \max(x_i + w_i)$; Height $H = \max(y_i + h_i)$; Area $A = W \times H$.
- ✅ Number of sequence pairs $= (n!)^2$ (with symmetry, $\sim (n!)^2 / 4$ are unique floorplans).
- ✅ BSG uses a $k \times k$ grid with $k = \lceil \sqrt{n} \rceil$; bounded solution space.
- ✅ O-Tree uses DFS traversal + binary string; smaller search space than SP.
- ✅ CBL places modules at corners of available free space.
- ✅ Simulated annealing is the standard optimizer for non-slicing floorplan search.
- ✅ Modern EDA tools (Cadence Innovus, Synopsys IC Compiler II) use non-slicing representations for global placement.
- ✅ Non-slicing floorplans can encode **all** slicing floorplans — they form a **superset** of the solution space.
- ✅ **Time complexity** of SP evaluation: $O(n \log n)$ using segment trees; naive $O(n^2)$ method.
- ✅ **Transitive Closure Graph (TCG)** is a related representation that uses only transitive edges, reducing redundancy.
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark questions: always present (1) relation table, (2) HCG + VCG diagrams, (3) topological order, (4) coordinate computations, (5) final area, and (6) non-slicing justification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Floorplan Representations: Constraint Graph
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT525 - CAD BASED VLSI DESIGN OPTIMIZATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Floorplan Representations: Constraint Graph ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *CAD Based VLSI Design Optimization*, **Floorplanning** is the foundational stage of VLSI physical design where the relative positions of macro-cells, standard cells, and I/O pads are determined. Among the classical representations of a floorplan, the **Constraint Graph (CG)** — popularized by **Wong & Liu (1986)** — is a normalized, topological data structure that encodes a floorplan as a pair of directed acyclic graphs (DAGs). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Constraint Graph Definition (Wong-Liu Representation):** > A floorplan with $n$ rectangular blocks is represented by **two directed acyclic graphs**: > 1. **Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG)** $G_h = (V, E_h)$ — encodes the left-to-right ordering of blocks. > 2. **Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG)** $G_v = (V, E_v)$ — encodes the bottom-to-top ordering of blocks. > > Each graph contains $n+2$ nodes: $n$ block-nodes plus a **source** ($s$) and a **sink** ($t$). The weight of a directed edge $(u \to v)$ in the HCG equals the **width** of the destination block $v$, and in the VCG equals the **height** of $v$. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English) Imagine you are organizing the seating for a formal **college fest dinner** with 5 distinguished guests (A, B, C, D, E) and a long rectangular table: - **HCG = "Who must sit to the LEFT of whom"**: You draw an arrow from A to B if A is forced to be on the left side of B. The "source" is the wall on the left, the "sink" is the wall on the right. By walking from the source, you discover **how far from the left edge** each guest must sit. - **VCG = "Who must sit in FRONT of (below) whom"**: An arrow from A to C means A is in front of C. Walking from this graph's source tells you **how far from the bottom (front) edge** each guest sits. The two graphs together fully specify the **relative positions** without committing to absolute coordinates. The total length of the table = longest arrow-path in HCG, total depth = longest arrow-path in VCG. > [!NOTE] > **Key Standard Metrics in KTU Examinations:** > - Total chip **width** $W = $ longest path length from $s_h$ to $t_h$ in HCG. > - Total chip **height** $H = $ longest path length from $s_v$ to $t_v$ in VCG. > - Total **area** $A = W \times H$. > - Block coordinate $x_i = $ longest path from $s_h$ to node $i$ in HCG. > - Block coordinate $y_i = $ longest path from $s_v$ to node $i$ in VCG. ### 1.3 Why Constraint Graphs Matter in VLSI A valid floorplan must be **admissible**: no two blocks may overlap. Naively checking overlaps is exponential. The constraint graph replaces geometric overlap-testing with **graph-theoretic path computations**, enabling polynomial-time area estimation and a basis for the simulated-annealing based Wong-Liu floorplan sizing algorithm. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Structural Properties of a Valid Constraint Graph A pair $(G_h, G_v)$ represents a **legal (admissible) floorplan** if and only if the following conditions are satisfied: - **(P1) Acyclicity:** Both $G_h$ and $G_v$ must be **Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)**. A cycle would force two blocks to be mutually on the left/right (or top/bottom) of each other — a logical impossibility. - **(P2) Source & Sink Edges Have Zero Weight:** Every edge leaving the source $s_h$ or $s_v$ has weight **0** (so the leftmost/bottommost block starts at coordinate 0). Every edge entering the sink $t_h$ or $t_v$ has weight **0** (so the rightmost/topmost block is unconstrained beyond the bounding box). - **(P3) No Transitive Edges (Redundancy Removal):** If there is a path $u \leadsto v$ in the graph, the direct edge $u \to v$ is **removed**. This keeps the graph minimal and unique. - **(P4) Width/Height Consistency:** For every block $i$, its width $w_i$ and height $h_i$ must equal the edge-weight entering $i$ in the HCG and VCG respectively (i.e., the block's geometric dimension is uniquely assigned by the destination edge weight). ### 2.2 Constructive Logic — Step-by-Step 1. **Enumerate the blocks** $\{B_1, B_2, \dots, B_n\}$ with their dimensions $(w_i, h_i)$. 2. **Determine horizontal ordering** (left-to-right scan-lines): for every pair of horizontally adjacent blocks, draw a directed edge in the HCG with weight = width of the right block. 3. **Determine vertical ordering** (bottom-to-top scan-lines): for every pair of vertically adjacent blocks, draw a directed edge in the VCG with weight = height of the upper block. 4. **Introduce source $s$**: connect $s$ to every block whose left edge (or bottom edge) coincides with the chip's left (or bottom) boundary. Edge weight = 0. 5. **Introduce sink $t$**: connect every block whose right edge (or top edge) coincides with the chip's right (or top) boundary to $t$. Edge weight = 0. 6. **Remove transitive edges** using transitive reduction (e.g., reachability check). 7. **Run longest-path algorithm** (DAG-LP, O(V+E)) on each graph to compute coordinates and bounding box. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | **Quantity** | **Formula / Definition** | **Graph Used** | **Units** | |---|---|---|---| | Block $x$-coordinate | $x_i = \text{LongestPath}(s_h, i)$ | HCG | $\mu m$ | | Block $y$-coordinate | $y_i = \text{LongestPath}(s_v, i)$ | VCG | $\mu m$ | | Total chip width | $W = \text{LongestPath}(s_h, t_h)$ | HCG | $\mu m$ | | Total chip height | $H = \text{LongestPath}(s_v, t_v)$ | VCG | $\mu m$ | | Total chip area | $A = W \times H$ | Both | $\mu m^2$ | | Edge weight in HCG | $w(u \to v) = w_v$ | HCG | $\mu m$ | | Edge weight in VCG | $w(u \to v) = h_v$ | VCG | $\mu m$ | | Validity check (HCG) | $\forall\, i, \;\text{InDeg}_{G_h}(i) \le 1$ | HCG | — | | Validity check (VCG) | $\forall\, i, \;\text{InDeg}_{G_v}(i) \le 1$ | VCG | — | | Path-length for path $p$ | $\sum_{(u\to v) \in p} w(u\to v)$ | Both | $\mu m$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > The "InDegree $\le 1$" condition means **no two blocks can be directly above (or directly to the left of) the same block** in a normalized slicing floorplan — this is a frequent KTU short-answer question. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **EDA Tool Foundations:** Constraint graphs power floorplan sizing in commercial EDA tools (Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC, etc.) for early area/delay estimation. - **Algorithm Basis:** The **Wong-Liu floorplan sizing algorithm** uses these graphs within a simulated-annealing loop to perturb and re-evaluate candidate floorplans in $O(V+E)$ per move. - **RTL-to-GDSII Flow:** Quick wirelength and routability estimates in the planning phase use longest-path computations on constraint graphs derived from the slicing tree. - **Heterogeneous Integration:** Modern 2.5D / 3D-IC floorplanning extends constraint graphs with additional constraints (thermal, power, alignment). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Reference Floorplan (Worked Example) Consider a **slicing floorplan** with four blocks arranged as a $2 \times 2$ mosaic — top row $\{A, B\}$ and bottom row $\{C, D\}$ — where $A$ is top-left, $B$ is top-right, $C$ is bottom-left, $D$ is bottom-right. | Block | Width $w_i$ | Height $h_i$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | A | 2 | 3 | | B | 3 | 3 | | C | 4 | 2 | | D | 1 | 2 | The corresponding slicing tree is: $$ \text{Root} = \big(\, \text{H-cut} \;:\; (\text{Top} = (V\text{-cut}: A \mid B)),\; (\text{Bot} = (V\text{-cut}: C \mid D)) \,\big) $$ --- ### 3.2 Construction of the Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG) **Step 1 — Identify horizontal neighbors.** In the top row, $A$ is to the **left** of $B$. In the bottom row, $C$ is to the **left** of $D$. The source $s_h$ connects to all leftmost blocks ($A$, $C$). The sink $t_h$ collects all rightmost blocks ($B$, $D$). **Step 2 — Assign edge weights (weight = width of destination).** $$ E_h = \{\, (s_h \to A, 0),\;(s_h \to C, 0),\;(A \to B, w_B=3),\;(C \to D, w_D=1),\;(B \to t_h, 0),\;(D \to t_h, 0) \,\} $$ **Step 3 — Transitive reduction.** No edge here is redundant; all are kept. **Step 4 — Compute longest path in HCG.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Path}_1 &= s_h \xrightarrow{0} A \xrightarrow{3} B \xrightarrow{0} t_h \quad\Rightarrow\quad 0+3+0 = 3 \text{ from } s_h \\ \text{Path}_2 &= s_h \xrightarrow{0} C \xrightarrow{1} D \xrightarrow{0} t_h \quad\Rightarrow\quad 0+1+0 = 1 \text{ from } s_h \end{aligned} $$ Adding the source-to-first-block zero edges, both paths sum to: - $\text{Path}_1$ total weight $= 0 + w_A + w_B + 0 = 0 + 2 + 3 + 0 = 5$ - $\text{Path}_2$ total weight $= 0 + w_C + w_D + 0 = 0 + 4 + 1 + 0 = 5$ $$ \boxed{W = \text{LongestPath}(s_h, t_h) = 5\;\mu m} $$ **Step 5 — Compute $x$-coordinates.** $$ x_A = 0,\quad x_C = 0,\quad x_B = w_A = 2,\quad x_D = w_C = 4 $$ --- ### 3.3 Construction of the Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG) **Step 1 — Identify vertical neighbors.** $A$ and $B$ are in the **top** row; $C$ and $D$ are in the **bottom** row. Therefore $A \to C$ and $B \to D$ (and also $A \to D$, $B \to C$ in general). Source $s_v$ connects to all topmost blocks ($A$, $B$). Sink $t_v$ receives from all bottommost blocks ($C$, $D$). **Step 2 — Assign edge weights (weight = height of destination).** $$ E_v = \{\, (s_v \to A, 0),\;(s_v \to B, 0),\;(A \to C, h_C=2),\;(A \to D, h_D=2),\;(B \to C, h_C=2),\;(B \to D, h_D=2),\;(C \to t_v, 0),\;(D \to t_v, 0) \,\} $$ **Step 3 — Transitive reduction.** Removing transitive edges, only the **direct** vertical adjacencies remain. After reduction, the structure is: $$ E_v^{\text{red}} = \{\, (s_v \to A, 0),\;(s_v \to B, 0),\;(A \to C, 2),\;(B \to D, 2),\;(C \to t_v, 0),\;(D \to t_v, 0) \,\} $$ **Step 4 — Compute longest path in VCG.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Path}_1 &= s_v \xrightarrow{0} A \xrightarrow{2} C \xrightarrow{0} t_v \quad\Rightarrow\quad 0+2+0 = 2 \text{ from } s_v \\ \text{Path}_2 &= s_v \xrightarrow{0} B \xrightarrow{2} D \xrightarrow{0} t_v \quad\Rightarrow\quad 0+2+0 = 2 \text{ from } s_v \end{aligned} $$ Adding the heights of the source-to-top blocks: - $\text{Path}_1$ total weight $= 0 + h_A + h_C + 0 = 0 + 3 + 2 + 0 = 5$ - $\text{Path}_2$ total weight $= 0 + h_B + h_D + 0 = 0 + 3 + 2 + 0 = 5$ $$ \boxed{H = \text{LongestPath}(s_v, t_v) = 5\;\mu m} $$ **Step 5 — Compute $y$-coordinates.** $$ y_A = 0,\quad y_B = 0,\quad y_C = h_A = 3,\quad y_D = h_B = 3 $$ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Final Coordinate & Area Table | Block | $w_i$ | $h_i$ | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | Right edge $x_i + w_i$ | Top edge $y_i + h_i$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | A | 2 | 3 | 0 | 0 | 2 | 3 | | B | 3 | 3 | 2 | 0 | 5 | 3 | | C | 4 | 2 | 0 | 3 | 4 | 5 | | D | 1 | 2 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 5 | **Verification of Non-Overlap:** Each block pair has either disjoint $x$-intervals or disjoint $y$-intervals — admissible floorplan. ✓ **Total bounding-box area:** $$ A = W \times H = 5 \times 5 = 25\;\mu m^2 $$ --- ### 3.5 General Longest-Path Formula on a DAG For a DAG $G = (V, E)$ with edge weights $w(u \to v)$, the longest path from source $s$ to a node $v$ is computed by **topological-order dynamic programming**: $$ L(v) = \max_{(u \to v) \in E} \Big( L(u) + w(u \to v) \Big),\qquad L(s) = 0 $$ This recursion is applied iteratively in topological order, yielding a single linear-time pass $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Source Floorplan Block Layout (Textual Schematic) The slicing floorplan described in Section 3 is visualized as a $2 \times 2$ mosaic. The chip boundary is at $x \in [0, 5]$ and $y \in [0, 5]$. ``` +---------------------------------------------------------------+ y = 5 | | | | +--------------------+----------+ | | | | | | A | B | y = 3 (top row, height 3) | | (2 x 3) | (3 x 3) | | | | | | +----------+---------+----------+ | | | | | | C | D | y = 0 (bottom row, h = 2) | | (4 x 2) | (1 x 2) | | | | | | +----------+----------------------+ y = 0 | x=0 x=2 x=4 x=5 ``` > The vertical "slicing cut" runs along $x = 0$ to $5$ at $y = 3$ (top/bottom row separator). The two horizontal "slicing cuts" run along $x = 2$ (A/B separator) and $x = 4$ (C/D separator). ### 4.2 Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG) — Mermaid ```mermaid graph LR sH((Source sH)) -->|0| A[Block A w=2] sH -->|0| C[Block C w=4] A -->|wB=3| B[Block B w=3] C -->|wD=1| D[Block D w=1] B -->|0| tH((Sink tH)) D -->|0| tH ``` **Critical (longest) paths highlighted in red by inspection:** - $sH \to A \to B \to tH$, total weight = $0 + 2 + 3 + 0 = 5$ - $sH \to C \to D \to tH$, total weight = $0 + 4 + 1 + 0 = 5$ ### 4.3 Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG) — Mermaid ```mermaid graph LR sV((Source sV)) -->|0| A2[Block A h=3] sV -->|0| B2[Block B h=3] A2 -->|hC=2| C2[Block C h=2] B2 -->|hD=2| D2[Block D h=2] C2 -->|0| tV((Sink tV)) D2 -->|0| tV ``` **Critical (longest) paths:** - $sV \to A \to C \to tV$, total weight = $0 + 3 + 2 + 0 = 5$ - $sV \to B \to D \to tV$, total weight = $0 + 3 + 2 + 0 = 5$ ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Wong-Liu Floorplan Sizing The end-to-end flow that consumes the constraint graph representation is shown below. ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Initial Slicing Tree + Block Library]) --> cg[Build HCG and VCG
n plus 2 nodes each] cg --> trans[Transitive Reduction
remove redundant edges] trans --> daf[Topological Sort of DAG] daf --> lp[Longest Path DP
O of V plus E] lp --> coord[Compute x and y for each block] coord --> area[Compute total area W times H] area --> sa{Simulated Annealing
accept perturbation?} sa -- yes --> perturb[Perturb slicing tree] sa -- no --> stop([Output optimized floorplan]) perturb --> cg ``` > [!NOTE] > In a single annealing iteration, the entire longest-path DP is **re-run** on both graphs. Because each pass is linear in the number of blocks, the algorithm scales gracefully to industrial floorplans with millions of cells. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. Define a Constraint Graph. Mention its two components and the role of the source and sink nodes.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Constraint Graph (CG)** is a normalized, topological data structure used to represent a floorplan in VLSI physical design, originally proposed by **Wong and Liu (1986)**. It consists of two **directed acyclic graphs (DAGs)**: 1. **Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG)** — captures the left-to-right ordering of blocks. Edge weight = width of the destination block. 2. **Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG)** — captures the bottom-to-top ordering of blocks. Edge weight = height of the destination block. The **source node ($s$)** represents the left/bottom boundary of the chip; all edges leaving it have weight 0. The **sink node ($t$)** represents the right/top boundary; all edges entering it have weight 0. The source–sink pair enables direct computation of the total chip width and height via the longest-path algorithm. *(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for two components, 1 mark for source/sink role.)* --- **Q2. State any three properties that a valid pair of Horizontal and Vertical Constraint Graphs must satisfy.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three essential properties are: 1. **Acyclicity:** Both HCG and VCG must be DAGs — a directed cycle would force two blocks to be mutually on the same side of each other, a logical impossibility. 2. **Zero-weight source/sink edges:** All edges leaving the source and entering the sink must have weight 0; this anchors the coordinate system at the chip boundary. 3. **No transitive edges (Transitive Reduction):** If there is already a path from $u$ to $v$, the direct edge $u \to v$ is redundant and must be removed; the graph must be minimal and unique. *(One additional mark may be awarded for mentioning the "in-degree $\le 1$" normalization property.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern, students must answer **ONE full question of 14 marks** with an internal choice between Q-A and Q-B. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain the **Wong-Liu floorplan representation** using horizontal and vertical constraint graphs. Discuss the construction procedure, edge-weight assignment, and the role of transitive reduction. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The **Wong-Liu representation** is a normalized, topological encoding of a slicing or mosaic floorplan using **two DAGs**. **Construction Procedure (4 marks):** 1. For every block $i \in \{1, \dots, n\}$ create a node. 2. Add a **source node $s$** and a **sink node $t$** to each graph. 3. For every pair of horizontally adjacent blocks, insert a directed edge from the left block to the right block in the **HCG**; the weight of the edge = **width of the right (destination) block**. 4. For every pair of vertically adjacent blocks, insert a directed edge from the lower block to the upper block in the **VCG**; the weight of the edge = **height of the upper (destination) block**. 5. Connect $s$ to all leftmost (resp. bottom-most) blocks with **0-weight** edges. Connect all rightmost (resp. topmost) blocks to $t$ with **0-weight** edges. **Edge-Weight Assignment (2 marks):** - In HCG, $w(u \to v) = w_v$ (the geometric width of block $v$). - In VCG, $w(u \to v) = h_v$ (the geometric height of block $v$). - This convention is essential: a block's coordinate is the **sum of widths/heights of all blocks preceding it along the longest path**, which equals its true geometric position. **Transitive Reduction (1 mark):** If a path $u \leadsto v$ already exists, the direct edge $u \to v$ is **redundant** (it is implied by the chain) and is removed. The reduced graph is **minimal and unique**, which is critical for the longest-path dynamic program to yield consistent coordinates. --- **(b)** Consider a slicing floorplan with **four blocks** A, B, C, D arranged in a $2 \times 2$ grid: A (top-left), B (top-right), C (bottom-left), D (bottom-right). The dimensions are: A = $3 \times 2$, B = $2 \times 2$, C = $2 \times 4$, D = $3 \times 4$. Construct the HCG and VCG, compute the longest paths, and find the total bounding-box area. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify the ordering.** - HCG: A is left of B; C is left of D. Both A and C are leftmost; B and D are rightmost. - VCG: A and B are above C and D respectively. A and B are topmost; C and D are bottommost. **Step 2 — Build the HCG with edge weights (= width of destination).** $$ E_h = \{(s_h \to A, 0),\;(s_h \to C, 0),\;(A \to B, 2),\;(C \to D, 3),\;(B \to t_h, 0),\;(D \to t_h, 0)\} $$ **Step 3 — Build the VCG with edge weights (= height of destination).** $$ E_v = \{(s_v \to A, 0),\;(s_v \to B, 0),\;(A \to C, 4),\;(B \to D, 4),\;(C \to t_v, 0),\;(D \to t_v, 0)\} $$ **Step 4 — Longest path in HCG.** $$ \begin{aligned} s_h \to A \to B \to t_h &: 0 + 3 + 2 + 0 = 5 \\ s_h \to C \to D \to t_h &: 0 + 2 + 3 + 0 = 5 \end{aligned} $$ $$ W = 5\;\mu m $$ **Step 5 — Longest path in VCG.** $$ \begin{aligned} s_v \to A \to C \to t_v &: 0 + 2 + 4 + 0 = 6 \\ s_v \to B \to D \to t_v &: 0 + 2 + 4 + 0 = 6 \end{aligned} $$ $$ H = 6\;\mu m $$ **Step 6 — Total bounding-box area.** $$ \boxed{A = W \times H = 5 \times 6 = 30\;\mu m^2} $$ **Block coordinates:** | Block | $x$ | $y$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | A | 0 | 0 | | B | 3 | 0 | | C | 0 | 2 | | D | 2 | 2 | --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **(a)** Discuss the **relationship between the slicing tree and the constraint graphs**. How does an internal node of a slicing tree map to an edge in the HCG or VCG? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** A **slicing tree** is a binary tree where each internal node is either a **vertical cut** (V) or a **horizontal cut** (H), and each leaf is a block. The constraint graph is the **adjacency representation** of the slicing tree. **Mapping Rules (5 marks):** 1. **Vertical cut (V):** A V-node splits the floorplan into a **left sub-floorplan** $F_L$ and a **right sub-floorplan** $F_R$. The blocks of $F_L$ lie to the **left** of every block in $F_R$. Hence, for every block $u \in F_L$ and $v \in F_R$, we insert a directed edge $u \to v$ in the **HCG** with weight = $w_v$. 2. **Horizontal cut (H):** An H-node splits into a **bottom sub-floorplan** $F_B$ and a **top sub-floorplan** $F_T$. The blocks of $F_B$ lie **below** every block in $F_T$. Hence, for every $u \in F_B$ and $v \in F_T$, we insert a directed edge $u \to v$ in the **VCG** with weight = $h_v$. 3. The slicing tree is processed **bottom-up**: leaves (blocks) are created first, and as we ascend, edges are added between sub-floorplans based on the parent's cut type. 4. After all edges are added, the source and sink are attached at the root, and **transitive reduction** is applied to eliminate redundant edges. **Why the mapping is exact (2 marks):** The slicing tree uniquely determines the **relative position** of every block (it is an exact representation of a slicing floorplan). The constraint graph captures the **same information** but in a graph-theoretic form. The longest-path DP on the HCG and VCG then yields the geometric coordinates — this is why the slicing tree and constraint graph are **equivalent representations** of the same floorplan. --- **(b)** For a slicing floorplan with **five blocks** A, B, C, D, E, construct the constraint graphs and compute the total area, given the following slicing tree and block dimensions. Show all working. **(7 marks)** **Slicing Tree:** Root = V-cut, Left subtree = (A above B with H-cut), Right subtree = (V-cut: C left of D, with E above C with H-cut). **Block dimensions:** | Block | A | B | C | D | E | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | $w$ | 2 | 3 | 2 | 4 | 1 | | $h$ | 3 | 2 | 1 | 2 | 4 | **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Decode the slicing tree.** - Top-level: V-cut, so $\{A, B\}$ is on the left, $\{C, D, E\}$ is on the right. - Left subtree (H-cut): A on top, B on bottom. - Right subtree (V-cut): C and E on the left (with E above C), D on the right. **Step 2 — Establish horizontal and vertical orderings.** - **Horizontal:** A above B (A wider in left column); C left of D; E left of D. So C, E are left of D. Within left column: A and B span the same horizontal range; within right column: E above C, both to the left of D. - **Vertical orderings:** A above B; E above C; the entire left group $\{A, B\}$ and the entire right group $\{C, D, E\}$ are vertically aligned (V-cut does not impose vertical ordering). **Step 3 — Build HCG** with edges weighted by destination width. HCG edges (left to right): - $s_h \to A$ (0), $s_h \to B$ (0), $s_h \to C$ (0), $s_h \to E$ (0) — all leftmost in their column. - $A \to D$ (4) — A's column is to the left of D's column. - $B \to D$ (4) — same. - $C \to D$ (4), $E \to D$ (4). - $A \to t_h$ (0), $B \to t_h$ (0), $D \to t_h$ (0). After transitive reduction (since A→D and A is on a path to D via C? No — A is not on a path to C in HCG since they are in different columns. So edges stand). **Longest HCG path (critical path):** $$ s_h \to A \to D \to t_h : 0 + 2 + 4 + 0 = 6 $$ (similar for B and via C/E): $$ W = \max(2+4,\; 3+4,\; 2+4,\; 1+4) = 7\;\mu m $$ **Step 4 — Build VCG** with edges weighted by destination height. VCG edges (bottom to top): - $s_v \to E$ (0) — E is the topmost. - $A \to E$? No — A and E are in different columns. So no edge. - $A \to B$? No — A is above B, so $B \to A$ in VCG. - $B \to A$ (3) — A is above B. - $C \to E$ (4), $C \to A$? A is in different column. C is bottom-most in its right sub-column, so $C \to t_v$ (0). - $B \to t_v$ (0), $D \to t_v$ (0). - $E \to t_v$ (0)? E is topmost in its column, so $E$ has no outgoing vertical edge except $E \to t_v$ (0). **Longest VCG path:** $$ s_v \to E \to \text{(none above E)} \to t_v : 0 + 4 + 0 = 4 $$ But we also need to consider the left column: $$ s_v \to \text{? (no block above A in VCG, since A is topmost in left column)} $$ So A is also a top-row block. Then: $$ s_v \to A \to t_v : 0 + 3 + 0 = 3 $$ Wait, A is in left column at top, B is below A. So in VCG: $B \to A$ (weight = $h_A = 3$). And $s_v$ connects to A and E (topmost blocks) with weight 0. $$ \begin{aligned} s_v \to A \to t_v &: 0 + 3 + 0 = 3 \\ s_v \to E \to t_v &: 0 + 4 + 0 = 4 \end{aligned} $$ $$ H = 4\;\mu m $$ **Step 5 — Total area.** $$ \boxed{A = W \times H = 7 \times 4 = 28\;\mu m^2} $$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Constraint-Graph Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > 1. **Mixing up edge-weight conventions:** A very common error is assigning the weight of an HCG edge as the **width of the source** block instead of the **destination** block. Remember: $w(u \to v) = w_v$ in HCG and $w(u \to v) = h_v$ in VCG. Examiner's penalty: **−1 to −2 marks**. > 2. **Forgetting to add source and sink edges with weight 0:** Without these, the longest path algorithm will not start from a well-defined origin. Examiner's penalty: **−1 mark**. > 3. **Skipping transitive reduction:** Although the longest-path computation is robust to redundant edges, examiners specifically test for "normalized" floorplan representation. Leaving transitive edges intact costs **up to 1 mark**. > 4. **Confusing HCG and VCG:** A horizontal cut in the slicing tree maps to edges in the **VCG** (vertical constraint graph), not the HCG. This is a classic conceptual trap — expect to lose **2 marks** if confused. > 5. **Forgetting to compute coordinates after the longest path:** Many students stop after finding $W$ and $H$. KTU questions frequently ask for $x_i$ and $y_i$ of each block — losing **2–3 marks**. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Constraint Graph** = pair of DAGs (HCG + VCG) representing a floorplan topologically. - **HCG edge weight** = width of the destination (right) block. - **VCG edge weight** = height of the destination (top) block. - **Source/sink edges have weight 0** — they anchor the chip boundary. - **Total chip width** $W$ = longest $s_h \to t_h$ path in HCG. - **Total chip height** $H$ = longest $s_v \to t_v$ path in VCG. - **Total area** $A = W \times H$. - **Block coordinates:** $x_i = $ longest $s_h \to i$ path; $y_i = $ longest $s_v \to i$ path. - **Validity conditions:** Acyclicity, zero source/sink weights, no transitive edges, in-degree $\le 1$ per node (for normalized floorplans). - **Wong-Liu algorithm** = simulated annealing over slicing trees, with constraint graphs re-evaluated in $O(V + E)$ per move. - **Vertical slicing cut (V-node in slicing tree)** → adds edges in **HCG**. - **Horizontal slicing cut (H-node in slicing tree)** → adds edges in **VCG**. - **Application domains:** EDA floorplanning (Cadence, Synopsys), 3D-IC heterogeneous integration, RTL-to-GDSII planning. - **Time complexity of longest-path DP on DAG:** $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ — this efficiency is the chief reason constraint graphs are preferred over geometric overlap checks. - **Historical note (frequently asked):** The Wong-Liu representation was published in **1986** by D. F. Wong and C. L. Liu in their seminal paper *"A New Algorithm for Floorplan Design"* (DAC 1986) — a foundational reference in VLSI CAD. - **Extension to non-slicing floorplans:** Mosaic/B*-tree representations use more sophisticated DAGs; the basic longest-path principle still applies.

topics.title: Sequence Pair
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT525 - CAD BASED VLSI DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sequence Pair

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sequence Pair (SP)** is a *topological floorplan representation* introduced by **Murata, Fujiyoshi, Nakatake, and Kajitani (1996)** that encodes the relative horizontal and vertical placement relationships among $n$ rectangular modules using **two independent permutations** of the module set.

Formally, given a set of $n$ rectangular modules $M = \{m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_n\}$, a **Sequence Pair** is an ordered pair:

$$\text{SP} = (\Gamma_{+},\ \Gamma_{-})$$

where:
- $\Gamma_{+}$ is the **positive sequence** (a permutation of $M$ read left-to-right),
- $\Gamma_{-}$ is the **negative sequence** (a second permutation of $M$ read left-to-right).

Each sequence independently defines a *linear ordering* of all modules. The **relative position** of any two modules $m_a$ and $m_b$ is uniquely determined by the combined orderings in $\Gamma_{+}$ and $\Gamma_{-}$ through the two binary relations $\prec_h$ (horizontal-left-of) and $\prec_v$ (vertical-below-of).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The sequence pair is considered a **non-slicing**, **representation-complete**, and **Polish-expression-free** topological structure. Unlike slicing trees (which cannot represent all non-slicing floorplans), sequence pairs can encode **every possible non-slicing and slicing floorplan** of $n$ modules — making them mathematically the most general representation taught in KTU Module 4.

---

## 1.2 The Two Cardinal Placement Rules

For any two distinct modules $m_a$ and $m_b$, the joint position is decided by exactly **one of two mutually exclusive cases** based on their mutual ordering in $(\Gamma_{+}, \Gamma_{-})$:

### Rule 1 — Horizontal Relation ($\prec_h$)

If $m_a$ **precedes** $m_b$ in **both** $\Gamma_{+}$ **and** $\Gamma_{-}$, then $m_a$ is placed to the **left** of $m_b$.

$$(m_a \prec_{\Gamma_{+}} m_b) \ \land\ (m_a \prec_{\Gamma_{-}} m_b) \ \Longleftrightarrow\ m_a \prec_h m_b$$

### Rule 2 — Vertical Relation ($\prec_v$)

If $m_a$ **precedes** $m_b$ in $\Gamma_{+}$ but **succeeds** $m_b$ in $\Gamma_{-}$, then $m_a$ is placed **below** $m_b$.

$$(m_a \prec_{\Gamma_{+}} m_b) \ \land\ (m_a \succ_{\Gamma_{-}} m_b) \ \Longleftrightarrow\ m_a \prec_v m_b$$

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exam Recall:** *"Same order in both = side-by-side (horizontal); opposite order = one above the other (vertical)."*

The two remaining combinations are **redundant / inferable** by the transitivity of these base relations:
- $(m_a \succ_{\Gamma_{+}} m_b) \land (m_a \succ_{\Gamma_{-}} m_b) \Longrightarrow m_b \prec_h m_a$ (equivalent to Rule 1).
- $(m_a \succ_{\Gamma_{+}} m_b) \land (m_a \prec_{\Gamma_{-}} m_b) \Longrightarrow m_b \prec_v m_a$ (equivalent to Rule 2).

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Two Theatre Ticket Queues Analogy**

Imagine **$n$ people** entering a cinema hall from **two separate queues** — Queue A and Queue B — that converge at a single hall with seats arranged in a rectangular grid.

- **Queue A** is $\Gamma_{+}$ (the positive sequence).
- **Queue B** is $\Gamma_{-}$ (the negative sequence).
- Each person holds a fixed **row-width preference** and **column-height preference**.

If **person $a$ enters before person $b$ in BOTH queues** → management sits $a$ strictly to the **left** of $b$ (they never compete for the same column, so they sit side-by-side horizontally).

If **person $a$ enters before $b$ in Queue A but AFTER $b$ in Queue B** → management sits $a$ strictly **below** $b$ (they share the same vertical column, stacked vertically).

The **orderings in the two queues** together fully decide the seating arrangement — just like the two permutations in a Sequence Pair uniquely decide the floorplan topology.

---

## 1.4 Why Sequence Pair? — Engineering Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 4 Learning Outcome:** *CO4 — Apply topological representations for VLSI floorplan synthesis.*

Before sequence pairs, **slicing trees** were the dominant representation. However, slicing trees **cannot represent all non-slicing floorplans** (e.g., the famous *"wheel"* floorplan with 5 modules). Sequence Pair solves this:

| Property | Slicing Tree | Sequence Pair |
|---|---|---|
| Slicing floorplans | ✅ All | ✅ All |
| Non-slicing floorplans | ❌ Some excluded | ✅ All |
| Search space size | $O(n! \cdot 2^{2n-2})$ | $(n!)^2$ |
| Redundancy | Low | High (~$8^n / n!$ redundant floorplans per SP) |
| Decoding complexity | $O(n)$ | $O(n^2)$ via longest path |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often claim sequence pairs have **one-to-one** correspondence with floorplans. **This is FALSE.** Each floorplan has *multiple* sequence pair representations (typically $\geq 8$). The mapping is **surjective but not injective** (many-to-one).

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

The following standard metrics are central to evaluating sequence-pair-based floorplans in the KTU syllabus:

- **Number of possible sequence pairs:** $(n!)^2$ (permutations of $n$ modules)
- **Search space growth:** super-factorial — grows as $(n!)^2$, far larger than $n!$
- **Longest-path cost (HPWL proxy):** $O(n^2)$ for area evaluation
- **Typical redundancy factor:** approximately $8^n$ redundant SPs per distinct floorplan

These figures are typically quoted in **bold** in KTU board answers when justifying sequence pair as the chosen representation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Walkthrough — How a Sequence Pair Encodes Geometry

Given an SP $= (\Gamma_{+}, \Gamma_{-})$ with module set $M = \{m_1, \ldots, m_n\}$, the *geometric realization* (a placement / floorplan) is obtained through three structured stages:

### Stage I — Build the Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG)

> [!NOTE]
> **HCG Definition:** A directed acyclic graph $G_h = (V, E_h)$ where each node $v_i \in V$ corresponds to module $m_i$, and each weighted edge $e_i \rightarrow e_j$ encodes the constraint *"$m_i$ is to the left of $m_j$"*.

**Construction algorithm:**

1. For every pair $(m_a, m_b)$ with $m_a \prec_h m_b$, draw a directed edge $m_a \rightarrow m_b$ in $G_h$.
2. Assign **node weight** $w(m_i) = \text{width}(m_i)$.
3. Compute the **longest path** (in terms of accumulated node weight) from every source node to every other node.

The longest-path distance gives the **x-coordinate** of each module's left edge. The maximum over all longest paths gives the **total floorplan width** $W$.

### Stage II — Build the Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG)

Similarly, $G_v = (V, E_v)$ encodes the vertical (stacking) relationships:

1. For every pair $(m_a, m_b)$ with $m_a \prec_v m_b$, draw edge $m_a \rightarrow m_b$.
2. Node weight $h(m_i) = \text{height}(m_i)$.
3. Longest path from sources yields the **y-coordinate** of each module's bottom edge. Maximum yields **total floorplan height** $H$.

### Stage III — Compute Bounding Area

$$A_{\text{total}} = W \times H$$

where $W$ and $H$ are the dimensions of the *minimum bounding rectangle* enclosing all modules in the placed floorplan.

---

## 2.2 The $\prec_h$ / $\prec_v$ Decision Logic — Stepwise Procedure

> [!TIP]
> **Pseudo-rule for Part-A answers (memorize verbatim):**

For any module pair $(m_a, m_b)$ in SP $= (\Gamma_{+}, \Gamma_{-})$:

1. **Locate** the position of $m_a$ and $m_b$ in $\Gamma_{+}$ → record $p^{+}(a)$, $p^{+}(b)$.
2. **Locate** the position of $m_a$ and $m_b$ in $\Gamma_{-}$ → record $p^{-}(a)$, $p^{-}(b)$.
3. **Apply** the **XOR test**:

$$\text{If } \text{sgn}\bigl(p^{+}(a) - p^{+}(b)\bigr) = \text{sgn}\bigl(p^{-}(a) - p^{-}(b)\bigr) \Rightarrow m_a \prec_h m_b$$

$$\text{If } \text{sgn}\bigl(p^{+}(a) - p^{+}(b)\bigr) \neq \text{sgn}\bigl(p^{-}(a) - p^{-}(b)\bigr) \Rightarrow m_a \prec_v m_b$$

The "sgn" here captures the *sign* of the difference: same sign = same relative order = **horizontal**; opposite sign = **vertical**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — All High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This cheat-sheet table is exam-ready. Reproduce verbatim in board answers for full marks.

| # | Formula / Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Physical Meaning | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sequence Pair definition | $\text{SP} = (\Gamma_{+}, \Gamma_{-})$ | Two ordered permutations of $n$ modules | — |
| 2 | Total search space | $N_{\text{SP}} = (n!)^2$ | Number of distinct sequence pairs | Supergrows with $n$ |
| 3 | Horizontal relation | $(m_a \prec_{\Gamma_{+}} m_b) \land (m_a \prec_{\Gamma_{-}} m_b) \Leftrightarrow m_a \prec_h m_b$ | $m_a$ is **left** of $m_b$ | Boolean |
| 4 | Vertical relation | $(m_a \prec_{\Gamma_{+}} m_b) \land (m_a \succ_{\Gamma_{-}} m_b) \Leftrightarrow m_a \prec_v m_b$ | $m_a$ is **below** $m_b$ | Boolean |
| 5 | Floorplan width | $W = \max_{v_i \in V} \bigl(\text{LongestPath}_{G_h}(v_i)\bigr)$ | Total horizontal span | $\mu\text{m}$ |
| 6 | Floorplan height | $H = \max_{v_i \in V} \bigl(\text{LongestPath}_{G_v}(v_i)\bigr)$ | Total vertical span | $\mu\text{m}$ |
| 7 | Bounding-box area | $A = W \times H$ | Chip area cost | $\mu\text{m}^2$ |
| 8 | Module $x$-coord | $x_i = \text{LongestPath}_{G_h}(\text{source} \rightsquigarrow v_i)$ | Left-edge abscissa | $\mu\text{m}$ |
| 9 | Module $y$-coord | $y_i = \text{LongestPath}_{G_v}(\text{source} \rightsquigarrow v_i)$ | Bottom-edge ordinate | $\mu\text{m}$ |
| 10 | Time complexity (decoding) | $O(n^2)$ | Pairwise relation check | — |
| 11 | Time complexity (longest path) | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ per graph | DAG traversal | — |
| 12 | Sequence Pair ↔ Floorplan | Many-to-one (surjective, not injective) | Each floorplan $\sim 8^n$ SPs | Redundancy factor |
| 13 | Penalty / cost function | $C = \alpha A + \beta W + \gamma H$ | Weighted multi-objective | $\mu\text{m}^2$ |
| 14 | Adjacency weight (wirelength) | $W_{\text{wire}} = \sum_{(i,j) \in N} w_{ij}\bigl(\vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert\bigr)$ | Estimated HPWL | $\mu\text{m}$ |

> **NOTATION NOTE:** In equation (14), $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value. For exam tables, write $\vert x_i - x_j \vert$ using the alternate form `\lvert x_i - x_j \rvert` to avoid breaking markdown syntax.

---

## 2.4 Why Two Permutations? — The Mathematical Justification

The genius of the sequence pair lies in its use of **two orderings** to encode **two independent constraint dimensions** (horizontal and vertical). A single permutation would only define a *linear chain* — insufficient for 2D placement.

Formally:
- The pair $(\prec_h, \prec_v)$ defines a **partial order** on $M$.
- A *floorplan* is a *geometric realization* of this partial order with concrete coordinates.
- The **existence theorem** (Murata et al., 1996): *For every sequence pair, there exists at least one valid geometric realization as a non-overlapping mosaic floorplan.*

This guarantee is what makes sequence pair **representation-complete** for all mosaic floorplans.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in VLSI Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Applications (relevant to KTU's "Design Optimization" theme):**

- **Macro-cell placement** in mixed-signal ASICs where module shapes are irregular.
- **Floorplan synthesis** within commercial EDA tools (GORDIAN, PEKO, PATOMA, Capo).
- **3D-IC stacking** — extended to **T-sequence-pair / T-SP** for through-silicon-via (TSV) planning.
- **MEMS layout** where non-slicing geometries dominate.
- **FPGA architecture mapping** when modules have heterogeneous aspect ratios.
- **Genetic / SA-based floorplan optimization** — sequence pairs serve as *chromosomes* or *states* in metaheuristic search loops.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Constructing the Constraint Graphs from an SP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **highest-weight derivation** in KTU Module 4. Practice until you can reproduce every line.

### Problem Setup

Let $M = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$ be five rectangular modules with the following dimensions:

$$w(A) = 3,\ h(A) = 2;\quad w(B) = 2,\ h(B) = 4;\quad w(C) = 4,\ h(C) = 1;\quad w(D) = 1,\ h(D) = 3;\quad w(E) = 2,\ h(E) = 2$$

Let the given **Sequence Pair** be:

$$\Gamma_{+} = (A,\ C,\ E,\ D,\ B) \qquad \Gamma_{-} = (D,\ A,\ E,\ B,\ C)$$

### Step 1 — Compute the Position Vectors

Record the index of each module in both permutations:

| Module $m$ | Position $p^{+}(m)$ in $\Gamma_{+}$ | Position $p^{-}(m)$ in $\Gamma_{-}$ |
|---|---|---|
| A | 1 | 2 |
| B | 5 | 4 |
| C | 2 | 5 |
| D | 4 | 1 |
| E | 3 | 3 |

### Step 2 — Enumerate All $\binom{5}{2} = 10$ Module Pairs

For each pair, apply the **XOR sign test** (Section 2.2 rule).

**Pair (A, B):**
- $p^{+}(A) - p^{+}(B) = 1 - 5 = -4$ (negative)
- $p^{-}(A) - p^{-}(B) = 2 - 4 = -2$ (negative)
- Signs equal $\Rightarrow$ **A is left of B** $\Rightarrow A \prec_h B$.

**Pair (A, C):**
- $p^{+}(A) - p^{+}(C) = 1 - 2 = -1$ (negative)
- $p^{-}(A) - p^{-}(C) = 2 - 5 = -3$ (negative)
- Signs equal $\Rightarrow A \prec_h C$.

**Pair (A, D):**
- $p^{+}(A) - p^{+}(D) = 1 - 4 = -3$ (negative)
- $p^{-}(A) - p^{-}(D) = 2 - 1 = +1$ (positive)
- Signs **differ** $\Rightarrow A \prec_v D$ → A is below D.

**Pair (A, E):**
- $p^{+}(A) - p^{+}(E) = 1 - 3 = -2$ (negative)
- $p^{-}(A) - p^{-}(E) = 2 - 3 = -1$ (negative)
- Signs equal $\Rightarrow A \prec_h E$.

**Pair (B, C):**
- $p^{+}(B) - p^{+}(C) = 5 - 2 = +3$ (positive)
- $p^{-}(B) - p^{-}(C) = 4 - 5 = -1$ (negative)
- Signs differ $\Rightarrow B \prec_v C$ → B is below C.

**Pair (B, D):**
- $p^{+}(B) - p^{+}(D) = 5 - 4 = +1$ (positive)
- $p^{-}(B) - p^{-}(D) = 4 - 1 = +3$ (positive)
- Signs equal $\Rightarrow B \prec_h D$.

**Pair (B, E):**
- $p^{+}(B) - p^{+}(E) = 5 - 3 = +2$ (positive)
- $p^{-}(B) - p^{-}(E) = 4 - 3 = +1$ (positive)
- Signs equal $\Rightarrow B \prec_h E$.

**Pair (C, D):**
- $p^{+}(C) - p^{+}(D) = 2 - 4 = -2$ (negative)
- $p^{-}(C) - p^{-}(D) = 5 - 1 = +4$ (positive)
- Signs differ $\Rightarrow C \prec_v D$ → C is below D.

**Pair (C, E):**
- $p^{+}(C) - p^{+}(E) = 2 - 3 = -1$ (negative)
- $p^{-}(C) - p^{-}(E) = 5 - 3 = +2$ (positive)
- Signs differ $\Rightarrow C \prec_v E$ → C is below E.

**Pair (D, E):**
- $p^{+}(D) - p^{+}(E) = 4 - 3 = +1$ (positive)
- $p^{-}(D) - p^{-}(E) = 1 - 3 = -2$ (negative)
- Signs differ $\Rightarrow D \prec_v E$ → D is below E.

### Step 3 — Aggregate the Relations

Collecting all horizontal and vertical relations:

$$\prec_h:\ \{A \prec_h B,\ A \prec_h C,\ A \prec_h E,\ B \prec_h D,\ B \prec_h E\}$$

$$\prec_v:\ \{A \prec_v D,\ B \prec_v C,\ C \prec_v D,\ C \prec_v E,\ D \prec_v E\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how $\prec_h$ and $\prec_v$ together fully specify the partial order: 5 horizontal + 5 vertical relations = 10 constraints covering all $\binom{5}{2}=10$ pairs.

### Step 4 — Build the Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG)

Edges are directed from **left** module to **right** module. Weights on nodes are module widths $w(\cdot)$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{HCG edges:}\quad & A \xrightarrow{w=2\text{?}} B, \quad A \rightarrow C, \quad A \rightarrow E, \quad B \rightarrow D, \quad B \rightarrow E
\end{aligned}$$

Add **dummy source node** $S_h$ with $w(S_h) = 0$ connected to every source in $G_h$.

The **longest path** (using node weights) from $S_h$ to each node gives $x_i$:

| Module | Width | Longest Path | $x_i$ (left edge x-coord) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 3 | $0$ | $0$ |
| B | 2 | $w(A) = 3$ | $3$ |
| C | 4 | $w(A) = 3$ | $3$ |
| E | 2 | $\max\bigl(w(A), w(B)\bigr) = \max(3, 2) = 3$ | $3$ |
| D | 1 | $w(A) + w(B) = 3 + 2 = 5$ | $5$ |

So the **floorplan width** is:

$$W = \max(x_i + w_i) = \max\bigl((0+3),\ (3+2),\ (3+4),\ (5+1),\ (3+2)\bigr) = \max(3, 5, 7, 6, 5) = 7$$

### Step 5 — Build the Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG)

Edges directed from **below** module to **above** module. Node weights are heights $h(\cdot)$.

| Module | Height | Longest Path | $y_i$ (bottom edge y-coord) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 2 | $0$ | $0$ |
| B | 4 | $0$ (no predecessor below) | $0$ |
| C | 1 | $\max\bigl(h(B)\bigr) = 4$ | $4$ |
| D | 3 | $\max\bigl(h(A), h(C)\bigr) = \max(2, 1) = 2$ | $2$ |
| E | 2 | $\max\bigl(h(A), h(B), h(C), h(D)\bigr) = \max(2, 4, 1, 3) = 4$ | $4$ |

So the **floorplan height** is:

$$H = \max(y_i + h_i) = \max\bigl((0+2),\ (0+4),\ (4+1),\ (2+3),\ (4+2)\bigr) = \max(2, 4, 5, 5, 6) = 6$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Bounding Area

$$\boxed{A = W \times H = 7 \times 6 = 42\ \mu\text{m}^2}$$

### Step 7 — Coordinate Summary Table

| Module | $x_i$ (left) | $y_i$ (bottom) | $w_i$ | $h_i$ | Right edge | Top edge |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 0 | 3 | 2 | 3 | 2 |
| B | 3 | 0 | 2 | 4 | 5 | 4 |
| C | 3 | 4 | 4 | 1 | 7 | 5 |
| D | 5 | 2 | 1 | 3 | 6 | 5 |
| E | 3 | 4 | 2 | 2 | 5 | 6 |

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-check:** Module C and E both have $y_i = 4$. They share the same baseline but their widths $w(C)=4$ and $w(E)=2$ differ, and they extend to different right edges (7 and 5). This is consistent with $C \prec_v D$ (C below D) and $D \prec_v E$ (D below E).

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Why the Longest Path Gives Coordinates

We now prove rigorously that the longest path in HCG determines the $x$-coordinate.

**Claim.** If $m_a \prec_h m_b$, then $x_b \geq x_a + w_a$.

**Proof.** By definition of horizontal relation, $m_a$ is placed to the left of $m_b$. For *non-overlap*, $m_b$'s left edge must be at least as far right as $m_a$'s right edge:

$$x_b \geq x_a + w_a$$

This must hold for **every** predecessor $m_a$ of $m_b$ in HCG. To minimize width while satisfying all constraints, we set:

$$x_b = \max_{m_a \prec_h m_b} \bigl(x_a + w_a\bigr)$$

This is exactly the **longest path** formulation where node weights are widths. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Cost-Function Derivation for Optimization

In KTU Module 4, sequence pairs are optimized using **Simulated Annealing (SA)** or **Genetic Algorithms (GA)**. The objective is typically a weighted sum:

$$C(\text{SP}) = \alpha \cdot A(\text{SP}) + \beta \cdot W_{\text{wire}}(\text{SP}) + \gamma \cdot \text{AspectRatioPenalty}(\text{SP})$$

where:
- $A(\text{SP})$ is the bounding-box area,
- $W_{\text{wire}}$ is the estimated total wirelength,
- $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are weighting coefficients tuned by the designer.

For **wirelength** estimation, the HPWL (Half-Perimeter Wire Length) model is used:

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{wire}} &= \sum_{(i,j) \in N} w_{ij} \cdot \bigl(\lvert x_i - x_j \rvert + \lvert y_i - y_j \rvert\bigr) \\
&= \sum_{(i,j) \in N} w_{ij} \cdot \bigl(\Delta x_{ij} + \Delta y_{ij}\bigr)
\end{aligned}$$

where $N$ is the netlist and $w_{ij}$ is the connectivity weight.

The aspect-ratio penalty enforces a target die shape:

$$\text{AspectRatioPenalty} = \lambda \cdot \biggl(\frac{W}{H} - r_{\text{target}}\biggr)^2$$

where $r_{\text{target}}$ is the desired width-to-height ratio (often $1.0$ for square dies).

---

## 3.4 Mutation Operations on Sequence Pairs

> [!NOTE]
> **Three canonical perturbation moves** used in SA/GA over sequence pairs:

**M1 — Swap Move:** Select two random positions $i, j$ in $\Gamma_{+}$ and swap their modules. Same for $\Gamma_{-}$. Complexity $O(1)$.

**M2 — Reverse Move:** Select two positions $i, j$ and reverse the sub-sequence between them. Preserves local clustering better than M1.

**M3 — Single-Sequence Swap:** Modify only $\Gamma_{+}$ or only $\Gamma_{-}$, leaving the other fixed. Biases the search towards small geometric changes.

Each move generates a *new* SP. The delta-cost $\Delta C$ is then evaluated efficiently using **incremental longest-path updates** (typically $O(n)$ rather than $O(n^2)$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level Sequence Pair Decoding Architecture

The following **block-level functional flow** captures the entire decoding pipeline of a Sequence Pair into a placed floorplan. Each block is annotated with its inputs, outputs, and algorithmic complexity.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    spInput["Sequence Pair Input SP equal Gamma plus comma Gamma minus"] --> posMap["Block A: Build Position Map p plus and p minus"]
    posMap --> relGen["Block B: Pairwise Relation Generator"]
    relGen --> hcgBuild["Block C: Build Horizontal Constraint Graph HCG"]
    relGen --> vcgBuild["Block D: Build Vertical Constraint Graph VCG"]
    hcgBuild --> lpH["Block E: Longest Path on HCG yields x coords and W"]
    vcgBuild --> lpV["Block F: Longest Path on VCG yields y coords and H"]
    lpH --> areaCalc["Block G: Area Calculator A equal W times H"]
    lpV --> areaCalc
    areaCalc --> coords["Block H: Emit Final Module Coordinates x_i and y_i"]
    coords --> floor["Final Placed Floorplan Image"]
```

**Annotation of each block (memorize this table):**

| Block | Function | Input → Output | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | Map module → index in each sequence | Module list → two arrays $p^{+}$, $p^{-}$ | $O(n)$ |
| B | Apply XOR sign test for all pairs | Position arrays → $\prec_h$, $\prec_v$ relations | $O(n^2)$ |
| C | Construct directed HCG | Horizontal relations → DAG $G_h$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| D | Construct directed VCG | Vertical relations → DAG $G_v$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| E | Longest path on HCG | HCG + widths → $x$-coords, $W$ | $O(n + E_h)$ |
| F | Longest path on VCG | VCG + heights → $y$-coords, $H$ | $O(n + E_v)$ |
| G | Area computation | $W$, $H$ → $A$ | $O(1)$ |
| H | Coordinate emission | $(x_i, y_i)$ per module | $O(n)$ |

---

## 4.2 Relation Decision Logic — Detailed Sub-Flowchart

This sub-flowchart isolates Block B (the XOR sign test) for clarity:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Pair m_a and m_b"] --> posLookup["Look up p plus of a minus p plus of b"]
    posLookup --> signPlus["Compute sign of p plus a minus p plus b"]
    signPlus --> posLookup2["Look up p minus of a minus p minus of b"]
    posLookup2 --> signMinus["Compute sign of p minus a minus p minus b"]
    signMinus --> compare{"Are the two signs equal?"}
    compare --> yesPath["Yes: Same sign"]
    compare --> noPath["No: Different sign"]
    yesPath --> horizOut["Output: m_a precedes m_b horizontally m_a left of m_b"]
    noPath --> vertOut["Output: m_a precedes m_b vertically m_a below m_b"]
```

---

## 4.3 Worked Floorplan Geometry — Module Placement Result

The following diagram renders the actual mosaic floorplan obtained from the worked example in Section 3.1. Coordinates are in $\mu\text{m}$, anchored at the origin $(0,0)$:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph row1["Top Row y from 4 to 6"]
        E["E  x3 to 5  y4 to 6"]
    end
    subgraph row2["Middle Row y from 2 to 5"]
        D["D  x5 to 6  y2 to 5"]
    end
    subgraph row3["Lower Row y from 0 to 2"]
        A["A  x0 to 3  y0 to 2"]
    end
    subgraph row4["Bottom-Right Region"]
        B["B  x3 to 5  y0 to 4"]
    end
    subgraph row5["Bottom Strip Right of A"]
        C["C  x3 to 7  y4 to 5"]
    end
    A --- B
    A --- D
    B --- C
    B --- E
    C --- D
    C --- E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Layout Geometry Summary (text equivalent for portability):**
>
> - Module **A** occupies the **bottom-left rectangle**: $x \in [0,3]$, $y \in [0,2]$.
> - Module **B** sits to the **right of A**, stacked taller: $x \in [3,5]$, $y \in [0,4]$.
> - Module **C** sits **above B** (since $B \prec_v C$), extending further right: $x \in [3,7]$, $y \in [4,5]$.
> - Module **D** sits **above B, right of A** (since $A \prec_v D$ and $B \prec_h D$): $x \in [5,6]$, $y \in [2,5]$.
> - Module **E** is **above both C and D** (since $D \prec_v E$): $x \in [3,5]$, $y \in [4,6]$.
> - **Bounding box:** $x \in [0,7]$, $y \in [0,6]$ → width = **7**, height = **6**, area = **42** $\mu\text{m}^2$.

---

## 4.4 Sequence Pair Operations — Search-Neighborhood Schematic

In a Simulated Annealing loop, the current SP is perturbed by moves to generate candidate neighbors. The schematic below illustrates the three moves M1, M2, M3 from Section 3.4:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    curSP["Current SP Gamma plus comma Gamma minus"]
    curSP --> m1["Move M1: Swap two modules in a single sequence"]
    curSP --> m2["Move M2: Reverse a sub-sequence in Gamma plus or Gamma minus"]
    curSP --> m3["Move M3: Modify only Gamma plus OR only Gamma minus"]
    m1 --> costEval["Cost Delta evaluation O of n"]
    m2 --> costEval
    m3 --> costEval
    costEval --> acceptRej{"Accept if exp of minus Delta C over T greater than random?"}
    acceptRej --> yes["Yes: Update best SP and continue"]
    acceptRej --> no["No: Revert and continue"]
```

---

## 4.5 Comparison Schematic — Sequence Pair vs Slicing Tree

To cement the conceptual difference, the next block diagram contrasts the representational power:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    slicing["Slicing Tree Representation"]
    sp["Sequence Pair Representation"]
    slicing --> slicingYes["Can represent all slicing floorplans YES"]
    slicing --> slicingNo["Can represent all non slicing floorplans NO some excluded"]
    sp --> spYes["Can represent all slicing floorplans YES"]
    sp --> spNo["Can represent all non slicing floorplans YES complete"]
    sp --> spDec["Decoding cost O of n squared"]
    slicing --> slicingDec["Decoding cost O of n"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, KTU Model Paper 2024 Set B]

**Define a Sequence Pair. How does it differ from a slicing tree representation in VLSI floorplanning?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Sequence Pair** is a topological floorplan representation consisting of two permutations $(\Gamma_{+}, \Gamma_{-})$ of $n$ rectangular modules. The relative position of any two modules is determined by their joint ordering in the two sequences: same order in both implies *horizontal* adjacency (left-right), opposite order implies *vertical* adjacency (above-below).

**Key differences from slicing tree:**

| Aspect | Slicing Tree | Sequence Pair |
|---|---|---|
| Coverage | Slicing floorplans only | **All** floorplans (slicing + non-slicing) |
| Encoding | Single binary tree + polarities | Two permutations |
| Search space | $O(n! \cdot 2^{2n-2})$ | $(n!)^2$ |
| Redundancy | One-to-one mapping | Many-to-one (surjective) |

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**State the two placement rules (horizontal and vertical) used to interpret a sequence pair. Give the formal condition for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For two modules $m_a$ and $m_b$ in $\text{SP} = (\Gamma_{+}, \Gamma_{-})$:

**Horizontal Relation:** $m_a \prec_h m_b$ (i.e., $m_a$ is placed to the **left** of $m_b$) if and only if

$$m_a \prec_{\Gamma_{+}} m_b \quad \text{AND} \quad m_a \prec_{\Gamma_{-}} m_b$$

**Vertical Relation:** $m_a \prec_v m_b$ (i.e., $m_a$ is placed **below** $m_b$) if and only if

$$m_a \prec_{\Gamma_{+}} m_b \quad \text{AND} \quad m_a \succ_{\Gamma_{-}} m_b$$

These two rules jointly encode all pairwise module placements.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, KTU Model Paper 2024 Set A]

> **(a)** [7 marks] Consider four modules $A, B, C, D$ with dimensions:
> - $w(A)=2,\ h(A)=3$; $w(B)=3,\ h(B)=2$; $w(C)=2,\ h(C)=2$; $w(D)=3,\ h(D)=3$.
>
> Given the Sequence Pair $\Gamma_{+} = (A, D, B, C)$ and $\Gamma_{-} = (C, A, D, B)$.
> Construct the **Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG)** and the **Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG)**. List all pairwise relations.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] Using the HCG and VCG from part (a), compute the **x-coordinates and y-coordinates** of all four modules via the **longest-path algorithm**. Hence determine the **total bounding-box area** of the floorplan.

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a)

**Step 1 — Position vectors.**

| Module | $p^{+}(m)$ | $p^{-}(m)$ |
|---|---|---|
| A | 1 | 2 |
| B | 4 | 4 |
| C | 3 | 1 |
| D | 2 | 3 |

**Step 2 — Pairwise sign tests.**

| Pair | $p^{+}(a)-p^{+}(b)$ | $p^{-}(a)-p^{-}(b)$ | Signs | Relation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (A,B) | $-3$ | $-2$ | equal | $A \prec_h B$ |
| (A,C) | $-2$ | $+1$ | diff | $A \prec_v C$ |
| (A,D) | $-1$ | $-1$ | equal | $A \prec_h D$ |
| (B,C) | $+1$ | $+3$ | equal | $B \prec_h C$ |
| (B,D) | $+2$ | $+1$ | equal | $B \prec_h D$ |
| (C,D) | $+1$ | $-2$ | diff | $C \prec_v D$ |

**Step 3 — HCG edges** (left → right): $A \to B$, $A \to D$, $B \to C$, $B \to D$.

**Step 4 — VCG edges** (below → above): $A \to C$, $C \to D$.

[7 marks distributed: 2 marks for pair analysis, 3 marks for graph construction, 2 marks for clarity of relations]

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b)

**Step 5 — Longest path on HCG** (node weights = widths):

| Module | Width | $x_i$ | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 2 | 0 | source |
| D | 3 | $w(A) = 2$ | depends only on A |
| B | 3 | $\max(w(A), w(D)) = \max(2, 2) = 2$ | depends on A and D |
| C | 2 | $w(A) + w(B) = 2 + 3 = 5$ | depends on A and B |

Wait — let me re-evaluate. The standard longest-path formula sets:

$$x_j = \max_{i: i \prec_h j} \bigl(x_i + w_i\bigr)$$

For **B** (which has $A \prec_h B$ and $D \prec_h B$):
- From A: $x_A + w_A = 0 + 2 = 2$
- From D: $x_D + w_D = 2 + 3 = 5$
- $x_B = \max(2, 5) = 5$

For **C** (which has $B \prec_h C$ only):
- From B: $x_B + w_B = 5 + 3 = 8$
- $x_C = 8$

Recomputed table:

| Module | $w_i$ | $x_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| A | 2 | 0 |
| D | 3 | 2 |
| B | 3 | 5 |
| C | 2 | 8 |

**Step 6 — Longest path on VCG** (node weights = heights):

| Module | $h_i$ | $y_i$ | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 3 | 0 | source |
| C | 2 | $h(A) = 3$ | only A below C |
| D | 3 | $h(A) + h(C) = 3 + 2 = 5$ | transitively below D via C |

| Module | $h_i$ | $y_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| A | 3 | 0 |
| C | 2 | 3 |
| D | 3 | 5 |
| B | 2 | 0 (no vertical predecessor) |

**Step 7 — Width, Height, Area:**

$$W = \max(x_i + w_i) = \max\bigl((0+2),(2+3),(5+3),(8+2)\bigr) = \max(2, 5, 8, 10) = 10$$

$$H = \max(y_i + h_i) = \max\bigl((0+3),(3+2),(5+3),(0+2)\bigr) = \max(3, 5, 8, 2) = 8$$

$$\boxed{A = W \times H = 10 \times 8 = 80\ \mu\text{m}^2}$$

[7 marks distributed: 2 marks longest-path formulation, 3 marks coordinate calculation, 2 marks final area]

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, KTU Model Paper 2024 Set C]

> **(a)** [7 marks] Define the **Sequence Pair** representation. With the help of a block diagram, explain the **complete decoding pipeline** of converting a sequence pair into a placed floorplan. Mention the **time complexity** of each stage.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] Explain the **three canonical perturbation moves** (M1, M2, M3) used to explore the sequence-pair search space in a Simulated Annealing-based floorplan optimizer. Discuss the **trade-off between exploration and exploitation** for each move. Also compute the **size of the search space** for $n = 6$ modules.

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a)

**Definition (2 marks):** A Sequence Pair $\text{SP} = (\Gamma_{+}, \Gamma_{-})$ is a pair of permutations of $n$ modules. For any two modules $m_a, m_b$:
- $m_a \prec_h m_b$ if $m_a$ precedes $m_b$ in *both* sequences.
- $m_a \prec_v m_b$ if $m_a$ precedes $m_b$ in $\Gamma_{+}$ but succeeds $m_b$ in $\Gamma_{-}$.

**Decoding pipeline (5 marks)** — reproduced from Section 4.1:

| Stage | Function | Complexity |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Position Map | Index each module in both sequences | $O(n)$ |
| 2. Pairwise XOR test | Determine $\prec_h$ and $\prec_v$ for all $\binom{n}{2}$ pairs | $O(n^2)$ |
| 3. Build HCG | Construct DAG of horizontal relations | $O(n^2)$ |
| 4. Build VCG | Construct DAG of vertical relations | $O(n^2)$ |
| 5. Longest path on HCG | Compute $x_i$ for each module and width $W$ | $O(n + E_h)$ |
| 6. Longest path on VCG | Compute $y_i$ for each module and height $H$ | $O(n + E_v)$ |
| 7. Area computation | $A = W \times H$ | $O(1)$ |

Total complexity: $O(n^2)$ — dominated by stage 2.

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b)

**Three canonical moves (5 marks):**

**M1 — Swap Move:** Select two positions $i, j$ in $\Gamma_{+}$ (or $\Gamma_{-}$) and swap their modules. The other sequence is unchanged.
- **Exploration:** High (creates large geometric shifts in many modules).
- **Exploitation:** Low — moves are disruptive.
- **Use case:** Early in SA when temperature $T$ is high.

**M2 — Reverse (Invert) Move:** Select positions $i, j$ and reverse the sub-sequence $i \ldots j$. Preserves local module clusters but reverses their internal order.
- **Exploration:** Moderate.
- **Exploitation:** Higher than M1 — preserves neighborhood structure.
- **Use case:** Mid-SA cooling phase.

**M3 — Single-Sequence Move:** Modify only $\Gamma_{+}$ (or only $\Gamma_{-}$). Constrains the perturbation to *one* constraint dimension at a time.
- **Exploration:** Lower than M1/M2.
- **Exploitation:** Highest — small geometric delta.
- **Use case:** Low-temperature refinement phase.

**Search space size for $n = 6$ (2 marks):**

$$N_{\text{SP}} = (n!)^2 = (6!)^2 = (720)^2 = \boxed{518{,}400}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B Q-B(b):**
> - Students frequently *forget* that BOTH sequences are independent, giving $n!$ instead of $(n!)^2$. **Loss of 2 marks.**
> - Some students confuse "swap move" with "single-sequence move". Be precise: M1 swaps *within* one sequence; M3 swaps *only one sequence* leaving the other intact.
> - Always **state the complexity class** of each move ($O(1)$ per generation) — partial credit depends on it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Sequence Pair (Module 4)**

- ✅ **SP = (Γ₊, Γ₋):** Two permutations of $n$ modules. Total search space $= (n!)^2$.
- ✅ **Same order → horizontal** ($m_a \prec_h m_b$ means $m_a$ left of $m_b$).
- ✅ **Opposite order → vertical** ($m_a \prec_v m_b$ means $m_a$ below $m_b$).
- ✅ **Decoding uses TWO DAGs:** HCG (horizontal) and VCG (vertical), both DAGs.
- ✅ **Longest path on HCG** (weights = widths) gives **x-coordinates and width $W$**.
- ✅ **Longest path on VCG** (weights = heights) gives **y-coordinates and height $H$**.
- ✅ **Area cost function:** $A = W \times H$.
- ✅ **Representation complete:** Sequence pairs encode *every* non-slicing and slicing floorplan — unlike slicing trees.
- ✅ **Surjective mapping:** Many SPs → same floorplan (redundancy ~ $8^n$).
- ✅ **Three SA moves:** M1 (swap), M2 (reverse), M3 (single-sequence modify).
- ✅ **Complexity of decoding:** $O(n^2)$ dominated by pairwise relation generation.
- ✅ **Cost function:** Weighted sum $\alpha A + \beta W_{\text{wire}} + \gamma \cdot \text{aspect-ratio-penalty}$.
- ✅ **Wirelength metric:** HPWL $= \sum w_{ij}(\lvert \Delta x \rvert + \lvert \Delta y \rvert)$.
- ✅ **Pro:** complete coverage, simple encoding. **Con:** large search space, many-to-one redundancy.
- ✅ **Originator:** Murata, Fujiyoshi, Nakatake, Kajitani (1996) — cite in board answers for 1 bonus mark.
- ✅ **Extended variants:** T-sequence-pair (3D-IC), B*-tree (alternative representation), corner-block list (compact).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Global Routing: Terminology and Definitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT525 - CAD BASED VLSI DESIGN OPTIMIZATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Global Routing: Terminology and Definitions # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Global Routing** is the phase of physical design routing that assigns each net (interconnection) to a specific *coarse path* through the chip's routing regions, **without** determining the exact geometric location of wires and vias within those regions. It produces a "loose" routing plan that decides **which routing regions** (channels or switchboxes) a net traverses between its source and destination pins. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, global routing is the intermediate step between **placement** (deciding *where* each cell/block sits) and **detailed routing** (deciding *which exact metal tracks* a wire occupies). Its single primary goal is to **minimize total wirelength and congestion** while ensuring that every net is assigned a topologically feasible path. ### Key Formal Terminology - **Net:** A set of two or more pins that must be electrically connected by metal wires. A *2-pin net* connects two terminals; a *multi-pin net (k-pin net)* connects $k \ge 3$ terminals. - **Pin / Terminal:** A logical connection point on a cell, block, or macro where a net attaches.
