```mermaid
    D --> H[Detailed Channel Routing]
    E --> I[Switchbox Routing Heuristic]
    F --> J[Region Decomposition]
    G --> K[Global Maze / Pattern Routing]
    H --> L[Verify DRC and LVS]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Routed Design Output]
```

## 4.2 Routing Region Type Classification

```mermaid
graph LR
    R[Routing Region] --> R1[Channel]
    R --> R2[Switchbox]
    R --> R3[L-shaped]
    R --> R4[T-shaped]
    R --> R5[Cross-shaped]
    R1 --> D1[Top and Bottom Pins Only]
    R1 --> D2[Open Both Ends]
    R2 --> S1[Pins on All Four Sides]
    R2 --> S2[Closed Both Ends]
    R3 --> L1[Two Channels at 90 Degrees]
    R4 --> T1[Three Channels at T Junction]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Global Router

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
        A1[Placement File DEF]
        A2[Technology LEF]
        A3[Netlist]
    end
    A1 --> B
    A2 --> B
    A3 --> B
    B[Grid Graph Builder] --> C[Capacity and Cost Model]
    C --> D[Initial Steiner Tree Estimator]
    D --> E[Sequential Maze Router]
    E --> F[Congestion Analyzer]
    F --> G{Overflow Detected}
    G -->|Yes| H[Rip-up and Reroute Engine]
    H --> E
    G -->|No| I[Timing Driven Refinement]
    I --> J[Final Routing Output]
```

## 4.4 Channel vs Switchbox — Pin Topology

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Channel
        CT[Top Pin Row]
        CB[Bottom Pin Row]
        CS[Empty Routing Space]
        CT --- CS
        CB --- CS
    end
    subgraph Switchbox
        ST[Top Pin Row]
        SB[Bottom Pin Row]
        SL[Left Pin Column]
        SR[Right Pin Column]
        SS[Closed Routing Space]
        ST --- SS
        SB --- SS
        SL --- SS
        SR --- SS
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The channel has pins on **only two opposite edges** and is open at both ends. The switchbox has pins on **all four edges** and is closed on every side. This single difference changes the routing problem from polynomial-time solvable (channel) to NP-hard (switchbox).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**Define a routing region. List any four standard representations used in VLSI routing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A routing region is the bounded geometric area on a metal layer where interconnect wires are physically placed to connect pins of a circuit.
Four standard representations are:
1. Grid graph representation (for global routing).
2. Channel representation (for row-to-row routing).
3. Switchbox representation (for macro intersections).
4. L-shaped / T-shaped / cross-shaped representations (for compound regions).

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four representations with one-line justification each.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is horizontal density of a channel? Why does it provide a lower bound on the channel height?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Horizontal density $\delta_h(x)$ at column $x$ is the total number of distinct nets that have at least one pin at column $x$ on either the top or bottom edge. Mathematically, $\delta_h(x) = \sum_n I_n(x)$.

It provides a lower bound on channel height because, at any column $x$, every net contributing to $\delta_h(x)$ must occupy a separate horizontal track — two nets cannot share the same track in the same column without shorting. Hence the channel must contain at least $\delta_h(x)$ tracks at column $x$, and therefore $H \geq \max_x \delta_h(x)$.

*(1 mark definition, 2 marks for justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **channel routing representation** and **switchbox routing representation**. Compare them in terms of (i) pin locations, (ii) algorithmic complexity, and (iii) flexibility. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a channel with the following top and bottom pin lists. Compute the horizontal density at every column and determine the minimum channel height $H_{min}$.

Top pins (left to right): $N_1, N_2, N_3, N_2, N_4, N_1, N_5, N_3$

Bottom pins (left to right): $N_3, N_5, N_1, N_4, N_2, N_2, N_1, N_4$

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Channel Routing Representation:**
A channel is a rectangular routing region bounded by two parallel rows of cells. Pins are placed only on the **top edge** and the **bottom edge**. Both horizontal ends of the channel are **open**, allowing nets to exit on either side. Channels are typically used for routing between standard cell rows. The minimum height $H_{min}$ equals $\max_x \delta_h(x)$.

**Switchbox Routing Representation:**
A switchbox is a rectangular region with pins on **all four sides** (top, bottom, left, right). Both horizontal ends are **closed**. Switchboxes occur at intersections of two channels or around pre-routed macro blocks. They are harder to route because no pin can exit the region.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Channel | Switchbox |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pin locations | Top and bottom only | All four sides |
| Open ends | Both horizontal ends open | Both ends closed |
| Algorithmic complexity | Polynomial (left-edge, YACR2) | NP-hard in general |
| Flexibility | High (nets can escape sideways) | Low (nets confined) |
| Typical use | Std-cell row-to-row | Macro corners / junctions |

*(3 marks: diagrams; 2 marks: definitions; 2 marks: comparison.)*

---

**Part (b) — Model Answer (7 marks):**

We construct a table of $\delta_h(x)$ at each column.

| Column $x$ | Top pin | Bottom pin | Nets contributing | $\delta_h(x)$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | $N_1$ | $N_3$ | $N_1, N_3$ | **2** |
| 2 | $N_2$ | $N_5$ | $N_2, N_5$ | **2** |
| 3 | $N_3$ | $N_1$ | $N_3, N_1$ | **2** |
| 4 | $N_2$ | $N_4$ | $N_2, N_4$ | **2** |
| 5 | $N_4$ | $N_2$ | $N_4, N_2$ | **2** |
| 6 | $N_1$ | $N_2$ | $N_1, N_2$ | **2** |
| 7 | $N_5$ | $N_1$ | $N_5, N_1$ | **2** |
| 8 | $N_3$ | $N_4$ | $N_3, N_4$ | **2** |

Now apply the lower bound formula:

$$
H_{min} = \max_{x \in \{1,\dots,8\}} \delta_h(x) = 2 \text{ tracks}
$$

Therefore the channel can be routed in **exactly 2 horizontal tracks**. This bound is tight because the left-edge algorithm achieves it.

*(2 marks: table; 3 marks: computation; 2 marks: conclusion and tightness remark.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **grid graph representation** of a routing region. How is it constructed? Mention its memory complexity and one limitation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a grid graph with 4 horizontal edges $e_1, e_2, e_3, e_4$ having capacities $c = \{3, 2, 4, 2\}$ and current usage $u = \{2, 2, 3, 1\}$, compute (i) the total congestion $C_{total}$, (ii) the total overflow $\eta_{overflow}$, and (iii) the per-edge congestion cost $w(e)$ assuming $w_0(e) = 1$ for all edges and penalty multiplier $p = 5$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Grid Graph Representation:**
A grid graph $G(V, E)$ partitions the chip's metal layer into a uniform array of rectangular tiles (cells). Each tile becomes a node $v_i \in V$. Adjacent tiles (horizontally or vertically) are connected by edges $e \in E$. Multi-layer routing adds layer-specific sub-graphs $G_{\ell}$ connected by via-edges.

**Construction steps:**
1. Determine the routing granularity $\Delta$ (typical: $\Delta = 1 \mu m$ or 5 routing tracks).
2. Tile the entire placement region into $M \times N$ cells.
3. Create a node per cell.
4. Add horizontal edges between neighbours; add vertical edges between neighbours.
5. Add inter-layer via edges for multi-layer routing.
6. Assign capacity $c(e)$ and base cost $w_0(e)$ to each edge based on layer and obstacles.

**Memory complexity:**

$$
V = M \cdot N, \qquad E \approx 2 V = 2 M N
$$

Storage is $O(MN)$ — linear in chip area.

**Limitation:**
The grid graph does not represent **within-cell** routing resources — fine-grained obstacles inside a cell are lost. Also, uniform tiling is wasteful when design rules differ across the chip.

*(2 marks: definition; 3 marks: construction; 2 marks: complexity and limitation.)*

---

**Part (b) — Model Answer (7 marks):**

Given: $c = (3, 2, 4, 2)$, $u = (2, 2, 3, 1)$, $w_0 = 1$, $p = 5$.

**(i) Total Congestion:**

$$
C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} u(e_i) = 2 + 2 + 3 + 1 = 8
$$

**(ii) Total Overflow:**
Compute $\max(0, u_i - c_i)$ for each edge:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_1 &: \max(0, 2 - 3) = 0 \\
e_2 &: \max(0, 2 - 2) = 0 \\
e_3 &: \max(0, 3 - 4) = 0 \\
e_4 &: \max(0, 1 - 2) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\eta_{overflow} = \sum_i \max(0, u_i - c_i) = 0
$$

The routing is **congestion-clean**.

**(iii) Per-edge Congestion Cost:**

$$
w(e_i) = w_0(e_i) + p \cdot \max(0, u_i - c_i)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
w(e_1) &= 1 + 5 \cdot 0 = 1 \\
w(e_2) &= 1 + 5 \cdot 0 = 1 \\
w(e_3) &= 1 + 5 \cdot 0 = 1 \\
w(e_4) &= 1 + 5 \cdot 0 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

All edges retain their base cost because there is no overflow.

*(2 marks: each sub-part, with one extra mark for correct formula citation in (iii).)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing density with the number of tracks actually used.** Students write "$H = \delta_{max}$" without explaining that this is a *lower bound*, not the *actual* height. Always say "minimum possible height".
>
> 2. **Forgetting that $\delta_h(x)$ counts distinct nets, not pin count.** If a net has pins on both top and bottom at the same column, it still contributes only **1** to $\delta_h(x)$ because it occupies one horizontal segment, not two. Counting pins instead of nets is a 1-mark killer error.
>
> 3. **Mis-stating channel vs switchbox.** Many students write "channel has pins on 4 sides" — this is the switchbox definition. Memorise the difference: *2 sides = channel*, *4 sides = switchbox*.
>
> 4. **Using `|` (vertical bar) inside markdown/Latex tables in the answer script.** Examiners deduct marks for ambiguous notation. Always use `\vert` or `\mid`.
>
> 5. **Omitting units and units-conversion clarity.** Writing $H = 2$ without saying "tracks" loses half a mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Routing Region** = the physical metal-layer area where wires are placed.
- **Representation** = the discrete data structure (grid graph / channel / switchbox / L-shape / T-shape) used by the router.
- **Grid graph** is used in **global routing**; **channels and switchboxes** are used in **detailed routing**.
- **Channel**: pins on **two opposite sides**, open ends, polynomial routing.
- **Switchbox**: pins on **all four sides**, closed ends, NP-hard routing.
- **Horizontal density** $\delta_h(x)$ counts **distinct nets**, not pins, at column $x$.
- **Minimum channel height** $H_{min} = \max_x \delta_h(x)$ — the Hashimoto-Stevens lower bound.
- **Grid graph complexity** is $O(MN)$ in memory, where $M \times N$ is the tile grid.
- **Total overflow** $\eta_{overflow} = \sum_e \max(0, u(e) - c(e))$ is the global router's primary metric.
- **Modern routers** combine wirelength objective and overflow penalty into a weighted sum $\Phi$.
- **Tightness**: Hashimoto-Stevens bound is tight for channels, generally not tight for switchboxes.
- **Pin exit freedom** distinguishes channels (high) from switchboxes (none).
- **Multi-layer routing** uses one graph per layer, connected by via edges.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maze Routing Algorithms: Lee's Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT525 - CAD BASED VLSI DESIGN OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Maze Routing Algorithms: Lee's Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lee's Algorithm** is a **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** based maze routing algorithm introduced by **C.Y. Lee in 1961** that guarantees finding the **shortest Manhattan path** (minimum number of grid cells) between a source terminal and a target terminal on a discretized routing grid, while strictly avoiding all pre-defined obstacles (blocked cells, pre-routed nets, and design-rule-forbidden regions).

In the context of KTU's CAD-based VLSI Design Optimization syllabus (Module 4 — Routing), Lee's Algorithm is classified as a **grid-based, exact, sequential maze router**. It is the **foundational reference algorithm** against which all other maze routers (Hadlock, Soukup, A* search, etc.) are benchmarked for correctness and optimality in the context of two-terminal net routing on a multi-layer VLSI routing plane.

### 1.2 Routing Plane Model (Discretized Grid)

The routing region is abstracted as a **rectangular 2-D grid (or 3-D for multi-layer)** where:

| Grid Element | VLSI Interpretation |
|---|---|
| Grid cell (passable) | Free routing track — may contain a wire segment |
| Grid cell (blocked) | Pre-routed net, obstacle, or design-rule violation zone |
| Source cell $S(x_S, y_S)$ | Origin terminal of the net to be connected |
| Target cell $T(x_T, y_T)$ | Destination terminal of the net to be connected |
| Connectivity | **4-directional (Manhattan)** or 8-directional (Manhattan + diagonal) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Flooding Pool

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Layman Explanation):** Imagine you drop a stone into the source cell of a water pool, and the water ripples outward in concentric square waves, one layer per second. Every blocked cell is a solid wall — water cannot enter it. Eventually, the very first ripple that touches the target cell tells you the **shortest number of steps** from source to target. Then, to actually trace the wire, you trace the wave backwards — from the target, always stepping into the cell that was flooded **one step earlier**, back to the source. This is exactly how Lee's Algorithm operates.

### 1.4 Core Properties of Lee's Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Guarantees of Lee's Algorithm:**
> 1. **Optimality:** If a path exists, the algorithm finds the path of **minimum number of grid cells** (i.e., shortest Manhattan path).
> 2. **Completeness:** It will find a path if and only if a path exists (it will report failure correctly if the target is unreachable).
> 3. **Determinism:** Given the same grid state, the output path is always the same (subject to tie-breaking rules during backtracing).
> 4. **Symmetry:** The path produced is symmetric in source and target — running the algorithm from either end gives equivalent results.

### 1.5 Key Engineering Metrics

The following **standard CAD metrics** are tracked when Lee's Algorithm is deployed inside a VLSI physical design flow:

- **Path Length $L$** (in grid units or $\mu m$)
- **Number of Bends $B$** (number of direction changes)
- **Via Count $V$** (for multi-layer routing)
- **Wirelength $W$** in microns
- **Congestion contribution** to global grid cells
- **Runtime $T$** in CPU seconds
- **Memory footprint $M$** in grid cells

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4):** Lee's Algorithm is the **canonical reference** for grid routing. The exam frequently tests the **two-phase decomposition** (Wave Propagation + Backtracing), the **data structures** (queue + label grid), and **complexity analysis** of the algorithm.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Algorithmic Decomposition — The Two-Phase Architecture

Lee's Algorithm rigorously decomposes the routing problem into **two clean, sequential phases**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase 1 — Wave Propagation (Flooding / Labelling Phase):**
> A Breadth-First Search wavefront expands outward from the source cell, labelling every reachable free cell with a numeric label equal to the minimum number of steps required to reach it from the source. The wave terminates when either (a) the target cell is reached, or (b) the wavefront is exhausted (no path exists).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase 2 — Backtracing (Path Retrieval Phase):**
> Starting at the target cell, the algorithm traces a path back to the source by repeatedly moving to a neighbouring cell whose label is exactly **one less** than the current cell's label. This produces the shortest Manhattan path in reverse order.

### 2.2 Phase 1 — Detailed Operational Logic

The Wave Propagation phase uses a **FIFO Queue** as the fundamental data structure. The grid is modelled as a 2-D array $\text{Grid}[m][n]$ where each cell stores a label value initialized to:

- $\text{Grid}[i][j] = 0$ for blocked (obstacle) cells
- $\text{Grid}[i][j] = -1$ (or a sentinel "unvisited" marker) for free cells initially

The label sequence is **monotonically increasing** in concentric "Manhattan rings" around the source, ensuring that the **first time a cell is reached, it is via the shortest path**.

**Pseudo-step logic for Phase 1:**

1. Initialize all free cells to label $-1$ (unvisited) and all blocked cells to label $0$.
2. Enqueue the source cell $S$ with label $1$ (distance = 1 from itself, or $0$ — convention-dependent).
3. While the queue is non-empty:
   - Dequeue the front cell $(i, j)$.
   - For each of its 4 (or 8) neighbours $(i', j')$:
     - If the neighbour is within grid bounds, is free (label $= -1$), and is not the target, set its label to $\text{Grid}[i][j] + 1$ and enqueue it.
     - If the neighbour is the target cell $T$, set its label to $\text{Grid}[i][j] + 1$ and **terminate** the wavefront (shortest path found).
4. If the queue empties without reaching the target, declare **routing failure** (no path exists).

### 2.3 Phase 2 — Detailed Backtracing Logic

Starting at the target cell $T$ with label $L_T$:

1. Set the current cell $= T$, with expected label $= L_T$.
2. Examine the 4 (or 8) neighbours.
3. Move to any neighbour whose label equals (current label $- 1$); record that neighbour as part of the path.
4. If multiple valid neighbours exist, apply the **tie-breaking rule** (e.g., prefer Up, then Left, then Down, then Right). Different tie-breaking rules yield different shortest paths of the same length.
5. Repeat until the source cell (label $= 1$ or $0$ depending on convention) is reached.
6. Reverse the recorded path to obtain the source-to-target wire route.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Monotonic Invariant:** At every step of backtracing, the label strictly decreases by 1. This is the mathematical guarantee that backtracing will never loop, never diverge, and will terminate exactly at the source.

### 2.4 Time and Space Complexity

Let $N = m \times n$ be the **total number of grid cells** in the routing region, and $k$ be the length of the shortest path (in cells).

| Metric | Formula | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| Time Complexity (Wave Phase) | $O(N)$ | Each cell is enqueued and dequeued at most once; each edge is examined a constant number of times |
| Time Complexity (Backtrace Phase) | $O(k)$ | Each cell on the path is visited once |
| Overall Time Complexity | $O(N)$ | Wave phase dominates; backtracing is linear in path length |
| Space Complexity (Label Grid) | $O(N)$ | One label value per grid cell |
| Space Complexity (Queue, worst case) | $O(N)$ | In the worst case, the entire grid is in the queue simultaneously |
| Total Space Complexity | $O(N)$ | Sum of label grid + queue + path storage |
| Shortest Path Length | $L = \text{Grid}[x_T][y_T] - 1$ | Target label minus 1 (in the convention where source label = 1) |

### 2.5 Memory Optimization Variants (KTU Reference Points)

| Variant | Memory Trick | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard Lee** | Full $N \times N$ label grid | Maximum $O(N)$ memory |
| **Two-Grid Lee** | Keep only current and previous wavefront layers | Memory $\to O(\text{frontier size})$, but backtracing becomes harder |
| **Coding/Compression** | Use bit-packing or run-length encoding of obstacles | Faster obstacle checks, more complex code |
| **Hadlock's Algorithm** | Counts detours, not distances | Faster on dense grids, but only for $k$-shortest paths |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Lee's Algorithm is the **conceptual backbone** of:

- **Global and detailed routing** in commercial VLSI place-and-route tools (in modified form).
- **PCB (Printed Circuit Board) routing** in tools like Cadence Allegro, Mentor Expedition.
- **Maze-solving robots** in robotics and embedded systems.
- **Network packet routing** in certain grid-based QoS algorithms.
- **FPGA routing architectures** in tools like VPR (Versatile Place and Route, University of Toronto).
- **Bioinformatics grid alignment** (conceptually similar to Smith-Waterman on a 2-D grid).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off Caveat:** Despite its optimality guarantee, **plain Lee's Algorithm is rarely used in production EDA tools** because of its $O(N)$ memory requirement, which is prohibitive for modern VLSI designs with millions of grid cells. It is, however, the **teaching benchmark** — and the conceptual root of every modern maze router.

### 2.7 The Manhattan Distance Lower Bound

For any path from $S$ to $T$, the **Manhattan Distance** provides a hard lower bound on the path length:

$$L_{\min} = \vert x_T - x_S \vert + \vert y_T - y_S \vert$$

Lee's Algorithm always produces a path of length $L = L_{\min}$ in the **absence of obstacles**. With obstacles, the path length is:

$$L = L_{\min} + \text{detour cost}$$

where the detour cost is the additional grid cells traversed to navigate around obstacles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Canonical Worked Example — 7×7 Grid with Obstacles

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example Setup:** Consider a $7 \times 7$ routing grid where:
> - Source $S$ is at cell $(1, 1)$ (row, col, 0-indexed).
> - Target $T$ is at cell $(5, 5)$.
> - Three blocked cells form a diagonal barrier: $(2, 3)$, $(3, 3)$, and $(4, 3)$.
> - A 4-directional (Manhattan) connectivity model is used.

We will execute Lee's Algorithm end-to-end to demonstrate both the wave propagation and backtracing phases.

### 3.2 Step 1 — Grid Initialization

Each cell is initialized as:
- $0$ for blocked cells
- $-1$ (unvisited) for free cells

The source cell $S(1,1)$ is seeded with label $1$ and enqueued.

**Initial Grid State (labels, with $-1$ shown as `.` for clarity):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Grid}_{\text{init}} =
\begin{bmatrix}
. & . & . & . & . & . & . \\
. & \mathbf{1} & . & . & . & . & . \\
. & . & . & 0 & . & . & . \\
. & . & . & 0 & . & . & . \\
. & . & . & 0 & . & . & . \\
. & . & . & . & . & \mathbf{T} & . \\
. & . & . & . & . & . & .
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Where $\mathbf{1}$ denotes the source label, $\mathbf{T}$ denotes the target (unvisited at this point), and $0$ denotes a blocked cell.

### 3.3 Step 2 — Wave Propagation Iteration by Iteration

The FIFO queue processes cells in order. We trace the wavefront step by step.

**Iteration 1 — Process $S(1,1)$, label $= 1$:**

- Examine 4 neighbours of $(1,1)$: $(0,1), (2,1), (1,0), (1,2)$. All are free and unvisited. Each gets label $= 1 + 1 = 2$.
- Queue after Iteration 1: $\{(0,1), (2,1), (1,0), (1,2)\}$, all with label $2$.

**Iteration 2 — Process $(0,1)$, label $= 2$:**

- Neighbours: $(-1,1)$ out of bounds, $(1,1)$ already visited, $(0,0)$ free, $(0,2)$ free.
- Assign label $3$ to $(0,0)$ and $(0,2)$.
- Queue grows with new label-$3$ cells.

**Iteration 2 (continued) — Process $(2,1)$:**

- Neighbours: $(1,1)$ visited, $(3,1)$ free, $(2,0)$ free, $(2,2)$ free.
- Assign label $3$ to $(3,1)$, $(2,0)$, $(2,2)$.

This pattern continues — every iteration extends the wave by one Manhattan ring.

**Key Mathematical Property of the BFS Wave:**

At any iteration $t$, the wavefront consists of all cells at **Manhattan distance $t$** from the source, **in the obstacle-free subgraph**. The label of a cell equals its **shortest obstacle-aware Manhattan distance** from the source:

$$L_{\text{Lee}}(i,j) = \min_{P \in \text{Paths}(S \to (i,j))} \text{len}(P)$$

### 3.4 Step 3 — Final Labelled Grid (After Wave Reaches Target)

The wave propagates until the target cell $(5,5)$ is reached. The final label grid is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Grid}_{\text{final}} =
\begin{bmatrix}
4 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 & 7 & 8 \\
3 & \mathbf{1} & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 \\
4 & 3 & 4 & 0 & 5 & 6 & 7 \\
5 & 4 & 5 & 0 & 5 & 6 & 7 \\
6 & 5 & 6 & 0 & 5 & 6 & 7 \\
7 & 6 & 7 & 8 & 7 & \mathbf{T} & 7 \\
8 & 7 & 8 & 9 & 8 & 7 & 8
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

with $\text{Grid}[5][5] = 8$ being the target's final label.

**Distance Verification:**

The Manhattan lower bound is:

$$L_{\min} = \vert 5 - 1 \vert + \vert 5 - 1 \vert = 4 + 4 = 8 \text{ cells}$$

The algorithm achieved $L = 8$ — but note the obstacle at $(2,3), (3,3), (4,3)$ is in column 3, **between** the source at column 1 and the target at column 5. We need to verify that the path **did** navigate around the obstacle or went over it. Looking at the grid, the path goes through column 2 (labels 2, 4, 6, 8 in rows 1, 2, 3, 4, 5) and crosses column 3 at row 0 (label 4) or row 5 (label 7). The shortest path goes via row 5 to bypass the barrier, achieving length 8.

### 3.5 Step 4 — Backtracing from Target to Source

Starting at $T(5,5)$ with label $8$:

**Path reconstruction (working backwards):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(5,5) \xrightarrow{L=8} &(5,4) \quad [\text{label } 7] \\
&\to (5,3) \quad [\text{label } 6] \\
&\to (5,2) \quad [\text{label } 5] \\
&\to (5,1) \quad [\text{label } 4] \\
&\to (4,1) \quad [\text{label } 3] \\
&\to (3,1) \quad [\text{label } 2] \\
&\to (2,1) \quad [\text{label } 1] \quad \text{(which equals source neighbour)} \\
&\to (1,1) \quad [\text{label } \mathbf{1}, \text{ source}]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reversed final path (source to target):**

$$S(1,1) \to (2,1) \to (3,1) \to (4,1) \to (5,1) \to (5,2) \to (5,3) \to (5,4) \to T(5,5)$$

**Path Statistics:**

- **Path Length $L$** $= 8$ cells
- **Number of Bends $B$** $= 1$ (one turn at $(5,1)$)
- **Wirelength $W$** $= 8 \times \text{cell\_pitch}$ microns

### 3.6 Algebraic Derivation of the Monotonic Invariant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Monotonic Label Invariant of Lee's Algorithm):** For any two cells $A$ and $B$ where $A$ is enqueued strictly before $B$, it must hold that $\text{label}(A) \leq \text{label}(B)$.

**Proof by Induction on the BFS Layers:**

**Base Case:** The source cell $S$ is the only cell enqueued with label $1$. All other cells are unvisited with label $-1 < 1$. The invariant holds trivially.

**Inductive Step:** Assume the invariant holds at the start of iteration $t$. Suppose a cell $C$ is dequeued at iteration $t$ with label $k$. For any free unvisited neighbour $C'$ of $C$, we set $\text{label}(C') = k + 1$ and enqueue it. Since $C$ was enqueued at the start of iteration $t$ or earlier, and all cells in the FIFO up to and including $C$ have labels $\leq k$ (by the inductive hypothesis), the newly enqueued $C'$ with label $k + 1$ preserves the invariant.

**Conclusion:** Labels in the FIFO are non-decreasing. Therefore, the BFS processes cells in **non-decreasing order of their distance from $S$**. Consequently, the **first label** assigned to a cell is its **shortest-path distance** from $S$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.7 Complexity Derivation

**Time Complexity Derivation:**

Each grid cell is enqueued at most once and dequeued at most once. Each dequeue examines at most $4$ neighbours (in 4-directional) or $8$ neighbours (in 8-directional). Therefore:

$$T_{\text{wave}}(N) = \sum_{\text{cell } c} O(1) \cdot \deg(c) = O(4N) = O(N)$$

Backtracing touches $L \leq N$ cells, each with $O(1)$ neighbour checks:

$$T_{\text{back}}(L) = O(L) \leq O(N)$$

Total:

$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{wave}} + T_{\text{back}} = O(N) + O(N) = O(N)$$

**Space Complexity Derivation:**

- Label grid: $N$ integers $\Rightarrow O(N)$
- FIFO queue: up to $N$ cells in the worst case $\Rightarrow O(N)$
- Path storage: $L$ cells $\Rightarrow O(L) \leq O(N)$

Total:

$$S_{\text{total}} = O(N) + O(N) + O(N) = O(N)$$

### 3.8 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Property | Lee's Algorithm | Hadlock's Algorithm | A* Search | Soukup's Algorithm |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Optimality | Yes (shortest) | Yes (shortest) | Yes (with admissible heuristic) | Yes |
| Time Complexity | $O(N)$ | Often faster in dense grids | Faster with good heuristic | Faster, but exponential worst case |
| Space Complexity | $O(N)$ | $O(N)$ | $O(N)$ | $O(N)$ |
| BFS vs DFS | BFS | BFS with detour count | Best-first search | DFS with backtracking |
| Heuristic Used | None (pure BFS) | Detour count only | Manhattan + obstacle heuristic | None |
| KTU Exam Frequency | **Very High** | Medium | Medium | Low |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Two-Phase Functional Architecture of Lee's Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Routing Request] --> B[Initialize Grid Labels]
    B --> C[Mark All Free Cells as -1 Unvisited]
    C --> D[Mark All Blocked Cells as 0 Obstacle]
    D --> E[Enqueue Source Cell S with Label 1]
    E --> F{Queue Empty?}
    F -- No --> G[Dequeue Front Cell C with Label L]
    G --> H[Examine 4 Manhattan Neighbours of C]
    H --> I{Neighbour N is Target T?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Set Label of T to L plus 1 and Terminate Wave]
    I -- No --> K{N is Free and Unvisited?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Set Label of N to L plus 1]
    L --> M[Enqueue N into FIFO]
    M --> H
    K -- No --> H
    H --> N{All Neighbours Examined?}
    N -- No --> H
    N -- Yes --> F
    F -- Yes --> O[Declare Routing Failure No Path Exists]
    J --> P[Phase 2 Begin Backtracing]
    P --> Q[Set Current Cell to Target T]
    Q --> R[Examine 4 Neighbours of Current]
    R --> S{Neighbour Label equals Current Label minus 1?}
    S -- Yes --> T[Move to that Neighbour and Record Path]
    S -- No --> R
    T --> U{Current is Source S?}
    U -- No --> R
    U -- Yes --> V[Reverse Recorded Path]
    V --> W[Return Source to Target Route]
    W --> X[End Routing Success]
```

### 4.4 Backtracing Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BT1[Start at Target Cell T] --> BT2[Read Current Label L cur]
    BT2 --> BT3[Scan 4 Neighbours]
    BT3 --> BT4{Neighbour Label equals L cur minus 1?}
    BT4 -- No --> BT3
    BT4 -- Yes --> BT5[Apply Tie Breaking Rule]
    BT5 --> BT6[Move to Selected Neighbour]
    BT6 --> BT7[Append Cell to Path List]
    BT7 --> BT8{Current equals Source S?}

```
