This is the essence of **Verification-driven Design**. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define VLSI simulation. List the different levels of abstraction at which VLSI circuits can be simulated.** #### Model Answer: **VLSI simulation** is the use of computer-based mathematical models and numerical algorithms to predict the behavior of a VLSI circuit before fabrication. It is essential to verify correctness, performance, power, and timing. **Levels of Abstraction (top-down):** 1. **Behavioral/System Level** — Algorithm-level description in SystemC / MATLAB. 2. **Register-Transfer Level (RTL)** — Register + combinational logic in Verilog/VHDL. 3. **Gate/Logic Level** — Netlist of standard cells. 4. **Switch Level** — Transistors as ideal switches. 5. **Circuit (Transistor) Level** — Full nonlinear SPICE simulation with BSIM models. > **Mark Distribution:** Definition = 1 mark; Five levels listed = 2 marks. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is the difference between static and dynamic simulation? Give one example tool for each.** #### Model Answer: | Aspect | Static Simulation | Dynamic Simulation | |---|---|---| | **Time** | Time-independent (DC) | Time-evolving (transient) | | **Purpose** | Bias point, DC noise margin | Glitches, races, delay | | **Solver** | Nonlinear algebraic | ODE / event-driven | | **Example Tool** | HSPICE `.OP` analysis | HSPICE `.TRAN` / ModelSim | > **Mark Distribution:** Comparison points = 2 marks; Tool examples = 1 mark. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **event-driven simulation algorithm** used in digital VLSI simulators. How does it achieve higher speed compared to simple-cycle simulation? **(b) [7 Marks]** A CMOS inverter has the following parameters: - $V_{DD} = 1.8\,\text{V}$, $V_{THn} = 0.4\,\text{V}$, $\vert V_{THp}\vert = 0.5\,\text{V}$ - $(W/L)_n = 2$, $(W/L)_p = 4$, $\mu_n/\mu_p = 3$ - Compute the **switching threshold $V_M$**. --- #### Model Solution for (a): **1. Initialization (1 mark):** All nets set to default logic value (X, 0, 1, Z). Primary inputs forced by stimulus. Time = 0. **2. Event Scheduling (2 marks):** A change in a net's logic state is called an **event**. Each event carries: - Timestamp `t_event` - Net identifier - New logic value Events are stored in a **priority queue** (min-heap) ordered by timestamp. **3. Event Processing Loop (2 marks):** ``` while (event_queue not empty and time < t_end): event = pop_min(event_queue) sim_time = event.timestamp for each gate in fanout(event.net): if all inputs of gate are stable: new_output = truth_table[gate.type, inputs] if new_output != gate.current_output: schedule event at (sim_time + gate.delay, gate.output, new_output) ``` **4. Speedup over Simple-Cycle Simulation (2 marks):** - **Simple-cycle** evaluates **every gate every clock tick** → $O(N \cdot T)$ complexity. - **Event-driven** evaluates only gates whose inputs changed → $O(N + E)$ where $E \ll N \cdot T$ in typical circuits. - For a quiescent circuit, event-driven is **near-zero cost**; simple-cycle is full cost. - **Speedup ratio** can exceed $100\times$ in steady state. > **Examiner Note (1 mark):** Mention **zero-delay**, **unit-delay**, and **pin-to-pin delay** as the three delay-model options. --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute drive strengths. Let base transconductance $k_0 = \mu_n C_{ox}$ (assume $C_{ox}$ constant). - $k_n = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n = 3\mu_p C_{ox} \cdot 2 = 6\,\mu_p C_{ox}$ - $k_p = \mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_p = \mu_p C_{ox} \cdot 4 = 4\,\mu_p C_{ox}$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the ratio. $$\sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}} = \sqrt{\frac{4}{6}} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \approx 0.8165$$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Substitute into the switching-threshold formula. $$V_M = \frac{V_{THn} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{THp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Plug in values. $$V_M = \frac{0.4 + 0.8165 \cdot (1.8 - 0.5)}{1 + 0.8165} = \frac{0.4 + 0.8165 \cdot 1.3}{1.8165} = \frac{0.4 + 1.0615}{1.8165} = \frac{1.4615}{1.8165}$$ $$\boxed{V_M \approx 0.805\,\text{V}}$$ > **Interpretation:** $V_M = 0.805\,\text{V}$ is above $V_{DD}/2 = 0.9\,\text{V}$? No — it's slightly **below** 0.9 V because the PMOS is over-sized. A perfectly symmetric design would yield $V_M = 0.9\,\text{V}$. --- ### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **different simulation levels** (behavioral, RTL, gate, switch, circuit) with their trade-offs in terms of accuracy, speed, and typical application. **(b) [7 Marks]** A clock distribution network is modeled as a 5-stage uniform RC ladder with $R = 25\,\Omega$ per segment and $C = 200\,\text{fF}$ per node. Compute the **Elmore delay** from input to output node. --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Comparison Table (5 marks):** | Level | Accuracy | Speed | Tool | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Behavioral** | Low (≈60%) | ★★★★★ | SystemC, MATLAB | Architectural exploration | | **RTL** | Medium (≈80%) | ★★★★ | ModelSim, VCS | Functional verification | | **Gate** | Medium-High (≈90%) | ★★★ | VCS, Questa | Functional + SDF timing | | **Switch** | High (≈95%) | ★★ | IRSIM | Transistor netlist w/o parasitic | | **Circuit** | Very High (≈99%) | ★ | HSPICE, Spectre | Critical-path analog, cell characterization | **Trade-off Discussion (2 marks):** - As level descends: accuracy ↑, speed ↓, setup-time ↑. - A real SoC design flow uses **all five** — behavioral for architecture, RTL for functionality, gate for SDF timing, circuit for cell library characterization, switch rarely (legacy). > **Examiner's Note:** Mention that **mixed-signal SoCs** require co-simulation across levels. --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Given:** $N = 5$, $R = 25\,\Omega$, $C = 200\,\text{fF} = 200 \times 10^{-15}\,\text{F}$. **Step 1 (3 marks):** Apply the Elmore delay formula for an N-stage ladder: $$\tau_{PD} = \frac{N(N+1)}{2} \cdot RC$$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute $RC$ product: $$RC = 25 \times 200 \times 10^{-15} = 5000 \times 10^{-15} = 5 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{s} = 5\,\text{ps}$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute $N(N+1)/2$: $$\frac{5 \cdot 6}{2} = 15$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute total Elmore delay: $$\tau_{PD} = 15 \cdot 5\,\text{ps} = 75\,\text{ps}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_{PD} = 75\,\text{ps}}$$ > **Engineering Insight:** For GHz designs, this 75 ps is roughly 25% of a 5 GHz clock period. Real clock trees use **H-tree** or **mesh** structures to reduce this further. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting $\sqrt{k_p/k_n}$**: Students often write $V_M = (V_{THn}+V_{THp})/2$. This is wrong — it's the **drives-strength weighted** threshold. > 2. **Confusing $\mu_n/\mu_p$ with $k_n/k_p$**: $k$ already includes $W/L$. Multiply $\mu$ by $W/L$ before computing the ratio. > 3. **Elmore vs. Logical Effort**: Elmore is RC-based; Logical Effort is dimensionless. Don't mix the two formulas. > 4. **Static vs. Dynamic Power**: Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if leakage is included in dynamic power (it's separate — $P_{stat}$). > 5. **Event-driven vs. Cycle-based**: Cycle-based is faster but **assumes synchronous** design. Event-driven handles async glitches. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST** - ✅ **VLSI Simulation = Virtual prototyping** of chips before fabrication. - ✅ **5 Abstraction Levels**: Behavioral → RTL → Gate → Switch → Circuit (top → bottom = faster → more accurate). - ✅ **Event-driven algorithm**: complexity $O(N+E)$; gates evaluated only on input change. - ✅ **SPICE/circuit simulation**: solves nonlinear ODEs via Newton-Raphson + Sparse LU; $O(N^{1.3-2})$ per timestep. - ✅ **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** vs. **Dynamic Simulation**: STA is faster but pattern-independent. - ✅ **Mixed-Signal**: requires co-simulation kernels (Verilog-AMS, SMASH). - ✅ **Switching Threshold**: $V_M = \dfrac{V_{THn} + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{THp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}}$ - ✅ **Dynamic Power**: $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$ (quadratic in $V_{DD}$). - ✅ **Elmore Delay** for N-stage RC ladder: $\tau = \dfrac{N(N+1)}{2} RC$. - ✅ **Thermal Voltage** at 300 K = **25.85 mV** (remember for BSIM calculations). - ✅ **Verification "V-Model"**: Spec ↔ Verify, RTL ↔ RTL-sim, Gate ↔ Gate-sim, Layout ↔ Timing-sim. - ✅ **Three Delay Models**: Zero-delay, Unit-delay, Pin-to-pin (SDF-annotated). - ✅ **Logic Values**: 0, 1, X (unknown), Z (high-impedance). - ✅ **Convergence failure in SPICE** = timestep-too-small error → design problem or wrong model card. - ✅ **Industry tools to remember**: HSPICE, Spectre, ModelSim, VCS, Questa, NC-Sim, PrimeTime, IRSIM. - ✅ **Why simulation matters**: Mask-set cost >$50M at 7nm; bugs post-tapeout are fatal. - ✅ **Testbench self-checking**: use `$finish` or `assert` to halt sim — never run open-loop.
